### [1] [Scaling Generative Foundation Models for Chest Radiography with Rectified Flow Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19460)
*Fabio De Sousa Ribeiro,Emma A. M. Stanley,Charles Jones,Tian Xia,Dominic C. Marshall,Laurent Renard Triché,Christopher V. Cosgriff,Panagiotis Dimitrakopoulos,Sotirios A. Tsaftaris,Ben Glocker*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 1.3B-parameter generative foundation model for chest radiographs trained from scratch on 1.6T tokens, enabling controllable, high-fidelity synthesis across demographics, views, and pathologies; claims expert-level indistinguishability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome generalization gaps and utility limitations of radiographic AI by enabling diverse, controllable synthetic data and robust evaluation across subpopulations and acquisition settings.

Method: Train from scratch a billion-parameter generative model on a heterogeneous chest radiograph dataset (1.2M images) with expert-guided metadata; support controllable generation/editing across demographic subgroups, views, and about a dozen pathologies; large-scale token dataset (1.6T tokens).

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art fidelity in radiograph synthesis; outputs indistinguishable from real radiographs to clinical experts; provides controllable synthesis across subgroups, views, and pathologies.

Conclusion: Establishes a new benchmark for chest radiograph synthesis, enabling dataset diversification and robust evaluation of diagnostic models; demonstrates potential for real-world clinical utility.

Abstract: We introduce the first generative foundation model for chest radiograph synthesis trained from scratch at the billion-parameter scale. Existing radiographic AI models often suffer from poor generalisation across patient subpopulations, institutions, and acquisition settings, resulting in limited real-world clinical utility. Controlled, high-fidelity synthesis of chest radiographs is a promising path toward diversifying clinical datasets and evaluating the robustness of diagnostic models. Therefore, we present the largest specialist generative foundation model for chest radiographs to date, with over 1.3B parameters, trained for 1.6T tokens on a curated, heterogeneous dataset comprising 1.2M radiographs and clinical expert-guided metadata. Our model supports controllable radiograph generation and editing across multiple demographic subgroups, acquisition views, and a dozen pathologies. Moreover, we significantly advance the state of the art in radiograph synthesis fidelity, producing images that are indistinguishable from real radiographs to clinical experts.

</details>


### [2] [LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression for Vision Transformer Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19483)
*Jiaqi Zhang,Ashton Lee,Anthony Wong,John Zou,Sami BuGhanem,Randall Balestriero*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LEAP introduces an adaptive curriculum for ViT feature-based knowledge distillation by progressively leveraging teacher features from easy to hard, enabling faster convergence, higher accuracy, and reduced training cost across model sizes and datasets; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the persistent teacher-student feature gap and high computational cost in distilling large Vision Transformers for edge deployment, enabling efficient training and better student performance.

Method: LEAP uses a layer-skipping, progression-based curriculum that treats intermediate teacher feature maps as targets of increasing difficulty. The student builds foundational representations before mastering higher-level abstractions. The approach includes adaptive difficulty selection and early-stopping of teacher inference during early training to reduce overhead, and is effective across various student sizes and dataset scales.

Result: On ImageNet-100 with ViT-S, achieves 90.1% accuracy (+12.24% over baseline). On ImageNet-1K retrieval tasks: Oxford (+3.84%) and Paris (+7.75%) improvements. Training efficiency gains include 25.1% reductions in FLOPs and 21% reductions in training time for ImageNet-100 via early stopping.

Conclusion: The LEAP curriculum effectively mitigates the teacher–student gap in feature-based KD for ViTs, accelerates convergence, improves accuracy, and reduces computation, with broad generalizability across student sizes and data regimes; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) with Vision Transformer (ViT) backbones, such as DINOv2, have become essential for downstream tasks like object recognition and semantic segmentation. The immense computational requirements of backbones often necessitate distillation into smaller architectures for edge deployment. Feature-based knowledge distillation (KD) often suffers from the teacher-student gap; the student struggles to imitate teacher's complex feature map due to its limited capacity. To mitigate this bottleneck, we propose LEAP: Layer-skipping Efficiency via Adaptive Progression, a training curriculum for ViT feature-based knowledge distillation. By utilizing the teacher's intermediate feature maps as a sequence of progressively more difficult targets, our curriculum allows the student to build a foundational representation before tackling higher-level abstractions. Our results demonstrate that this paradigm significantly accelerates convergence through adaptive difficulty selection across various student model sizes and dataset scales. With our curriculum, the LEAP-distilled ViT-S achieves 90.1% accuracy on ImageNet-100, a +12.24% improvement compared with baseline. On ImageNet-1K, LEAP achieves +3.84% and +7.75% improvement for the instance retrieval task on the Oxford and Paris datasets, respectively. Furthermore, the curriculum enables 25.1% savings in training FLOPs and 21% savings in training time on ImageNet-100 by implementing early-stopping for teacher inference during the initial stages of training. Code is available at https://github.com/KevinZ0217/LEAP

</details>


### [3] [LooseControlVideo: Directorial Video Control using Spatial Blocking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19495)
*Shariq Farooq Bhat,Niloy J. Mitra,Kalyan Sunkavalli*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Loosely controllable text-to-video via sparse 3D boxes as a blocking proxy; DNOCS encoding of 3D size, orientation and depth-ordered occlusions; fine-tuning Wan 2.2 on DNOCS-labeled video data; achieves substantial gains over 2D-box and flow baselines on nuScenes, HO-3D, BEHAVE (trajectory error, rigid motion, occlusion).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable precise and intuitive 3D-aware control for multi-object video synthesis without dense, frame-accurate guidance; current depth-conditioned methods are labor-intensive and brittle for dynamic, deformable scenes.

Method: Represent scene layout and dynamics with sparse oriented 3D boxes used as a blocking proxy; introduce DNOCS encoding to capture 3D size, orientation, and depth-ordered occlusions; fine-tune Wan 2.2 on a DNOCS-annotated video dataset; allow localized refinements (e.g., adjust a jump trajectory or add an interaction) with minimal global disruption.

Result: Significant performance gains over 2D-box and flow-based baselines across nuScenes, HO-3D, BEHAVE: 1.2x–3x improvement in Trajectory Error; ~2x improvement in Rigid Motion Consistency; ~1.5x–2x improvement in Occlusion Accuracy.

Conclusion: Oriented 3D primitives with a DNOCS encoding provide a strong geometric prior that enables controllable, high-quality multi-agent video authoring, with support for local edits and improved realism in occlusions and dynamics.

Abstract: Precise 3D spatial orchestration in text-to-video generation remains a significant challenge, particularly for multi-object scenes where semantic layout and temporal dynamics are often entangled. While existing depth-conditioned models achieve good structural fidelity, they necessitate dense, frame-accurate guidance that is labor-intensive to author for dynamic events involving deformable objects. We present LooseControlVideo, a framework that enables intuitive and expressive control by using sparse, oriented 3D boxes as a "blocking" proxy. This allows users to author high-level layout and trajectory while leveraging a video generative model to generate realistic occlusions, dynamics and interactions. We achieve this by fine-tuning a Wan 2.2 backbone on a video dataset annotated with DNOCS, a novel encoding for 3D size, orientation and depth-ordered occlusions. Furthermore, our method allows for localized refinement, such as adjusting a jump trajectory or adding an interaction, with minimal disruption to the global scene context. Extensive evaluations on the nuScenes, HO-3D, and BEHAVE benchmarks demonstrate that LooseControlVideo significantly outperforms existing 2D-box and flow-based baselines. Our findings indicate a 1.2x to 3x improvement in Trajectory Error; 2x improvement in Rigid Motion Consistency; and a 1.5x to 2x increase in Occlusion Accuracy over current state-of-the-art layout-conditioned models, demonstrating that oriented 3D primitives provide good geometric prior for complex, multi-agent video authoring.

</details>


### [4] [ImageWAM: Do World Action Models Really Need Video Generation, or Just Image Editing?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19531)
*Yuyang Zhang,Wenyao Zhang,Zekun Qi,He Zhang,Haitao Lin,Jingbo Zhang,Yao Mu,Xiaokang Yang,Wenjun Zeng,Xin Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ImageWAM uses pretrained image-editing models for robot action prediction, avoiding video generation to improve efficiency and focus on action-relevant changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video-based World Action Models (WAMs) are costly due to dense multi-frame prediction, waste capacity on non-action-relevant details, and risk of error accumulation in long-horizon imagination; a more task-aligned prior is desirable.

Method: Leverage pretrained image-editing models; do not decode target frames during inference; condition a flow-matching action expert on KV caches from image-editing denoising; ground task instructions to localized visual changes via edit pretraining; use caches as compact world-action context.

Result: ImageWAM outperforms standard VLA baselines and competitive WAMs without extra policy pretraining across simulators and real-world; reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs; attention analysis shows caches focus on task-relevant changes.

Conclusion: Image editing-based world-action modeling is an effective, more efficient alternative to video-based WAMs for action prediction, aligning editing priors with current-to-target visual changes and reducing computation while maintaining performance.

Abstract: World Action Models (WAMs) commonly rely on video generation to bridge visual world modeling and robot control. However, video-based WAMs face three coupled limitations: dense multi-frame future tokens make inference costly, full video prediction spends capacity on action-irrelevant temporal and appearance details, and long-horizon future imagination may introduce errors that mislead action prediction. These issues raise a simple question: Does world action model really need video generation? We propose ImageWAM, a simple WAM framework that repurposes pretrained image editing models for robot action prediction. In contrast to video generation, image editing provides a better-matched prior: it only needs to model a target-frame transformation, focuses on action-relevant current-to-target visual differences, and grounds task instructions to localized visual changes through edit pretraining. In practice, ImageWAM does not decode the target frame at inference time; instead, it conditions a flow-matching action expert on the KV caches produced by image-editing denoising, using them as a compact world-action context. ImageWAM outperforms standard VLA baselines and matching competitive WAMs without additional policy pretraining across different simulator and real-world experiments. It also reduces FLOPs to 1/6 and latency to 1/4 of video-based WAMs. Attention analysis further shows that editing caches focus on task-relevant change regions, supporting image editing as an effective alternative to video-based world-action modeling.

</details>


### [5] [PerceptionDLM: Parallel Region Perception with Multimodal Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19534)
*Yueyi Sun,Yuhao Wang,Jason Li,Ye Tian,Tao Zhang,Jacky Mai,Yihan Wang,Haochen Wang,Jinbin Bai,Ling Yang,Yunhai Tong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based multimodal LLM enabling parallel multi-region perception and captioning; introduces ParaDLC-Bench; achieves speedups with competitive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive MLLMs are inefficient for perceiving and describing many regions in an image; parallel region perception could greatly improve inference efficiency.

Method: PerceptionDLM-Base foundation with diffusion LLMs; efficient prompting and structured attention masking enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions; parallel decoding at sequence and token levels to generate region captions.

Result: Maintains competitive region captioning performance while achieving substantial inference speedups for multi-region perception tasks; claims to be first to realize parallel region captioning with diffusion LLMs; code, models, and datasets released.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the potential of diffusion LLMs for efficient, parallel visual perception; introduces ParaDLC-Bench for evaluating parallel perception and perception efficiency; opens avenue for scalable multi-region understanding with diffusion models.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual understanding tasks. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits their efficiency for perception tasks that require captioning multiple regions. In this work, we propose PerceptionDLM, a multimodal diffusion language model optimized for efficient parallel region perception. Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, a strong foundational baseline that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source diffusion MLLMs, our architecture fully leverages the parallel decoding nature of DLMs. Specifically, we introduce efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, allowing the model to generate region descriptions in parallel at both the sequence and token levels. This design significantly improves inference efficiency compared with existing approaches that process regions sequentially. To systematically evaluate the parallelism property of visual perception capability for DLMs, we construct a new Parallel Detailed Localized Captioning Benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) by scaling the DLC-Bench to include multiple region masks per image, enabling joint evaluation of both caption quality and inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that PerceptionDLM maintains competitive performance in region captioning while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks. Our results highlight the potential of multimodal diffusion language models for efficient, parallel visual perception. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging the advantages of diffusion language models. Code, models, and datasets are released.

</details>


### [6] [Mix-QVLA: Task-Evidence-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization of Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19565)
*Navin Ranjan,Andreas Savakis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Mix-QVLA introduces a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models, aligning quantized variants to full-precision reference decisions using gradient-weighted evidence maps, soft-bottleneck optimization, and phase-aware sensitivity to allocate bits under budgets, improving accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve task-relevant evidence in quantized VLA models. Conventional PTQ may degrade evidence distribution across boundary activations and task phases, harming decision quality and efficiency.

Method: Quantize variants anchored to full-precision action-token references. Compute normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compare full-precision vs quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion. Use a soft-bottleneck objective to produce layer-wise sensitivity scores. Model phase-dependent sensitivity to capture shifts in layer importance during task execution. Use evidence- and time-aware scores to drive mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets.

Result: On OpenVLA-style policies, Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, it reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, preserves 96.3 average success vs 97.1 for BF16, and achieves 1.52x inference speedup.

Conclusion: By focusing on task-relevant evidence across functional boundaries and task phases, Mix-QVLA enables more effective mixed-precision quantization for VLA models, achieving substantial memory and speed gains with minimal accuracy loss.

Abstract: We propose Mix-QVLA, a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models. Mix-QVLA anchors each quantized variant to the full-precision action-token reference decision and evaluates whether quantization preserves task-relevant evidence across key VLA functional boundaries. It computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, capturing changes in both the strength and allocation of decision-supporting evidence. A soft-bottleneck objective aggregates boundary-level degradation into layer-wise sensitivity scores. Mix-QVLA further models sensitivity throughout task execution, capturing phase-dependent shifts in layer importance rather than assuming a fixed sensitivity profile. The resulting evidence- and time-aware scores guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies show that Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3 average success compared with 97.1 for the BF16 model, and achieves a 1.52x inference speedup.

</details>


### [7] [Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings for Controllable and Generalizable Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19584)
*Chengzhi Mao,Xudong Lin,Wen-Sheng Chu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LIVE uses language to guide the vision encoder at inference, producing task-centric embeddings without task-specific retraining, enabling controllable, generalizable visual representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision models are typically fixed feature extractors that rely on heavy task-specific retraining of downstream models; a more flexible, instruction-driven approach could improve adaptability and reduce reliance on large downstream models.

Method: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE) leverages language as high-level guidance to modulate the vision encoder during inference, producing task-relevant embeddings and allowing dynamic task adaptation without retraining.

Result: LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (noted as +34 MMVP), exceeds performance of bigger vision-language models on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks.

Conclusion: An instruction-driven paradigm yields adaptive, controllable vision representations and points toward a scalable path for instruction-guided visual intelligence without task-specific retraining.

Abstract: Vision foundation models are typically trained as static feature extractors, placing the burden of task adaptation onto large downstream models. We propose an alternative paradigm: instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, we use language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder. Our method, Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE), leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, removing the need for task-specific retraining. This enables the encoder to focus on contextually relevant aspects of the input, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations. Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (+34 points on MMVP), surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks -- offering a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence.

</details>


### [8] [GB-LSR: A Fast Local Spectral Image Representation with a Single Global Bandwidth for Continuous Reconstruction and Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19617)
*Max Shad,Naeem Khoshnevis*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GB-LSR is a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction using a single global trainable bandwidth scalar. It partitions images into non-overlapping patches carrying Fourier basis coefficients predicted from shared encoder features, enabling continuous-coordinate reconstruction with cost independent of image size. It outperforms matched-budget LIIF/LTE/WIRE on native reconstruction and is significantly faster, with an ASR extension showing competitive PSNR-Y and substantial speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve efficiency and scalability of continuous image reconstruction by removing per-patch adaptive bandwidth complexity, while maintaining or improving reconstruction quality and inference speed.

Method: Partition the image domain into non-overlapping square patches. Each patch carries coefficients for a truncated Fourier basis predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. Use a single trainable global bandwidth scalar shared across all patches and images; reconstruction at any continuous coordinate is a fixed-size basis contraction whose cost is independent of image size. Compare three bandwidth-handling variants: (1) a trainable global scalar (main), (2) a fixed global scalar, and (3) a per-patch bandwidth field.

Result: On native reconstruction benchmarks (Kodak, Set14, Urban100), the main variant achieves 2.8-3.6 dB higher PSNR and 0.11-0.15 lower LPIPS than matched-budget amortized LIIF/LTE/WIRE re-implementations, with about four times faster inference than the slowest baseline. Per-patch adaptive-bandwidth variants offer no improvement. In an ASR extension, GB-LSR delivers competitive PSNR-Y and runs 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN and 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4. Removing 4-corner local-ensemble averaging yields 1.77x speedup with 35% lower peak memory and negligible PSNR change; widening the RDN encoder from 64 to 96 channels provides a small PSNR gain with 1.58x speedup and 31% lower peak memory. Native claims pertain to the matched-budget amortized protocol; ASR claims pertain to a canonical SR protocol.

Conclusion: A single global bandwidth scalar suffices for high-quality, efficient continuous image reconstruction with a fixed-grid local spectral representation. The approach delivers favorable accuracy-speed trade-offs and robustness across native reconstruction and ASR benchmarks; simple bandwidth handling outperforms more complex per-patch variants.

Abstract: We present GB-LSR (Global-Bandwidth Local Spectral Representation), a fixed-grid local spectral representation for continuous image reconstruction. The image domain is partitioned into non-overlapping square patches, each carrying coefficients for a truncated Fourier basis predicted from shared convolutional-encoder features. A single trainable scalar bandwidth is shared globally across all patches and images, and reconstruction at any continuous coordinate is a fixed-size basis contraction whose cost is independent of image size. We study three bandwidth-handling variants: a trainable global scalar (main), a fixed global scalar, and a per-patch bandwidth field. On a standardized native-reconstruction benchmark across Kodak, Set14, and Urban100, the main variant outperforms matched-budget amortized LIIF / LTE / WIRE re-implementations by 2.8-3.6 dB PSNR and 0.11-0.15 LPIPS, while running at roughly one-quarter of the slowest baseline's inference cost. The single global scalar suffices empirically: per-patch adaptive-bandwidth alternatives do not improve over it on either a closed-form locality diagnostic or an end-to-end ablation. In a separate arbitrary-scale super-resolution (ASR) extension, GB-LSR achieves competitive PSNR-Y under a canonical-style SR protocol and runs 1.44x faster than LIIF-RDN and 3.25x faster than LTE-SwinIR at x4; within the same extension, a variant trained and evaluated without 4-corner local-ensemble averaging gives a 1.77x speedup with 35% lower peak memory and negligible PSNR change, while additionally widening the RDN encoder from 64 to 96 channels gives a small positive PSNR shift with a 1.58x speedup and 31% lower peak memory. Native-reconstruction claims are scoped to the matched-budget amortized protocol, and ASR claims are scoped to a separate canonical-style SR protocol.

</details>


### [9] [Learning When to Denoise: Optimizing Asynchronous Schedules for Latent Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19662)
*Bingshuo Qian,Xiang Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Learn an asynchronous schedule for multi-representation diffusion models to improve convergence speed and final image quality with less training, via a schedule-corrected objective and a convex, monotone parametric schedule, achieving state-of-the-art-like results on ImageNet-256 with a 675M-parameter XL backbone and AutoGuidance; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-representation diffusion models can benefit from carefully timed denoising of each representation. Fixed or heuristic schedules may limit performance. Learning the schedule could accelerate convergence and improve final quality.

Method: Formulate asynchronous flow matching across multiple representation spaces. Introduce a schedule-corrected objective that keeps local noising-time weights fixed while the schedule adapts. Use a convex and monotone parametric class to instantiate the schedule. Learn the schedule via a fast joint probe using <1% extra training compute.

Result: On ImageNet 256×256 with a 675M-parameter XL backbone: AutoGuidance at 200 epochs achieves FID 1.05, matching 800-epoch SFD-XL with 4x less training. At 600 epochs, FID 1.02, outperforming 1B-parameter SFD-XXL baseline FID 1.04 with a smaller model. Unguided setting: 200 epochs FID 2.37 (better than 800-epoch SFD-XL 2.54 with 4x less training); 600 epochs improves to FID 2.14. Code: GitHub link.

Conclusion: Learning an asynchronous, convex, monotone schedule for multi-representation diffusion yields faster convergence and competitive or superior final image quality with reduced training cost. The schedule-corrected objective preserves local noise-time weights while adapting to the schedule, enabling stable optimization across representations.

Abstract: Multi-representation diffusion models can improve visual synthesis by denoising complementary views of an image, but their performance depends critically on the asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised. We propose to learn this schedule. Our method formulates asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces and uses a schedule-corrected objective that keeps each representation's local noising-time weights fixed as the schedule changes. We instantiate the schedule with a flexible parametric class that is convex and monotone by construction, and learn it using a fast joint probe with less than 1% additional training compute. On ImageNet 256x256, the learned schedule substantially improves both convergence speed and final quality under a matched 675M-parameter XL backbone. With AutoGuidance, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 1.05, matching the 800-epoch SFD-XL baseline with 4x less training. Training to 600 epochs further improves to FID 1.02, outperforming the 1B-parameter SFD-XXL result of FID 1.04 while using a smaller model. In the unguided setting, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 2.37, already below the best 800-epoch SFD-XL result (2.54) at 4x less training, and improves to FID 2.14 at 600 epochs. Code is available at https://github.com/bsq532087/LWD

</details>


### [10] [TeleMorpher: Toward Robust Simultaneous Motion-Location Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19676)
*Haengbok Chung*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A one-shot framework (TeleMorpher) enables simultaneous motion and location editing in videos by leveraging motion priors, a target motion-guided video, and ground-truth motion cues. It disentangles foreground/background, applies a training-free pose warp using motion priors, and injects warped motion into a baseline editor; introduces LPIPS-based metrics for background consistency and motion-edit fidelity. It demonstrates superior quantitative and qualitative results on in-the-wild and TaiChi datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of robust, joint motion-location editing in video generation/editing, enabling controllable edits that modify both how a subject moves and where they are, while preserving appearance and structure.

Method: 1) Separate foreground (protagonist) from background using pre-trained segmentation/inpainting. 2) Apply a training-free pose warping guided by a motion prior derived from a target motion-centric video. 3) Inject the warped motion video into a baseline motion editor during inference to align source and target motions with preserved appearance. 4) Propose two LPIPS-based metrics: background-consistency before/after editing and motion-edit fidelity via skeleton differences of the protagonist.

Result: Empirical evaluations on in-the-wild videos and the TaiChi dataset show TeleMorpher achieves superior performance across quantitative metrics and qualitative assessments, including real-human evaluations.

Conclusion: The framework offers a practical and controllable solution for joint motion-location editing with improved fidelity and reliability, leveraging segmentation/inpainting and a training-free pose warp; success depends on quality of motion priors and segmentation/inpainting modules and may introduce artifacts if inputs are challenging.

Abstract: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in image and video generation and editing. While recent studies have extended these efforts toward motion editing, simultaneously transforming both motion and location-despite its practical importance-remains largely unexplored. To better understand robust motion-location editing, we first analyze the fundamental factors that degrade its quality. Based on this analysis, we propose TeleMorpher, one of the first one-shot frameworks to the best of our knowledge, for simultaneous motion-location editing. Our approach leverages motion priors, a target motion-centric video generated from an off-the-shelf model as motion-editing guidance, and the ground truth motion to enable more controllable and precise motion-location editing. Via this, our framework works as follows: (1) we first disentangle the protagonist and the background via pre-trained segmentation and inpainting models. (2) Then, we introduce a training-free pose warping that edits the protagonist's motion with the motion prior as the guidance. (3) The result of warped motion video is directly injected into a baseline motion editor during inference, mitigating the difference between source and target motions while preserving the appearance of the source video. (4) To enhance the reliability of quantitative evaluations, we propose two new LPIPS-based metrics that measure the background consistency before and after the motion editing and the fidelity of motion editing performance via measuring the difference between the extracted protagonist's skeletons from source and target videos. Experiments with in-the-wild videos and the TaiChi dataset demonstrate that TeleMorpher achieves superior performance across both quantitative and qualitative measurements (real-human evaluation), underscoring its effectiveness.

</details>


### [11] [Vortex: Multi-Modal Fusion System for Intelligent Video Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19682)
*Duc-Tho Nguyen,Hieu-Hoc Tran-Minh,Khanh-Hoa Lam,Hoang-Nhut Ly,Huu-Phuc Huynh,Thanh-Tien Tran,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vortex is a multimodal video retrieval system for a challenge, combining adaptive keyframe extraction, vision-language and speech-derived metadata, and a hybrid retrieval that fuses CLIP and SigLIP2 using Reciprocal Rank Fusion, with Rocchio feedback and a multi-stage temporal search; built on Milvus/Elasticsearch; demonstrated strong competition results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for scalable, semantically rich and temporally aware video search that leverages complementary multimodal embeddings and interactive feedback.

Method: Keyframe extraction; metadata generation via vision-language and ASR models; embeddings from CLIP and SigLIP2 fused by Reciprocal Rank Fusion; Rocchio relevance feedback; multi-stage temporal search for sequential event alignment; scalable indexing on Milvus and Elasticsearch.

Result: Preliminary: 79.6/88 (90.5%); Final: Excellent overall with Outstanding QA; demonstrates complementary strengths and effectiveness of hybrid retrieval.

Conclusion: Vortex provides a robust, scalable foundation for intelligent, context-aware, interactive video retrieval and offers avenues for future research in multimodal, temporally-aware search.

Abstract: This paper presents Vortex, the multimodal video retrieval system developed by our team, FocusOnFun, for the Ho Chi Minh City AI Challenge 2025, designed to advance intelligent multimedia search and temporal reasoning. The system integrates adaptive keyframe extraction, multimodal metadata generation from vision-language and speech models, and a hybrid retrieval strategy that fuses CLIP and SigLIP2 embeddings through Reciprocal Rank Fusion to balance global and fine-grained semantics. To enhance interactivity, Vortex incorporates Rocchio-based relevance feedback and a multi-stage temporal search mechanism for sequential event alignment. Built on Milvus and Elasticsearch, the architecture enables scalable indexing and efficient retrieval. Evaluated in the official competition, our FocusOnFun team's system achieved a score of 79.6/88 (90.5\%) in the Preliminary Round and was further evaluated in the Final Round, achieving an `Excellent' overall performance with `Outstanding' results in the question-answering (QA) task. This demonstrating the complementary strengths of CLIP and SigLIP2 and confirming the effectiveness of the hybrid retrieval approach. The system establishes a robust foundation for future research in intelligent, context-aware, and interactive video retrieval.

</details>


### [12] [Exploring Multi-Modal Large Language Models and Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Fashion Image Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19684)
*Nguyen Cao Hoang,Hoang Bui Le,Nam Vo Hoang,Trung-Nghia Le*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework for composed image retrieval in fashion that uses a multi-modal LLM (LLaVA) to generate attribute-aware triplets and a two-stage fine-tuning strategy, leveraging CLIP-based negatives and prompts to improve fine-grained, compositional retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Composed image retrieval is challenged by subtle attribute variations (color, pattern, texture) and scarce annotated data. Existing negative sampling is simplistic, hindering learning of fine-grained relationships in fashion imagery.

Method: Integrate a multi-modal LLM (LLaVA) to produce attribute-aware triplets from a reference image and a modified text description. Apply a two-stage fine-tuning strategy to enhance contrastive learning. Utilize pretrained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP-ViT/B32) to generate and concatenate sentence-level prompts with relative captions and to expand the pool of negatives via static representations, enabling scalable contrastive training.

Result: Experimental results indicate improved compositional reasoning and finer-grained retrieval performance, demonstrating the framework’s feasibility and potential for fashion retrieval. The approach yields better alignment between visual and textual attributes and more robust handling of attribute variations.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively combines LLM-generated attribute reasoning with staged fine-tuning and expanded negative sampling to advance composed image retrieval in fashion, reducing reliance on large annotated datasets and showing potential for broader application in attribute-centric cross-modal retrieval.

Abstract: Composed image retrieval retrieves a target image using a composed query of a reference image and a modified text description. In the fashion domain, this task requires understanding subtle attribute variations such as color, pattern, and texture. However, existing approaches face limitations due to scarce annotated data and simplistic negative sampling. We propose a novel framework that integrates a multi-modal large language model (LLaVA) to generate attribute-aware triplets and introduces a two-stage fine-tuning strategy to enhance contrastive learning. We leverage pretrained vision-language models, such as CLIP-ViT/B32, to generate and concatenate sentence-level prompts with the relative caption and to scale the number of negatives using static representations. Experimental results demonstrate enhanced compositional reasoning and improved fine-grained retrieval behavior, underscoring the feasibility and potential of the proposed framework for fashion retrieval.

</details>


### [13] [NEST: Narrative Event Structures in Time for Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19706)
*Ali Asgarov,Kaushik Narasimhan,Najibul Haque Sarker,Hani Alomari,Chia-Wei Tang,Anushka Sivakumar,Zaber Ibn Abdul Hakim,Shaurya Mallampati,Chris Thomas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NEST is a new large-scale multimodal benchmark for long-video narrative understanding, featuring 1005 full-length movies, 102 annotated multimodal narrative events, and four tasks (ETD, EL, EAE, ERE). Baselines reveal strong gaps in event discovery (ETD<8%, EL<6%, EAE<11%), while event relation extraction (ERE) is more tractable given events (35.45% F1 zero-shot; 44.42% F1 after fine-tuning).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current long-video benchmarks emphasize retrieval or shallow understanding and miss narrative-level structure, cross-temporal dependencies, and multi-modal grounding. There is a need to evaluate how low-level actions cohere into events, how events interrelate over long timescales, and how narratives unfold, including long gaps and reframing scenes.

Method: Assemble NEST: 1005 full-length movies (~98 minutes each) annotated with 102 multimodal narrative events grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio. Annotate events with grounding and connect them via relations capturing narrative structure (temporal ordering, hierarchical composition, long-range dependencies). Propose baselines for four tasks: event trigger detection (ETD), event localization (EL), event argument extraction (EAE), and event relation extraction (ERE). Evaluate with task-specific metrics (F1-like scores).

Result: ETD < 8%, EL < 6%, EAE < 11% indicate strong difficulty in discovering and labeling events. ERE achieves 35.45% F1 zero-shot and 44.42% F1 after fine-tuning, suggesting relational understanding among given events is more achievable.

Conclusion: NEST offers a challenging but informative benchmark for long-video narrative understanding, highlighting the gap in discovering narrative events while showing promise for learning narrative relations once events are provided. The dataset enables study of multimodal narrative cues and long-range dependencies across entire films, motivating future advances in event-centric video understanding.

Abstract: Recent progress in vision-language models has enabled the processing of increasingly long video sequences, but the ability to handle extended token streams does not translate to understanding of narrative structure in long videos. Existing long video benchmarks focus on needle-in-a-haystack retrieval rather than evaluating how low-level actions form events, how events interact across time, and how narratives progress, for example, whether a model can connect an early setback, such as a job loss to a later relationship breakup, despite long gaps, intervening scenes, or flashbacks that reframe what occurred. We introduce NEST (Narrative Event Structures in Time for Long Video Understanding), a dataset of 1005 full-length movies (avg. 98 minutes), each annotated with 102 multimodal narrative events grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio. NEST captures multimodal narrative events with structured annotations grounded in visual content, dialogue, and audio, and links them through relations that reflect narrative structure, including temporal ordering, hierarchical composition, and long-range dependencies. We introduce baselines for event trigger detection (ETD), event localization (EL), event argument extraction (EAE), and event relation extraction (ERE). The benchmark is highly challenging for grounded event discovery, with ETD below 8%, EL under 6%, and EAE below 11%. In contrast, ERE is more tractable once events are given, reaching 35.45% F1 zero-shot and 44.42% F1 after fine-tuning.

</details>


### [14] [One-Shot Novel View and Pose Human Image Synthesis via 3D Prior Guided Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19718)
*Shenjian Gong,Kangkan Wang,Shanshan Zhang,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based, single-image method for novel view and pose synthesis using 3D priors (normal map and color prompt) to guide generation, enabling occluded parts and arbitrary poses, with a self-reconstruction refinement; demonstrates strong cross-dataset generalization and outperforms prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 2D pose-based pose transfer struggles with complex or ambiguous poses; generalizable NeRF approaches rely on features that may be unavailable for occluded/invisible parts. A single-image, geometry-informed generative pipeline could yield more reliable, high-fidelity results across diverse poses.

Method: A conditional denoising diffusion model that decomposes synthesis into sequential denoising steps. Introduces 3D priors (3D normal map and color prompt) as geometry and color conditions. Transfers the reference to the target pose through diffusion steps, enabling occluded/invisible part synthesis. Adds a self-reconstruction-based customized refinement at test time to enhance fine details for novel subjects.

Result: Experimental results on public datasets show significant improvements over previous methods and better generalization across datasets; code will be released publicly.

Conclusion: The approach achieves high-quality novel view and pose synthesis from a single image, including occluded parts, handles complex poses, and generalizes well across datasets, supported by empirical results.

Abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of one-shot novel view and pose human image synthesis. The existing methods transfer the reference human image to a target pose using a set of 2D pose keypoints or synthesize human images based on generalizable human NeRF which uses human model priors to extract point-wise features. However, pose transfer based methods can not handle complex human pose using ambiguous 2D pose as the condition, while generalizable human NeRFs may be inaccurate to recover occluded/invisiable human parts without extracted reliable features. To solve these problems, we propose a novel approach for novel view and pose synthesis from a singe human image via conditional denoising diffusion model. Our diffusion model divides the novel view and pose synthesis problem into a sequence of conditional denoising steps. Specifically, to generate humans with complex and arbitrary poses, we introduce 3D human priors, i.e., 3D normal map and color prompt, as geometry and color conditions into the generation process. By transferring the reference human into the target human with a series of diffusion steps, our diffusion model enables high-quality synthesis including the occluded/invisible parts. Further, we propose a self-reconstruction based customized refinement to enhance fine details when tested on novel persons.Experimental results on different public datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms previous methods and also shows better generalization ability across datasets. The code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/Yankeegsj/3DPGDM.

</details>


### [15] [QueryGaussian: Scalable and Training-Free Open-Vocabulary 3D Instance Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19733)
*Xiuyuan Zhu,Ke Lu,Zijie Yang,Chao Yue,Jian Xue,Dongming Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free, instance-level 3D instance retrieval framework (QueryGaussian) that overcomes scene-level embedding bottlenecks, enabling scalable, open-vocabulary retrieval in city-scale scenes with large memory efficiency and speed gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle memory/compute explosion and OOM issues in scene-level embedding for open-vocabulary 3D retrieval by adopting an instance-level, decoupled semantic-geometric approach for scalable city-scale scenes.

Method: An instance-level query mechanism that uses pre-trained 2D vision models to interpret prompts, lifts 2D segmentation masks into 3D via a concurrent maximum-weight association strategy, and applies a temporal fusion module with multi-stage adaptive density clustering to resolve projection ambiguity and maintain consistency.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while delivering substantial efficiency: GPU memory usage reduced by >70% and inference speed increased by ~180x; enables rapid retrieval on city-scale scenes with tens of millions of Gaussians on consumer hardware.

Conclusion: QueryGaussian provides a training-free, scalable solution for open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval by decoupling semantic understanding from geometry, mitigating memory bottlenecks of scene-level approaches and enabling practical deployment at city-scale.

Abstract: Efficiently retrieving specific 3D instances from large-scale scenes via natural language prompts remains a formidable challenge in multimedia analysis. Existing approaches predominantly follow a "scene-level embedding" paradigm, which requires distilling high-dimensional semantic features into every 3D primitive. This strategy suffers from a fundamental architectural bottleneck: memory and computational costs scale linearly with scene complexity, inevitably triggering out-of-memory (OOM) failures in city-scale environments. To address this barrier, we propose QueryGaussian, a training-free framework for expeditious and scalable open-vocabulary 3D instance retrieval. Unlike holistic semantic distillation, QueryGaussian employs an instance-level query mechanism that decouples semantic understanding from geometric representation. Specifically, we leverage pre-trained 2D vision models to interpret user prompts and lift segmentation masks into 3D via a concurrent maximum-weight association strategy, ensuring semantic-visual consistency. To mitigate projection ambiguity, we introduce a temporal fusion module with multi-stage adaptive density clustering. Experimental results demonstrate that QueryGaussian not only matches the accuracy of state-of-the-art methods but also delivers a decisive efficiency leap, reducing GPU memory usage by over 70% and accelerating inference by 180x. Crucially, QueryGaussian enables expeditious instance retrieval on city-scale scenes containing tens of millions of Gaussians using consumer-grade hardware.

</details>


### [16] [VFACamou: View-Fused Adversarial Camouflage for Environment-Adaptive Physical Evasion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19736)
*Shihui Yan,Hu Liu,Junyu Shi,Zihui Zhu,Ziqi Zhou,Yufei Song,Youming Geng,Minghui Li,Shengshan Hu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework for physically robust adversarial camouflage on wearable UAV targets, combining UV-volume rendering and diffusion-based texture generation with illumination-aware losses and multi-scale training to maintain attack effectiveness and natural appearance under dynamic viewpoints, poses, and lighting, demonstrated against multiple detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Physical-world adversarial camouflage for UAV reconnaissance is challenged by continuous geometric changes and illumination variation. Previous 2D perturbations fail to generalize to changing viewpoints, and restriction to visually unnatural textures limits real-world deployment.

Method: An end-to-end system that (1) uses UV-volume rendering to preserve appearance under varying scales/poses, (2) employs a diffusion-based texture generator for realistic patterns, (3) introduces an illumination color consistency estimator to align UV textures with surrounding background, (4) applies a multi-scale dynamic training strategy to handle viewpoint shifts and body deformation.

Result: Extensive experiments show strong, stable attack performance across multiple mainstream detectors with high perceptual naturalness. The method reduces human detection rates and avoids obvious artifacts, indicating practical viability in real scenes.

Conclusion: The proposed framework achieves robust, natural-looking physical adversarial camouflage for wearable patterns on dynamic UAV targets, addressing both generalization to viewpoint/lighting changes and perceptual realism, outperforming prior 2D perturbation approaches.

Abstract: Adversarial camouflage in the physical world remains highly challenging, particularly under UAV reconnaissance where targets undergo continuous geometric changes and extreme illumination variations. Existing methods either optimize 2D digital perturbations that fail to generalize to dynamic viewpoints or produce visually unnatural textures that cannot be deployed in real scenarios. Therefore, we propose an end-to-end framework for adversarial camouflage generation that automatically produces wearable adversarial patterns and maintains stable attack performance in real physical environments with changing viewpoints, poses, and lighting conditions. Our method integrates UV-volume rendering with a diffusion-based texture generator, enabling consistent appearance under varying scales, poses, and lighting conditions. To ensure environmental realism, we propose an illumination color consistency estimator that extracts dominant background attributes and guides a natural texture loss to align the generated UV texture with the surrounding environment. A multi-scale dynamic training strategy further enhances robustness against viewpoint shifts and body deformation. Extensive experiments across multiple mainstream detectors demonstrate that our method achieves strong and stable physical attack performance while maintaining high perceptual naturalness, reducing human detection rates without introducing unnatural artifacts.

</details>


### [17] [Occ-VLM: Occupancy Grounded Vision Language Model for Indoor Scene Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19776)
*Jianing Li,Zhou Fang,Yijiang Liu,Li Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Occ-VLM is a pure-2D, 3D-aware vision-language framework that uses posed RGB images and a 2D vision encoder, reconstructs 3D occupancy as a geometric prior to link 2D tokens with 3D space, and decodes via an LLM for unified 3D scene understanding. It achieves SOTA in multi-view occupancy and competitiveness with 3D-input VLMs on 3D VQA/dense captioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLM-based 3D scene understanding either relies on explicit 3D inputs or a separate 3D geometry encoder, which decouples 3D perception from rich 2D semantics learned in VLM pre-training. A unified 3D vision-language representation from pure 2D inputs is desirable.

Method: Occ-VLM operates on posed RGB images with a single 2D vision encoder. It reconstructs 3D occupancy as an auxiliary geometric prior to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space, then decodes these tokens with a Large Language Model for unified scene understanding.

Result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction and competitive performance with 3D-input VLMs on 3D VQA and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that a unified 3D vision-language representation can be achieved from posed 2D imagery using a single 2D encoder and a geometric prior, enabling robust 3D scene understanding without explicit 3D inputs or geometry encoders.

Abstract: Recently, vision-language models (VLMs) have made significant progress in 3D scene understanding, driving advances in applications such as embodied intelligence and robotic vision. However, existing approaches typically either rely directly on explicit 3D inputs (e.g., point clouds or RGB-D sequences), or introduce an additional 3D geometry encoder to derive 3D-aware visual tokens from 2D images. Such designs structurally decouple 3D geometric perception from the rich 2D semantics learned via vision-language pre-training, hindering the development of a unified 3D vision-language representation. In this work, we propose Occ-VLM, a novel framework for 3D scene understanding that operates purely on posed RGB images and employs a single 2D vision encoder. Specifically, Occ-VLM reconstructs 3D scene occupancy as an auxiliary geometric prior, which is utilized to spatially associate foreground 2D tokens with 3D space. These tokens are then decoded by a Large Language Model (LLM) for unified scene understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Occ-VLM achieves both accurate geometric perception and robust vision-language reasoning: it attains state-of-the-art performance on multi-view occupancy prediction, while performing on par with 3D-input VLMs on 3D Visual Question Answering (VQA) and 3D dense captioning benchmarks.

</details>


### [18] [HypOProto: Hyperbolic Ordinal Prototypes for Left Ventricular Filling Pressure Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19804)
*Victoria Wu,Nima Hashemi,Hooman Vaseli,Christina Luong,Purang Abolmaesumi,Teresa S. M. Tsang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HypOProto introduces a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for LVFP classification from B-mode echo, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with interpretable visualizations via a novel HyperPAS loss and a frozen explainable backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVFP classification traditionally relies on the Doppler-derived E/e' ratio, which is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings. There is a need for a non-Doppler, interpretable method that preserves clinical ordinal structure.

Method: A frozen, explainable backbone is coupled with a hyperbolic prototype framework. Prototypes are arranged along the E/e' scale to encode ordinal relationships, with borderline cases near the hyperboloid root and certainty increasing outward. A Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss enforces inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space.

Result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining interpretability, highlighting clinically relevant regions in visualizations. It is claimed to be the first prototype-based LVFP classifier for echocardiography; code is released.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic prototypes naturally encode ordinal clinical information and support interpretable decision-making for LVFP classification, offering potential for deployment in settings lacking Doppler data, with further external validation and ablation studies warranted.

Abstract: Echocardiography (echo) is a widely used imaging modality for assessing cardiac function, with Left Ventricular Filling Pressure (LVFP) serving as a critical physiological marker for conditions such as heart failure. Standard LVFP classification into normal \emph{vs} elevated categories relies on the Doppler-derived $E/e'$ ratio, which is operator-dependent and often unavailable in resource-limited settings, motivating methods that infer LVFP directly from B-mode echo. Existing deep learning approaches achieve high performance but remain largely black-box, limiting clinical interpretability. We propose HypOProto, a hyperbolic, ordinal prototype-based framework for interpretable LVFP classification using a frozen, explainable foundation model backbone. HypOProto arranges prototypes along the physiological $E/e'$ scale, placing borderline cases near the hyperboloid root where small angular differences separate similar cases, while normal and elevated cases occupy outward positions reflecting increasing diagnostic certainty. This hyperbolic geometry encodes clinically meaningful ordinal relationships and improves interpretability. We also introduce a novel Hyperbolic Prototype Angular Separation (HyperPAS) loss, enforcing inter-class prototype separation in hyperbolic space. HypOProto achieves SOTA performance while maintaining transparency, and highlights clinically relevant regions in visualizations. This work represents the first prototype-based framework for LVFP classification in echo. Our code can be found at https://github.com/DeepRCL/HypOProto.

</details>


### [19] [ParaScale: Scale-Calibrated Camera-Motion Transfer via a Gauge-Invariant Parallax Number](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19805)
*Zijie Meng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ParaScale enables scale-faithful transfer of monocular camera motion across depth scales by matching a scale-invariant Parallax Number Pi, introducing the Parallax Consistency Error (PCE) as a metric, and providing a plug-and-play module between pose extraction and injection that preserves rotation while re-realizing motion against the target depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular camera-motion transfer suffers from scale mismatch because image motion from translation scales with ||T||/Z. A depth-scale gauge is needed, since raw trajectories are not directly comparable across scenes with different depths; a gauge-invariant descriptor is required to preserve perceptual parallax across scales.

Method: Introduce Parallax Number Pi = ||ΔT|| / Z̄ as a dimensionless, gauge-invariant descriptor of how strongly a camera move is felt. ParaScale reads Pi from a reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene's depth per frame, leaving rotation unchanged. It sits between pose extraction and pose injection, requires no retraining, and is compatible with any pose-conditioned generator.

Result: Empirically, ParaScale maintains realized parallax on the identity line across scale regimes spanning four orders of magnitude and multiple backbones, and reduces the Parallax Consistency Error (PCE) by more than 3× compared with uncalibrated transfer, with no loss of visual fidelity.

Conclusion: The Parallax Number Pi is the appropriate scale-invariant quantity to preserve for scale-faithful transfer of monocular camera motion. ParaScale provides a practical, plug-and-play solution and introduces PCE as a metric to expose scale mismatch.

Abstract: Transferring the camera motion of a reference video to a freshly generated one lets creators reuse cinematic moves. Yet reference and target often live at incompatible scales -- a sweep across a galaxy versus a nudge across a desk -- and naively reusing the recovered trajectory yields either imperceptible or violently exaggerated motion. We trace this to a geometric fact: translation-induced image motion scales as ||T||/Z, so a monocular trajectory is meaningful only up to a depth-scale gauge. We distill this into the Parallax Number Pi = ||Delta T|| / Zbar, a dimensionless, gauge-invariant descriptor of how strongly a camera move is felt, and prove that it -- not the raw trajectory -- is the quantity that scale-faithful transfer must preserve. ParaScale is a plug-and-play module that reads Pi off any reference video and re-realizes it against the target scene's own depth, per frame, leaving rotation untouched. Sitting between pose extraction and pose injection, it requires no retraining and drops into any pose-conditioned generator. We further introduce the Parallax Consistency Error (PCE), a scale-symmetric metric that -- unlike the similarity-aligned TransErr -- exposes scene-scale mismatch. Across scale regimes spanning four orders of magnitude and multiple backbones, ParaScale keeps the realized parallax on the identity line and cuts PCE by more than 3x over uncalibrated transfer with no loss of visual fidelity.

</details>


### [20] [Training-Free Metrics for Synthetic Object Detection Data: A Proxy for Detector Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19817)
*Myeongseok Nam,Donghoon Yeo,Seungwook Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CCDM is a pre-computable metric family that ranks synthetic training sets for object detection; on VisDrone-DET, its scores have a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with downstream YOLOv8 performance, outperforming existing metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating synthetic data usefulness for object detection is expensive because it requires training detectors. A fast proxy metric is needed to pre-select promising synthetic datasets without full retraining.

Method: Introduce Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), a family of pre-computable metrics that quantify the conditional domain alignment between synthetic and real data to rank candidate synthetic training sets for detection.

Result: On VisDrone-DET with YOLOv8, CCDM achieves Spearman correlation of 1.0 with downstream performance and surpasses existing synthetic-data evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: CCDM provides an efficient proxy for synthetic-data utility in object detection, enabling rapid data curation and potential generalization to other vision tasks.

Abstract: With the recent advent of image generative models, synthetic data are increasingly being used to supplement limited real datasets for training computer vision models. However, not all synthetic datasets improve performance equally, and their effectiveness can only be assessed by training a downstream model, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. This problem is pronounced in the task of object detection, where the required annotations are much more dense due to bounding boxes. In this paper, we propose a pre-computable metric family, dubbed Conditional-Composition Domain Match (CCDM), which serves as a proxy for the relative utility of candidate synthetic training sets for downstream detection. Experiments on the VisDrone-DET dataset show that the CCDM metric families achieve a Spearman correlation of 1.0 with the downstream performance of YOLOv8, clearly outperforming existing metrics for synthetic image evaluation.

</details>


### [21] [CSWinUNETR: Segmentation of Thin Anatomical Structures in Medical Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19824)
*Junho Moon,Haejun Chung,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CSWinUNETR introduces a cross-shaped stripe self-attention backbone with multi-scale detail attention and sparse snake convolution, enabling robust 2D/3D thin-structure segmentation without post-processing and achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous anatomical structures is hard due to low contrast, frequent discontinuities, and severe class imbalance; standard CNN/Transformer-based methods produce fragmented predictions and fail to recover fine branches, indicating need for a generalizable backbone that captures long-range principal-axis context and preserves fine details.

Method: Proposes CSWinUNETR: a general-purpose backbone employing cross-shaped stripe self-attention with cyclic shifts to model long-range context; introduces a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module to fuse multi-resolution contextual features; adds sparse-control dynamic snake convolution to reconstruct dense curvilinear kernels from sparse control points, enabling faithful following of tortuous geometry; designed as a versatile backbone for 2D/3D thin-structure segmentation across domains.

Result: Extensive experiments on four benchmarks spanning ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology show CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses; code available at the linked repository.

Conclusion: CSWinUNETR provides a generalizable, strong backbone for 2D/3D thin-structure segmentation, effectively preserving fine vascular/dermal details while leveraging long-range context, and avoiding reliance on task-specific post-processing.

Abstract: Accurate segmentation of thin, tortuous anatomical structures, such as retinal vessels, cerebral vasculature, and facial wrinkles, remains challenging due to low contrast, frequent discontinuities, and severe class imbalance. Although recent convolutional and Transformer-based models have improved performance, they often yield fragmented predictions and fail to recover fine branches. We propose CSWinUNETR, a general-purpose backbone for 2D and 3D thin-structure segmentation. It employs cross-shaped stripe self-attention to model long-range principal-axis context and incorporates cyclic shifts to enhance information exchange across stripes. To better preserve fine-grained details, we further introduce a detail-enhanced multi-scale self-attention module that aggregates contextual features from multi-resolution representations. In addition, we propose sparse-control dynamic snake convolution, which reconstructs reliable dense curvilinear kernels from sparsely predicted control points to better follow tortuous geometry. Extensive experiments on four benchmarks across ophthalmology, neurovascular imaging, and dermatology demonstrate that CSWinUNETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods without task-specific post-processing or topology-aware losses. The code is available at https://github.com/labhai/CSWinUNETR.

</details>


### [22] [3D-PLOT-LLM: Part-Level Object Tokens for 3D Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19828)
*Jintang Xue,Xinyu Wang,Yixing Wu,Jingwen Chen,C. -C. Jay Kuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: 3D-PLOT-LLM introduces part-aware 3D LLM capabilities by reorganizing input tokens: partitioning patches into K regions, inserting per-region markers and a dedicated <part_k> token before each region, and applying a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) to condition markers on region statistics and adjacency. This enables part-level reasoning with a frozen point encoder and yields strong part-aware performance with under 1M trainable parameters, without segmentation decoders or bounding-box heads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of part-level grounding in 3D MLLMs while maintaining parameter efficiency. Avoid heavy segmentation decoders or bounding-box heads by leveraging the LLM vocabulary to reference parts directly.

Method: Partition the frozen point encoder's patches into K locally coherent regions. Prepend a learnable per-region marker and a reserved vocabulary token <part_k> before each region's patch tokens. Use a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module to condition each marker on the region's spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors. Train only the marker-related components while keeping the point encoder frozen, achieving part-grounded outputs via vocabulary-level references.

Result: On PartVerse-QA, 3D-PLOT-LLM achieves caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459 and Exact-match 13.78% (GPT-4o judge: 44.68). On 3DCoMPaT-GrIn, it outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, and SegPoint across text-output metrics, and surpasses ShapeLLM on 3 of 4 benchmarks (up to +3.03 GPT-4o judge over PointLLM). For Objaverse captioning, adding PartVerse-QA at Stage 2 yields +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o, outperforming PointLLM-PiSA on 4 of 5 traditional metrics. All with under 1M new trainable parameters, and no segmentation decoder or bounding-box head.

Conclusion: Part-level grounding in 3D MLLMs can be achieved efficiently by introducing a vocabulary-based part interface via per-region markers and MSR, avoiding heavyweight decoders while delivering strong part-aware performance across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: 3D multimodal large language models (3D MLLMs) describe a 3D object as a whole but cannot address, name, or reason about its parts. Prior part-aware attempts add segmentation decoders, heavier 3D encoders, or bounding-box grammars at substantial parameter cost. We take a fundamentally different path: we reorganize the input token stream so that parts become directly addressable through the LLM's own vocabulary. Our model, 3D-PLOT-LLM, partitions the frozen point encoder's patches into K locally coherent regions and inserts, before each region's patch tokens, a learnable per-region marker and a reserved vocabulary token <part_k>; a Marker-Space Refinement (MSR) module then conditions each marker on its region's spatial statistics and adjacency neighbors. The model thus cites parts in its output and follows prompts that refer to parts by token, a capability absent from prior object-level 3D MLLMs. To probe this interface, we construct PartVerse-QA, a vocabulary-level part-QA benchmark adapted from PartVerse mesh annotations (77K training pairs and 588 held-out queries on disjoint object splits), on which 3D-PLOT-LLM reaches caption-to-slots Jaccard 0.459 and Exact-match 13.78%, with a slot-to-caption GPT-4o judge of 44.68. On the 3DCoMPaT-GrIn part-aware grounded description benchmark, 3D-PLOT-LLM outperforms PointLLM, Kestrel, PARIS3D, and SegPoint on every text-output metric, and ShapeLLM on 3 of 4, with up to +3.03 GPT-4o judge over PointLLM. On Objaverse whole-object captioning, adding PartVerse-QA at Stage 2 yields +0.65 SBERT and +1.85 GPT-4o over PointLLM, and tops PointLLM-PiSA on 4 of 5 traditional metrics (SBERT, SimCSE, BLEU-1, METEOR) despite targeting a different (part-grounded) objective. All with under 1M new trainable parameters on a frozen point encoder, an order of magnitude below prior part-aware 3D MLLMs, and no segmentation decoder or bounding-box head.

</details>


### [23] [Neural Events: Discrete Asynchronous Autoencoders for Event-Based Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19835)
*Roberto Pellerito,Daniel Gehrig,Shintaro Shiba,Davide Scaramuzza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Re-tokenizes event streams into a small set of neural events with learnable discrete codes; triggers on code flips to yield a compressed, informative data stream. Achieves comparable or superior detection/classification accuracy while halving the event rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras generate dense streams of low-information events. Efficiently compressing these streams into informative tokens is essential to balance temporal fidelity with throughput.

Method: Introduce neural events: a local spatio-temporal context window encoded by a discrete learnable code. When the code flips, a neural event is emitted, forming a highly compressed stream. The codes are learned to maximize information per event for downstream networks.

Result: On object detection and classification tasks, networks trained on neural events match or surpass state-of-the-art methods while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.

Conclusion: Neural events offer a highly informative, compressed representation of event streams, enabling efficient yet accurate perception on neuromorphic data.

Abstract: Event cameras capture dynamic scenes with exceptional temporal fidelity by representing them as a continuous stream of microsecond resolution \textit{events}. Each individual event, however, only carries minimal semantic value, merely signaling a localized brightness change. To derive meaningful signals, downstream algorithms need to quickly integrate cues from a potentially massive torrent of low-information events. Current architectures, however, are easily overwhelmed, struggling to balance capturing fine-grained temporal dynamics and maintaining a manageable data throughput. This paper proposes a framework to re-tokenize event streams into a small set of highly informative \textit{neural events}, each representing a local spatio-temporal context window with a discrete learnable code. Every time this code flips, a neural event is triggered, yielding a highly compressed data stream. We demonstrate that, across object detection and classification, networks trained on neural events are on par or surpass the performance of state-of-the-art approaches while reducing the event rate by a factor of 2.0.

</details>


### [24] [OTCHA: Optimal Transport-driven Confidence-aware Latent Hub Alignment for Multi-View Medical Image Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19838)
*Jiwoong Yang,Haejun Chung,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OTCHA refines cross-view fusion in multi-view medical imaging using latent hub tokens and optimal-transport-based alignment with partial matching, boosting robustness across view configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical multi-view images (e.g., mammography, chest radiography) are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts that can contaminate fused representations; conventional fusion of per-view features is fragile under varying view configurations.

Method: Introduce a set of learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, compute an optimal-transport plan between patch tokens and hub tokens considering feature similarity and geometry, augmented with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching. The transport plan yields token-wise matching confidence to gate hub-mediated message passing and weights an OT-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement.

Result: Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets show consistent improvements over baselines across anatomies and view configurations. Code is released at the provided URL.

Conclusion: OTCHA provides a confidence-aware, OT-based token refinement framework for multi-view fusion in medical imaging, improving robustness to misalignment and view-specific artifacts.

Abstract: Multi-view imaging, such as mammography and chest radiography, is a standard component of clinical practice. However, medical images are often unregistered and contain view-specific artifacts or irrelevant background cues that can obscure diagnostically relevant findings. Many existing methods directly fuse per-view representations, allowing such irrelevant content to contaminate the fused embedding and reducing robustness under varying view configurations. We propose OTCHA, a confidence-aware latent hub token alignment module based on optimal transport (OT) that refines patch tokens before fusion for multi-view classification. OTCHA introduces a set of learnable latent hub tokens shared across views. For each view, we compute an OT plan between patch tokens and hub tokens that jointly considers feature similarity and geometry, and augment the OT formulation with token-conditional dustbins to enable partial matching and discard irrelevant tokens. The resulting transport plan provides token-wise matching confidence, which gates hub-mediated message passing and weights a novel optimal-transport-based representation alignment loss to stabilize refinement. Experiments on three multi-view medical image datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over competing baselines across diverse anatomies and view configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/OTCHA.

</details>


### [25] [ViCoStream: Streaming VideoLLMs Can Run Beyond 100 FPS with Stage-Wise Coordinated Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19849)
*Yang Tan,Junlong Tong,Linan Yue,Hao Wu,Pengfei Fang,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViCoStream is a stage-wise, coordinated streaming framework for Video LLMs that binds visual preprocessing, encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding into a real-time pipeline, achieving high throughput and low TTFT via chunk-wise overlap, token control, bounded attention, and retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable real-time streaming Video LLM inference by addressing system-level bottlenecks and the throughput–accuracy trade-offs, beyond module-level accelerations.

Method: Propose ViCoStream: a stage-wise coordinated streaming pipeline with chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval. Conduct a systematic bottleneck-migration study examining chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope, and evaluate on Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across streaming benchmarks.

Result: ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and under 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU, with accuracy close to full-history baselines across multiple streaming benchmarks.

Conclusion: Coordinated streaming with bounded per-chunk compute/memory enables real-time Video LLM deployment; insights into how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape throughput–accuracy, guiding future system design.

Abstract: Streaming VideoLLMs must continuously process incoming video while maintaining low query latency, making both video-ingestion throughput and query-time responsiveness critical for real-time deployment. Existing methods largely focus on accelerating individual modules, such as visual encoding, token pruning, or KV-cache compression, but provide limited insight into whether the resulting system can sustain real-time streaming performance. We formulate streaming VideoLLM inference as a coordinated pipeline spanning visual preprocessing, visual encoding, token dropping, and LLM prefilling/decoding. Building on this formulation, we propose ViCoStream (Video Coordinated Streaming), a stage-wise coordinated streaming framework that combines chunk-wise execution, CUDA-stream overlap, visual token control, bounded visual attention, and query-side retrieval to bound per-chunk computation and memory costs. We further provide a systematic study of bottleneck migration, revealing how chunk size, token retention, attention locality, and retrieval scope shape the throughput-accuracy trade-off. Experiments with Qwen2.5-VL-3B/7B-Instruct across multiple streaming benchmarks show that ViCoStream achieves 134 FPS video throughput and less than 50 ms TTFT on a single A100 GPU while maintaining accuracy close to full-history baselines.

</details>
