### [26] [PSCT-Net: Geometry-Aware Pediatric Skull CT Reconstruction via Differentiable Back-Projection and Attention-Guided Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19867)
*Dong Yeong Kim,Jaewon Choi,Youmin Shin,Jungyu Lee,Myeongseop Kim,Jinwook Choi,Joo Whan Kim,Young-Gon Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PSCT-Net provides geometry-aware 3D CT reconstruction from sparse bi-planar X-rays using differentiable back-projection, attention-guided projection, and bidirectional long-range dependencies, plus a private pediatric skull dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate radiation exposure in pediatric craniofacial CT and address ill-posedness of sparse-view reconstruction caused by geometry-agnostic feature lifting.

Method: Introduce differentiable back-projection to build a spatial volumetric prior; AGP-3D to learn voxel-wise 2D-3D correspondences; BiM-3D to capture long-range dependencies with linear complexity; curate PedSkull-CT dataset (normal and pathological).

Result: The abstract claims reduced depth ambiguity and improved osseous boundary delineation via geometry-aware reconstruction; validated on PedSkull-CT; no quantitative metrics provided in abstract.

Conclusion: PSCT-Net advances sparse-view 3D skull CT reconstruction in pediatrics by integrating geometry-aware back-projection and non-linear voxel mapping, and contributes a dedicated pediatric skull CT dataset.

Abstract: Computed Tomography (CT) is essential for diagnosing pediatric craniofacial abnormalities, yet poses radiation risks to developing anatomies. Reconstructing 3D CT from sparse bi-planar X-rays offers a low-dose alternative but is severely ill-posed. Existing methods employ geometry-agnostic feature lifting, naively projecting 2D features into 3D without explicit spatial modeling, causing depth ambiguity and degraded osseous boundaries. We present PSCT-Net, a geometry-aware framework with differentiable back-projection. Differentiable back-projection establishes a spatially faithful volumetric prior, alleviating depth ambiguity. An Attention-Guided Projection (AGP-3D) module then learns non-linear voxel-wise correspondences between 2D regions and 3D locations. A Bidirectional Mamba (BiM-3D) module captures long-range volumetric dependencies with linear complexity. We further curate a private institutional pediatric skull CT cohort, PedSkull-CT, comprising normal and pathological cases for internal evaluation, addressing the gap in adult-centric, trunk-focused datasets.

</details>


### [27] [Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19882)
*Tongqing Shi,Ge Yan,Tuomas Oikarinen,Tsui-Wei Weng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MM-CBM extends CBMs to a multimodal CLIP-based framework with dual concept bottlenecks, enabling interpretable zero-shot classification and image retrieval, with substantial accuracy gains and close-to-black-box performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address fixed-class generalization and information leakage in Concept Bottleneck Models and extend interpretability to multimodal tasks.

Method: Introduce dual Concept Bottleneck Layers that align image and text embeddings into a shared, interpretable concept space, integrated with CLIP to support multimodal supervision; enables interpretable zero-shot classification and retrieval.

Result: Achieves up to 51.26% average accuracy improvement across four benchmarks; maintains accuracy within ~5% of black-box models.

Conclusion: MM-CBM generalizes CBMs to multimodal CLIP settings, improving generalization to new tasks while preserving interpretability and competitive performance.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance the interpretability of deep learning networks by aligning the features extracted from images with natural concepts. However, existing CBMs are constrained in their ability to generalize beyond a fixed set of predefined classes and the risk of non-concept information leakage, where predictive signals outside the intended concepts are inadvertently exploited. In this paper, we propose Multimodal Concept Bottleneck Model (MM-CBM) to address these issues and extend CBMs into CLIP. MM-CBM utilizes dual Concept Bottleneck Layers (CBLs) to align both the image and text embeddings into interpretable features. This allows us to perform new vision tasks like zero-shot classification or image retrieval in an interpretable way. Compared to existing methods, MM-CBM achieves up to 51.26% accuracy improvement on average across four standard benchmarks. Our method maintains high accuracy, staying within ~5% of black-box performance while offering greater interpretability.

</details>


### [28] [SurgVista: Long-Horizon Surgical World Modeling with Plausible Instrument-Tissue Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19889)
*Wentao Pan,Wuyang Li,Shengyuan Liu,Xinyu Liu,Hengyu Liu,Yixuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SurgVista introduces two training recipes to fix spatial interaction incoherence and temporal drift in surgical world models, plus SurgWorld-Bench for evaluation; delivers consistent gains over state-of-the-art, especially for long-horizon predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery requires realistic world models that can be trained with limited expert data and safe exploration. Prior methods suffer from spatially incoherent tissue deformation and escalating errors during long autoregressive rollouts, hindering policy learning.

Method: Deformation Consistency Regularization: derive scene-point trajectories from training videos and apply latent contrastive learning to enforce cross-frame coherence of instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training: perturb conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations tailored to long-horizon drift to sustain visual fidelity during extended rollouts.

Result: SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity. Gains increase with prediction horizon, indicating improved long-range reliability.

Conclusion: The combination of deformation-consistent regularization and drift-aware augmentation yields more physically plausible and temporally stable world-model predictions for autonomous surgery, and SurgWorld-Bench enables rigorous, diverse evaluation of such models.

Abstract: Scaling robot policy learning for autonomous surgery is challenging, as expert demonstrations are expensive and in vivo exploration poses substantial safety risks. Surgical world models address this by generating realistic, action-conditioned future frames from an initial observation, but existing methods exhibit two persistent failure modes: spatial interaction incoherence, where visible instrument contact fails to induce spatially consistent tissue deformation, and temporal fidelity collapse, where prediction errors compound across autoregressive rollouts and progressively corrupt visual quality. We present SurgVista, a surgical world model that mitigates both failures through two training recipes. Deformation Consistency Regularization extracts scene-point trajectories from training videos and enforces cross-frame coherence through latent contrastive learning, strengthening physically consistent instrument-tissue dynamics. Drift Adaptation Training mitigates long-horizon drift by perturbing conditioning frames with online prediction residuals and photometric augmentations calibrated to long-horizon drift statistics, sustaining visual fidelity over extended rollouts. To enable rigorous evaluation, we further introduce SurgWorld-Bench, featuring diverse procedure types, long-range rollouts, and decoupled metrics for instrument-motion accuracy and tissue-response fidelity. Extensive experiments show that SurgVista consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods across visual quality, temporal consistency, and interaction fidelity, with gains widening as the prediction horizon grows.

</details>


### [29] [Linear Recurrent Unit with Semantic Modulation for Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19901)
*Mingyu Choi,Woo Kyoung Han,Sunghoon Im,Kyong Hwan Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LRU-based restoration network with SMU for efficient single-image super-resolution, achieving state-of-the-art performance with complexity comparable to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LRU's stable linear recurrence shows promise for long-range dependencies but static parameterization and single-scan limit 2D vision; extending to SR requires dynamic modulation and 2D handling.

Method: Introduce SMU that modulates LRU, performs spatial categorization, and enhances features via learned prototypes; integrate into SR restoration network; train and evaluate on standard SR benchmarks; achieves competitive efficiency.

Result: Experiments show quantitative and qualitative superiority over recent SOTA; efficiency on par with existing methods; source code released at provided URL.

Conclusion: SMU enables balancing performance and efficiency, extending LRU to 2D SR; potential applicability to other 2D vision tasks.

Abstract: Linear recurrent unit (LRU), designed with a principled formulation for stable linear recurrence, has demonstrated promising accuracy and robustness on long-range dependency tasks. However, its static parameterization and single-scan method limits its applicability to 2D vision tasks. In this study, we propose a LRU-based restoration network with a semantic modulating unit (SMU) to achieve a harmonious balance between performance and efficiency in single-image super-resolution. The SMU plays three key roles: LRU modulation, spatial categorization, and feature enhancement through learned prototype. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method quantitatively and qualitatively surpasses recent state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our approach achieves superior performance with computational complexity on par with existing methods. The source code and models are available at https://github.com/MingyuChoi-run/LSM

</details>


### [30] [Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder for Endoscopic Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19908)
*Ivan De Boi,Xinxing Shi,Xiaoyu Jiang,Tim J. M. Jaspers,Francisco Caetano,Mauricio A. Alvarez,Fons van der Sommen,Sam Van der Jeught*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces GPVAE: temporal Gaussian-process prior in a VAE for endoscopic video restoration, using EndoVAE CNN backbone and GastroNet-5M ViT encoders, with two scalable GP approximations (HPA, SPA) and a DUCKNet masking pipeline for specular regions. Demonstrates notable image reconstruction RMSE improvements and improved downstream trajectory accuracy, at the expense of longer training time; provides uncertainty estimates per frame.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Endoscopic video are degraded by transient corruptions (specular highlights, motion artifacts, missing frames). Temporal continuity is essential for reliable restoration and downstream tasks (3D reconstruction, navigation); uncertainty-aware restoration can guide clinical interpretation.

Method: Replace the factorized latent prior in a VAE with a temporal Gaussian process prior over latent sequences. Use endoscopy-specific encoders: a convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M. Employ two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Handle specular reflections via a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction loss. Train and evaluate on the C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, comparing against matched VAE baselines. Assess image reconstruction with RMSE and downstream trajectory performance with classical VO and PoseNet-based metrics. Provide per-frame GP posterior uncertainty estimates.

Result: Best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9% on average, and up to 26.1% relative to matched VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE reduced by 12.7% on average across classical visual odometry and a pretrained PoseNet. Training time per epoch increased by 27.3%. The GP posterior yields per-frame uncertainty that reflects temporal support and serves as a confidence signal for restored frames.

Conclusion: Temporal GP priors with scalable GP approximations enable uncertainty-aware restoration of degraded endoscopic video, improving image quality and downstream tracking while providing actionable uncertainty estimates. There is a trade-off in training time; the approach leverages domain-specific encoders and specular-masking to tackle challenging artifacts.

Abstract: Endoscopic video analysis is essential for gastrointestinal diagnosis and computer-assisted interventions, but video sequences are routinely degraded by specular reflections, motion artifacts, and missing frames. These transient corruptions can distract clinicians, reduce image interpretability, and disrupt downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and navigation. Effective restoration therefore requires methods that exploit temporal continuity rather than treating frames in isolation. We introduce a Gaussian Process Prior Variational Autoencoder (GPVAE) framework for endoscopic video restoration that replaces the standard factorized latent prior with a temporal Gaussian process prior, enabling interpolation of missing frames with uncertainty-aware reconstruction.
  The framework combines endoscopy-specific encoders, including a convolutional EndoVAE backbone and pretrained Vision Transformer encoders from GastroNet-5M, with two scalable GP approximations: Hierarchical Prior Approximation (HPA) and Sparse Precision Approximation (SPA). Specular reflections are handled using a DUCKNet-based masking pipeline that excludes corrupted pixels from the reconstruction objective. On the C3VDv2 colonoscopy dataset, the best GPVAE variants reduced image reconstruction RMSE by 21.9\% on average, and by up to 26.1\%, relative to matched VAE baselines. Downstream trajectory RMSE was reduced by 12.7\% on average across classical visual odometry and a pretrained PoseNet, at an average increase of 27.3\% in training time per epoch. Finally, the GP posterior provides per-frame uncertainty estimates that reflect temporal support and offer a confidence signal for restored frames.

</details>


### [31] [SpatialSV: Internalizing Interpretable 3D Spatial Awareness in MLLMs via Task-Oriented Visual Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19915)
*Jiayu Tang,Yuchen Zhou,Chao Gou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpatialSV internalizes 3D spatial awareness in multimodal LLMs by guiding 2D features to explicit 3D representations (depth, camera poses, point clouds) through task-oriented visual supervision, providing an interpretable, reconstructible spatial diagnosis while reducing reliance on external tools and enabling semi-supervised generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs either depend on external tools to inject spatial priors (adding inference overhead) or rely on latent feature distillation (opaque, with limited geometric constraints). There is a need for intrinsic, interpretable 3D grounding within the model to enhance spatial reasoning and diagnose its spatial knowledge.

Method: Introduce SpatialSV with task-oriented visual supervision that forces the model to uplift 2D visual features into explicit 3D forms (depth maps, camera poses, point clouds). The 3D reconstructions act as interpretable proxies for the model’s spatial knowledge. The approach supports semi-supervised learning, leveraging unlabeled data to scale spatial representation learning.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple base models and benchmarks demonstrates that SpatialSV enhances and interprets the spatial intelligence of MLLMs. It yields improved performance on spatial reasoning tasks and provides a transparent diagnostic via the 3D reconstructions. Semi-supervised experiments show strong generalization when unlabeled visual data are available, indicating scalable, interpretable spatial representation learning.

Conclusion: SpatialSV offers robust, intrinsic 3D spatial grounding for MLLMs with inherent interpretability. By elevating 2D features to explicit 3D representations, it enables transparent diagnostics and scalable learning from unlabeled data, mitigating the need for external tools or opaque distillation while improving spatial intelligence.

Abstract: Unlocking the spatial intelligence of multimodal large language model (MLLMs) is crucial for understanding and interacting with the 3D world. Prevailing approaches typically inject spatial priors via external tools, which impose significant inference overhead, or rely on latent feature distillation, which remains uninterpretable and lacks fine-grained geometric constraints. To address these issues, we propose SpatialSV, a framework designed to internalize robust 3D spatial awareness within MLLMs while simultaneously offering inherent interpretability. Deviating from passive feature imitation, SpatialSV employs task-oriented visual supervision, compelling the model to actively lift its 2D visual features into explicit 3D representations, including depth maps, camera poses, and point clouds. Crucially, this 2D-to-3D lifting process provides a transparent window into the model's representations: the resulting 3D reconstructions serve as an intuitive proxy for visualizing and diagnosing the quality of the model's intrinsic spatial knowledge. Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of SpatialSV in enhancing and interpreting MLLMs' spatial intelligence. Furthermore, the framework exhibits strong generalization in semi-supervised settings, validating its potential to leverage unlabeled visual data for scalable, interpretable spatial representation learning.

</details>


### [32] [CARE: Competence-Aware Reward Shaping for Adaptive Reasoning Length in Video-MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19927)
*Chengwen Liu,Hao Peng,Jisheng Dang,Hong Peng,Bin Hu,Tat-Seng Chua*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CARE proposes competence-aware reward shaping for adaptive reasoning length in multimodal video reasoning. It uses an EMA-based competence estimate, staged reward emphasis from exploration to efficiency, batch-level normalization of reasoning effort, and a posterior amplifier, integrated with GRPO. Results show improved accuracy, stability, and token efficiency, with an inverted-U reasoning-length trajectory and shorter, more informative traces at convergence; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed, simplistic reasoning-length control in RL-based multimodal reasoning fails to adapt to evolving model competence, causing either insufficient exploration or inefficient decoding. A dynamic, competence-aware mechanism is needed to allocate reasoning budget over training.

Method: Maintain a smoothed competence estimate via exponential moving average of pass rates. Route training into progressive stages that shift reward preference from long-form to concise reasoning as competence grows. Normalize reasoning effort with batch-level statistics to separate verbosity from task complexity. Introduce a posterior amplifier to strengthen reward signals for unexpectedly strong performance on hard samples. Integrate into the GRPO training pipeline with no additional inference overhead.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks show consistent improvements in reasoning accuracy and RL stability, as well as significant gains in token efficiency. The training dynamics exhibit an inverted-U trajectory for reasoning length; convergence yields shorter yet more informative reasoning traces. Source code is provided.

Conclusion: CARE effectively enables adaptive allocation of the reasoning budget during RL-based multimodal reasoning, improving performance and efficiency. The approach is practical (no inference overhead) and demonstrated across diverse video reasoning benchmarks, with potential applicability to broader multimodal tasks.

Abstract: In multimodal video reasoning, reinforcement learning-based methods typically rely on simplistic and inflexible reasoning-length control strategies that fail to adapt to the model's evolving competence. This mismatch may suppress necessary exploration at early stages, while encouraging redundant reasoning and inefficient decoding once the model becomes more competent. In this paper, we propose CARE, a competence-aware reward shaping framework for adaptive reasoning length optimization in multimodal reasoning. Specifically, CARE maintains a smoothed competence estimate via an exponential moving average of pass rates, and uses it to route training into progressive stages that shift the reward preference from exploration-oriented long-form reasoning to efficiency-oriented concise reasoning. To avoid conflating verbosity with intrinsic task complexity, CARE further normalizes reasoning effort with batch-level statistics, and introduces a posterior amplifier to strengthen reward signals for unexpectedly strong performance on historically difficult samples. The proposed mechanism is seamlessly integrated into the GRPO training pipeline and incurs no additional inference-time overhead. Extensive experiments on multiple video reasoning and general video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that CARE consistently improves reasoning accuracy, stabilizes reinforcement learning, and significantly enhances token efficiency. Moreover, CARE exhibits a characteristic inverted-U trajectory of reasoning length during training, and yields shorter yet more informative reasoning traces at convergence, indicating effective adaptive allocation of reasoning budget. We provide the source code for our proposed CARE framework and experiments at https://github.com/1Pansy/Video-CARE.

</details>


### [33] [Spatial-Aware Reduction Framework: Towards Efficient and Faithful Visual State Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19932)
*Jindi Lv,Aoyu Li,Yuhao Zhou,Zheng Zhu,Xiaofeng Wang,Qing Ye,Yueqi Duan,Wentao Feng,Jiancheng Lv*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STORM is a training-free, spatial-aware token reduction framework that preserves 2D structure during pruning of Mamba vision backbones, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy especially on VMamba, and competitiveness with ViT on PlainMamba.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token reduction methods are spatially agnostic and disrupt the 2D structural premise required by the selective scanning mechanism in Mamba variants, causing severe performance degradation under compression.

Method: Reformulate token reduction as a structured operation on spatial units with localized constraints to maintain grid topology and neighborhood coherence; deploy as a plug-and-play module that imposes explicit spatial awareness without any training.

Result: STORM achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy in training-free settings across diverse vision Mamba backbones; VMamba shows up to 63.3% absolute improvement in top-1 accuracy, while PlainMamba experiences only about 1.0% accuracy drop, performing on par with ViT.

Conclusion: Introducing spatial awareness into token reduction preserves structural integrity during compression, enabling robust, training-free pruning for long-vision models and showing strong empirical performance, especially on VMamba.

Abstract: Mamba demonstrates strong efficiency in modeling long visual sequences. However, when token reduction is applied to structurally enhanced Mamba variants, these models exhibit a severe performance collapse. We attribute this degradation to the spatially agnostic nature of existing reduction methods, which violate the two-dimensional structural premise required by the selective scanning mechanism. In this work, we propose STORM, a spatial-aware token reduction framework designed to maintain structural integrity throughout the compression process. STORM reformulates reduction into a structured operation on spatial units, enforcing localized constraints to maintain both grid topology and neighborhood coherence. As a plug-and-play module, STORM equips existing reduction pipelines with explicit spatial awareness without any training. Empirical results demonstrate that STORM achieves state-of-the-art pruning accuracy across diverse vision Mamba backbones under training-free settings. Notably, STORM delivers a substantial accuracy recovery on VMamba, outperforming prior methods by up to 63.3\% in top-1 accuracy. Meanwhile, STORM incurs only a 1.0\% accuracy drop on PlainMamba, achieving performance comparable to ViT.

</details>


### [34] [Speeding up the annotation process in semantic segmentation industrial applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19934)
*Marta Fernandez-Moreno,Margarita Guerrero,Rosalia Rementeria,Pablo Mesejo,Raul Moreno*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Quantifies and achieves substantial labeling-time savings for pixel-wise steel microstructure segmentation using unsupervised pre-annotation; releases the largest public high-resolution steel dataset; provides a DL baseline and industrial deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotation bottlenecks in high-resolution semantic segmentation, especially in materials science; need to quantify the effectiveness of unsupervised methods in accelerating labeling and to enable reproducibility through public data.

Method: Apply unsupervised computer vision algorithms to pre-annotate high-resolution microstructure images, measure reductions in labeling time, and compare against labeling from scratch. Additionally, assemble and publicly release a large, high-resolution steel microstructure segmentation dataset (MIT-licensed with DOI), train a deep learning model on it, validate with field experts, and deploy in an industrial setting as a baseline.

Result: Labeling time reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours (approx. 78% reduction). Release of the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date, with full annotations and MIT license. A deep learning model trained on the dataset, validated by field experts, and deployed in an industrial setting as an initial benchmark.

Conclusion: First study to quantify the acceleration of labeling through unsupervised pre-annotation in high-resolution semantic segmentation for materials science. Provides public dataset and industrially validated baseline, enabling reproducibility and subsequent improvements; future work should address annotation quality, generalization to other materials, and detailed methodological descriptions of the unsupervised methods used.

Abstract: Current machine learning models commonly require large and well-annotated datasets. However, the annotation process often becomes a bottleneck, with increased complexity leading to higher chances of human errors. Within this context, our goal in this paper is to leverage unsupervised algorithms to improve data annotation efficiency for complex semantic segmentation problems in industrial materials science. Previous research has quantified labeling time and others explored unsupervised methods. However, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to quantify how much unsupervised algorithms accelerate the labeling process. We aim to validate the extent to which this laborious process can be accelerated, focusing on semantic segmentation tasks that involve annotating each pixel of high-resolution images, such as the microstructure characterization challenge in materials science. Specifically, we demonstrate that by using unsupervised computer vision algorithms, the time required for the labeling process can be reduced from 170 hours to 37 hours, achieving an approximate reduction of 78\%. The dataset we work with includes large images of dimensions 1280x959 and 960x703, which further increases the complexity of the annotation task. Despite these challenges, we create and share the largest public steel microstructure segmentation dataset to date, available under MIT License with permanent DOI, contributing a fully annotated, high-resolution dataset to the field. Additionally, this is the first work to compare the labeling time from scratch (a common approach in previous studies) to the labeling time when using these unsupervised algorithms as a pre-annotation step. Furthermore, we provide a Deep Learning model trained on this dataset, validated by field experts, and deployed in an industrial setting, serving as an initial benchmark for this public dataset.

</details>


### [35] [Triangular Consistency as a Universal Constraint for Learning Optical Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19938)
*Yi Xiao,Carlos Rodriguez Coronel,Jing Zhan,Haniyeh Ehsani Oskouie,Alex Wong,Dong Lao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Triangular consistency is a universal, geometry-based regularizer for optical flow training that enforces that composing two estimated flows yields a third flow, applicable to image pairs, multi-frame sequences, and synthetic augmentations, with negligible overhead and no extra annotations; it improves performance across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optical flow estimation benefits from geometric constraints; existing cycle/temporal constraints are limited to specific setups. A simple, architecture-agnostic constraint can reinforce physically plausible flow without extra labels.

Method: Define and enforce triangular consistency: given flows A (t→s) and B (s→u), the composed flow A∘B should match the direct flow C (t→u). Apply this constraint across (i) image pairs (cycle consistency), (ii) multi-frame sequences (longer-range motion via chaining), and (iii) image pairs with controlled synthetic transformations (data augmentation). Implement as an additive loss term during training, requiring no additional annotations and incurring minimal overhead; plug-and-play with any optical-flow model and supervision type.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows consistent improvements across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer-learning settings; negligible computational overhead; no extra annotations needed; performances are robust across architectures and datasets.

Conclusion: Triangular consistency provides a principled, geometry-driven, architecture-agnostic regularizer for optical flow that is simple to implement and yields broad training-time benefits.

Abstract: We propose triangular consistency as a first-principled constraint for optical flow, which is agnostic to network architecture, supervision type, and dataset, and applies to both image-pair and multi-frame settings. This simple but powerful constraint is to compose two flows to induce a third flow and enforce consistency among the three. The composed flows may arise from (i) image pairs, yielding cycle consistency; (ii) multiple video frames, producing longer-range motion through temporal chaining; or (iii) image pairs combined with controlled synthetic transformations, which becomes data augmentation. This triangular consistency introduces negligible computational overhead and requires no additional annotations. Since it is derived directly from the geometry of optical flow, it does not rely on model-specific assumptions and serves as a ``universal'' plug-and-play component for optical flow training. Experiments show consistent improvement across supervised, unsupervised, and transfer learning settings.

</details>


### [36] [DiffMath: Symbol- and Graph-Aware Latent Diffusion Transformer for Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19939)
*Wei Pan,Xuhan Zheng,Yilin Shi,Huiguo He,Hiuyi Cheng,Dezhi Peng,Minghui Liao,Lianwen Jin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiffMath is a diffusion-based framework for handwritten mathematical expression generation that eliminates the need for symbol-level positional supervision by leveraging a RelAST representation and a structure-preserving latent space, achieving structurally coherent outputs and improved OCR performance through synthetic data augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high annotation costs of symbol-level bounding boxes and the limited scalability of existing HMEG methods by exploiting the hierarchical LaTeX structure as a structural prior and adopting latent diffusion without explicit spatial supervision.

Method: Three components: (1) RelAST: a generation-oriented representation that compresses MathML into triplets [S, R, D] where each token encodes symbol identity, spatial relation, or nesting depth; (2) MathVAE: learns structure-preserving latent representations using symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization; (3) MathDiT: conditional denoising in the structured latent space with Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) guided by a global symbol-count prior to enforce structural coherence.

Result: Empirical results indicate that DiffMath yields structurally consistent handwritten expressions, outperforms existing HMEG methods, and improves downstream OCR performance when used for synthetic data augmentation.

Conclusion: DiffMath demonstrates a scalable, structure-aware approach to handwritten math expression generation by integrating a compact relational syntax (RelAST), a structure-preserving latent space (MathVAE), and diffusion-based conditional generation (MathDiT) guided by LaTeX structure and symbol-count priors.

Abstract: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Generation (HMEG) is challenging due to the complex two-dimensional layouts and long-range structural dependencies of mathematical expressions. Existing methods typically rely on explicit spatial supervision, such as symbol-level bounding boxes, which incurs high annotation costs and limits scalability. In this work, we propose DiffMath, a symbol- and graph-aware latent diffusion framework that leverages the hierarchical structure inherent in LaTeX as a structural prior, eliminating the need for positional supervision. First, we design a Relational Abstract Syntax Tree (RelAST), a generation-oriented representation that distills MathML trees into compact triplet sequences [S, R, D], where each token directly encodes a symbol identity, spatial relation, or nesting depth. Second, we introduce MathVAE, which learns structure-preserving latent representations through symbol-aware and relation-aware perceptual regularization, ensuring that the latent space captures both character semantics and spatial topology. Third, MathDiT performs conditional denoising in this structured latent space, further guided by a global symbol-count prior via Adaptive Layer Normalization (AdaLN) to improve structural coherence. Experiments show that DiffMath produces structurally consistent handwritten expressions, achieves superior performance over existing methods, and improves the accuracy of downstream OCR models through synthetic data augmentation.

</details>


### [37] [Timage: A Generative Text-in-Image Paradigm for Fine-Tuning Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19944)
*Yifeng Wu,Huimin Huang,Ruiluo Wu,Chunyi Lin,Guanhua Chen,Xian Wu,Wang Song,Ruize Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Timage renders the query as a typeset overlay on the image, generated by a constrained Schrödinger Bridge, to provide explicit spatial alignment for multimodal reasoning, achieving strong performance with a 7B backbone on VMCBench and demonstrating input-level reconstruction as a general, architecture-neutral improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs struggle with fine-grained spatial grounding because textual queries lack explicit pixel-space anchors; prior fixes (weight rewiring or verbose prompts) either fail to reliably anchor attention or harm core capabilities.

Method: Propose Timage: convert multimodal understanding into an alignment problem via an overlay rendered directly on the image. The overlay layout and glyphs are produced by a two-stage constrained Schrödinger Bridge: Region Search (move noise toward query-aligned regions with a hard occlusion barrier) and Appearance Shaping (control glyph size via an ink-budget regularizer to keep text legible and balanced). The overlay serves as an explicit attention beacon to steer the model's spatial focus.

Result: With a 7B backbone, Timage substantially surpasses larger proprietary systems and tuned baselines on the VMCBench benchmark, indicating strong gains from input-level reconstruction.

Conclusion: Explicit input reconstruction through a differentiable, alignment-driven overlay is a powerful, architecture-neutral mechanism to strengthen multimodal reasoning.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often lose track of the right image regions during fine-grained spatial reasoning, because a textual query rarely carries any explicit geometric anchor into the pixel domain. Prevailing remedies either rewire the model's weights or pad the prompt with verbose instructions, yet neither reliably pins the language to the correct visual coordinates without eroding the backbone's general competence. We introduce Timage, a paradigm that recasts multimodal understanding as an alignment problem solved at the input: the query is drawn, as a typeset overlay, onto the image itself. The placement and appearance of this overlay are produced by a Constrained Schrödinger Bridge (cSB), an entropic optimal-transport sampler that factorizes layout synthesis into two coupled stochastic stages. The first stage, Region Search, transports noise toward query-aligned image zones while obeying a hard occlusion barrier that protects salient foreground content; the second stage, Appearance Shaping, sizes the glyphs through an ``ink-budget'' regularizer so that the rendered text stays legible and visually balanced. The resulting overlay behaves as an explicit attention beacon that channels the model's focus along spatial semantics. On the VMCBench suite, Timage paired with a modest 7B backbone clearly overtakes far larger proprietary systems as well as parameter-tuned baselines. The study positions deliberate input reconstruction as a powerful, architecture-neutral lever for strengthening multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [38] [Confidence Calibration for Multimodal LLMs: An Empirical Study through Medical VQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19950)
*Yuetian Du,Yucheng Wang,Ming Kong,Tian Liang,Qiang Long,Bingdi Chen,Qiang Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Analyses accuracy-confidence alignment in medical MLLMs and improves calibration with MS-FBI and auxiliary expert LLM; reduces Expected Calibration Error by ~40% across three Medical VQA datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical MLLMs' confidence often mismatches true accuracy, risking misdiagnosis; need systematic calibration tailored to healthcare.

Method: Introduce MS-FBI (Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation) combined with auxiliary expert LLM assessment to calibrate confidence in Medical Visual Question Answering.

Result: ECE reduced by about 40% on three Medical VQA datasets; improved reliability of MLLMs in clinical contexts.

Conclusion: Domain-specific calibration is crucial for trustworthy AI-assisted diagnosis in healthcare; the proposed approach enhances safety and trustworthiness of medical MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show great potential in medical tasks, but their elicited confidence often misaligns with actual accuracy, potentially leading to misdiagnosis or overlooking correct advice. This study presents the first comprehensive analysis of the relationship between accuracy and confidence in medical MLLMs. It proposes a novel method that combines Multi-Strategy Fusion-Based Interrogation (MS-FBI) with auxiliary expert LLM assessment, aiming to improve confidence calibration in Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). Experiments demonstrate that our method reduces the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by an average of 40\% across three Medical VQA datasets, significantly enhancing MLLMs' reliability. The findings highlight the importance of domain-specific calibration for MLLMs in healthcare, offering a more trustworthy solution for AI-assisted diagnosis.

</details>


### [39] [SketchKeyAnime: Reference-anchored Sparse Key-Sketch Animation Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19958)
*Meixi Li,Xianlin Zhang,Yue Zhang,Xueming Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SketchKeyAnime is a diffusion-based video generation framework that produces controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key sketches plus a reference image, using dual-branch conditioning, Sketch Cross Attention with gating, and Adaptive Weighted Loss; it outperforms baselines on Sakuga-42M with notable reductions in EDMD and FVD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of existing animation methods that require dense frame inputs or complete sketch sequences. The goal is low-cost, input-sparse, controllable animation generation that preserves appearance and temporal coherence.

Method: Proposes a dual-branch conditioning mechanism for local geometric constraints and semantic-temporal context. Introduces Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch inputs with learnable gating. Employs Adaptive Weighted Loss to emphasize supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions.

Result: Experimental evaluation on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M shows SketchKeyAnime consistently surpasses representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided baselines. It achieves a 31.9% reduction in EDMD and a 9.5% reduction in FVD relative to the best-performing baseline, indicating improved sketch fidelity and temporal coherence.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates strong potential for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation with robust appearance consistency and temporal stability.

Abstract: Traditional animation production relies heavily on manual drawing and iterative refinement, particularly for key-pose design, in-betweening, and character coloring. While existing animation and video generation methods have made notable progress, they typically depend on RGB boundary frames, dense frame-wise conditions, or complete sketch sequences, limiting their applicability under low-cost input conditions. We present SketchKeyAnime, a video diffusion framework for generating structurally controllable, appearance-consistent, and temporally coherent animations from sparse key-sketch inputs. Given a single reference RGB image and a few temporally indexed key sketches, SketchKeyAnime introduces a dual-branch conditioning mechanism to encode local geometric constraints alongside semantic-temporal context. It leverages Sketch Cross Attention to fuse reference image and sketch conditions with learnable gating, and incorporates an Adaptive Weighted Loss to strengthen supervision on key-sketch frames and line-art regions. Experimental results on the Aesthetic subset of Sakuga-42M show that our approach consistently outperforms representative animation interpolation and sketch-guided generation baselines. Compared to the best-performing baseline, SketchKeyAnime reduces EDMD by 31.9\% and FVD by 9.5\%, demonstrating superior sketch fidelity and temporal coherence, while achieving the best overall performance across most quantitative metrics. These results validate the proposed framework and highlight its potential for low-cost, highly controllable animation creation.

</details>


### [40] [Addressing Detail Bottlenecks in Latent Diffusion for RGB-to-SWIR Image Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19961)
*Kaili Wang,Martin Dimitrievski,Jose Maria Salvador,Ben Stoffelen,David Van Hamme,Lore Goetschalckx*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes for latent diffusion models recover spatial detail and improve conditioning: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE). They boost downstream detection (mAP) and small-object performance in RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes, achieve state-of-the-art FID, and generalize zero-shot to new benchmarks, with public release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent diffusion models compress images into latent representations, which discards fine spatial details and uses naive downsampling in the conditioning path, hurting downstream perception tasks such as object detection. There is a need for lightweight, backbone-agnostic mechanisms to preserve spatial information and provide richer conditioning signals without sacrificing diffusion efficiency.

Method: Introduce two modules: (1) Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) that injects high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections to retain spatial structure during reconstruction; (2) Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) that replaces naive downsampling in the conditioning pathway with a learnable conditioning signal. The approach is backbone-agnostic and tested on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes using two denoisers (U-Net and DiT). Evaluations include detection mAP and COCO-small object performance, as well as FID, plus zero-shot generalization to RASMD.

Result: The proposed fixes yield substantial downstream gains: up to 2x improvement in detection mAP over the latent diffusion baseline, and up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small). They attain state-of-the-art FID. The study observes a weak correlation between FID and detection performance, motivating multi-axis evaluation. Results generalize zero-shot to the public RASMD benchmark, and the authors plan public release of data, checkpoints, and code.

Conclusion: SCAE and LGE provide lightweight, backbone-agnostic enhancements to LDM-based image-to-image translation that preserve spatial details and improve conditioning, delivering notable gains in downstream perception without compromising generation quality. The work underscores the importance of multi-axis evaluation (FID vs. detection) and shows promising generalization to unseen benchmarks, with broad applicability to driving-scene translation. Future work could explore application to additional domains, cost analyses, and more fine-grained ablations of the conditioning signals.

Abstract: Latent diffusion models (LDMs) enable efficient image-to-image translation but discard fine spatial details during compression, degrading downstream perception tasks. We identify two bottlenecks: the autoencoder, which loses spatial information, and the conditioning pathway, which further degrades the source signal through naive downsampling. We propose two lightweight, backbone-agnostic fixes: a Source-Conditioned Autoencoder (SCAE) that injects high-resolution source features into the decoder via skip connections, and a Learnable Guidance Encoder (LGE) that replaces naive downsampling with a learned conditioning signal. Evaluated on RGB-to-SWIR translation for driving scenes with two denoiser backbones (U-Net and DiT), our approach improves detection mAP by up to 2x over the latent diffusion baseline, with up to 3.4x gains on small objects (COCO-small, <32^2 px^2), while achieving state-of-the-art FID. We further show that FID and detection performance are poorly correlated, motivating multi-axis evaluation. Results generalise zero-shot to the public RASMD benchmark. We will publicly release test data with annotations, all checkpoints, and training code.

</details>


### [41] [ROSE: Benchmarking the Perception-to-Action Gap in Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19965)
*Yihao Wang,Zijian He,Jie Ren,Keze Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ROSE is a controlled benchmark that tests multimodal LLMs' ability to translate the same visual scene into different context-specific actions, revealing a sizable gap between counting and region-conditioned actions and a model-dependent bottleneck beyond simple coordinate grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how reliably MLLMs can turn shared visual evidence into actions when task context changes, by isolating visual evidence from context and evaluating action grounding.

Method: Introduce ROSE with fixed visuals, varying region constraints and symbolic outputs; employ coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks; evaluate nine recent MLLMs; include global-click and matched local controls to parse grounding vs other bottlenecks.

Result: Model performance drops up to 44.5 percentage points when moving from counting tasks to region-conditioned actions; humans reach 98.8%. Some models count correctly but fail on actions; controls show grounding accounts for only part of the loss, indicating a distinct model-dependent bottleneck in converting shared evidence into context-specific actions.

Conclusion: ROSE reveals a nontrivial bottleneck in MLLMs’ action grounding under context changes, beyond basic visual-grounding; provides a diagnostic benchmark and highlights the need for improved alignment between evidence and context-driven actions.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are increasingly expected to act on visual information, yet the same scene may require different actions under different task contexts. How reliably can a model turn the same visual evidence into the action required by the current context? To answer this question, we introduce \textsc{ROSE} (\textbf{R}eference-conditioned \textbf{O}ddity and \textbf{S}ymbolic \textbf{E}xecution), a controlled benchmark that holds the visual scene fixed while varying region constraints and required symbolic outputs. Through coupled counting and coordinate-action tasks, \textsc{ROSE} tests whether models can infer an implicit majority reference and act on the resulting fine-grained visual evidence under changing contexts. Across nine recent MLLMs, performance drops by as much as 44.5 percentage points from counting-oriented tasks to region-conditioned action, despite 98.8\% human performance. The gap persists on paired scenes and regions for which the same model returns the correct count, while global-click and matched local controls show that coordinate grounding explains only part of the loss, revealing a distinct, model-dependent bottleneck in turning shared visual evidence into context-specific actions.

</details>


### [42] [Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion for Domain-Robust Whole-Slide Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19966)
*Yucheng Xing,Ling Huang,Pei Liu,Jingying Ma,Jiaqing Xu,Kai He,Mengling Feng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAEFS uses semantic anchors from VQA and evidential fusion to achieve cross-center generalization in WSI-based survival prediction, outperforming baselines with a 10.2% improvement in C-index.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain shift in histopathology whole-slide images caused by staining and scanner artefacts limits generalization. High-level semantic pathology concepts (e.g., tumor grade, micro-environmental architecture) may offer domain-invariant representations that align with human diagnostic logic.

Method: Derive semantic anchors from WSIs via Visual Question Answering (VQA). Employ a dual-stream WSI evidence extraction architecture. Use Dirichlet-based Subjective Logic to model uncertainty. Fuse semantic and visual evidence using a cautious conjunction rule to avoid overconfident fusion from correlated sources. Train on a single source domain and evaluate zero-shot on four unseen domains.

Result: Zero-shot evaluation across four unseen domains shows SAEFS outperforms state-of-the-art models in prediction accuracy and reliability, with an average C-index improvement of 10.2%. VQA-derived semantic features exhibit significantly lower cross-center divergence than pixel-derived features, indicating robust cross-center applicability.

Conclusion: Semantic-anchored evidential fusion enables robust, domain-generalizable survival prediction from WSIs by leveraging high-level semantic anchors, evidential fusion, and uncertainty modeling, reducing cross-center variability and improving performance.

Abstract: Whole-slide images (WSIs) are widely used for computational cancer prognosis. However, most existing methods primarily focus on in-domain performance and fail to generalize across clinical centers. This limitation stems from their reliance on pixel-derived representations that are highly susceptible to domain-specific artifacts caused by staining protocols and scanner hardware. We hypothesize that high-level pathology semantics, such as tumor grade and micro-environmental architecture, provide a domain-invariant semantic representation that mirrors the robust diagnostic logic of human pathologists. Therefore, we propose a Semantic-Anchored Evidential Fusion Survival (SAEFS) framework, where SAEFS derives semantic anchors from WSIs via Visual Question Answering (VQA), employs a dual-stream WSI evidence extraction architecture, uses Dirichlet-based Subjective Logic to model uncertainty, and fuses semantic and visual evidence through a cautious conjunction rule to avoid overconfident fusion from correlated sources. Trained exclusively on one source domain and evaluated zero-shot across four unseen domains, SAEFS consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models both in prediction accuracy and reliability, improving the average C-index by 10.2%. Quantitative analyses further show that VQA-derived semantic features exhibit significantly lower cross-center divergence than pixel-derived features, highlighting their robustness for cross-center clinical applications.

</details>


### [43] [CrossFlow: One-Step Generation Across Latent and Pixel Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19970)
*Xiyuan Wang,Xiao Zhang,Yang Li,Ruoxi Jiang,Zhao Zhong,Liefeng Bo,Muhan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CrossFlow introduces a cross-space flow that maps noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images, replacing the decoder in latent diffusion. It uses a velocity-free one-step objective, enabling a single model to act as latent-to-pixel generator and decoder replacement, achieving 1.62 FID on ImageNet-1k 256×256 with one evaluation and showing ablations that latent encoding and pixel-space losses are crucial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In latent diffusion, the latent-space prediction objective and the final pixel-space decoder are mismatched, since the model optimizes latent trajectories while final quality depends on the decoder handling latent outputs. A unified cross-space objective aims to preserve latent efficiency while delivering high-quality pixel outputs without a separate decoder at inference.

Method: Define a cross-space flow with a velocity-free one-step objective: train using the latent trajectory but supervise the final image directly, mapping from noisy latent inputs to pixel-space. The model also utilizes a latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses. This yields a decoder-replacement capable latent-to-pixel generation and supports one-function-evaluation inference.

Result: Empirical results on class-conditional ImageNet-1k at 256×256: CrossFlow-XL achieves 1.62 FID with a single function evaluation. Ablation studies indicate the latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual/adversarial losses are important for fidelity, suggesting cross-space supervision can combine latent efficiency with direct pixel-space supervision without a separate decoder at inference.

Conclusion: Cross-space flow provides a viable path to unify the efficiency of latent representations with end-task pixel-space supervision, enabling decoder-free inference and potentially improved sample fidelity; prompts further exploration of cross-space objectives and loss combinations.

Abstract: Most diffusion and flow-matching generators define the prior, probability path, and prediction target in the same representation space. Latent diffusion improves efficiency by moving this path into an autoencoder latent space, but the final sample is still produced by a separately trained decoder. This separation creates a mismatch: the generator is optimized for latent-space prediction, while final quality depends on how the decoder handles generated latents that may differ from clean encoder outputs. We introduce CrossFlow, a cross-space flow formulation that maps noisy latent inputs directly to pixel-space images. The key technical step is a velocity-free one-step objective: the latent trajectory defines the training path, but the supervised prediction is an image rather than a latent displacement. This lets one model act both as a one-step latent-to-pixel generator and as a decoder replacement for latent diffusion pipelines. On class-conditional ImageNet-1k at $256\times256$, CrossFlow-XL achieves 1.62 FID with one function evaluation. Ablations show that the latent encoder and pixel-space perceptual and adversarial losses are important for fidelity. These results indicate that cross-space flow objectives can combine the efficiency of latent representations with direct pixel-space supervision, without requiring a separate decoder at inference.

</details>


### [44] [Vision-Reasoning-Guided Occlusion Removal from Light Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19985)
*Mohamed Youssef,Oliver Bimber*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A vision-reasoning-guided framework fuses light field integration with vision-language priors to remove occlusions and recover details under dense vegetation, using multi-sample fusion for consistency; achieves state-of-the-art SSIM on 4-Syn and generalizes across acquisition settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusions from dense foreground vegetation impede scene recovery in natural environments; existing light-field integration (LFI) or vision-language model (VLM) approaches alone struggle under severe occlusion; a combined physical-semantic prior can improve visibility and fidelity.

Method: 1) Integrate multi-view data via light field integration to suppress occlusions; 2) inject a vision-language model as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures; 3) apply multi-sample fusion to merge multiple hypotheses, reducing hallucinations and improving consistency.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance with highest average SSIM on four synthetic light field benchmark scenes (4-Syn) and robust generalization across structured and unstructured acquisition settings; validated on both synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Integrating physical imaging constraints with vision-language reasoning yields robust perceptual recovery under severe occlusion, with practical implications for search-and-rescue and exploratory robotics.

Abstract: Occlusion-robust scene recovery remains a major challenge in computational imaging, particularly in natural environments where dense foreground vegetation severely limits visibility. We propose a vision-reasoning-guided light field occlusion removal framework that combines the visibility recovery capability of light field integration (LFI) with the semantic reasoning capacity of vision-language models (VLMs). Multi-view observations are first integrated via LFI to suppress foreground occlusions and produce an initial visibility-enhanced representation. A VLM is then incorporated as a conditional semantic prior to restore degraded structures and recover fine details, guided by the observed measurements. To improve recovery consistency and reduce hallucination artifacts, we introduce a multi-sample fusion strategy that aggregates multiple generated hypotheses into a unified estimate. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving the highest average SSIM across four synthetic light field benchmark scenes (4-Syn) and strong generalization across structured and unstructured acquisition settings. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining physical imaging constraints with vision-language reasoning for robust perception under severe occlusion, with applicability to search-and-rescue and exploratory robotic navigation.

</details>


### [45] [QG-MIL: A Gated Transformer Aggregator for Domain-Agnostic Multiple Instance Learning in Medical Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20027)
*Luca Zedda,Davide Antonio Mura,Cecilia Di Ruberto,Maurizio Atzori,Muhammed Furkan Dasdelen,Carsten Marr,Andrea Loddo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QG-MIL applies four architectural enhancements to a gated transformer MIL aggregator to stabilize training and distribute attention across instances, achieving consistent improvements across six medical-imaging benchmarks without auxiliary losses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attention-based MIL aggregators in medical imaging suffer from attention concentration, leading to overconfident, unstable predictions and poor cross-domain generalization; a more uniform, robust aggregation is desired across MIL scales.

Method: Introduce QG-MIL with four synergistic components: RMSNorm-based pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, fine-grained attention output gating, and SwiGLU-style feed-forward modules; no auxiliary losses, masking, or multi-stage regularization.

Result: Evaluated on six benchmarks (whole-slide pathology and cell-level hematology) across two MIL scales. QG-MIL variants outperform baselines on all tasks, average +6.1 macro F1 points. Attention overlays and attention mass analyses indicate more distributed instance weighting. Ablations show components contribute; cross-domain performance is robust with lower variance. Code released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Architectural choices in QG-MIL stabilize MIL training and promote uniform attention distribution across MIL scales, delivering consistent cross-domain gains without extra supervisory losses; results support broader applicability and reproducibility.

Abstract: Attention-based Multiple Instance Learning aggregators in medical imaging are prone to attention concentration, producing overconfident and unstable predictions. We introduce QG-MIL, a gated transformer aggregator that addresses this through four synergistic architectural components: RMSNorm-based pre-normalization, per-head QK normalization, fine-grained attention output gating, and SwiGLU-style feed-forward modules. Together, these design choices stabilize training and distribute attention more uniformly across instances without auxiliary losses, masking, or multi-stage regularization. We evaluate QG-MIL across six benchmarks spanning whole-slide pathology and cell-level hematology, covering two fundamentally different MIL scales. The best-performing QG-MIL variants outperform leading baselines on all six benchmarks, with an average improvement of +6.1 mean macro F1 points. Attention overlays and attention mass analysis confirm more distributed instance weighting. Ablation studies show that while individual components can match the full model on specific datasets, the QG-MIL design provides the most consistent cross-domain performance and tightest variance when compared to selected baselines. We release a configurable implementation to support reproducibility at: https://github.com/unica-visual-intelligence-lab/QG-MIL

</details>


### [46] [ReA-OVCD: Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection via Semantic and Spatial Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20032)
*Hongming Zhu,Huaji Chen,Bowen Du,Sicong Liu,Qin Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free framework for open-vocabulary change detection (ReA-OVCD) that derives candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies and refines them with semantic & boundary checks to improve reliability and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional instance-level or direct pixel-wise change detection struggles with fine-grained semantic variations and boundary artifacts in open-vocabulary settings; a reliable, efficient approach is needed that can flexibly model changes via text prompts without training.

Method: 1) Generate candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies for detailed localization. 2) Apply SCR (Semantic Change Reasoning) to jointly assess distributional divergence and response variation to suppress incidental inconsistencies. 3) Apply BCR (Boundary-aware Change Refinement) to validate candidate regions using interior pixel support, mitigating boundary misalignment artifacts. 4) Training-free design for efficiency.

Result: Extensive experiments on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND show consistent SOTA performance with F1^C gains between 2.13% and 9.75% and higher computational efficiency.

Conclusion: ReA-OVCD provides reliable, efficient open-vocabulary change detection by combining semantic and spatial reasoning without training, with publicly available code.

Abstract: Unlike traditional remote sensing change detection that relies on predefined categories, Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (OVCD) identifies land cover changes flexibly using arbitrary text prompts. However, existing methods suffer from an inherent trade-off when modeling changes: instance-level comparison overlooks fine-grained semantic variations (e.g., partial building extensions), while direct pixel comparison proves unreliable, yielding unstable responses and boundary artifacts due to semantic ambiguity and spatial inconsistency. To this end, we propose an efficient training-free Reliability-Aware Open-Vocabulary Change Detection (ReA-OVCD) framework. It first derives candidate change regions from pixel-wise semantic discrepancies to ensure flexible and detailed localization. To ensure reliability, it subsequently introduces a collaborative refinement strategy to explicitly model change validity from both semantic and spatial perspectives. Specifically, we develop a Semantic Change Reasoning (SCR) module that reassesses changes by jointly analyzing distributional divergence and response variation, enabling the suppression of incidental inconsistencies while preserving reliable semantic shifts. In addition, a Boundary-aware Change Refinement (BCR) module is designed to mitigate artifacts stemming from boundary misalignment and uncertainty through validating whether candidate regions are supported by reliable interior pixels. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets (LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, DSIFN, and SECOND) demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, achieving $\mathrm{F}_{1}^{C}$ improvements of 2.13\% to 9.75\% with higher computational efficiency. The code is publicly available at \https://github.com/Funny0101/ReA-OVCD

</details>


### [47] [PU-UNet: Stable Multiplicative Interactions for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20035)
*Ziyuan Li,Osamah Sufyan,Uwe Jaekel,Babette Dellen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PU-UNet adds stable product-unit residual blocks to a U-Net for medical image segmentation, yielding consistent Dice/IoU improvements with negligible overhead and reduced false positives, enabled by stability techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explicit multiplicative feature interactions can complement additive layers in dense prediction. Traditional product units use log-exp formulations that are numerically unstable in deep networks, limiting their use. The work aims to provide stable multiplicative learning in a residual U-Net for segmentation.

Method: Integrate product-unit residual blocks into a residual U-Net, employ smooth positivity mapping with log-domain clipping for numerical stability, locate blocks in low-resolution stages, and evaluate on ISIC 2018, Kvasir-SEG, and BUSI. Compare to a matched Residual U-Net baseline; report Dice, IoU, params, FLOPs, latency; include ablations to assess the role of product-unit interactions and stabilization design.

Result: Dice scores: ISIC 2018 = 0.942, Kvasir-SEG = 0.959, BUSI up to 0.925. PU-UNet consistently improves Dice and IoU over the matched baseline with negligible increases in parameters, FLOPs, and inference latency; false-positive rate on normal BUSI cases drops from 0.077 to zero. Ablations indicate gains arise from product-unit interactions, are strongest when placed at low-resolution stages, and are aided by the stabilization design.

Conclusion: Stable product-unit residual learning is an effective way to enhance U-Net-based segmentation by using explicit multiplicative feature interactions without incurring significant computational costs.

Abstract: Many dense prediction networks rely on additive feature transformations and model higher-order feature interactions only implicitly. Product units provide an explicit mechanism for multiplicative feature modeling, but their logarithmic--exponential formulation can cause numerical instability, which has limited their use in deep dense prediction networks. In this work, we propose Product-Unit U-Net (PU-UNet), a residual U-Net that integrates stable product-unit residual blocks into rich low-resolution stages for medical image segmentation. The proposed formulation combines smooth positivity mapping with log-domain clipping, enabling stable multiplicative feature learning with negligible computational overhead. On ISIC 2018, Kvasir-SEG, and BUSI, PU-UNet achieves Dice scores of 0.942, 0.959, and up to 0.925, respectively. Compared with a matched Residual U-Net baseline, PU-UNet consistently improves Dice and IoU while keeping parameters, FLOPs, and inference latency nearly unchanged, and reduces the image-level false-positive rate on normal BUSI cases from 0.077 to zero. Ablation studies suggest that the gains are associated with product-unit interactions, are strongest under low-resolution placement, and benefit from the proposed stabilization design. These results suggest that stable product-unit residual learning can be an effective way to enhance U-Net-style segmentation networks with explicit multiplicative interactions.

</details>


### [48] [FUSE: Frequency-domain Unification and Spectral Energy Alignment for Multi-modal Object Re-Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20044)
*Xuanhao Qi,Tom H. Luan,Yukang Zhang,Jinkai Zheng,Zhou Su,Shuwei Li,Lei Tan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FUSE: a frequency-domain framework for multi-modal ReID that uses spectral decomposition to separate features into low/mid/high-frequency subspaces and enforces energy alignment across modalities, with learnable frequency modulation to improve robustness; achieves substantial mAP and Rank-1 gains on RGBNT201/100 and MSVR310.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multi-modal ReID methods overemphasize low-frequency cues (color, illumination) and suffer from unstable cross-modal alignment due to imbalanced spectral representations. There is a need to model mid/high-frequency information (geometry, texture, identity-discriminative details) and to align energy across modalities.

Method: Introduce Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) to adaptively partition features into low/mid/high-frequency subspaces for hierarchical spectral modeling. Introduce Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) to enforce energy alignment and subspace complementarity across modalities via frequency-consistency regularization. Add learnable frequency modulation to boost robustness to illumination changes and heterogeneous sensor conditions.

Result: Empirical evaluation on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 shows improvements of 9.1% in mAP and 9.5% in Rank-1, indicating strong performance gains and supporting an interpretable spectral-domain approach for multi-modal representation learning.

Conclusion: FUSE establishes an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal ReID, demonstrating that spectral disentanglement and energy alignment across low/mid/high-frequency subspaces can yield robust, discriminative representations across modalities.

Abstract: Despite significant progress in multi-modal Re-Identification (ReID), existing methods tend to emphasize low-frequency cues. Consequently, they focus on attributes such as color, illumination, and coarse appearance, while overlooking mid and high-frequency structures that encode geometric, textural, and identity-discriminative details. This imbalance leads to incomplete spectral representations and unstable cross-modal alignment. To overcome these limitations, we introduce FUSE, a frequency-domain framework that reformulates multi-modal ReID as a two-stage process of spectral disentanglement and energy alignment. The proposed Spectral Decomposition Module (SDM) adaptively partitions features into low, mid, and high-frequency subspaces, enabling hierarchical spectral modeling. The Cross-Modal Alignment Module (CAM) further enforces energy alignment and subspace complementarity across modalities via frequency-consistency regularization. In addition, FUSE incorporates learnable frequency modulation to enhance robustness under varying illumination and heterogeneous sensor conditions. Extensive experiments on RGBNT201, RGBNT100, and MSVR310 show that FUSE achieves 9.1\% mAP and 9.5\% Rank-1 improvements, establishing an interpretable frequency-domain paradigm for multi-modal representation learning.

</details>


### [49] [See-and-Reach: Precise Vision-Language Navigation for UAVs within the Field of View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20045)
*Fanfu Xue,En Yu,Yantian Shen,Zhikun Hu,Hongjun Wang,Yang Yang,Xindi Wang,Jiande Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes UAV-VLN-FOV to isolate the see-and-reach stage in UAV navigation and introduces 3DG-VLN, a vision-language waypoint predictor guided by dynamic 3D direction cues to improve fine-grained grounding and 3D motion, supported by a high-resolution 2,717-trajectory benchmark and real-world validation. Achieves 13.82% higher success rate over baselines; code and benchmark released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To diagnose and improve the terminal reaching capability of aerial agents by decoupling target grounding from the holistic search task and enabling precise, 3D-aligned reaching given visible targets.

Method: Introduce UAV-VLN-FOV to isolate see-and-reach; develop 3DG-VLN that fuses high-resolution front-view and downward-view observations and online updates of target-relative direction; employ dynamic 3D direction cues for vision-language waypoint prediction; construct a high-resolution benchmark with 2,717 trajectories and 3D waypoint annotations; validate in real-world trials.

Result: 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines with a 13.82% improvement in success rate on the benchmark; the proposed method enables more accurate 3D reaching when the target is visible; real-world tests confirm practicality.

Conclusion: Fine-grained visual grounding and online directional alignment via dynamic 3D cues enable reliable see-and-reach navigation for UAVs, and the new high-resolution benchmark provides a diagnostic platform; code and benchmark are publicly available.

Abstract: UAV Vision-Language Navigation (UAV-VLN) is typically formulated as a holistic search-and-reach problem, where long-range target discovery and final target approach are optimized and evaluated jointly. This formulation makes it difficult to assess a critical capability of aerial embodied agents, namely whether a UAV can accurately ground a visible target and translate vision-language evidence into precise 3D motion once the target enters its field of view. To address this limitation, we introduce UAV-VLN-FOV, a target-visible navigation task that isolates the see-and-reach stage and enables a more diagnostic evaluation of terminal reaching ability. We further propose 3DG-VLN, a vision-language waypoint prediction framework guided by dynamic 3D direction cues to enhance fine-grained visual grounding and spatial direction alignment for precise target reaching. Specifically, 3DG-VLN adaptively processes high-resolution front-view and downward-view observations to preserve fine-grained visual and geometric details for target grounding. It also updates the target-relative direction online during closed-loop navigation, allowing the agent to maintain spatial alignment with the target and reduce accumulated direction drift. To support this task, we construct a dedicated high-resolution benchmark which contains 2,717 trajectories with target-oriented high-level instructions, high-resolution front-view and downward-view egocentric observations, and continuous 3D waypoint annotations. Experiments show that 3DG-VLN outperforms competitive UAV-VLN baselines, achieving a 13.82\% improvement in success rate. Real-world trials further demonstrate the potential of 3DG-VLN for practical see-and-reach navigation. The source code and benchmark are available at https://github.com/xuefanfu/3DG-VLN.

</details>


### [50] [Variable-Length Tokenization via Learnable Global Merging for Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20076)
*Dong Hoon Lee,Seunghoon Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A merging-based variable-length tokenizer for latent diffusion models enables cross-length representation alignment by merging similar tokens rather than truncating, using a learnable global merging scheme to be data-independent, achieving improved gFID-compute trade-off on ImageNet 256×256.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent diffusion models are constrained by a fixed tokenization compression ratio. Variable-length tokenizers that truncate sequences cause token semantics to depend on position and misalign across lengths, hindering a single variable-length diffusion model from effectively operating across different lengths.

Method: Introduce a merging-based variable-length tokenizer. Encourage similar tokens to merge, enabling cross-length representation alignment when the diffusion transformer follows the merging pattern. To avoid data-dependence of merging patterns, propose learnable global merging which is data-independent and compatible with diffusion transformers.

Result: On ImageNet 256×256 generation, the merging-based VLT integrated with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off compared to prior VLT methods.

Conclusion: Merging-based VLTs with a compatibility-focused learnable global merging enable cross-length alignment and improved compute efficiency for diffusion-based image synthesis, demonstrating the viability of token-merging strategies for variable-length tokenization.

Abstract: Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) have become dominant in visual synthesis, but their quality-compute trade-off is largely constrained by the tokenizer's fixed compression ratio. Variable-length tokenizers (VLTs) promise adaptive compression by varying token counts, allowing diffusion models to flexibly balance quality and compute. However, conventional VLTs modulate length by truncating ordered token sequences, which makes token semantics depend on token position and breaks representational alignment across lengths. This leads to a cross-length shift in the latent distribution that hinders a single variable-length diffusion model from operating effectively. To address this, we propose a novel variable-length tokenizer that modulates length by merging tokens. We show that encouraging similar tokens to merge enables direct cross-length representation alignment when the diffusion transformer operates according to the merging pattern.
