Since conventional merging methods are data-dependent, making the merging pattern inaccessible during generation, we introduce learnable global merging, which is data-independent, to ensure compatibility with diffusion transformers. On ImageNet 256$\times$256 generation, our merging-based variable-length tokenizer integrated with a diffusion transformer achieves a superior gFID-compute trade-off compared to prior VLT methods. Code is available at [this https URL](https://github.com/movinghoon/lgm)

</details>


### [51] [The Hidden Evolution of Disguised Visual Context inside the VLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20077)
*Wish Suharitdamrong,Tony Alex,Muhammad Awais,Sara Atito*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A controlled study compares in-context prompts versus layer-wise injection for visual tokens in LLMs, revealing that tokens evolve into distinct, architecture-dependent representations with varying frequency characteristics; representation quality per layer, not just attention, drives performance across visual tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how integration architecture shapes the transformation of visual signals into language-aligned representations and to provide a fair, identical-training-condition comparison across paradigms and modalities.

Method: Train and evaluate VLM-LLM systems under identical conditions for two paradigms: (i) in-context visual tokens as prompts in the input sequence; (ii) layer-wise injection of visual tokens into intermediate layers. Assess single-image, multi-image, and video benchmarks. Analyze internal representations and attention patterns to characterize the evolution of visual information within the LLM.

Result: Visual tokens begin as disguised visual context with raw, linguistically unstructured representations. As they propagate through the LLM, representations are progressively reshaped differently depending on the integration paradigm, each capturing distinct frequency characteristics of the visual signal. Attention allocation alone is insufficient; performance correlates with the quality of visual representations at each layer, and the integration method dictates which visual features are effectively utilized and how well they align with the language space.

Conclusion: The integration architecture fundamentally shapes the features the VLM can leverage and its alignment with language. Focusing on per-layer representation quality is crucial, and different paradigms offer complementary strengths across tasks; a fair, architecture-aware evaluation is essential for understanding multi-modal LLMs.

Abstract: Visual tokens enter Large Language Models (LLMs) as raw, foreign signals. How they are transformed into meaningful representations and interact with the language space depends entirely on the integration architecture. Whether by treating visual tokens as in-context prompts within the input sequence or injecting them directly into the LLM's intermediate layers. A controlled comparison and understanding of how these architectural choices affect visual information and its internal transformation to integrate with the LLM remains underexplored. We provide a fair comparison by evaluating in-context and layer-wise injection VLM integration paradigms under identical training conditions across single image, multi-image, and video benchmarks. In doing so, we uncover a hidden evolution where visual tokens enter the LLM as disguised visual context, raw representations lacking linguistic structure, but are progressively reshaped depending on the integration paradigm, each capturing fundamentally different frequency characteristics of the visual signal. We show that this evolution inside the LLM determines what visual features the VLM can utilize effectively, how visual representations align with the language space, and ultimately how each paradigm performs across different tasks. We further demonstrate that attention allocation alone is insufficient, and that performance is driven by the quality of visual representations at each layer.

</details>


### [52] [Holo-World: Unified Camera, Object and Weather Control for Video World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20083)
*Xiangchen Yin,Wenzhang Sun,Jiahui Yuan,Zijie Liu,Yinda Chen,Wei Li,Dachun Kai,Chunfeng Wang,Xiaoyan Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Presents Holo-World, a unified controllable video world model that preserves a source scene or edits it to a target weather state from a single image, with explicit camera/object controls and optional weather instructions; introduces HoloStateData and a Unified Scene Adapter to separate scene preservation from weather transfer, plus Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video world models struggle to generate weathered scenes from scratch or from videos with future structure; a first-frame-anchored, controllable, weather-aware model is needed to preserve scene structure while enabling weather change.

Method: 1) Build HoloStateData by converting diverse videos into unified control samples for camera, object, and weather supervision. 2) Propose Holo-World, a unified controllable video world model that takes a single image and uses a Unified Scene Adapter to factorize scene preservation and weather transfer into distinct parameter subspaces, leveraging rendered background, geometry buffers, and object controls to maintain structure while modeling weather-dependent appearance and particle effects. 3) Use Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG to guide scene and weather residuals separately, avoiding over-amplification of the full condition.

Result: Experiments show Holo-World preserves precise camera and object control with consistent scene structure, while successfully transferring to diverse target weather states; outperforms video-to-video weather editing baselines on weather-state generation.

Conclusion: Demonstrates effective separation of scene structure and weather effects, enabling robust single-image-to-video generation with controllable camera/object dynamics and weather transfer; project page available.

Abstract: Video world models are moving toward preserving an observed world under controllable camera and object motion while allowing its environmental state to change. Yet these controls remain isolated, and weather generation typically relies on a source video or reconstructed scene that already specifies future structure. We study a first-frame-anchored source-to-state setting, where the model starts from a single image and follows explicit camera and object controls and an optional weather instruction, then generates a video that either preserves the source world or transfers it to a target weather state. To address these challenges, we first build HoloStateData, a state video dataset that turns diverse videos into unified control samples for camera, object, and weather supervision. Second, we introduce Holo-World, a unified controllable video world model that jointly controls scene from a single image. Its Unified Scene Adapter factorizes world preservation and weather transfer into distinct parameter subspaces, using rendered background, geometry buffers, and object controls to maintain controlled scene structure while modeling weather-dependent appearance and particle effects. Additionally, Scene-Weather Decomposed CFG guides scene and weather residuals separately, strengthening target weather effects without over-amplifying the full condition. Quantitative and qualitative experiments demonstrate that Holo-World maintains precise camera and object control with consistent scene structure while transferring scenes into diverse target weather state, outperforming video-to-video weather editing baselines on weather-state generation. Our project page is available at \url{https://xiangchenyin.github.io/Holo-World/}.

</details>


### [53] [EventVLA: Event-Driven Visual Evidence Memory for Long-Horizon Vision-Language-Action Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20092)
*Ganlin Yang,Zhangzheng Tu,Yuqiang Yang,Sitong Mao,Junyi Dong,Tianxing Chen,Jiaqi Peng,Jing Xiong,Jiafei Cao,Jifeng Dai,Wengang Zhou,Yao Mu,Tai Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: End-to-end sparse visual evidence memory (EventVLA) with Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) and foundational anchors; improves long-horizon robotic manipulation; +40% average success vs state-of-the-art; introduces RoboTwin-MeM benchmark; evaluated on 17 sim and 4 real tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory bottlenecks in long-horizon Vision-Language-Action policies arise when task-relevant cues become occluded or unobservable. Existing memory methods suffer: (i) information bottlenecks, (ii) high latency from decoupled dual systems, or (iii) unselective buffers that accumulate visual redundancies. A selective, foresight-driven memory that stores sparse, causally relevant events could mitigate these issues.

Method: Two-tier memory architecture: foundational visual anchors to retain initial/short-term context and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM). KEM directly predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA latent embeddings to autonomously store sparse, task-critical visual events. The approach enables foresight-driven evaluation of the future causal utility of current observations, preserving transient evidence before it becomes unobservable. End-to-end training aligns memory selection with downstream manipulation performance. A diagnostic benchmark, RoboTwin-MeM, targets non-Markovian manipulation with interactive visual evidence.

Result: Empirical evaluation across 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks shows EventVLA achieving an average +40% improvement in success rate over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs.

Conclusion: EventVLA mitigates information bottlenecks by storing only causally relevant visual events via KEM, guided by foresight into future utility. Foundational anchors retain short-term context while KEM handles long-horizon sparsity. RoboTwin-MeM provides a focused evaluation for non-Markovian visual evidence in manipulation. The work suggests practical gains for long-horizon robotic autonomy and prompts further study on scalability, ablations, and real-time performance.

Abstract: Memory remains a critical bottleneck for long-horizon robotic manipulation, as standard Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies often fail when task-relevant cues become occluded or unobservable over time. While existing memory-augmented methods utilize historical context, they either suffer from severe information bottlenecks, incur high latency via decoupled dual systems, or rely on unselective buffers that accumulate massive visual redundancies. To address these limitations, we introduce EventVLA, an end-to-end framework founded on the concept of sparse visual evidence memory that comprises two core components: foundational visual anchors to retain initial and short-term contexts, and a dynamic Keyframe Evidence Memory (KEM) module. Specifically, KEM directly predicts future keyframe probabilities from the VLA's latent embeddings to autonomously capture and store sparse, task-critical visual events. This foresight-driven mechanism empowers the policy to dynamically evaluate the future causal utility of current observations, preserving transient visual evidence before it becomes unobservable. Furthermore, we propose RoboTwin-MeM, a diagnostic benchmark specifically designed to evaluate non-Markovian manipulation tasks with interactive visual evidence. Extensive evaluations show that across 17 memory-requiring simulation tasks and 4 real-world bimanual tasks, EventVLA achieves an average success rate improvement of +40% over state-of-the-art memory-augmented VLAs.

</details>


### [54] [MakeupMirror: Improving Facial Attribute Preservation in Diffusion Models for Makeup Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20094)
*Nefeli Andreou,Angel Martínez-González,Sabine Sternig,Matthieu Guillaumin,Epameinondas Antonakos,Michael Opitz*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based makeup transfer method MakeupMirror improves facial feature fidelity and skin tone preservation over prior diffusion methods (Stable-Makeup) by conditioning on facial geometry with ControlNets, region-specific transfer, and skin-tone modulation, plus a Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to speed inference. It achieves higher facial recognition similarity (+60%), lower skin-tone difference (-50%), 0.7s latency, and 94% expert acceptance on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and MakeupSelfies datasets, suggesting progress toward production-level VTO for makeup AR/shopping.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based makeup transfer methods (e.g., Stable-Makeup) enhance realism but struggle with identity preservation and skin tone consistency across subjects, limiting production-ready virtual try-on and AR experiences.

Method: (1) Facial geometry conditioning via ControlNets to retain facial fidelity. (2) Region-specific makeup transfer controls for precise application on skin, eyes, lips. (3) Skin tone-based transfer modulation to prevent skin tone shifts in cross-subject transfers. (4) Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to accelerate inference while maintaining quality.

Result: Quantitative gains over Stable-Makeup: +60% relative facial recognition similarity, -50% relative skin tone difference; latency ~0.7 seconds; expert acceptance rate 94% on identity criteria. Evaluations on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and newly collected MakeupSelfies datasets.

Conclusion: MakeupMirror advances diffusion-based makeup transfer toward production-level VTO/AR applications by better preserving identity and skin tone and enabling faster inference, demonstrated across diverse datasets.

Abstract: Makeup transfer models enable fun augmented reality (AR) experiences as well as virtual try-on (VTO) for online makeup shopping. While recent state-of-the-art diffusion based solutions such as Stable-Makeup dramatically improve the accuracy and realism of makeup transfer, they still face limitations in identity and skin color preservation, making production-level VTO for makeup shopping unrealistic. In this work, we propose MakeupMirror, a diffusion-based approach to makeup transfer that makes significant progress towards preserving facial features and skin tone. We introduce several technical innovations over Stable-Makeup: (1) integration of facial geometry conditioning with ControlNets to maintain facial fidelity; (2) region-specific makeup transfer control to enable precise makeup application across facial regions such as skin, eyes and lips; (3) skin tone-based makeup transfer modulation that prevent skin tone alteration in cross-subject transfer scenarios; and (4) integration of a Levenberg-Marquardt Langevin sampler to speed up inference while maintaining generation quality. Our experiments on CPM-Real, Makeup Wild, and (herein newly collected, more diverse) MakeupSelfies datasets show that MakeupMirror improves relative facial recognition similarity by +60%, reduces relative skin tone difference by -50% over Stable-Makeup, with a latency of 0.7s, while achieving expert acceptance rate of 94% across core facial identity preservation criteria.

</details>


### [55] [Stitching and dimensionality effects on large artificially generated volume datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20095)
*Lucas von Chamier,Jan Philipp Albrecht,Dagmar Kainmüller*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Stitching artifacts in patch-based generation affect downstream segmentation in large-scale CycleGANs for cryo-EM; FID is insensitive to these artifacts; 3D stitching offers marginal gains at high cost; 2D training is more stable; ensembling directions helps only for low-quality outputs; perceptual metrics are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation in biomedical imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how patch-based generation and stitching affect the quality and downstream utility of generative models for style-transfer in biomedical imaging, with a focus on large images where patching is necessary.

Method: Systematic comparison of three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using CycleGANs trained on cryo-EM datasets. Evaluation of perceptual quality (FID) and downstream mitochondria segmentation performance. Additional analysis with ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions.

Result: FID scores fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that degrade downstream segmentation; 3D models with artifact-free stitching marginally outperform 2D models, but the computational cost is rarely justified; 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes; ensembling improves low-quality volumes but not high-quality outputs.

Conclusion: Maximizing generative performance on large scientific datasets requires mitigating stitching artifacts, and perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging.

Abstract: Generating large images via deep learning requires patching input data to accommodate hardware memory limitations, then assembling output patches, a process that can introduce stitching artifacts when neighboring patches do not align at borders. While these artifacts are known to affect segmentation tasks, their impact on generative models for style-transfer remains poorly understood. We investigated three stitching approaches and two patch dimensionalities (2D vs 3D) using cycleGAN models trained on cryo-electron microscopy datasets. We evaluated both perceptual quality and performance on downstream mitochondria segmentation. Our key findings reveal that: (1) FID scores fail to detect subtle stitching artifacts that significantly impact downstream segmentation performance, (2) 3D models with artifact-free stitching marginally outperform 2D models on downstream tasks, though the improvement barely justifies the computational cost, and (3) 2D models train more stably due to larger batch sizes. Additionally, we demonstrate that ensembling predictions from three orthogonal directions can improve low-quality volumes but provides no benefit for high-quality outputs. These results demonstrate that maximizing generative model performance on large scientific datasets requires careful consideration and mitigation of stitching artifacts, and that perceptual metrics alone are insufficient for evaluating domain adaptation quality in biomedical imaging.

</details>


### [56] [WeGenBench: A Multidimensional Diagnostic Benchmark towards Text-to-Image Model Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20100)
*Qian Liang,Xiaomin Li,Ying Zhang,Jia Xu,Lihao Ni,Hongrui Li,Jingjing Li,Jing Lyu,Chen Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WeGenBench is a 4,000-prompt bilingual benchmark for text-to-image generation with multi-dimensional annotations, cross-category evaluation, and VLM-based quality metrics plus reasoning traces to diagnose model weaknesses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks inadequately capture multi-dimensional, cross-lingual generation performance and fail to identify category-specific deficiencies. A comprehensive, interpretable evaluation framework is needed.

Method: Construct 4,000 prompts balanced between Chinese and English, annotate with scene classifications and language-specific tags, employ cross-dimensional evaluation to locate weaknesses, develop VLM-based metrics for domain-specific quality, and provide reasoning trajectories for verification. Benchmark state-of-the-art models to analyze limitations.

Result: WeGenBench enables precise identification of model shortcomings across categories and languages; the new metrics provide a more nuanced quality assessment and the reasoning traces allow verification of results; systematic benchmarking reveals limitations in current models.

Conclusion: WeGenBench offers a comprehensive, interpretable framework for evaluating text-to-image generation across languages and content dimensions, facilitating targeted improvements and fair comparisons among models.

Abstract: Recent text-to-image generation models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in synthesizing highly realistic images from text inputs alone. Although existing benchmarks can evaluate the generation capabilities of various models to some extent, they struggle to comprehensively and accurately measure performance across multiple dimensions, often failing to reveal the inherent deficiencies of models in specific categories. To address these limitations, we propose WeGenBench, a novel benchmark designed for the comprehensive, multi-perspective evaluation of text-to-image generation capabilities. Our benchmark comprises a total of 4,000 test prompts across two primary categories, meticulously balanced between Chinese and English to evaluate bilingual and cross-cultural generation capabilities. Beyond macroscopic scene classification, we annotate each prompt with multi-dimensional tags tailored to the distinct content and challenges of each language, thereby refining the generation tasks into more specific sub-categories. Through a cross-dimensional evaluation mechanism leveraging both scene classifications and multi-dimensional tags, WeGenBench can precisely pinpoint model shortcomings in specific generation categories. Furthermore, to measure generation quality more accurately, we design and validate several novel evaluation metrics by integrating Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which assess model performance on domain-specific tasks from three core aspects. Crucially, our approach yields both the assessment outcomes and the detailed reasoning trajectories, facilitating a rigorous verification of the accuracy and soundness of the evaluation results. Finally, we conduct systematic benchmarking on current state-of-the-art methods and provide an in-depth analysis of the limitations present in existing models.

</details>


### [57] [Geometry-Preserving in 3D Gaussian Splatting for LiDAR-Camera Extrinsic Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20103)
*Kyoleen Kwak,Daeho Kim,Jeong Woon Lee,Hyoseok Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Targetless LiDAR-camera calibration using 3DGS with multi-view depth supervision and blocked photometric gradients, preserving metric geometry and outperforming prior methods on driving datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for accurate, scalable, and automatic extrinsic calibration without manual targets; existing cross-modal features are scarce and 3DGS-based methods risk drifting geometry toward rendering quality; thus a method that preserves metric geometry is needed.

Method: Aggregate multi-view LiDAR data to provide dense depth supervision for the 3D Gaussian Splatting proxy, while severing photometric gradient flow from updating the Gaussian spatial parameters to prevent geometry drift; rely on depth-consistency rather than photometric reprojection error for calibration.

Result: Empirically evaluated on public driving datasets; consistently yields higher calibration accuracy than prior targetless methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach improves geometric fidelity of the differentiable proxy and enhances extrinsic calibration performance in targetless LiDAR-camera setups.

Abstract: Accurate LiDAR-camera calibration is essential for robust multi-modal perception. Targetless approaches avoid manual setup but remain limited by the scarcity of discriminative cross-modal features. Recent methods address this by reconstructing the scene within a differentiable model, enabling extrinsic optimization through dense photometric supervision. Among these, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has been widely adopted as a geometric proxy that bridges LiDAR and camera within a single differentiable framework. However, since 3DGS was originally designed for novel view synthesis, existing methods tend to prioritize rendering quality, causing the proxy geometry to drift from the true LiDAR structure. We propose a framework that preserves the metric geometry of the Gaussian proxy by aggregating multi-view LiDAR observations for dense depth supervision and blocking photometric gradients from updating the Gaussian spatial parameters. We validate our method on public driving datasets, where it consistently outperforms existing targetless methods in calibration accuracy.

</details>


### [58] [EFIQA: Explainable Fundus Image Quality Assessment via Anatomical Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20108)
*Pengwei Wang,José Morano,Qian Wan,Hrvoje Bogunović*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EFIQA offers label-free, spatially explainable image quality assessment for fundus photography by leveraging anatomical priors through a two-stage approach that first detects anomalies via masked inpainting and then maps features to quality maps using a shallow adapter on a frozen model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcomes two drawbacks of supervised IQA: dependence on specific labeling criteria and lack of spatial explainability. Aims for generalizable, explainable quality assessment without quality labels by exploiting anatomical priors.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) unsupervised anomaly detector via masked anatomical inpainting to locate degraded vasculature; (2) distill this prior into a lightweight adapter that maps features from a frozen foundation model to precise spatial quality maps.

Result: External-dataset evaluation shows the label-free EFIQA with minimal adaptation outperforms supervised methods in terms of accuracy and provides explainable, spatial quality maps across diverse datasets and quality criteria.

Conclusion: EFIQA demonstrates that leveraging anatomical priors yields robust, interpretable IQA without labeled quality data, with strong potential for real-world deployment in ophthalmic imaging.

Abstract: Image quality control is vital for a wide range of downstream applications. Deep learning-based image quality assessment methods typically train classifiers on dataset-specific quality labels, inheriting two limitations: (1) generalization is tied to the labeling criteria of the training set and (2) these methods cannot provide spatial feedback on where the quality is degraded, lacking explainability. In this work, we propose EFIQA, a framework that requires no quality-related supervision and produces spatial quality maps by design. Rather than learning ``what is degradation" from human-annotated labels, EFIQA learns ``what should be there" by leveraging anatomical priors. For fundus photography, we instantiate this as a two-stage approach, by first training an unsupervised anomaly detector via masked anatomical inpainting to identify regions of missing vasculature, and then distilling this prior knowledge into a shallow adapter mapping features of a frozen foundation model to precise quality maps. External-dataset evaluation demonstrates that this label-free approach with minimal adaptation achieves better performance and explainability compared with supervised methods across benchmarks with different quality criteria, highlighting its potential for real-world applications.

</details>


### [59] [FrozenDrive: Zero-Shot Text-Guided Driving Scene Generation and Data Augmentation with Parameter-Free Frozen Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20110)
*Yuhwan Jeong,Hyeonseong Kim,Daehyun We,Seonkyu Song,Jinnyeong Yang,Hyun-Kurl Jang,Youngho Yoon,Kuk-Jin Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A parameter-free, diffusion-based framework (FrozenDrive) preserves pretrained diffusion knowledge to generate coherent, multi-view driving scenes conditioned on driving-stack signals and text, achieving cross-view and temporal consistency without fine-tuning and improving rare-object fidelity; enhanced data boosts autonomous driving models under adverse conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations in diffusion-based synthetic driving data: lack of robust multi-view and temporal consistency without backbone fine-tuning, over-reliance on training distribution, poor fidelity for rare/underrepresented classes, and weak performance under adverse weather/settings.

Method: Introduce a knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention mechanism within a frozen diffusion backbone, conditioning on rich driving-stack signals and text prompts. Add an object-focused constraint to improve fidelity for rare categories. No weather/scene-specific fine-tuning; single-pass generation.

Result: On nuScenes, FrozenDrive-generated data substantially improves autonomous driving models, especially in night/rain scenarios, showing stronger robustness and surpassing prior baselines.

Conclusion: A single-pass, parameter-free approach that maintains pretrained diffusion knowledge while delivering strong cross-view alignment and temporal coherence, enabling scenario-targeted data generation without fine-tuning and benefiting downstream AD models.

Abstract: Synthetic data for autonomous driving is surging, powered by diffusion models that promise scalable scene generation. Yet key obstacles remain, as enforcing multi-view and temporal consistency often relies on backbone fine-tuning or added layers, which erodes pre-trained knowledge and weakens text alignment. Models also stay close to the training distribution, struggling under adverse weather and unseen configurations, and fidelity favors frequent over rare classes. We address these gaps with FrozenDrive, a controllable generative framework that preserves a pretrained diffusion models knowledge while achieving strong consistency. FrozenDrive conditions on rich driving-stack signals and text prompts, and introduces knowledge-preserving spatio-temporal attention to impose cross-view alignment and temporal coherence in a single pass within a parameter-free frozen diffusion backbone. An additional object-focused constraint improves per-object fidelity for rare categories. Without any weather- or scene-specific fine-tuning, our model synthesizes globally coherent multi-view driving scenes from text, particularly under adverse and rare conditions, and surpasses prior baselines. On nuScenes, FrozenDrive augmented data significantly improves AD models performance, especially at night and in rain, demonstrating stronger robustness when trained with our scenario-targeted data.

</details>


### [60] [Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer: Scalable 3D CT Volume Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20112)
*Zhenkai Zhang,Markus Hiller,Krista A. Ehinger,Tom Drummond*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Two-stage voxel-level diffusion model PRDiT with a local patch denoiser and a global diffusion transformer, delivering high-resolution 3D CT volumes with improved distributional similarity metrics over SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution 3D CT generation is computationally intensive and optimization-unstable with existing generative models, often constrained by autoencoder bottlenecks and insufficient preservation of fine anatomical details.

Method: A two-stage training framework: (1) a local denoiser using an MLP-based blind estimator on overlapping 3D patches to capture low-frequency structures; (2) a global residual diffusion transformer with memory-efficient attention to model and refine high-frequency residuals across whole volumes, enabling voxel-level generation in a coarse-to-fine manner.

Result: On LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT, PRDiT consistently outperforms HA-GAN, 3D LDM, and WDM-3D, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD, and Wasserstein distance scores.

Conclusion: PRDiT demonstrates scalable, stable voxel-level 3D CT generation with improved detail preservation by decoupling low- and high-frequency modeling and using memory-efficient global attention; the approach mitigates optimization challenges without autoencoder bottlenecks.

Abstract: Generating high-resolution 3D CT volumes with fine details remains challenging due to substantial computational demands and optimization difficulties inherent to existing generative models. In this paper, we propose the Pixel-Level Residual Diffusion Transformer (PRDiT), a scalable generative framework that synthesizes high-quality 3D medical volumes directly at voxel-level. PRDiT introduces a two-stage training architecture comprising 1) a local denoiser in the form of an MLP-based blind estimator operating on overlapping 3D patches to separate low-frequency structures efficiently, and 2) a global residual diffusion transformer employing memory-efficient attention to model and refine high-frequency residuals across entire volumes. This coarse-to-fine modeling strategy simplifies optimization, enhances training stability, and effectively preserves subtle structures without the limitations of an autoencoder bottleneck. Extensive experiments conducted on the LIDC-IDRI and RAD-ChestCT datasets demonstrate that PRDiT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models, such as HA-GAN, 3D LDM and WDM-3D, achieving significantly lower 3D FID, MMD and Wasserstein distance scores.

</details>


### [61] [SAM3 Self-Distillation for Fine-Grained GOOSE 2D Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20130)
*Xuesong Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adapting a visual foundation model (SAM3) for 2D fine-grained semantic segmentation with a lightweight decoder, plus self-distillation from SAM3 using ground-truth boxes, image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation by input rescaling, and a key performance boost from aggressive photometric distortion; achieves 69.73% mIoU (4th place) on the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D test set (1,815 images).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To leverage a strong vision foundation model for fine-grained segmentation and to study simple, effective techniques—distillation, TTA, and photometric perturbations—that can maximize performance without heavy architecturally changes.

Method: (1) Replace/augment the image encoder with SAM3's encoder and a lightweight decoder for segmentation. (2) Self-distillation: use ground-truth boxes to prompt SAM3 as a teacher on classes where it outperforms the student, reusing SAM3’s outputs to supervise the model. (3) Image-level multi-scale TTA: simulate multi-scale inference by rescaling the input image (not the model) to achieve multi-scale evidence without changing fixed input size. (4) Adopt aggressive photometric distortion from a prior winning entry as a key performance booster.

Result: Achieved a composite mIoU of 69.73% on the official 1,815-image test set and placed 4th in the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that a foundation-model–backboned segmentation system can attain competitive results with lightweight decoders and selective training-time and test-time strategies. Self-distillation from the same foundation model, input-rescaled TTA, and photometric perturbations emerge as significant contributors. It also highlights cross-pollination from previous winning methods as a fruitful source of gains.

Abstract: We describe our 4th-place entry to the ICRA 2026 GOOSE 2D Fine-Grained Semantic Segmentation Challenge, which reached a composite mean Intersection-over-Union (mIoU) of 69.73% on the official 1,815-image test set. Our model adapts the image encoder of a recent visual foundation model, Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3), with a lightweight decoder. Beyond this, we contribute two techniques and one empirical finding: (i) a self-distillation scheme that re-uses SAM3 itself, prompted with ground-truth boxes, as a teacher on the classes where it outperforms our own model; (ii) an image-level multi-scale test-time augmentation scheme that restores multi-scale inference for a fixed-input-size model by rescaling the image rather than the model input; and (iii) the finding that an aggressive photometric distortion from a winning 2025 GOOSE 2D entry, transplanted onto our pipeline, is its single largest source of improvement.

</details>


### [62] [TriFlow: Generating Artist-Like 3D Mesh Topology via Nearest-Vertex Vector Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20131)
*Haoxuan Li,Ziya Erkoç,Daniele Sirigatti,Vladislav Rosov,Lei Li,Angela Dai,Matthias Nießner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TriFlow: a NVF-based, flow-matching approach for topology-controlled 3D mesh generation from geometry, combining NVF clustering with constrained QEM, yielding artist-like meshes with improved topology and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for high-quality, topology-controlled 3D meshes that closely match input geometry, overcoming limitations in topology generalization and computational efficiency of prior learning-based methods.

Method: Define a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) on the surface. Train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize NVF conditioned on input geometry (SDFs). Cluster surface regions using the generated NVF and perform topology-aware constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification to extract coherent meshes with artist-like topology.

Result: TriFlow shows stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared with state-of-the-art learning-based approaches; Chamfer Distance reduced by 90% and an 8x speedup.

Conclusion: The framework yields geometry-faithful, topology-controlled meshes with structured connectivity and substantial efficiency gains, indicating strong potential for practical 3D mesh synthesis from geometry constraints.

Abstract: We present TriFlow, a new generative approach for producing compact 3D meshes with artist-like triangle topology directly from input geometry conditions such as signed distance fields. Our key insight is to represent mesh topology as a nearest-vertex vector field (NVF) defined over the surface, where each point encodes its association to the nearest triangle vertex in the local barycentric frame. We train a latent flow-matching model to synthesize this field, enabling topology generation conditioned on the input geometry. To extract a coherent mesh, we cluster surface regions using the generated NVF and guide a constrained quadric error metric (QEM) mesh simplification with topology-aware optimization. This yields output meshes that closely match the input geometry while exhibiting structured, artist-like connectivity. Experiments demonstrate that TriFlow achieves stronger generalization and significantly improved topology quality compared to state-of-the-art learning-based approaches, alongside 90% lower Chamfer Distance and an 8x speedup.

</details>


### [63] [SA-VIS: Sparse frame Annotations for training Video Instance Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20140)
*Edoardo Mello Rella,Ajad Chhatkuli,Shipra Jain,Ender Konukoglu,Luc Van Gool*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sparse-frame Annotation VIS (SA-VIS) with Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) enables effective VIS training from sparsely annotated frames, achieving nearly dense performance with only 1/5 of frames annotated and notable AP gains on YouTube-VIS (2019/2021/2022) and OVIS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online VIS requires dense, frame-by-frame annotations and heavy compute. The paper argues that modeling instances and their evolution in videos can be learned from sparse labels and proposes a simple, low-cost module to bridge the gap between sparse- and dense-annotation training.

Method: Introduce Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) that aggregates low-dimensional features from the image encoder across multiple frames, combined with light-weight frame-specific Instance Queries, to form Sparse frame Annotation VIS (SA-VIS) and enable end-to-end training with sparse labels.

Result: SA-VIS shows strong improvements over the baseline on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and Occluded VIS (OVIS); only a 0.4% drop when annotating only 1/5 of the frames; >1% AP improvement in limited-annotation scenarios.

Conclusion: A simple, low-compute design effectively bridges the accuracy gap between sparse- and dense-annotation VIS, enabling competitive performance with substantially reduced annotation requirements and broad applicability across VIS benchmarks.

Abstract: Recent online video instance segmentation (VIS) methods have achieved impressive results, thus becoming the preferred approach to segment instances in videos. Despite the resurgence of impressive single image models, the online (or semi-online) VIS approaches outperform single-image models (e.g., based on SAM) by using long sequences of densely annotated frames during training. However,such a training setup of VIS is expensive in the sense of compute as well as dense annotations required. In order to solve these major flaws, we argue that the effective modeling of the instances and their evolution in videos do not require densely annotated frames. To that end, we propose a simple and effective module, called Past-frames Feature Propagation (PFP) which aggregates low-dimensional features from the image encoder of multiple frames. This simple low-compute module provides tremendous learning capability in using sparse video frame labels for end-to-end training. Combined with a light-weight frame-specific Instance Queries, our Sparse frame Annotation VIS (SA-VIS) significantly improves performance over its baseline. Most interestingly, our simple design that avoids complexities effectively bridges the gap in accuracy between training on sparsely and densely annotated video sequences. This translates to a mere 0.4% drop in performance of SA-VIS when using annotations for only 1/5 of the images in the dataset. Empirically, SA-VIS shows strong improvements over the baseline on YouTube-VIS 2019/2021/2022 and Occluded VIS (OVIS) and an over 1% improvement in AP on the state-of-the-art in a limited annotations scenario.

</details>


### [64] [HEad and neCK TumOR (HECKTOR) 2025: Benchmark of Segmentation, Diagnosis, and Prognosis in Multimodal PET/CT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20143)
*Numan Saeed,Salma Hassan,Shahad Hardan,Lishan Cai,Xinglong Liang,Moona Mazher,Abdul Qayyum,Yansong Bu,Mengye Lyu,Yue Lin,Mingyuan Meng,Chuanyi Huang,Lisheng Wang,Dalal Chamseddine,Shamimeh Ahrari,Beining Wu,Yifei Chen,Fuyou Mao,Hao Zhang,Baixiang Zhao,Surajit Ray,Muzi Guo,Lei Xiang,Jakob Dexl,Michael Ingrisch,Adrien Depeursinge,Arman Rahmim,Mathieu Hatt,Vincent Andrearczyk,Mohammad Yaqub*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HECKTOR 2025 benchmarks automated head-and-neck cancer analysis using multimodal PET/CT and EHR; evaluates segmentation (GTVp/GTVn), RFS prediction, and HPV classification across 1,100+ patients; top results: Dice 0.75, CI 0.66, balanced accuracy 0.56; emphasizes clinical translation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address manual segmentation variability and the challenge of noninvasive prognosis/patient stratification in HNC by providing a large-scale, multi-institutional benchmark.

Method: Analyzes 15 final submissions from 35 registered teams on a held-out test set, using 1,100+ patients from 10 centers; tasks include segmentation, survival prediction (RFS), and HPV status classification; reports Dice, concordance index, and balanced accuracy.

Result: Top-performing models achieved Dice ~0.75 for GTVp/GTVn, CI ~0.66 for RFS, and balanced accuracy ~0.56 for HPV; overall performance suggests feasible automated workflows but variability by lesion characteristics; multi-task learning potential.

Conclusion: HECKTOR 2025 demonstrates the viability of automated HNC analysis in research settings and provides a benchmark for clinical translation; further improvements needed in robustness, generalization, and integration with clinical workflows.

Abstract: Head and neck cancers (HNC) represent a significant global health burden, with accurate tumor delineation being essential for effective radiotherapy planning. The complexity of the oropharyngeal anatomy, combined with the heterogeneous appearance of tumors on imaging, makes manual segmentation time-intensive and subject to inter-observer variability. Beyond segmentation, predicting long-term clinical outcomes, such as recurrence-free survival (RFS), and determining human papillomavirus (HPV) status from noninvasive imaging, remain challenging yet clinically valuable goals. The HECKTOR 2025 challenge addresses these needs by establishing a comprehensive benchmark for automated HNC analysis using multimodal PET/CT imaging and electronic health records. Building on previous editions (2020-2022), this challenge features an expanded multi-institutional dataset comprising over 1,100 patients from 10 centers worldwide. Participants were tasked with three complementary objectives: (1) segmenting primary gross tumor volumes (GTVp) and metastatic lymph nodes (GTVn), (2) predicting recurrence-free survival, and (3) classifying HPV status. The challenge attracted 35 registered teams, with 15 final submissions evaluated on a held-out test set. Top-performing algorithms achieved a mean Dice similarity coefficient of 0.75 for segmentation, a concordance index of 0.66 for survival prediction, and a balanced accuracy of 0.56 for HPV classification. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the submitted methodologies, evaluates their performance across different lesion characteristics, and discusses their implications for clinical translation in automated oncology workflows and decision support systems.

</details>


### [65] [NAMESAKES: Probing Identity Memorization in Text-to-Image Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20155)
*Morris Alper,Vasudha Varadarajan,Moran Yanuka,Angelina Wang,Hadar Averbuch-Elor*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A black-box behavioral probe distinguishes memorized vs fabricated faces in text-to-image models, without using reference photos or training data, backed by the NAMESAKES dataset of public figures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address privacy risks from T2I models recreating or memorizing individuals; enable practical, non-invasive memorization detection without model internals or data access.

Method: Introduce a fully black-box behavioral probe to assess memorization. Construct the NAMESAKES dataset (over 1k names and faces of public figures, with perturbed names). Evaluate state-of-the-art T2I models to predict identity memorization and separate memorized from unrecognized names, analyzing differences across model families.

Result: The probe substantially predicts identity memorization and can separate memorized from unrecognized names; reveals varying patterns of memorization across different model families.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical privacy-auditing tool for T2I models. The NAMESAKES dataset enables standardized benchmarking. Black-box inference broadens applicability, though memorization behavior may vary by model family and prompts require careful interpretation.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) models generate realistic likenesses of some individuals when prompted with their names, raising privacy concerns. However, distinguishing whether a generated face is memorized or fabricated currently requires ground-truth photos, access to training data, or white-box access to model internals, limiting applicability. We introduce a fully black-box behavioral probe that distinguishes between these regimes while requiring no reference photos or prior knowledge of training data. To benchmark this task, we present the NAMESAKES dataset of over one thousand names and faces of public figures spanning a wide range of fame levels, along with perturbed, less famous names. Experiments on state-of-the-art T2I models show that our probe substantially predicts identity memorization and separates memorized from unrecognized names, with further insights into differences across model families.

</details>


### [66] [ARTEMIS: Agent-guided Reliability-aware Temporal Mask Evolution for Imperfectly Supervised Video Polyp Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20161)
*Tong Wang,Siwen Wang,Yaolei Qi,Jinxing Zhou,Yuting He,Guanyu Yang,Yutong Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ARTEMIS: a reliability-guided, agent-assisted framework for imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation that combines SAM2- and sparse supervision, performs bidirectional temporal propagation with a debate-and-judge vision-language agent, and trains with reliability-aware robust learning to achieve state-of-the-art results on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under weak supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imperfectly supervised VPS is clinically valuable but hard due to weak contrast, ambiguous boundaries, motion blur, specular highlights, and sparse pixel-level guidance. SAM2-based pseudo-labeling alone yields geometry degradation, boundary leakage, and ignores temporal reliability. There's a need to leverage temporal consistency and reliability signals to utilize sparse supervision effectively.

Method: 1) Initialize coarse masks from supervision: SAM2 converts points/scribbles; dense labels serve as reliable anchors. 2) A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision. 3) Propagate anchors bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable/unlabeled frames. 4) Train segmenter with temporal reliability-aware robust learning: reliability-guided reference selection, a Reference Prototype Transport Module, and a reliability-aware robust loss. These components assess mask reliability, evolve anchors over time, transport target identity across frames, and down-weight noisy supervision rather than discarding difficult samples.

Result: On SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612, across scribble, point, and limited-label settings, ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art performance.

Conclusion: ARTEMIS demonstrates that agent-guided, reliability-aware temporal mask evolution enables effective learning from imperfect supervision for VPS, offering robust temporal propagation, reduced boundary errors, and improved performance without discarding challenging samples; code will be released.

Abstract: Imperfectly supervised video polyp segmentation (VPS) aims to learn dense, temporally consistent masks from inexpensive supervision, including weak annotations (points, scribbles) and semi-supervision with few densely labeled frames. This setting is clinically valuable but challenging due to weak contrast, ambiguous boundaries, motion blur, and specular highlights, compounded by sparse pixel-level guidance. While SAM2 can generate dense masks from sparse inputs, direct pseudo-labeling often yields geometry-degraded masks with boundary leakage, underutilizes temporal consistency, and ignores reliability. To address these issues, we propose ARTEMIS, a unified framework for imperfectly supervised VPS driven by agent-guided reliability-aware temporal mask evolution. ARTEMIS initializes coarse masks from available supervision: SAM2 converts points/scribbles, while dense labels serve as reliable anchors. A debate-and-judge vision-language agent selects reliable temporal anchors under weak supervision, which are propagated bidirectionally with SAM2 to refine unreliable or unlabeled frames. Finally, ARTEMIS trains the segmenter using temporal reliability-aware robust learning, incorporating reliability-guided reference selection, a Reference Prototype Transport Module, and reliability-aware robust loss. These components assess mask reliability, evolve anchors over time, transport target identity across frames, and down-weight noisy supervision instead of discarding difficult samples. Experiments on SUN-SEG and CVC-ClinicDB-612 under scribble, point, and limited-label settings demonstrate that ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code will be released at https://github.com/wangtong627/ARTEMIS.

</details>


### [67] [Evaluating and Enhancing Negation Comprehension in Remote Sensing MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20177)
*Haochen Han,Jue Wang,Alex Jinpeng Wang,Fangming Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RS-Neg is a benchmark to evaluate negation understanding in multimodal remote sensing LLMs, featuring automated negation data generation and a dynamic visual focus verification; it exposes RS MLLMs' struggles with negation and introduces NeFo, a test-time learning method that leverages about 5% unlabeled test data to improve negation comprehension and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Negation understanding in remote sensing multimodal models is underexplored yet critical for real-world decision-making (e.g., identifying absent or false features). A dedicated benchmark and effective training/inference strategies are needed to prevent hallucinations and improve reliability.

Method: 1) Automated data generation pipeline that uses large language models to synthesize diverse negation queries on RS imagery. 2) Dynamic visual focus module to verify negation-related evidence. 3) NeFo: a test-time learning approach that explicitly encodes the logical role of negation into model optimization, using about 5% unlabeled test samples.

Result: Evaluations show advanced RS MLLMs suffer from negation-related hallucinations and significant performance drops. NeFo substantially improves negation understanding and generalizes to unseen tasks when provided with ~5% unlabeled test data.

Conclusion: RS-Neg provides a first comprehensive framework for negation in RS MLLMs. NeFo demonstrates effective test-time adaptation to improve negation competency, with code/data to be released upon acceptance.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in various Remote Sensing (RS) tasks. However, their ability to comprehend negation remains underexplored, limiting deployment in real-world applications where models must explicitly identify what is false or absent, e.g., emergency responders need to locate non-flooded routes for evacuation. To comprehensively study this limitation, we introduce RS-Neg, the first benchmark to evaluate negation understanding across region-level to scene-level tasks. Specifically, we design an automated data generation pipeline for RS imagery, using LLMs to synthesize diverse negation queries, and introduce a dynamic visual focus module for verification. Our evaluation reveals that advanced RS MLLMs struggle with negation, exhibiting hallucinations and substantial performance degradation. To close this gap, we propose NeFo, a novel test-time learning method that explicitly incorporates the logical role of negation into the model optimization. Remarkably, using about 5\% unlabeled test samples, NeFo significantly improves the negation understanding of models and shows strong generalization to unseen tasks. Code and data will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [68] [HilDA: Hierarchical Distillation with Diffusion for Advancing Self-Supervised LiDAR Pre-trainin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20189)
*Maciej Wozniak,Jesper Ericsson,Hariprasath Govindarajan,Truls Nyberg,Thomas Gustafsson,Patric Jensfelt,Olov Andersson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HilDA is a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that leverages hierarchical and global distillation plus a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to align with Vision Foundation Models and enforce spatiotemporal consistency, achieving state-of-the-art results in cross-modal distillation and several 3D perception tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address annotated-data scarcity in autonomous driving and overcome limitations of using vision foundation models as black-box teachers. The authors aim to exploit VFMs' layer-wise semantic structure and the rich spatiotemporal information in LiDAR sequences to improve LiDAR representations.

Method: HilDA employs (1) hierarchical distillation with multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment across network layers, (2) global context distillation for scene-level semantics, and (3) a temporal occupancy diffusion objective to enforce spatiotemporal consistency in LiDAR sequences. The framework is self-supervised and designed for cross-modal distillation with VFMs as teachers.

Result: HilDA achieves state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperforms prior distillation methods on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction. The authors provide code at the stated URL.

Conclusion: HilDA advances LiDAR backbone pretraining by integrating hierarchical, global, and temporal cues to better capture semantic and geometric information, enabling stronger cross-modal distillation and improved downstream 3D perception performance.

Abstract: Leveraging Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) for camera-to-LiDAR knowledge distillation offers a promising solution to the scarcity of annotated data needed to represent the immense geometric and kinematic diversity of real-world autonomous driving (AD). However, current approaches typically treat VFMs as black-box teachers, relying exclusively on frame-wise feature similarity. Consequently, they do not fully exploit the teacher's layer-wise semantic structure and global context, as well as the rich spatiotemporal information inherent in LiDAR sequences. We propose HilDA, a self-supervised pretraining framework for LiDAR backbones that better captures the semantic what and geometric where needed for driving tasks. HilDA combines hierarchical distillation comprising multi-layer distillation for progressive semantic alignment and global context distillation for scene-level semantics, with a temporal occupancy diffusion objective promoting spatiotemporal consistency. Models pre-trained with HilDA achieve state-of-the-art results on cross-modal distillation benchmarks and outperform models trained via prior distillation approaches on 3D object detection, scene flow, and semantic occupancy prediction. Code available at: https://maxiuw.github.io/hilda.

</details>


### [69] [Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long: Exploring Dataset Distillation for Continual Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20196)
*Hyun-Kurl Jang,Jihun Kim,Hyeokjun Kweon,Kuk-Jin Yoon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DO-ALL creates a compact, privacy-preserving source representation via dataset distillation and uses distilled anchors to stabilize continual test-time adaptation (CTTA). It matches each target sample to its semantically closest anchor, enabling source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization, improving long-term robustness without storing raw data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Source-free CTTA methods degrade under long-term distribution shifts due to compounding self-training errors and forgetting, and practical deployments often cannot retain raw source data due to privacy/licensing. A compact, distilled source representation could provide stable references without exposing data.

Method: Before deployment, perform dataset distillation to obtain a small set of synthetic anchors summarizing the source distribution. During adaptation, match each target sample to its most semantically aligned anchor and use that anchor as a reference for CTTA operations (source replay, representation alignment, manifold-smoothing). The framework is plug-and-play and can augment existing CTTA algorithms.

Result: Empirically improves long-term robustness across CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and the CCC benchmark, indicating stable and continuous adaptation without raw source data. Code is provided at the referenced repository.

Conclusion: DO-ALL demonstrates that dataset distillation can enable stable, lifelong CTTA under privacy constraints by providing a compact source representation that anchors adaptation.

Abstract: Continual Test-Time Adaptation (CTTA) aims to maintain model performance under evolving target domains by adapting online without labeled data. However, practical deployments often cannot retain the source dataset due to privacy or licensing constraints, and purely source-free CTTA methods tend to become unstable under long-term distribution shift, suffering from compounding self-training errors and catastrophic forgetting. We introduce DO-ALL (Distill Once, Adapt Life-Long), a plug-and-play framework that revisits source information in a compact and privacy-conscious form via Dataset Distillation (DD). Before deployment, DO-ALL performs DD to produce a small set of synthetic distilled anchors that summarize the source distribution. During adaptation, each target sample is matched with its most semantically aligned anchor, which provides a stable reference for various CTTA via source replay, representation alignment, and manifold-smoothing regularization. DO-ALL can be seamlessly integrated into existing CTTA algorithms, consistently improving long-term robustness across CIFAR100-C, ImageNet-C, and the CCC benchmark. This demonstrates the potential of leveraging DD to enable stable and continuous adaptation without retaining raw source data. The code is available at https://github.com/blue-531/DOALL.

</details>


### [70] [Evaluation of Image Matching for Art Skills Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20199)
*Asaad Alghamdi,Michael Poor,Trung-Nghia Le,Tam V. Nguyen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A computer-vision approach to quantify drawing skill by matching drawings to templates, comparing SIFT-based keypoint matching vs Siamese networks; finds SIFT-based matching more effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide objective, scalable assessment of drawing skill to complement or replace subjective human judgments, reducing tedious manual evaluation.

Method: Compute SIFT features for hand-drawn images and templates and perform keypoint matching; also implement a Siamese neural network to measure image similarity between drawings and templates.

Result: SIFT-based keypoint matching emerged as more effective for detecting drawing skills; demonstrates feasibility of automated skill assessment using image similarity.

Conclusion: Classical SIFT features are advantageous for this task; computer-vision-based similarity can approximate human judgments of drawing skill, with potential for automated assessment pipelines.

Abstract: While some individuals possess a natural talent for drawing, mastering this skill requires dedicated training and practice. Determining one's skill in the art of drawing requires proper comprehensive assessment. In this paper, we propose a method to measure drawing skill by by matching the hand-drawn image with the original template. Existing techniques often involve complex processes. However, advancements in computer vision allow us to train computers to perform these comparisons at a human-like level, thereby resolving the tedious and overwhelming traditional process. Using computer vision applications, determining image similarity involves identifying the level of similarities in an image with a reference image. We have implemented and analyzed the SIFT feature and Siamese network to measure image similarity. Our results indicate that it is feasible to assess art skill levels. Through feature analysis, we found that SIFT-based key point matching provides a more effective means of detecting drawing skills.

</details>


### [71] [DeepForestVisionV2: Ecology-Driven Taxonomy Expansion for Camera-Trap Monitoring in African Tropical Forests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20223)
*Hugo Magaldi,Theau d'Audiffret,Etienne Francois Akomo-Okoue,Bala Amarasekaran,Naomi Anderson,Claire Auger,Noemie Cappelle,Daniel Cornelis,Raphael Cornette,Tobias Deschner,Gabriel Dubus,Davy Fonteyn,Rosa M. Garriga,Jennifer Hatlauf,Innocent Kasekendi,Raymond Katumba,Aram Kazandjian,Alfred Ngomanda,Stephan Ntie,Simone Pika,Xavier Rufray,Harold Rugonge,John Justice Tibesigwa,Peter van Lunteren,Hadrien Vanthomme,Joeri A. Zwerts,Sabrina Krief*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DeepForestVisionV2 expands to 64 classes (61 animals + human/vehicle/blank) and retains the offline workflow, improving accuracy across varied African forest deployments (forest interior, riverbanks, park edges) while increasing taxa identification and reducing false alarms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address granularity and robustness gaps in the original 35-class DeepForestVision, enabling reliable detection of arboreal, semi-aquatic, and anthropogenic elements across vertical stratification, openness, and edge interfaces in African tropical forests.

Method: Ecology-driven model expansion from 35 to 64 classes, maintaining the offline pipeline. Trained on 1,535,010 photos and 243,354 videos from multi-country African projects. Evaluation uses a cross-country cropped-photo validation set and three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning deployment gradients.

Result: On validation: 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, 0.81 balanced accuracy. Deployment benchmarks show increased taxa from 22 to 29 in forest interiors and 4 to 9 at riverbanks; park-edge accuracy 0.62→0.86; false alarms reduced from 11 to 0.

Conclusion: DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility and robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings while expanding taxa and maintaining an offline workflow.

Abstract: Camera-trap monitoring in African tropical forests increasingly extends beyond closed-canopy interiors to riverbanks, clearings, and park edges. Among available open tools for African forest camera-trap classification, DeepForestVision is the only one providing a matched offline workflow for both photographs and videos, and previous work showed that it outperformed other available baselines on a comparable benchmark. However, it was designed for closed-canopy, ground-level forest interiors and uses a 35-class prediction space that becomes too coarse when deployments encounter arboreal primates, birds, semi-aquatic taxa, or human-associated confounders such as livestock. We present DeepForestVisionV2, an ecology-driven expansion from 35 to 64 prediction classes (61 animal classes plus human, vehicle, and blank) designed to address three recurrent deployment gradients: vertical stratification, scene openness, and anthropogenic interfaces. DeepForestVisionV2 retains the same offline workflow and is trained on 1,535,010 photographs and 243,354 videos from multi-country African tropical-forest projects. Evaluation combines a cross-country cropped-photo validation set, used to assess robustness across sites and camera-trap settings, with three held-out Uganda video benchmarks spanning the targeted gradients. On the validation set, DeepForestVisionV2 reaches 0.86 accuracy, 0.82 macro-F1, and 0.81 balanced accuracy. On the deployment benchmarks, it preserves or improves baseline accuracy despite its harder classification task, while increasing the number of identified taxa from 22 to 29 in forest-interior videos and from 4 to 9 at riverbanks. In the park-edge use case, it raises accuracy from 0.62 to 0.86 and reduces false alarms from 11 to 0. These results show that DeepForestVisionV2 materially improves field utility while preserving robustness across sites, habitats, and camera-trap settings.

</details>


### [72] [Cinematic Compositing Using Character-Environment-Harmonized Video Generation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20233)
*Tianyi Xiang,Mingming He,Li Ma,Jing Liao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes an end-to-end video diffusion framework for cinematic compositing that jointly models bidirectional C2E (Character-to-Environment) and E2C (Environment-to-Character) interactions, including interactive props. Introduces a tri-mask-guided RGB-D denoising architecture, a prior-driven data curation pipeline for relighting pairs without heavy rendering, and a reference-conditioned mechanism for controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement. Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current cinematic compositing methods struggle to capture bidirectional physical and photometric interactions between characters, props, and their surroundings, leading to inconsistent relighting and integration; a dedicated framework addressing C2E and E2C interactions is needed for realistic, dynamic video synthesis.

Method: An end-to-end video diffusion model that jointly handles C2E and E2C interactions. Key components include a tri-mask-guided architecture to segregate character, props, and environment; RGB-D joint denoising to enforce physical consistency across modalities; a data curation pipeline using priors to assemble high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering; and a reference-conditioned mechanism to control environment synthesis and prop replacement.

Result: Extensive experiments show substantial improvements over existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing, indicating better physical consistency and photometric realism in composed sequences.

Conclusion: The framework provides a unified, controllable, physically grounded approach to dynamic video compositing that jointly models character–environment interactions and lighting harmonization, enabling high-fidelity, prop-aware relighting and environment synthesis.

Abstract: Cinematic compositing aims to integrate green-screen characters into novel environments while maintaining physical and photometric realism. Previous methods often fail to capture the complex bidirectional interactions between characters and their surroundings, which we characterize as Character-to-Environment (C2E) physical interaction and Environment-to-Character (E2C) lighting harmonization. To address this, we propose an end-to-end video diffusion framework that jointly models C2E and E2C interactions, specifically handling the challenges of interactive props. Our approach introduces a tri-mask-guided architecture with RGB-D joint denoising to ensure physically consistent interactions among the character, props, and environment. We further develop an efficient prior-driven data curation pipeline to construct high-quality relighting pairs without expensive rendering. Finally, a reference-conditioned mechanism enables controllable environment synthesis and precise prop replacement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in cinematic-quality dynamic video compositing.

</details>


### [73] [BAFIS: Dataset + Framework to assess occupational Bias and Human Preference in modern Text-to-image Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20241)
*Thomas Klassert,Adrian Ulges,Biying Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A bias-focused evaluation of modern text-to-image models reveals gender/ethnicity biases in occupation imagery, using human feedback (BAFIS) and a multilingual synthetic dataset (21,140 images) to complement traditional metrics and compare with German employment statistics; findings show biases persist and correlate only partially with objective metrics, arguing for incorporating human preferences to build fairer models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address pervasive biases in text-to-image generation and the limitations of automatic metrics by integrating human preference data. The study aims to quantify biases across multiple models, introduce a platform (BAFIS) for collecting human judgments, and situate results within real-world labor statistics.

Method: Evaluate five contemporary TTI models (Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, FLUX.1-dev). Assess gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment. Develop BAFIS to gather human bias feedback. Construct a multilingual prompts-based synthetic image dataset (n=21,140). Compare results against German Federal Employment Agency statistics.

Result: Demonstrates systematic biases in text-to-image models. Conventional metrics show partial correlation with subjective human ratings, indicating limitations of automated evaluation alone. Human feedback reveals bias patterns that may differ from what automated metrics capture.

Conclusion: Incorporate human preferences into evaluation and model development to foster fairer and more inclusive text-to-image systems; bias mitigation should be informed by human judgments and contextualized with real-world data.

Abstract: Generative artificial intelligence has the potential to improve productivity and transform the production of creative content. However, existing research indicates that image generation models are significantly influenced by biases. This work investigates the inherent biases and language-induced biases present in text-to-image models within the context of occupation-related image generation, complementing established metrics with human preference feedback. We present a comprehensive evaluation of five current text-to-image models: Midjourney v6.1, Stable Diffusion 3 Medium, DALL-E 3, Playground v2.5, and FLUX.1-dev , focusing on gender and ethnicity bias, image quality, and prompt alignment. To facilitate this evaluation, we developed the "Battle-Arena for Fair Image Synthesis" (BAFIS), a platform designed to collect human feedback on bias in generated images. Furthermore, we created a dataset comprising 21,140 synthetic images generated using multilingual prompts, which serves as a basis for our analysis. We further place our results within a broader social context by comparing them to official statistics from the German Federal Employment Agency. Our findings reveal systematic biases in text-to-image models, with established evaluation metrics in partial correlation with subjective user ratings. Thus, our research emphasizes the need for including human preferences to develop fairer and more inclusive text-to-image models.

</details>


### [74] [SPOT-E: Test-Time Entropy Shaping with Visual Spotlights for Frozen VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20244)
*Bo Yin,Xiaobin Hu,Chengming Xu,Ruolin Shen,Mo Yang,Jiangning Zhang,Peng-Tao Jiang,Cheng Tan,Shuicheng YAN*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SPOT-E is a plug-and-play test-time method that improves evidence readout in vision-language models by shaping answer entropy with low-entropy anchors and question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via lightweight GRPO-based tuning, yielding consistent gains and robustness across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs struggle with evidence-intensive tasks because decisive visual cues are small and easy to miss; existing inference-time interventions are open-loop and don't verify evidence usage, limiting grounding improvements.

Method: Study answer-span prediction entropy as an internal feedback signal and show naive entropy minimization is ambiguous. Introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective to reduce answer uncertainty while preserving high-confidence tokens. Instantiate as SPOT-E: produces question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance through lightweight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

Result: SPOT-E yields consistent gains across benchmarks and VLM families and improves robustness to visual corruptions. Code is publicly available at the provided repository.

Conclusion: The approach enables improved evidence readout without retraining, is plug-and-play at test time, and generalizes across models and tasks, supported by empirical gains and robustness improvements.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) often underperform on evidence intensive tasks because decisive visual evidence are small, localized, and easy to overlook, leading to failures in evidence readout even when high-level reasoning is intact. Prior inference-time visual interventions can improve grounding without retraining, but they are largely open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify whether highlighted evidence is actually used. We study answer-span prediction entropy as a model-internal feedback signal and show that naive entropy minimization is ambiguous, since low entropy may arise from evidence-grounded confidence or shortcut collapse. To resolve this ambiguity, we introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective that reduces answer uncertainty while preserving baseline high-confidence tokens. We instantiate this principle in SPOT-E, a plug-and-play test-time method that produces question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via light-weight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Across all benchmarks and different VLM families, SPOT-E yields consistent gains and improved robustness under visual corruptions. Code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/YinBo0927/SPOT-E}

</details>


### [75] [Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification for Weakly Supervised Histopathology Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20250)
*Duc T. Nguyen,Hoang-Long Nguyen,Thanh-Ha DO,Huy-Hieu Pham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) introduces a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) to purify intermediate features during forward pass, enabling high-fidelity activation maps within a single training loop. It outperforms multi-stage WSSS methods on LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS and reduces training time by 2–5x, with code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of established multi-stage WSSS in computational pathology, namely high computational cost and error propagation from shallow-layer texture biases, by eliminating post-hoc refinement and enabling end-to-end corrected feature representations.

Method: Propose HFRM that leverages deep global semantic context to filter out local anomalies in shallow layers during the forward pass, producing high-quality activation maps within a single training loop (single-stage). Integration within existing WSSS framework to guide CAM generation without separate pseudo-mask refinement stages.

Result: Empirical evaluation on LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS shows SSHR surpasses state-of-the-art multi-stage methods and reduces training time by 2–5×.

Conclusion: SSHR offers an efficient, accurate single-stage alternative for WSSS in histopathology, accelerating clinical translation; code is available for reproducibility.

Abstract: Existing weakly supervised semantic segmentation (WSSS) methods in computational pathology rely on a multi-stage paradigm: class activation map (CAM) generation, offline pseudo-mask refinement, and fully supervised retraining. While established, this decoupled approach presents fundamental limitations. The multi-stage process not only incurs high computational training costs but also suffers from error propagation: local texture biases in shallow CNN layers generate false-positive artifacts that subsequent refinement steps often fail to correct. To address these persistent challenges through a simple yet highly effective approach, we propose the Single-Stage Hierarchical Rectification (SSHR) framework. Rather than passively refining CAMs post-hoc, our method proactively purifies intermediate feature representations during the forward pass. We introduce a Hierarchical Feature Rectification Module (HFRM) that utilizes deep global semantic context to filter out local anomalies in shallow layers. This mechanism generates high-fidelity activation maps directly within a single training loop. Experiments on the LUAD-HistoSeg and BCSS datasets demonstrate that SSHR outperforms state-of-the-art multi-stage methods. Furthermore, SSHR reduces training duration by 2 to 5 times.
