UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance across three egocentric video understanding tasks - action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation on three challenging ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines and demonstrating that structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations.

</details>


### [100] [TimeProVe: Propose, then Verify for Efficient Long Video Temporal Reasoning in Activities of Daily Living](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20561)
*Arkaprava Sinha,Dominick Reilly,Siddharth Krishnan,Hieu Le,Srijan Das*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hybrid, cost-efficient LVQA framework (TimeProVe) uses an Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module to generate query-conditioned evidence windows with lightweight reasoning, followed by targeted VLM verification; introduces OpenTSUBench (OTB) for temporally grounded ADL reasoning; achieves strong accuracy with substantial reductions in VLM calls and cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify sparse, temporally localized evidence in hours-long videos without incurring the prohibitive cost of dense VLM processing or risking missed local, motion-centric cues from caption-based reasoning.

Method: ACE module converts temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers and supporting evidence windows using lightweight LLM reasoning; follow-up verification uses an expensive VLM only on targeted segments; introduces OpenTSUBench (OTB) benchmark for real-world ADL scenarios.

Result: TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%; reduces VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%; achieves competitive results on Charades-STA without explicit temporal grounding training and reaches state-of-the-art when enhanced with grounding VLMs.

Conclusion: A cost-efficient, temporally grounded LVQA framework that balances accuracy and efficiency via structured candidate evidence and selective VLM verification, validated on ADL-focused benchmarks and competitive on standard LVQA tasks.

Abstract: Long Video Question Answering (LVQA) requires identifying sparse, query-relevant evidence within hours-long untrimmed videos. Existing approaches either process videos densely with large vision-language models (VLMs), incurring prohibitive computational cost, or rely on sparse caption-based reasoning, which often misses temporally localized and motion-centric evidence. We introduce TimeProVe, a cost-efficient hybrid framework for temporally grounded reasoning in long videos. TimeProVe first employs lightweight modules to generate action-grounded answer--evidence hypotheses and subsequently invokes an expensive VLM only for targeted verification. The core of our framework lies in the Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module, which converts temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers and supporting evidence windows through lightweight LLM reasoning. We further introduce OpenTSUBench (OTB), an open-ended benchmark designed to evaluate temporally grounded reasoning in real-world Activities of Daily Living (ADL) scenarios. Experiments show that TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%, while reducing VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%. Furthermore, without explicit temporal grounding training, TimeProVe achieves competitive performance on Charades-STA, and reaches state-of-the-art results when enhanced with grounding VLMs.

</details>


### [101] [JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20563)
*Siang-Ling Zhang,Huai-Hsun Cheng,Tsung-Ju Yang,Yu-Lun Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A fast, training-free framework for text-driven 3D visual illusions that decouples generation into two stages: cross-space dual-branch denoising for geometric fusion and view-conditioned texture synthesis; achieves coherent dual-semantic 3D illusions in 3-5 minutes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address slow optimization-based methods and naive stitching that cause color oversaturation and visible seams, by enabling coherent geometry and multiple-view semantics in a single 3D object.

Method: Stage 1: cross-space dual-branch denoising that decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and SDF blending to ensure seamless fusion. Stage 2: view-conditioned texture synthesis that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry.

Result: Generates highly realistic dual-semantic 3D illusions in 3-5 minutes; significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency.

Conclusion: Presents a fast, training-free pipeline for text-driven 3D visual illusions, achieving strong geometry–semantics fidelity and efficiency, validated by experiments and a project page.

Abstract: Creating 3D visual illusions, a single 3D mesh that reveals entirely different semantics from various viewing angles, is a fascinating but tough challenge. Existing optimization-based methods are slow and can produce oversaturated colors. In contrast, naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects. This results in visible unnatural seams and semantic leaks. In this paper, we present a fast and training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions. Our approach decouples the generation into two stages. First, we propose a cross-space dual-branch denoising process. This process dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Field (SDF) blending, which ensures seamless geometric fusion. Second, we introduce a view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes. It significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency. Project page: https://siang1105.github.io/JanusMesh.github.io/

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [102] [Computational Identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19361)
*Lucius E. J. Bynum,Rajesh Ranganath,Kyunghyun Cho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces computational identifiability as a finite, data-driven alternative to theoretical identifiability in causal inference, and validates it via experiments and a public codebase.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Theoretical identifiability relies on asymptotic or idealized assumptions; real analyses with finite data and ambiguous graphs require a practical notion of identifiability based on computable search for estimators.

Method: Proposes a finite computational search procedure that, given a prior over parameters and a specified search algorithm, aims to find an empirical estimator that achieves a predefined error tolerance. If such an estimator is found, identifiability holds under the procedure and assumptions. Demonstrates across scenarios such as small samples, ambiguous graphical criteria, mixed observational–interventional data, and counterfactuals. Includes experiments and code.

Result: Shows that computational identifiability can address practical identifiability questions; provides empirical evidence that a finite search can identify estimators under the specified constraints; results span multiple settings; code made available.

Conclusion: Computational identifiability provides a pragmatic bridge between theory and practice, enabling identification in scenarios where traditional identifiability is incomplete; invites further study and application; code available at the referenced repository.

Abstract: Identification conditions describe the computability of a target query or parameter of interest as a function of the type and amount of information available. In causal identification, this information is often expressed in the form of a causal graph, and data are observed or collected for some subset of variables in the graph. Target queries may be for a single effect alone or for a class of effects in a given model. The derivation of an identification algorithm then defines mathematically the process by which the desired causal effect(s) can be uniquely determined, theoretically, in expectation. Identifiability in expectation, or 'theoretical identifiability,' generally assumes asymptotic properties, infinite data, or other mathematically idealized conditions. In this paper, we explore a fundamental distinction between this theoretical, idealized notion of identifiability and a proposed alternative that is computation-bound. The framework we propose - 'computational identifiability' - is to instead define a finite computational search procedure for an empirical estimator. If this process finds an estimator empirically, within a desired error tolerance, then identifiability is satisfied, conditional on the specified assumptions of the search (i.e., a prior distribution over the parameters) and conditional on the search procedure itself. Through several experiments, we demonstrate how this framework allows us to answer fine-grained, practical identification questions, such as identification with small finite samples, with ambiguous graphical criteria, with mixed observational-interventional data, and across counterfactual data and estimands. Code is available at https://github.com/lbynum/metadentify.

</details>


### [103] [When to Trust, How to Distill: Multi-Foundation Model Guidance for Lightweight, Robust Scientific Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19363)
*Rupasree Dey,Abdul Matin,Nathan Orwick,Yao Zhang,Shrideep Pallickara,Sangmi Lee Pallickara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Guard is a two-part distillation framework for Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) that uses a Contextual Router to select among multiple teachers and an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature to modulate distillation, enabling efficient, accurate, edge-friendly forecasts across climate domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSFMs provide rich temporal dynamics but suffer domain misalignment in zero-shot usage and high computation; there is a need to leverage misaligned yet potentially complementary teachers to build lightweight, accurate forecasters for resource-limited sensors.

Method: Instance-wise distillation reframed as a routing problem; Contextual Router chooses the most relevant teacher based on local statistics; Uncertainty-Gated Temperature acts as a circuit-breaker to attenuate distillation when teacher confidence diverges from domain reality. Evaluation across meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, and energy grids, comparing to a fixed-weight multi-teacher baseline.

Result: RMSE reduction relative to fixed-weight multi-teacher distillation baseline; effective distillation from suboptimal zero-shot FMs; misaligned teachers provide corrective benefits on 28.5% of the hardest instances; enables high-precision forecasting suitable for edge deployment.

Conclusion: Guard enables efficient, accurate forecasting for edge deployment by leveraging misaligned yet complementary foundation models; code available at the reported GitHub repository.

Abstract: The deployment of Time-Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) in physical sciences is hindered by a critical trade-off: while these models encode rich, universal temporal dynamics, they suffer from severe distributional misalignment when applied zero-shot to specific scientific domains, and their computational cost prohibits deployment in edge-computing sensor networks. We address a fundamental challenge: How can we extract latent structural knowledge from misaligned foundation models (FM) to train lightweight, specialized forecasters? We propose Gated Uncertainty-Aware Routing for Distillation (Guard), a novel framework that reframes multiteacher distillation as an instance-wise decision process with two adaptive mechanisms: (1) a Contextual Router that dynamically selects the most relevant teacher based on local input statistics, exploiting complementarity across diverse foundation models; and (2) an Uncertainty-Gated Temperature mechanism that acts as a "circuit-breaker," automatically attenuating distillation strength when teacher confidence diverges from domain reality. We evaluate our proposed lightweight framework on four climate-critical domains: meteorology, ecosystem carbon flux, soil moisture, and energy grids. Our method significantly reduces RMSE relative to a fixed-weight multi-teacher distillation baseline, successfully distilling knowledge from pretrained FMs (teachers) even when they exhibit suboptimal zero-shot accuracy due to distribution shift between the original and target data domains. We demonstrate that these domain-misaligned teachers can still serve as critical correctives, outperforming the globally superior FMs on 28.5% of the hardest instances. Ultimately, this enables high-precision scientific forecasting suitable for resource-constrained edge deployment. Code is available at https://github.com/RupasreeDey/GUARD-KDD2026.

</details>


### [104] [Fisher-Geometric Sharpness and the Implicit Bias of SGD toward Flat Minima](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20469)
*Md Sakir Ahmed,Kumaresh Sarmah,Hemen Dutta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reparametrization-invariant notion of sharpness based on the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) to explain SGD generalization; shows invariance to smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations; connects SGD noise to FIM, derives SDE, and PAC-Bayes bound; empirical validation on MNIST/CIFAR-10; experiments show SR tracks generalization better than Euclidean sharpness, with scaling in line with theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the lack of invariance in Euclidean flatness measures and ground the generalization of flat minima in the intrinsic geometry of the model's statistical manifold; address Dinh et al.'s critique by adopting an information-geometric, reparametrization-invariant framework.

Method: Define Riemannian sharpness via the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) geometry; analyze mini-batch SGD gradient noise as having covariance proportional to the FIM; derive the stationary distribution of the resulting stochastic differential equation (SDE); prove exponential concentration of mass at Riemannian-flat minima; obtain a PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled by SR; discuss practical estimation via diagonal FIM (approximate invariance) vs structured estimators like K-FAC; empirical evaluation on MNIST and CIFAR-10 across varying η and batch sizes.

Result: Demonstrates that true FIM-based sharpness is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations; diagonal estimators approximate invariance; SR correlates with generalization and scales with η/B as theory predicts; Euclidean sharpness fails to capture this invariant behavior; experimental results support the proposed link between geometry, SGD noise, and generalization.

Conclusion: Provides a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize in deep nets; introduces SR as a principled, geometry-grounded measure; connects information geometry with generalization through SDE analysis and PAC-Bayes bounds, with empirical validation on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: A widely held intuition in deep learning is that stochastic gradient descent (SGD) implicitly favors flat minima and that flat minima generalize better, but standard Euclidean measures of flatness such as the trace or maximum eigenvalue of the loss Hessian are not invariant under reparametrizations that preserve the network function, which undermines the theoretical foundations of this narrative. In this study we resolve this issue by grounding flatness in the Riemannian geometry of the statistical manifold induced by the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM). We define Riemannian sharpness mathematically and prove that it is invariant under smooth, function-preserving reparametrizations, which directly addresses the critique of Dinh et al. in the paper ``Sharp minima can generalize for deep nets''.We note that this invariance is a property of the true FIM; the diagonal empirical estimator used in practice (and in all experiments below) inherits invariance only approximately, and exact invariance under arbitrary reparametrizations would require structured estimators such as K-FAC. We formalize the gradient noise of mini-batch SGD as having a covariance structure proportional to the FIM, derive the stationary distribution of the resulting stochastic differential equation, and then show that the probability mass is exponentially concentrated at Riemannian-flat minima. A PAC-Bayes generalization bound controlled explicitly by SR formally links this geometric bias to test performance. Our experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 confirm that SR reliably tracks generalization in ways that Euclidean sharpness does not, and that its scaling with $η/B$ matches the theoretical predictions. Together these results provide a rigorous, reparametrization-invariant account of why flat minima generalize.

</details>


### [105] [Closing the Social-Semantic Gap: SPSD for Edge-Based Prompt Compression in Cloud LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19364)
*Abhinit Sen,Ajeet Kumar,Manaranjan Pradhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: On-device 4-bit distilled prompts significantly reduce cloud LLM input tokens with preserved response quality and measurable energy savings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate cloud-scale energy cost in LLM inference by compressing socially rich but semantically redundant prompts (the Social-Semantic Gap) using on-device distillation.

Method: Edge-based pipeline SPSD using a 4-bit quantised Small Language Model (Gemma-2-2B-Instruct Q4_K_M) to distill user prompts before sending to a cloud LLM (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct). Evaluation on 248 prompts with blind LLM-as-judge across 121 pairs; safety gates for sensitive content.

Result: Mean input token savings of 99.9 tokens per distilled call (all 146 calls saved). Non-inferior response quality within a 1-point non-inferiority margin on a 15-point rubric; 43% ties, 28% distilled wins, 29% raw wins. Cosine similarity: mean 0.682, median 0.712; 54.1% of pairs above 0.70. Per-call energy saving: 70–270 uWh under stated assumptions.

Conclusion: On-device prompt distillation can substantially cut cloud LLM input-token cost while preserving response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin; safety-critical content routed through rule-based gates.

Abstract: The prefill stage of Large Language Model (LLM) inference is a growing contributor to cloud-scale energy cost. Many consumer-support and conversational prompts contain social scaffolding: politeness markers, apologetic preamble, repetition, and rapport-building language that is important for human communication but carries low marginal information for machine reasoning. We call this discrepancy the Social-Semantic Gap. We present SPSD (Sentiment Preserving Semantic Distillation), an edge-based pipeline that compresses user prompts using a 4-bit quantised Small Language Model before transmission to a cloud-deployed LLM. Evaluation on a 248-prompt corpus using Gemma-2-2B-Instruct (Q4_K_M) as the SLM and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct as the cloud evaluation model yields a mean input token saving of 99.9 tokens per distilled call, with all 146 distilled calls yielding positive savings. Response quality, assessed by blind LLM-as-judge scoring across 121 pairs, is non-inferior to the raw path within a pre-specified 1-point margin on a 15-point rubric; the judge awarded 43 percent ties, 28 percent distilled wins, and 29 percent raw wins. Cosine similarity is mixed: mean 0.682, median 0.712, with 54.1 percent of pairs above the 0.70 reference threshold. Safety-critical domains are conservatively routed to passthrough via rule-based gates. Per-call net energy saving is estimated at 70-270 uWh under stated assumptions. SPSD shows that on-device prompt distillation can reduce cloud LLM input-token cost while preserving response quality within a practical non-inferiority margin.

</details>


### [106] [Performance Analysis and Optimization of 3D Generative Diffusion Models across GPU Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19365)
*Jeeho Ryoo,Yongchan Jung,Muhammad Ali Khaliq,Weidong Zhang,Jiatong Han,Byeong Kil Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Comprehensive GPU-performance analysis of Med-DDPM for 3D MRI; identifies bottlenecks in cuDNN/implicit-GEMM and demonstrates two architecture-aware optimizations (TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last) that yield large improvements without sacrificing synthesis quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the substantial GPU resource demands and heterogeneous kernel behavior in diffusion-based 3D MRI synthesis by understanding performance bottlenecks and proposing architecture-aware optimizations.

Method: Profiling across three NVIDIA architectures; kernel-level runtime breakdowns, instruction mix, memory utilization, warp-level activities, and profiler priority-score estimates; evaluate two optimizations (TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout) on Med-DDPM.

Result: Training dominated by cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM; inefficiencies from memory-access patterns, tensor-layout conversions, limited Tensor Core utilization. Optimizations reduce SM cycles up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45x to 9.98x, IPC +7% on A100, without degrading synthesis quality.

Conclusion: Architecture-aware optimizations can substantially boost diffusion-based 3D MRI synthesis efficiency on GPUs; TF32 Tensor Core activation and 3D channels-last layout are effective strategies for improving runtime and resource utilization without compromising output quality.

Abstract: Diffusion models have become essential for high-fidelity 3D MRI synthesis, yet their deployment remains constrained by substantial GPU resource demands arising from hundreds of U-Net evaluations per sample and a highly heterogeneous kernel behavior. This paper performs a comprehensive performance analysis of the state-of-the-art medical diffusion model, Med-DDPM, across three generations of NVIDIA architectures to study kernel-level runtime breakdowns, instruction-mix characteristics, memory system utilization, warp-level activities, and profiler priority-score estimates. We show that training is overwhelmingly dominated by cuDNN convolution and implicit-GEMM kernels, with inefficiencies arising from memory-access patterns, tensor-layout conversions, and limited Tensor Core utilization. Guided by these insights, we evaluate two architecture-aware optimizations TF32 Tensor Core activation and a 3D channels-last layout and demonstrate that they reduce SM cycles by up to 100x, cut dynamic instructions by 100x, raise Tensor Core utilization from 1.45 to 9.98x, and increase IPC by 7% on A100, all without degrading synthesis quality.

</details>


### [107] [Information Lattice Learning as Probabilistic Graphical Model Structure Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19366)
*Haizi Yu,Lav R. Varshney*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ILL provides a principled, interpretable rule-learning framework by projecting signals onto a hierarchy of abstractions and interpreting learned rules as marginal constraints over quotient variables; entropy lifting yields a log-linear factor graph; the approach links to graphical models and maximum entropy while not forming a Bayesian network.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To learn interpretable, hierarchical rules from signals and connect them to probabilistic graphical models, enabling a unified symbolic-probabilistic learning framework and guiding inference, identifiability, and hybrid methods.

Method: Alternating projections of the signal onto a partition lattice encoding abstractions; lift selected rules back to the signal domain; define quotient variables; a rule is the marginal law of a quotient variable; general lifting yields joint distributions satisfying the marginal constraints; special lifting uses maximum-ignorance with an L2 uniformity principle; Shannon-entropy lifting yields a log-linear factor graph with factors indexed by learned abstractions. The lattice edges encode refinement/coarsening, not conditional dependence, so ILL is structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables.

Result: Rules correspond to marginal constraints; lifting yields joint distributions satisfying those constraints; entropy lifting produces log-linear factor graphs; clarifies that ILL is not a Bayesian network but a framework for structure learning of constraint-based factor graphs; relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models and opens directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

Conclusion: This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models and suggests future work on inference, identifiability, and integrating symbolic and probabilistic learning.

Abstract: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable rules of a signal by alternately projecting the signal onto a partition lattice that encodes a hierarchy of abstractions and lifting selected rules back to the signal domain. When the signal is a probability mass function, we show the probabilistic rules learned by ILL admit a natural probabilistic graphical model (PGM) interpretation and develop this interpretation in detail. A partition in ILL induces a deterministic quotient variable, and a rule is the marginal law of that quotient variable. A rule set is therefore a collection of marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions. General lifting is the feasible family of all joint distributions satisfying those constraints, while special lifting chooses a maximum-ignorance reconstruction, implemented in ILL by an L2 uniformity principle closely related to maximum entropy. Under a Shannon-entropy lifting, the same constraints yield a log-linear factor graph whose factors are indexed by learned abstractions. The information lattice itself, however, is not a Bayesian network: its edges encode refinement and coarsening of abstractions, not conditional dependence. Thus ILL is best viewed as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models, while suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

</details>


### [108] [Weibull Weight-Scale Parameter Evolution under AdamW Training Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19367)
*Tiexin Ding*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We analyze a two-parameter Weibull framework for transformer weight dynamics under AdamW, revealing a dominant three-force decomposition that explains the growth, overshoot, and relaxation of the weight-scale parameter λ during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ grows, overshoots, and relaxes in AdamW training, and link its dynamics to force components arising from the optimizer and weight decay in transformer training.

Method: Derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: alignment force (weight–update direction correlation), injection force (adaptive step magnitude), and decay force (decoupled weight decay). Apply to self-trained Pythia-70M with ground-truth optimizer moments across seeds; quantify force budgets; analyze saturation where alignment and decay balance. Reconstruct λ(t) from RMS-to-Weibull and decompose residual offset into bridge and integration components. Develop a spline displacement method to recover alignment force from sparse checkpoints (approx. 92–94% accuracy).

Result: Alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88–94% of the absolute force budget across four seeds and robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from growth to relaxation of λ. The RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is small (~5–6% in densely sampled regions) and decomposes into bridge and integration components. The spline displacement method recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with ~92–94% accuracy (roughly twice the naive two-point baseline). Peak λ varies with training-data coherence, indicating a data-dependent component. Code and data are provided.

Conclusion: The weight-scale dynamics under AdamW can be effectively explained by a three-force decomposition, with alignment driving growth and near-saturation balance between alignment and decay causing relaxation. The approach yields quantitative explanations for λ(t) behavior and offers a practical method to estimate alignment from sparse data; a data-coherence effect on λ peak invites further study.

Abstract: Building on a two-parameter Weibull framework for diagnosing transformer weight distributions, we study why the Weibull weight-scale parameter $λ$ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training. We derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: an alignment force measuring the correlation between weights and the adaptive update direction, an injection force from adaptive step magnitude, and a decay force from decoupled weight decay. On self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88-94% of the absolute force budget across four random seeds and remaining robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. These force dynamics directly govern the squared-norm component underlying $λ(t)$; the remaining RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is measurable and decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling approximately 5-6% in densely sampled regions. To extend the analysis to real models where optimizer moments are unavailable, we introduce a spline displacement method that recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with approximately 92-94% accuracy, about twice the naive two-point baseline. We further observe that the peak value of $λ(t)$ varies with training-data coherence in our experiments, suggesting a data-dependent component of weight-scale growth that we leave to a controlled follow-up study. Code and data are available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public.

</details>


### [109] [Zero-Inflated Gaussian Distributions Enable Parameter-Space Sparsity in Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19369)
*Andreas Faust,Sven Nitzsche,Juergen Becker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) EDA for sparse continuous optimization, modeling sparsity and active values jointly via a latent Gaussian framework, achieving faster convergence and better final performance than dense EDAs and hand-crafted sparse methods on Lunar Lander.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EDAs excel for black-box optimization but struggle with sparsity in continuous spaces. Existing sparse optimizers rely on hand-crafted sparsity operators, reintroducing bias. A joint, hierarchy-free model of sparsity and values is needed with identifiable latent structure and efficient estimation.

Method: Propose ZIG sampling laws with a latent Gaussian model that has separate indicator (sparsity) and value dimensions. This captures sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and interactions between sparsity and values. Prove identifiability of latent parameters; develop amortized inversion-based estimators to recover them. The approach enables joint optimization of sparsity patterns and active values without a predefined hierarchy.

Result: Latent correlation structures are accurately recovered by the estimators. On the Lunar Lander benchmark, the ZIG-EDA converges faster and achieves higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while using only a small fraction of active parameters.

Conclusion: The ZIG-EDA provides a principled, hierarchy-free framework for sparse EDAs, enabling joint inference of sparsity patterns and active values with identifiable latent parameters and practical estimators, yielding improved performance in sparse continuous optimization.

Abstract: Estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) are a powerful class of evolutionary methods for black-box optimization, especially when little is known about the structure of the objective. Whereas classical evolutionary algorithms rely on hand-designed mutation and crossover operators, hard to devise for unknown problem structures, and a source of bias, EDAs sidestep operator design entirely: they fit a probability distribution to the best individuals and sample the next generation from it. EDAs are well established on continuous parameter spaces, but they have not previously been generalized to sparse ones, in which most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Existing sparse black-box optimizers therefore reintroduce exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid: hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes alternating between support set and active values, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions. We close this gap by proposing multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and the interactions between the two, so sparsity patterns and active values are optimized jointly, hierarchy-free. We show that the latent parameters of this model are identifiable from observed samples, unlike in the missing-data settings where related constructions originate, and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators for them. The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures, and on the Lunar Lander benchmark the resulting ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.

</details>


### [110] [Human-like autonomy emerges from self-play and a pinch of human data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19370)
*Daphne Cornelisse,Julian Hunt,Zixu Zhang,Waël Doulazmi,Kevin Joseph,Jaime Fernández Fisac,Eugene Vinitsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A minimal self-play reinforcement learning framework for driving that uses a small amount of human demonstrations as a regularizer on top of a safe goal-reaching objective, achieving strong alignment with human trajectories with much less data and training time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-play driving can yield efficient policies but often learns alien behaviors due to misalignment with human norms; existing approaches rely on brittle reward engineering or domain randomization and require large human datasets.

Method: Introduce a human-demo regularization term atop a minimal safe goal-reaching reward, enabling effective integration of limited human data (e.g., 30 minutes) to steer self-play policies toward human-like driving.

Result: Policies align with held-out human trajectories; data-efficient (30 minutes of human data, 2500x less than some imitation methods); training completes in ~15 hours on a consumer-grade GPU; code and videos available.

Conclusion: A small amount of human data can substantially improve human-compatibility of self-play driving policies without extensive reward engineering, achieving practical data efficiency.

Abstract: Self-play reinforcement learning has recently emerged as a way to train driving policies without any human data. It uses cheap, large-scale simulations to substitute expensive, large-scale human driving demonstrations. A key limitation of this approach is that policies trained through pure self-play can learn effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with people. Previous works attempt to mitigate such behavioral misalignments through extensive reward engineering and domain randomization, which are brittle and labor-intensive. Instead of completely discarding human demonstrations, our method treats them as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. Like the spice in a good stew, we find that a little human data goes a long way: our method uses only 30 minutes of human demonstrations, 2500x fewer than comparable imitation learning approaches. Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories and complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. Videos and full source code are available at https://spiced-self-play.com/.

</details>


### [111] [ProMUSE: Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty-guided Staged Evidential Alzheimer Disease Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19371)
*Long Doan,Branden Chen,Ethan Litton,Huan Huang,Jiajing Huang,Yixin Xie,Weihua Zhou,Nandakumar Narayanan,Chen Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProMUSE enables cost-efficient, uncertainty-aware multimodal AD diagnosis by staging modality acquisition: start with low-cost clinical data, quantify uncertainty, and progressively incorporate MRI/PET only when needed, using DST-based fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MRI/PET are expensive and not universally accessible; there is a need for early AD diagnosis using cheaper data sources while maintaining accuracy.

Method: An evidential classifier operates on clinical data to produce Dirichlet-based uncertainty; when uncertainty exceeds a threshold, additional MRI or PET features are incorporated. Belief and uncertainty from each modality are fused via Dempster-Shafer theory to yield a calibrated multimodal prediction.

Result: On ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks, ProMUSE matches or exceeds full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50–90%, demonstrating notable cost savings without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: ProMUSE offers a practical, uncertainty-aware framework for real-world AD screening by lowering acquisition costs and relying on progressively integrated multimodal data when warranted.

Abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a fatal disorder that destroys memory and cognitive skills in the elderly population. Most treatments for AD are effective in the early stage, leading to an increasing demand for early AD diagnosis. AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data such as clinical assessments, structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) imaging. However, MRI and PET acquisition remain costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows. We propose ProMUSE, a Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty Guided Staged Evidential Network that adaptively determines when additional modalities are necessary, helping reduce the overall cost of data acquisition while maintaining accuracy. ProMUSE first performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data and quantifies uncertainty via a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, ProMUSE progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing modality-wise belief and uncertainty through Dempster-Shafer theory to obtain a calibrated multimodal prediction. This staged acquisition strategy enables accurate diagnosis while minimizing reliance on expensive imaging. Experiments on ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks demonstrate that ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, yielding substantial cost savings. These results highlight ProMUSE as a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening.

</details>


### [112] [cAPM: Continual AI-Assisted Pace-Mapping with Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19373)
*Dylan O'Hara,Pradeep Bajracharya,Casey Meisenzahl,Karli Gillette,Anton J. Prassl,Gernot Plank,Saman Nazarian,Roderick Tung,John L Sapp,Linwei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces cAPM, a continual, transfer-learning AI framework for pace-mapping in ventricular tachycardia (VT) that uses a task-agnostic surrogate to map pacing sites to 12-lead ECGs, with active learning and replay-based continual learning to transfer knowledge across targets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce pacing-data requirements and enable cross-target knowledge transfer in pace-mapping for VT ablation, addressing the data-efficiency and transfer limitations of prior methods.

Method: A task-agnostic surrogate neural network maps pacing sites to 12-lead ECG morphology. An active-learning strategy selects the most informative pacing site per VT target. A continual learning approach with replay preserves knowledge across sequential targets. Evaluated in an in-silico testbed with sequential localization tasks across varied physiological conditions and ventricular geometries.

Result: cAPM achieved an 81% probability of localizing within 5 mm (clinical tolerance) using ~4.5 pacing sites, outperforming the state-of-the-art method which achieved 38% with ~13.7 pacing sites; this held with and without replay of past data samples.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates strong data-efficiency and cross-target transfer in pace-mapping, supporting progression to in-vivo preclinical and clinical studies for guiding VT pace-mapping.

Abstract: Ventricular tachycardia is a life-threatening rhythm disorder and a major cause of sudden cardiac death. Pace-mapping is a clinical procedure for identifying the intervention target during catheter ablation of VT. It requires clinicians to pace different sites in the ventricles and rapidly interpret the resulting electrocardiograms to determine where to pace next or whether a target site has been identified. Active learning AI models have been proposed to guide clinicians to the next pacing site, showing promise in reducing the number of pacing sites and improving the efficiency of pace-mapping. Existing methods require retraining each target without the ability to transfer knowledge across multiple VTs within the same patient or across patients. We introduce cAPM for continuous AI-assisted pace-mapping to capture and transfer knowledge accumulated from past pace-mapping data to reduce the number of pace-mapping data needed for future target VTs. This is made possible by a task-agnostic surrogate neural network that learns the mapping from pacing sites to 12-lead ECG morphology, an active-learning strategy that refines this surrogate model by selecting the most informative pacing site for each target, and a continual learning strategy to do so sequentially while retaining knowledge from prior targets. Evaluated on an in-silico testbed consisting of sequentially-presented localization tasks across different physiological conditions and ventricular geometries, cAPM with and without replay of past data samples achieved an 81% probability of localizing within clinical tolerance (5 mm accuracy) using 4.5 pace-mapping sites, compared to the state-of-the-art active-learning method achieving 38% probability using 13.7 pacing sites. These results provide a strong basis for preparing cAPM towards in-vivo preclinical and clinical studies where it can be used to guide pace-mapping.

</details>


### [113] [Protein Representation Learning with Secondary-Structure and Energy-Filtered Hydrogen-Bond Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19374)
*Mohamed Mouhajir,Limei Wang,El Houcine Bergou,Hajar El Hammouti,Lamiae Azizi,Dongqi Fu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a secondary-structure-aware graph neural network for proteins, incorporating SS annotations and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond edges to improve representations and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard graph representations often rely on sequence adjacency or simple geometric proximity, which do not fully capture the three-dimensional organization and energetic interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonds) critical to folding. Incorporating secondary structure and energetics provides a biologically grounded inductive bias for protein representation learning.

Method: Residue-level nodes are augmented with secondary structure assignments. Graph edges are constructed from hydrogen-bond interactions filtered by their energetic strength. A GNN learns representations that capture local motifs and long-range couplings, enabling improved protein analysis. The approach is evaluated on common benchmarks, and the code is released.

Result: The method yields consistent improvements over existing graph-based approaches on standard protein benchmarks. The learned graph representations also offer enhanced interpretability, with connectivity aligning to established structural motifs.

Conclusion: Incorporating secondary structure information and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond topology provides an effective inductive bias for protein representation learning, improving performance and interpretability. The authors release code at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Graph-based representations are widely used in protein modeling, yet many existing approaches rely primarily on sequence adjacency or geometric proximity, which only partially reflect the principles governing protein folding. Proteins instead adopt complex three-dimensional conformations organized around secondary structure elements, such as $α$-helices and $β$-sheets, which encode recurring local motifs and stabilizing hydrogen-bond interactions. In this work, we introduce a secondary-structure-aware graph neural network for protein representation learning. Residue-level node representations are augmented with secondary structure assignments, and graph edges are constructed from hydrogen-bond interactions filtered by their energetic strength. This design enables the model to capture both local structural context and long-range couplings that are central to protein stability and function. We evaluate the proposed approach on commonly used protein benchmarks and observe consistent improvements over existing graph-based methods. In addition, the resulting graph representations offer enhanced biological interpretability, as the learned connectivity aligns with established structural motifs. These findings suggest that incorporating secondary structure and energy-filtered hydrogen-bond topology provides an effective inductive bias for protein representation learning. The code is released at https://github.com/mohamedmohamed2021/SSProNet

</details>


### [114] [Physics-Informed Discovery of Yield Functions in Plasticity via Convex Neural Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19375)
*Hyeonbin Moon,Donghyuk Cho,Jecheon Yu,Jeong Whan Yoon,Seunghwa Ryu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A physics-informed, convex neural network framework discovers anisotropic yield functions from displacement and reaction-force data without direct stress or yield data, by embedding a learnable yield function inside elastoplastic FE updates and enforcing convexity, homogeneity, and tension-compression symmetry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifying anisotropic yield surfaces from full-field measurements is challenging due to indirect observables, sparse loading directions, and nontrivial analytical forms; a data-driven yet physically constrained approach is needed.

Method: Represent yield function as a convex NN constrained to positive homogeneity of degree one and tension-compression symmetry, embedded within differentiable elastoplastic stress integration; train via physics-informed loss (force equilibrium) across multiple loading cases, using displacement and reaction-force data; validated on FE benchmarks with von Mises, Hill 1948, Yld2000-2d; assess yield contour accuracy, noise sensitivity, identifiability, epistemic uncertainty; support with polynomial surrogate.

Result: The framework achieves accurate yield contours comparable to standard yield surfaces, remains robust to displacement noise, enables identifiability through plastically active stress states, epistemic uncertainty is quantified, and supports surrogate polynomial representations for deployment.

Conclusion: Mechanics-constrained, data-driven discovery of anisotropic yield functions is feasible from displacement and force data without direct stress/yield observations, preserving the elastoplastic structure for integration and enabling broader applicability.

Abstract: Identifying anisotropic yield functions remains challenging since yielding is not directly observed in full-field mechanical measurements, directional calibration can require many loading directions, and selecting an appropriate analytical form is nontrivial. This study proposes a physics-informed framework for discovering yield functions from full-field displacement data and reaction force data, without stress observations, plastic strain measurements, direct yield surface data, or a prescribed parametric yield function. The framework identifies the yield function as a mechanically constrained constitutive component inside elastoplastic stress integration, rather than through direct stress-space supervision. The yield function is represented by a convex neural network that enforces convexity and positive homogeneity of degree one while imposing the assumed tension-compression symmetry, and this neural yield function is trained with a differentiable stress update and a physics-informed force equilibrium loss across multiple loading cases. The proposed framework is validated using finite element (FE) benchmark studies with von Mises, Hill 1948, and Yld2000-2d yield functions, assessing yield contour agreement, displacement-noise sensitivity, identifiability through plastically active stress states, epistemic uncertainty, and polynomial-surrogate deployment. This study provides a mechanics-constrained pathway for discovering anisotropic yield functions from displacement and force data while keeping the identified component within the structure of elastoplastic stress integration.

</details>


### [115] [Cost-Optimal LLM Routing with Limited User Feedback under User Satisfaction Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19376)
*Herbert Woisetschläger,Arastun Mammadli,Ryan Zhang,Shiqiang Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SLARouter is an online, cost-aware LLM routing method that learns a cost-optimal policy from sparse, one-sided feedback while guaranteeing SLA compliance, yielding up to 2.2x cost savings without per-benchmark tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a fundamental tension between inference cost and response quality under Service Level Agreements (SLAs). Existing cost-aware routing methods rely on complete feedback, offline training, and per-workload tuning and often lack SLA guarantees or adaptivity, creating inefficiency in production.

Method: An online routing algorithm that learns a cost-optimal policy from sparse, one-sided user feedback in production, with theoretical guarantees for both cost optimality and strict SLA compliance.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse LLM benchmarks shows SLA constraints are met without per-benchmark tuning, and operating costs are reduced by up to 2.2x compared with existing baselines.

Conclusion: SLARouter enables cost-efficient, SLA-guaranteed LLM serving in production, offering strong theoretical guarantees and practical cost savings without extensive feedback or per-benchmark tuning.

Abstract: Inference costs for large language model (LLM) applications are rapidly growing, driven by surging demand and rising infrastructure cost. Users expect high-quality responses, and in commercial settings this is formally codified in Service Level Agreements (SLAs), creating a fundamental tension between cost and quality. Recent progress on cost-aware LLM request routing has shown potential to resolve this tension, but existing approaches rely on complete feedback signals, offline training, extensive per-workload tuning, and most lack SLA guarantees or inference-time adaptivity. We introduce SLARouter, an online routing algorithm that learns a cost-optimal policy from the sparse, one-sided user feedback available in production systems. SLARouter provides theoretical guarantees for both cost optimality and strict SLA compliance. Experiments across a wide range of LLM benchmarks show that SLARouter satisfies SLA constraints without the need for per-benchmark tuning, reducing operating cost by up to 2.2x over existing baselines.

</details>


### [116] [Emyx: Fast and efficient all-atom protein generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19377)
*Nicholas J. Williams,Ward Haddadin,Matteo P. Ferla,Constantin Schneider,Nicholas B. Woodall,Ruby Sedgwick,Christian D. Madsen,Andrew L. Hopkins,Edward O. Pyzer-Knapp*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A compact 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model, Emyx, for enzyme design, achieves state-of-the-art on AME benchmark with far lower training cost by reformulating flow matching into the EDM diffusion sampling framework; uses lightweight transformer-based conditioning and sparse connectivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational cost and increase design diversity by moving away from heavy all-atom structure-prediction architectures toward models that condition on sparse geometric constraints.

Method: Conditional flow matching architecture with standard transformer blocks, lightweight conditional representations, sparse connectivity; exact reparametrisation of flow matching interpolant into the EDM noise-level framework; training on AME benchmark; 682 GPU-hours; 140M parameters.

Result: Outperforms Proteína-Complexa and RFdiffusion3 on the AME benchmark across multiple criteria: global fold recovery, catalytic geometry accuracy, structural novelty, scaffold diversity, geometric validity; training cost ~4x less than RFdiffusion3.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that a small, efficiently-parameterized conditional flow model can achieve state-of-the-art enzyme design performance while significantly reducing training cost; bridges flow matching with EDM diffusion sampling for improved efficiency.

Abstract: Computational enzyme design requires generating proteins that scaffold catalytic residues and ligands, a task that demands both geometric accuracy and structural diversity from the underlying generative model. Current all-atom generators inherit expensive architectures from structure prediction, leading to high training costs and limited sample diversity. We argue that much of this complexity is unnecessary for generators, which condition on sparse geometric constraints rather than rich co-evolutionary signals. Emyx is a 140M-parameter conditional flow matching model that concentrates capacity within standard transformer blocks, replacing heavy embedding stacks with lightweight conditional representations and sparse connectivity. We additionally derive an exact reparametrisation of the flow matching interpolant into the EDM noise-level framework, bridging flow matching training efficiency with state-of-the-art sampling methods designed for diffusion models without retraining. Despite being the smallest model, Emyx outperforms both Proteína-Complexa and RFdiffusion3 against the AME enzyme design benchmark across success rate under strict evaluation requiring both global fold recovery and catalytic geometry accuracy, structural novelty, scaffold diversity, and geometric validity, while training in just $682$ GPU-hours, roughly $4\times$ less than RFdiffusion3.

</details>


### [117] [A Hybrid GNN-FEM Framework for Phase-Field Fracture Simulation. Physics-Preserving Hybridization for Generalizable Surrogate Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19378)
*Hyeonbin Moon,Yongjin Choi,Seunghwa Ryu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid GNN-FEM framework for phase-field fracture modeling that replaces the phase-field update with a GNN surrogate inside a staggered FEM scheme, preserving mechanical equilibrium, enabling significant cost reduction while maintaining accuracy and strong generalization across geometries, loads, properties, and discretizations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the high computational cost and limited generalization of conventional phase-field fracture simulations, which solve history-dependent, nonlinear systems incrementally using FEM.

Method: Integrate a graph neural network surrogate into the standard staggered phase-field fracture solver, replacing the phase-field update at each load increment while keeping the FEM displacement solver. Use an increment-structured learning target, dimensionless features on a mesh-based graph, and a physics-informed loss derived from the phase-field governing equation to ensure consistency. The surrogate leverages a selective learning target rather than learning the full trajectory.

Result: Numerical experiments show reduced computational cost relative to conventional FEM while preserving accuracy; the method generalizes robustly to different geometries, loading conditions, material properties, and discretizations.

Conclusion: The hybrid GNN-FEM framework delivers physically consistent, efficient, and generalizable phase-field fracture modeling by exploiting an incrementally structured surrogate within a variational FEM framework, with potential applicability to other history-dependent SciML problems.

Abstract: Scientific machine learning (SciML) has emerged as a promising approach for accelerating simulations of complex physical systems, yet achieving physically consistent and generalizable predictions for nonlinear, history-dependent problems remains a central challenge. In this study, we propose a hybrid GNN--FEM framework for efficient and generalizable phase-field fracture modeling. While phase-field approaches provide a robust variational framework for simulating complex crack evolution, their high computational cost limits practical applications because they require solving coupled, nonlinear, and history-dependent systems within an incremental finite element procedure. To address this challenge, a graph neural network surrogate is integrated into the conventional staggered scheme, replacing the phase-field update at each load increment while retaining the FEM-based displacement solver to enforce mechanical equilibrium and boundary conditions. By preserving the incremental solution structure, the framework remains consistent with history-dependent fracture evolution without requiring the surrogate to approximate the full solution trajectory. This selective surrogate strategy emphasizes the identification of a physically meaningful and incrementally structured learning target, rather than relying on brute-force data generation to learn the full fracture process. The proposed framework achieves strong generalization across varying geometries, loading conditions, material properties, and discretizations through dimensionless feature design, a graph-based formulation on mesh-based domains, and a physics-informed loss derived from the governing phase-field equation. Numerical experiments demonstrate that the hybrid approach reduces computational cost while maintaining accuracy compared with conventional FEM, and exhibits robust predictive performance across diverse problem settings.

</details>


### [118] [How Linear Is a Transformer Feed-Forward Block? Per-Block Linear Recoverability Is Learned, Not Architectural](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19379)
*Stuart Whipp*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents R^2_lin, a closed-form, optimizer-free measure of how linear a transformer FFN block is, revealing strong block-level nonlinear diversity and enabling targeted compression while warning about baselines and ill-conditioned activations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify the actual nonlinearity of trained transformer FFN blocks and separate learned nonlinear behavior from architecture, to better understand computation and guide safe compression.

Method: Decompose each FFN block into an exact least-squares linear map plus a residual; compute the coefficient of determination (R^2_lin) on held-out data; analyze across GPT-2, Pythia-160m, and llama-160m blocks; test a low-rank bilinear probe on the residual; perform targeted compression by replacing recoverable blocks with single-layer equivalents; report the closed-form LS ceiling to avoid under-converged baselines.

Result: R^2_lin is highly heterogeneous and non-monotone with depth across models (from >0.99 to <0.3); not dictated by activation function; same-width GELU models can have sharply different linear recoverability; a low-rank bilinear probe recovers only a few points of R^2 with gains uncorrelated to residual nonlinearity; recoverable blocks admit large single-layer replacements (e.g., GPT-2 early FFN at 8x fewer params with +0.77 perplexity); non-recoverable blocks indicate unsafe compression; measured nonlinear residuals are distributed and higher-order beyond a simple product; methodological pitfall noted: trained linear baselines can under-converge on ill-conditioned activations; they report the exact closed-form ceiling. 

Conclusion: Linearity is a learned, block-specific property rather than an architectural trait; R^2_lin provides a robust diagnostic for quantifying nonlinearity, guiding compression strategies, and highlighting pitfalls in baseline approaches; the nonlinear contribution in FFNs appears distributed and higher-order, challenging simplistic low-rank probes.

Abstract: Transformer feed-forward networks (FFNs) are often treated as nonlinear stores of computation, yet how nonlinear a trained FFN block actually is has rarely been measured. We treat each FFN as a position-wise input-to-output map and split it into the exact least-squares linear approximation plus a residual. The held-out variance the closed-form linear map explains defines a block's linear recoverability (R^2_lin), an optimiser-free measure of its linearity.
  Across all twelve blocks of GPT-2, Pythia-160m, and llama-160m, R^2_lin is highly heterogeneous and non-monotone with depth, ranging from near-linear (>0.99) to strongly nonlinear (<0.3) between adjacent blocks, and is not set by the activation function: same-width GELU models GPT-2 and Pythia-160m have sharply different profiles, so recoverability is a learned property of individual trained blocks, not an architectural one. A low-rank bilinear probe of the residual recovers only a few points of R^2, with gain uncorrelated with residual nonlinearity: the unrecovered computation is not a single position-wise product but higher-order or distributed structure.
  The measurement also serves as a targeted compression signal: recoverable blocks admit large single-layer replacements (GPT-2's early FFN at 8x fewer parameters for +0.77 perplexity), while low-recoverability blocks flag where this is unsafe. It further exposes a methodological pitfall: trained linear baselines can badly under-converge on ill-conditioned transformer activations, so we report the exact closed-form least-squares ceiling throughout.

</details>


### [119] [VERITAS: Verifier-Guided Proof Search for Zero-Shot Formal Theorem Proving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19399)
*Manish Acharya,Zhenyu Liao,Yueke Zhang,Kevin Leach,Yu Huang,Yifan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VERITAS is a zero-shot two-phase framework for LLM-based formal provers that converts verifier feedback into guided proof search via (1) Best-of-N sampling and (2) a critic-guided MCTS that uses Phase 1 failures as explicit negatives; it preserves Phase 1 proofs, achieving improvements on miniF2F and a new 55-theorem CombiBench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM provers collapse rich verifier signals (syntax errors, type mismatches, partial progress) into a binary pass/fail, hindering efficient proof search and lemma recovery. VERITAS aims to exploit all verifier signals via a feedback-driven exploration loop.

Method: Phase 1: Best-of-N sampling that collects verifier signals across multiple runs. Phase 2: a critic-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) that ingests Phase 1 failures as explicit negative examples; Phase 2 preserves all Phase 1 theorems to attribute new solves to feedback-driven exploration.

Result: MiniF2F accuracy: 40.6% (vs Best-of-5 36.9%, Portfolio 26.2%). VERITAS-CombiBench: 7.3% (55-theorem benchmark; Best-of-5 1.8%, Portfolio 3.6%). Indicates guided feedback improves search on some benchmarks and that unguided sampling harms when correct lemmas must be recovered iteratively. Artifacts released on GitHub.

Conclusion: A zero-shot, feedback-driven two-phase protocol can outperform unguided or single-phase baselines by preserving proven lemmas and using verifier signals to steer search, though gains are benchmark-dependent and rely on effective lemma-name recovery.

Abstract: LLM-based formal provers often collapse rich verifier signals (syntax errors, type mismatches, partial goal progress) into a binary pass/fail bit. We present VERITAS, a zero-shot framework that routes every verifier signal back into proof search through a two-phase protocol: Best-of-N sampling first, then a critic-guided MCTS pass that ingests Phase 1 failures as explicit negative examples. The protocol preserves every theorem solved by its own Phase 1 sweep, so Phase 2's additional solves are attributable to feedback-driven exploration. VERITAS reaches 40.6% on miniF2F (vs. an independently run Best-of-5 at 36.9%, Portfolio 26.2%) and 7.3% on VERITAS-CombiBench, a 55-theorem combinatorics benchmark we release on which Best-of-5 (1.8%) falls below Portfolio (3.6%), exposing that unguided sampling hurts when correct lemma names must be recovered iteratively from verifier feedback. Artifacts are available on GitHub.

</details>


### [120] [Thermodynamic Signatures of Reasoning: Free-Energy and Spectral-Form-Factor Diagnostics for Hallucination Detection in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19404)
*Salim Khazem*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Free-Energy Signatures (Fes) is a spectral descriptor for attention Laplacians in LLMs that treats each layer's Laplacian as a Hamiltonian and extracts thermodynamic potentials (partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity) and an RMT spectral form factor. It provides theoretical guarantees (stability, expressiveness, PAC bound) and empirical improvements in detection of hallucination (higher AUROC) across multiple models and benchmarks, without modifying the LLMs. In unsupervised mode, an RMT-deviation score yields label-free AUROC ~0.71. Spectral statistics show correct generations are more Wigner-Dyson, hallucinations more Poisson. Code is in supplementary materials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation:  Hallucinations in LLMs are a deployment risk. Prior spectral diagnostics summarize the Laplacian spectrum with few statistics, wasting structure. A richer, layer-wise spectral descriptor could better capture reasoning quality and detect hallucinations without retraining models.

Method:  Treat each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian. Compute its thermodynamic potentials: partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity. Include the random-matrix-theory (RMT) spectral form factor. Prove Lipschitz stability under attention perturbations; show expressiveness by approximating moment-derived spectral functionals under regularity/grid assumptions; derive a finite-sample PAC bound on AUROC for a training-free detector built from Fes. Evaluate on six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks, comparing against LapEig and GoR-4. Also analyze RMT deviation and spectral statistics (Wigner-Dyson vs Poisson). Make code/configs available in supplementary material.

Result:  Fes-based descriptors yielded the strongest aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines, beating LapEig by about 6.5 AUROC points and GoR-4 by about 2.4 points on average, without updating the LLMs. In fully unsupervised mode, an RMT-deviation score achieved mean AUROC 0.71. Correct generations exhibit more Wigner-Dyson-like spectra, while hallucinations trend Poisson-like. The paper also provides a finite-sample PAC bound for AUROC and Lipschitz stability results. Code is provided in the supplementary materials.

Conclusion:  Fes enriches finite spectral summaries with rich thermodynamic and RMT-inspired features, enabling effective, training-free detection of hallucination without model updates. The approach combines theoretical guarantees with strong empirical performance and reveals distinct spectral regimes for correct vs. incorrect generations.

Abstract: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) is deployment-critical, and recent work shows that the spectrum of attention-derived graph Laplacians carries strong signal about reasoning quality. Prior spectral diagnostics, however, summarize the Laplacian spectrum by a handful of eigenvalues or hand-picked scalars, leaving most of its structure unused. We propose Free-Energy Signatures (Fes), a spectral descriptor that treats each layer's attention Laplacian as a Hamiltonian and extracts its thermodynamic potentials partition function, free energy, spectral entropy, heat capacity together with the random-matrix-theory (RMT) spectral form factor. We prove three results: (i)~Lipschitz stability of Fes under attention perturbation; (ii)~an expressiveness result showing that Fes enriches finite spectral summaries and approximates moment-derived spectral functionals under explicit regularity and grid-resolution assumptions; and (iii)~a finite-sample PAC bound on the AUROC of a training-free detector built from Fes. Empirically, across six open-weight LLMs and six benchmarks, a lightweight probe on Fes descriptors achieves the strongest aggregate AUROC among attention-spectral baselines, improving over LapEig by $+6.5$ AUROC points and over GoR-4 by $+2.4$ points on average, while requiring no update to the underlying LLM. In the fully unsupervised setting, an RMT-deviation score achieves mean AUROC $0.71$, providing a label-free but weaker detector. A complementary RMT analysis shows that correct generations exhibit more Wigner-Dyson like spectral statistics, whereas hallucinations exhibit more Poisson-like statistics. The anonymized code and config are provided in the supplementary material.

</details>


### [121] [FlexLAM: Resolving the Bottleneck Trade-off in Latent Action Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19408)
*Takanori Yoshimoto,Yang Hu,Naruya Kondo,Tatsuya Matsushima*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlexLAM replaces fixed-capacity bottlenecks in latent-action models with variable-length latent actions learned via nested dropout, achieving better or equal performance across token budgets, enabling inference-time budget adjustment without retraining, and improving Ego4D transition reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard latent-action models are constrained by a fixed-capacity bottleneck that either discards transition cues (too tight) or preserves excess variation requiring alignment labels to resolve (too loose). A variable-length interface aims to capture core transition structure first and add detail as needed, improving efficiency and robustness under label scarcity.

Method: Train latent actions with nested dropout to produce prefix-valid, variable-length codes without introducing new architectures or loss terms. Evaluate under scarce-label supervision and a low-return single-task alignment stress test, comparing to fixed-capacity LAMs. Assess inference-time token-budget adjustment without retraining and Ego4D transition reconstruction.

Result: A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every token budget under standard scarce-label supervision and a low-return alignment stress test. It supports inference-time budget adjustment without retraining and improves Ego4D transition reconstruction.

Conclusion: Variable-length latent actions offer an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to fixed-capacity bottlenecks in latent-action models and related video-pretrained interfaces, enabling better alignment with limited labels and flexible inference budgets.

Abstract: Latent actions provide a compact interface between action-free video and downstream decision-making, yet existing Latent Action Models (LAMs) force every transition through a fixed-capacity bottleneck. We identify a bottleneck trade-off: overly tight codes can discard transition cues needed for action alignment, while overly loose codes preserve additional transition variation that must be resolved when alignment labels are scarce or narrowly distributed. FlexLAM replaces this fixed capacity with variable-length latent actions trained by nested dropout, yielding prefix-valid codes that capture compact transition structure first and add detail only when needed, without new architectures or losses. A single FlexLAM matches or surpasses separately trained fixed-capacity LAMs at every evaluated token budget under standard scarce-label supervision and under a low-return single-task alignment stress test, indicating that FlexLAM is not merely adjustable at inference time but learns a better latent-action interface at the same token budgets. The same model supports inference-time token-budget adjustment without retraining, and FlexLAM improves Ego4D transition reconstruction. These results suggest that variable-length latent actions are an architecture-free, drop-in upgrade to the fixed-capacity bottleneck in latent action models, latent-action world models, and video-pretrained action interfaces.

</details>


### [122] [Spectral DPPs via NEPv: A Scalable Continuous Relaxation of Determinantal MAP for Diversity-Aware Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19411)
*Richard Yi Da Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reformulate DPP-MAP as a Stiefel-manifold optimization yielding a nonlinear eigenproblem with eigenvector dependence (NEPv). Introduces a self-consistent field (SCF) iteration, OurMethod, achieving near-linear-time complexity via kernel mat-vecs and a limited iteration count; focuses on relaxation and scaling with empirical benchmarking deferred.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need scalable, principled diversity selection (data curation, coresets, active learning, retrieval diversification) in massive candidate pools where DPP-MAP is NP-hard and standard methods scale poorly.

Method: Cast DPP-MAP as an optimization over the Stiefel manifold. Derive a nonlinear eigenvalue problem with eigenvector dependency (NEPv) whose fixed point corresponds to a KKT condition for the MAP objective. Solve via a self-consistent field (SCF) iteration that updates the operator using the current eigenvector. Analyze contraction via a spectral-gap-based argument. Complexity is O((n d k + n k^2) t) with t small, relying on matrix-vector products with the kernel and compatibility with low-rank/feature-map kernels.

Result: Provides a principled iterative solver with convergence guarantees for the NEPv and a scalable path to DPP-MAP optimization. Demonstrates near-linear scaling in n and integration with common kernel/kernelized representations. The paper centers on relaxation, solver, and scaling; full empirical benchmarking is deferred to a planned study.

Conclusion: Presents a novel, scalable solver for DPP-MAP through NEPv and SCF, enabling data-centric KA tasks; empirical validation on real data is planned but not included in this work.

Abstract: Selecting a small, diverse, high-quality subset from a massive pool of candidates is a recurring primitive in modern machine learning -- data curation and coreset selection for training and fine-tuning large models, active-learning batch acquisition, prompt and exemplar selection for in-context learning, retrieval diversification, and experimental design. Determinantal Point Processes (\DPP s) give a principled, well-calibrated notion of diversity for this task, but their \emph{MAP} objective -- pick a size-$k$ subset $S$ maximizing $\logdet(L_S)$ -- is NP-hard, and the standard greedy and sampling algorithms scale superlinearly in the ground-set size $n$. This cost is prohibitive precisely in the data-centric regime where diversity matters most, where $n$ ranges over millions to billions of candidate examples, features, or embeddings. We recast \DPP-MAP as a continuous optimization problem over the Stiefel manifold, and show that its first-order optimality conditions form a \emph{Nonlinear Eigenvalue Problem with eigenvector dependency} (\NEPv) of a previously unstudied form. This \NEPv\ admits a self-consistent field (\SCF) iteration with a spectral-gap-based local contraction guarantee, giving a principled iterative solver where the diversity objective drives an eigenvector-dependent operator. The resulting algorithm, \OurMethod, requires only matrix-vector products with the kernel and runs in time $O\!\big((ndk+nk^2)\,t\big)$ for a small number of iterations $t$, scaling near-linearly in $n$ and integrating directly with low-rank and feature-map kernels common in ML. This paper focuses on the relaxation, solver, and scaling analysis; full real-data benchmarking is left to a planned empirical study.

</details>


### [123] [Spectral Retrieval-Augmented Time-Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19412)
*Huu Hiep Nguyen,Minh Hoang Nguyen,Dung Nguyen,Hung Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Retrieval-augmented forecasting improved by spectral-aware, frequency-domain representations with recency-weighted retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary time series exhibit complex patterns that are hard to memorize; traditional time-domain retrieval ignores spectral structure and treats all history equally.

Method: Transform time series into windowed frequency representations; measure similarity via a joint amplitude- and phase-aware metric; apply exponential moving average weighting to prioritize recent windows during retrieval and forecasting.

Result: Empirical results on benchmark datasets show SpecReTF outperforms time-domain retrieval baselines, delivering higher forecasting accuracy on diverse non-stationary series.

Conclusion: SpecReTF addresses spectral blindness and recency limitations in retrieval-based forecasting by leveraging frequency-domain cues and recency-aware weighting, yielding improved predictive performance.

Abstract: Time series forecasting leverages historical patterns to predict future values, but traditional methods face challenges when dealing with complex, non-stationary patterns that are difficult to memorize during training. Retrieval-augmented approaches have emerged as promising solutions by retrieving similar historical patterns to enhance predictions.
