However, existing retrieval methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: spectral blindness, which overlooks critical frequency-domain characteristics that capture underlying periodic structures, and temporal recency, which treats all historical data equally without emphasizing recent, more relevant patterns. In this paper, we propose SpecReTF, a novel retrieval method that addresses these issues by converting time series into windowed frequency representations, measuring similarity with a combined metric that captures both amplitude and phase information. To balance recency and historical context, we apply an exponential moving average weighting scheme that emphasizes recent windows. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that SpecReTF outperforms time-domain retrieval methods, achieving superior forecasting accuracy across diverse, non-stationary time series.

</details>


### [124] [Does Text Actually Help? Uncovering and Resolving Text Collapse in Multimodal Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19413)
*Huu Hiep Nguyen,Minh Hoang Nguyen,Dung Nguyen,Hung Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Text collapse in multimodal time-series forecasting is mitigated by REST-TS, which orthogonally supervises the text branch on the residual of a numeric forecast, yielding state-of-the-art results and better text-utilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a strong need to incorporate domain-relevant textual reports into forecasting, but existing multimodal models rely too heavily on the numerically autocorrelated input, causing the text pathway to under-contribute. Addressing this asymmetry can unlock the value of textual information.

Method: A numeric backbone first produces an independent forecast. The text branch is exclusively supervised to predict the structured components of the residual (the prediction gap not explained by numbers). This residual-exclusive supervision prevents the text pathway from mimicking the numeric signal and forces it to extract genuine content from the input description.

Result: REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, and shows consistently greater utilization of the text branch than prior methods, indicating successful extraction of meaningful textual content.

Conclusion: REST-TS effectively mitigates text collapse by reorienting supervision to the residual, turning the asymmetry into a design principle and enabling the text branch to contribute substantive, domain-relevant information to forecasts.

Abstract: Multimodal time series forecasting, which pairs numerical sequences with domain-relevant textual reports, promises to inject world knowledge into forecasting pipelines. However, we uncover a critical failure mode in existing frameworks that we term text collapse: the text branch converges to a content-independent transformation, contributing negligible discriminative signal regardless of the input description. We argue that text collapse is a consequence of a fundamental asymmetry in time series forecasting: the numerical input is strongly autocorrelated with the output, making the numerical backbone inherently dominant, while the text branch, despite carrying complementary and often critical information, is insufficiently utilized, leading to its systematic underexploitation. To address this, we propose \textbf{REST-TS} (\textbf{R}esidual-\textbf{E}xclusive \textbf{S}upervision for \textbf{T}ext in \textbf{T}ime \textbf{S}eries), which turns the asymmetry into a design principle: the numerical backbone produces its own independent numerical forecast, and the text branch is exclusively supervised to predict the structured components of the residual, the prediction gap that numbers cannot explain. Because no numerical pathway can reduce these losses, the text branch must extract genuine content from the input description. Evaluated across diverse real-world domains and backbone architectures, REST-TS achieves state-of-the-art performance and consistently demonstrates greater text-branch utilization than existing frameworks, providing strong empirical evidence that supervising the text branch on the residual compels it to extract genuine content from the input.

</details>


### [125] [MortarBench: Evaluating Mortgage Loan Origination Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19416)
*Matthew Toles,Yunan Lu,Manav Munjal,Bojun Liu,Yuanhao Deng,Stephanie Selig,Derek Rindner,Cheng Li,Zhou Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Synthetic loan origination benchmark shows LLM underperformance and non-English name bias; CRIT improves accuracy and reduces bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create public, edge-case-rich benchmarks for loan origination and to address observed weaknesses in current LLMs.

Method: Use a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate realistic, edge-case-rich loan origination tasks; evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs (including closed-source models); analyze biases (notably non-English name perception); propose CRIT for confidence calibration.

Result: LLMs achieve up to 77.1% exact-match accuracy; CRIT increases accuracy to 80.5%; biases related to non-English names detected and reduced; CRIT also improves risk-management steering.

Conclusion: MortarBench reveals key limitations of current LLMs in loan origination tasks and demonstrates that CRIT provides effective confidence calibration to boost accuracy and reduce bias in risk assessment.

Abstract: Loan origination is the process by which a lender creates a new loan, from application and underwriting through approval and funding. This process serves a critical role in evaluating the eligibility and level of risk posed by an applicant. Recently, firms have begun using mortgage loan agents to augment human loan officers, despite a lack of any public benchmark. To fill this gap, we present MortarBench, a loan origination agent benchmark. MortarBench uses a financial data synthesis and mutation pipeline to generate examples with broad edge case coverage that match real-world distributions and questions. We find that state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs) perform poorly, with closed-source models achieving at most 77.1\% exact match accuracy. We also discover systematic biases in LLM perception of foreignness related to non-English names. Noting these weaknesses, we introduce CRIT, a confidence calibration framework. Our method increases accuracy to 80.5\% while improving risk management steering and reducing bias.

</details>


### [126] [3D-DLP: Self-Supervised 3D Object-Centric Scene Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19451)
*Ellina Zhang,Madhaven Iyengar,Amir Zadeh,Chuan Li,Deepak Pathak,David Held,Tal Daniel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 3D-DLP introduces self-supervised, object-centric 3D scene representations by decomposing RGB-D/voxel inputs into a fixed set of latent particles that encode 3D position, size, and appearance, enabling interpretable segmentation and controllable scene generation; improves robotic manipulation over baselines relying on dense 3D inputs; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for compact, interpretable 3D scene representations that factorize a scene into discrete objects to improve manipulation, generalization, and data efficiency, addressing the limitations of dense, unstructured 3D inputs.

Method: Extend the Deep Latent Particles framework to 3D by learning a fixed set of particles from RGB-D or voxel data. Each particle encodes disentangled attributes (3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, appearance features) and yields per-particle segmentation maps via an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective. The approach produces a scene-centric, object-level representation that supports manipulation and scene editing.

Result: Experiments on simulated and real datasets show the latent space is interpretable and controllable: moving particle positions and decoding yields novel configurations. Downstream robotic manipulation using these 3D particles outperforms baselines that lack explicit 3D object-centric structure or rely on dense, memory-intensive inputs.

Conclusion: 3D latent particles offer compact, interpretable 3D scene representations that facilitate controllable generation and improved robotic manipulation, representing a scalable object-centric approach for 3D understanding. Code and videos are available at the provided URL.

Abstract: We introduce 3D-DLP, a self-supervised object-centric representation learning model that decomposes scene-level RGB-D or voxel observations into a set of 3D latent particles. Building on the Deep Latent Particles (DLP) framework, each particle encodes disentangled attributes, including 3D keypoint position, bounding box dimensions, and appearance features, and represents a distinct entity in the scene. The model learns interpretable per-particle segmentation maps through an end-to-end self-supervised reconstruction objective. We demonstrate on both simulated and real-world datasets that the learned latent space is interpretable and controllable: by manipulating particle positions and decoding, we can generate novel scene configurations. Furthermore, we show that leveraging these compact 3D latent particles for downstream robotic manipulation improves performance over baselines that either lack explicit 3D information or rely on memory-intensive dense 3D inputs without object-centric structure. Code and videos are available at https://eubooks3003.github.io/3d-dlp.

</details>


### [127] [Can In-Context Learning Support Intrinsic Curiosity?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19476)
*Eric Elmoznino,Sangnie Bhardwaj,Johannes von Oswald,Rajai Nasser,Blaise Agüera y Arcas,João Sacramento,Rif A. Saurous,Guillaume Lajoie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICL can serve as an update-free surrogate for intrinsic rewards in learning-progress based data collection, but only in a broad subclass of non-temporal tasks; in general MDPs, unbiased estimation fails due to bias or infeasibility, while experiments in continuous/symbolic domains validate the theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational bottleneck of evaluating learning-progress rewards for data collection by leveraging in-context learning as an update-free world model.

Method: Theoretical analysis proving impossibility in general MDPs and a positive result for non-temporal settings (e.g., active learning, Bayesian experimental design); formulation of ICL-derived rewards based on prediction errors and counterfactual contexts; empirical validation across continuous and symbolic environments.

Result: Impossibility result: unbiased intrinsic rewards based on ICL predictions are biased or not implementable in general MDPs. Positive result: for a broad non-temporal subclass, ICL-derived rewards bound and converge to true learning progress. Experiments show data-collection policies driven by ICL rewards achieve near-optimal exploration.

Conclusion: ICL can substitute for update-based world models to drive intrinsic curiosity, but its applicability is limited to non-temporal settings; in general MDPs, care must be taken due to bias, while targeted domains like active learning and Bayesian design provide a viable path.

Abstract: Effective machine learning depends not only on how we model data, but also on what data we choose to collect. While large sequence models have revolutionized data modeling, the problem of automated data selection, or "intrinsic curiosity", remains a significant challenge. Classic approaches incentivize exploration by rewarding an agent based on its "learning progress", which measures how much a newly acquired observation improves a world model's predictive ability. However, evaluating these rewards traditionally requires expensive inner loops of gradient descent updates within each trajectory, rendering them computationally impractical at scale. In this work, we investigate whether the emergent in-context learning (ICL) capabilities of sequence models can eliminate this bottleneck by serving as immediate, update-free world models. Specifically, we evaluate whether an exploration policy can be trained to maximize learning progress, using solely the prediction errors and counterfactual context manipulations of an in-context learner. We first prove that in general Markov decision processes, this is in fact impossible in an unbiased way: the resulting intrinsic rewards either suffer from nuisance terms that bias their estimation of true learning progress, or they cannot be implemented using an in-context learner's prediction errors. Conversely, we prove a positive result for a broad subclass of non-temporal settings, encompassing active learning and Bayesian Experimental Design: here, ICL-derived rewards successfully bound and asymptotically converge to the true learning progress. We corroborate our theory with controlled experiments across continuous and symbolic environments, demonstrating that our ICL-driven framework successfully trains curious data-collection policies that explore optimally.

</details>


### [128] [Insulin4RL: Real-Time Insulin Management in the Intensive Care Unit for Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19481)
*Thomas Frost,Steve Harris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Insulin4RL, a healthcare offline RL dataset with real, irregular inputs/actions for insulin titration from MIMIC-IV, enabling evaluation under realistic sampling. Includes 375k decisions from 12,209 ICU patients; baseline offline RL results and a standardized FQE evaluation protocol; outlines future research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the limitations of temporally discretised EHR data in offline RL for clinical decision-making, which can create artificial scenarios and hinder generalisability of retrospective evaluations.

Method: Curates Insulin4RL from MIMIC-IV with irregular, naturally sampled trajectories for insulin infusion decisions; provides dataset description, baseline model-free offline RL results, and a standardized evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation (FQE).

Result: A large, real-world ORL dataset with irregular sampling, specific to insulin titration in ICU; baseline benchmarks for offline RL; a clear FQE-based evaluation protocol.

Conclusion: The dataset enables more realistic evaluation of ORL methods in clinical settings and highlights directions for future research, including broader method studies and generalisation to irregular-time decision problems.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning (ORL) offers the potential to improve the quality of clinical decision-making using historical electronic health record (EHR) data. Current training and evaluative practices in this field rely heavily on EHR datasets that have been temporally discretised into fixed, regular time intervals. Discretisation creates fictional representations of complex clinical scenarios and compromises the generalisability of retrospective model evaluations. In this paper, we introduce Insulin4RL, a healthcare ORL dataset featuring naturally irregular inputs and actions from real clinical trajectories. Derived from MIMIC-IV, Insulin4RL comprises over 375,000 labelled decisions across 12,209 patients requiring insulin infusion titration in the Intensive Care Unit. The dataset can thus be used for research into ORL model performance under realistic clinical sampling assumptions. We provide a description of the dataset's structure and characteristics, baseline performance metrics using model-free offline reinforcement learning, and a standardised evaluation protocol using fitted Q-evaluation. We conclude with suggested areas for future research that could be addressed using this resource.

</details>


### [129] [Concept Flow Models: Anchoring Concept-Based Reasoning with Hierarchical Bottlenecks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19489)
*Ya Wang,Adrian Paschke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Concept Flow Models (CFMs) replace flat concept bottlenecks with a hierarchical decision tree to improve interpretability and reduce information leakage, while maintaining predictive performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBMs provide interpretability but suffer from information leakage when concept count nears embedding dimension; spurious correlations undermine trust. A hierarchical, localized concept flow aims to constrain concept usage and enable auditable reasoning.

Method: Construct a hierarchical decision tree from visual embeddings, assign discriminative concepts to levels, and train differentiable concept weights via probabilistic tree traversal. Internal nodes focus on localized concept subsets, progressively narrowing the prediction to a hierarchical class structure.

Result: CFMs achieve predictive performance comparable to flat CBMs while substantially reducing information leakage by limiting effective concept usage. They produce stepwise, transparent decision flows suitable for auditing within hierarchical class frameworks.

Conclusion: Hierarchical concept flow improves interpretability and robustness without sacrificing accuracy, enabling auditable, stepwise reasoning. Potential challenges include tree construction, embedding quality, and training stability across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) enhance interpretability by projecting learned features into a human-understandable concept space. Recent approaches leverage vision-language models to generate concept embeddings, reducing the need for manual concept annotations. However, these models suffer from a critical limitation: as the number of concepts approaches the embedding dimension, information leakage increases, enabling the model to exploit spurious or semantically irrelevant correlations and undermining interpretability. In this work, we propose Concept Flow Models (CFMs), which replace the flat bottleneck with a hierarchical, concept-driven decision tree. Each internal node in the hierarchy focuses on a localized subset of discriminative concepts, progressively narrowing the prediction scope. Our framework constructs decision hierarchies from visual embeddings, distributes semantic concepts at each hierarchy level, and trains differentiable concept weights through probabilistic tree traversal. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that CFMs match the predictive performance of flat CBMs, while substantially mitigating information leakage by reducing effective concept usage. Furthermore, CFMs yield stepwise decision flows that enable transparent and auditable model reasoning with hierarchical class structures.

</details>


### [130] [Algebraic Dead Directions in LayerNorm Transformers: A Forward-Pass-Only Diagnostic at LLM Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19491)
*Tejas Pradeep Shirodkar,P. J. Narayanan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A cheap, algebraic dead-direction exists in LayerNorm transformers: the inverse LayerNorm scale defines an exact kernel of the post-final-norm activation covariance, computable from parameters alone, enabling a direction that aligns with the smallest Fisher-information direction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify degeneracies in pretrained transformers near singular minima without expensive computations, by deriving a parameter-based dead direction specific to LayerNorm.

Method: Derive γ^{-1}/||γ^{-1}|| as an exact algebraic kernel of the covariance after final normalization; test across 14 pretrained models (9 LayerNorm, 5 RMSNorm; 160M–35B; language and vision). Compare with the bottom singular direction via one forward pass and SVD; assess random initialization vs trained checkpoints; inspect residual-stream singular values and normalization classification from parameters.

Result: For random initialization, the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction to four decimals in 9/9 LayerNorm models; no such direction in 5/5 RMSNorm models due to missing mean-subtraction projector. At trained checkpoints, covariance eigenvalue along this direction grows by ~10^3× and more dead directions appear; one-forward-pass readout captures singular structure from the predicted coordinate. The smallest residual-stream singular value is preserved block-to-block in 13/14 transformers on their own inputs (Gemma4-31B is the exception, with a genuine dead direction read by the same method). The kernel direction’s presence lets one classify a transformer’s normalization from the parameters alone.

Conclusion: A cheap, parameter-based dead-direction readout exists for LayerNorm transformers, revealing a meaningful degeneracy tied to the mean-subtraction projector and enabling a classification of normalization type. This reveals robust, cross-model singular-structure behavior and provides a diagnostic tool for understanding Fisher-information degeneracies in LN-based architectures.

Abstract: Pretrained transformers sit near singular minima of the loss, where the Fisher information metric degenerates along dead directions: directions in parameter space along which the directional Fisher vanishes. Locating such a direction normally needs a forward pass and an eigendecomposition of activations, or a sampling-based complexity estimate; none returns a direction computable from the network's parameters alone. We give one, for LayerNorm transformers. The inverse-scale direction $γ^{-1}/\|γ^{-1}\|$ of the LayerNorm affine is an exact algebraic kernel of the post-final-norm centred activation covariance, for any input distribution, and induces a corresponding dead direction in parameter space. It is read from the LN scale parameter alone, with no forward or backward pass and no eigensolve: the cheapest dead-direction read, specific to LayerNorm. We test it on $14$ pretrained transformers ($9$ LayerNorm, $5$ RMSNorm; $160$M-$35$B; language and vision objectives). At random initialisation the predicted direction matches the measured bottom singular direction (one forward pass, direct SVD) to four decimal places on $9/9$ LayerNorm models, and is correctly absent on $5/5$ RMSNorm models, which lack the mean-subtraction projector that creates it. On the trained checkpoint the covariance eigenvalue along this direction deepens by ${\sim}10^3\times$ and further dead directions open; the random-init-to-trained gap is a one-forward-pass, per-checkpoint readout of singular structure along the predicted coordinate. Two consequences follow in closed form: the residual stream's smallest singular value is preserved block-to-block on $13/14$ transformers measured on their own input distribution, the one exception (Gemma$4$-$31$B) a genuine dead direction the same read pinpoints; and the kernel direction's presence classifies a transformer's normalisation from the parameters alone.

</details>


### [131] [Calibrating Generative Models to Feature Distributions with MMD Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19496)
*Nathaniel L. Diamant,Brian L. Trippe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: kCGM calibrates generative models to target feature distributions using MMD with an unbiased score-function estimator and KL regularization, improving alignment with target classes (e.g., antibiotics) while preserving validity; applicable to autoregressive, diffusion, and discrete models across proteins/DNA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models trained on broad spaces may produce samples that are plausible but misaligned with a target distribution of key features. Direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and reduce validity; a method is needed to steer distributions without sacrificing other properties.

Method: Propose kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM): minimize a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to stay close to the pretrained model. Applicable to different model classes (autoregressive, continuous/diffusion, discrete diffusion) with feature-level supervision.

Result: On a 174-antibiotic target set, direct finetuning degrades chemical validity while matching features poorly; kCGM achieves better feature matching and higher validity. Demonstrations extend to protein and DNA generation, showing adaptability across model types using only feature-level supervision.

Conclusion: kCGM provides a principled, flexible calibration framework for generative models that aligns feature distributions to target sets while preserving validity and staying close to the pretrained model; demonstrated across molecules, proteins, and DNA with multiple generative architectures.

Abstract: Generative models can produce individually plausible samples while deviating substantially from a target set in the distribution of key features. For example, a model pretrained on broad drug-like chemical space may generate molecules whose molecular features differ from those of a therapeutic class of interest, such as known antibiotics. Correcting such distributional miscalibration is challenging: direct finetuning on the target set can overfit and does not control which features are matched. To fill this gap, we introduce kernel Calibrating Generative Models (kCGM). kCGM minimizes a maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between generated and target feature distributions using an unbiased score-function estimator, with KL regularization to remain close to the pretrained model. On a target set of 174 antibiotics, direct finetuning sacrifices chemical validity for feature-distribution matching, whereas kCGM improves target feature matching while increasing validity. We further demonstrate kCGM in protein and DNA generation tasks, showing it can adapt autoregressive, continuous-space diffusion, and discrete diffusion models using only feature-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/smithhenryd/cgm.

</details>


### [132] [Interactive Pareto navigation for deep multi-task learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19521)
*Augustina C. Amakor,Konstantin Sonntag,Sebastian Peitz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A predictor-corrector framework for multi-task learning that integrates user preferences and the geometry of the Pareto set, using a Krylov subspace method to compute tangents without Hessians, enabling efficient interactive exploration demonstrated on toy problems and deep learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Managing many objectives in multi-task learning is computationally demanding; weighted-sum approaches can misrepresent the decision maker's preferences and the Pareto geometry, and exhaustive exploration is expensive in deep learning. An efficient, preference-aware method is needed.

Method: PPE uses predictor steps tangent to the Pareto-optimal manifold guided by the decision maker's preferences, followed by a corrector step to yield a new trade-off. Tangent-space computation avoids explicit Hessians by employing a Krylov subspace method based on matrix-vector products accessible via automatic differentiation, ensuring efficiency; the process supports interactive exploration in toy and deep learning settings.

Result: The approach is demonstrated on toy problems and deep learning examples, showing that PPE can efficiently and robustly incorporate preferences and navigate the Pareto front during optimization.

Conclusion: PPE provides an efficient, interactive framework for preference-aware Pareto exploration in multi-task learning, leveraging tangent-space Krylov methods to avoid second-order computations and enabling robust navigation of the Pareto front.

Abstract: In multi-task learning, handling an increasing number of objectives can quickly become challenging, both in terms of the computational resources and the decision maker's capacity to choose appropriate trade-offs. A widely used approach is thus to aggregate the individual losses in a single loss function by a weighted sum. This often fails to capture either the decision maker's preferences as a result of the shape of the Pareto front, or requires multiple adjustments and computations which becomes prohibitively expensive in deep learning applications. To address these issues, we introduce a novel framework, Preference Pareto Exploration (PPE), which enforces the decision maker's preferences while accounting for the geometry of the Pareto set in an interactive exploration process. PPE is based on a predictor-corrector method that performs predictor steps tangential to the manifold of Pareto-optimal solutions, following the decision maker's preference. The subsequent corrector step results in a new trade-off reflecting this preference. To avoid explicit Hessian computations when characterizing the tangent space of the manifold, we employ a Krylov subspace method that relies solely on matrix-vector products. These products can be efficiently obtained via automatic differentiation, ensuring both efficiency and robustness throughout the optimization process. The method's functionality and performance are demonstrated using both toy problems and examples from deep learning.

</details>


### [133] [Techniques for Peak Memory Reduction for LoRA Fine-tuning of LLMs on Edge Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19528)
*Hassan Dbouk,Matthias Reisser,Prathamesh Mandke,Likhita Arun Navali,Christos Louizos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory-efficient LoRA fine-tuning for LLMs on user data using quantization, selective activation caching with disk offloading, softmax subset approximation, and logits masking; achieves up to 26–28x peak memory reduction on 3B models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Allow personalized, privacy-preserving fine-tuning on consumer hardware by dramatically lowering peak memory during LoRA-based fine-tuning without sacrificing model performance.

Method: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing via selective activation caching plus disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, (4) logits masking.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B showing up to 26x and 28x peak memory reduction, enabling on-device fine-tuning on resource-constrained hardware.

Conclusion: A complementary set of memory-saving techniques can render LoRA fine-tuning feasible on consumer devices without compromising model quality, broadening privacy-preserving personalization options.

Abstract: Fine-tuning of Large Language Models (LLMs) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on an end-user's data offers personalized experiences while keeping data private, but faces severe memory constraints on consumer hardware. Peak memory during fine-tuning often exceeds device limits, especially for models with billions of parameters and long-context training data. This paper introduces a suite of complementary techniques to reduce memory footprint without sacrificing model quality: (1) base model quantization with on-the-fly dequantization, (2) memory-efficient checkpointing combining selective activation caching and disk offloading, (3) softmax approximation using semantically relevant token subsets, and (4) logits masking. Experiments on Llama-3.2 3B and Qwen-2.5 3B demonstrate up to $26\times$ and $28\times$ reduction in peak memory, enabling fine-tuning on resource-constrained devices.

</details>


### [134] [Tracking Representation Dynamics in Large Language Models with Persistent Homology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19542)
*Naman Malhotra,Jay Ambadkar,Abhinav Gupta,Kushal Kasivel,Abbas Schwarz,Kamillo Ferry,Anthea Monod*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Using persistent homology to monitor how internal representations evolve during supervised alignment of LLMs; most topological changes occur early, with a transient peak and rapid stabilization; alignment objectives imprint distinct topological trajectories; instruction-tuned vs pretrained models show different evolution patterns; offers a representation-level, topology-based complement to behavioral metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how internal activation-space topology evolves during alignment fine-tuning, and whether topology can reveal dynamics not captured by downstream metrics.

Method: Track activation-space topology with persistent homology across fine-tuning checkpoints for four transformer LMs (1B–7B params) under three alignment objectives (helpful, harmless, mixed). Analyze dense checkpoint sequences to identify transient peak in topological activity, stabilization, and objective- or model-specific trajectories; compare instruction-tuned and pretrained models.

Result: Most topological reorganization happens in the early training phase. A transient peak in topological activity appears, followed by rapid stabilization. Different alignment objectives produce distinguishable topological trajectories; instruction-tuned and pretrained models exhibit qualitatively different evolution patterns. Persistent homology provides a representation-level perspective that complements traditional behavioral metrics.

Conclusion: Persistent homology reveals dynamic, representation-level changes during alignment that are not evident from behavior alone, offering a complementary tool for analyzing how fine-tuning shapes neural representations in LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models are commonly aligned through supervised fine-tuning, yet little is known about how their internal representations evolve during this process. We study alignment dynamics using persistent homology by tracking the topology of activation spaces throughout fine-tuning. Across four transformer language models ranging from 1B to 7B parameters and three alignment objectives corresponding to helpful, harmless, and mixed training data, we find that the majority of topological reorganization occurs during the earliest stages of training. A dense checkpoint analysis reveals a transient peak in topological activity followed by rapid stabilization. We further show that different alignment objectives induce distinguishable topological trajectories, while instruction-tuned and pretrained models exhibit qualitatively different patterns of evolution. Our results suggest that persistent homology provides a complementary perspective on alignment, revealing representation-level changes that are not apparent from behavioral metrics alone.

</details>


### [135] [Predicting Mergeability of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19549)
*Lin Tang,Wei Zhang,Jing Li,Hongyu Chen,Ming Zhao,Yuxuan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces MergeProbe, a lightweight predictor that forecasts whether two LoRA adapters will retain their utility after merging, using early training signals; enables proactive decisions (merge/reweight/prune/route) and achieves strong retention on MERGE-PEFT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adapter merging can cause destructive interference; post-hoc evaluation is costly and late feedback hinders efficient model adaptation; forecasting mergeability aims to reduce wasted effort and improve deployment reliability.

Method: Formalize mergeability as the degree of retention after merging; extract early signals (first few percent of training) focusing on (i) alignment of low-rank updates and gradients across tasks and (ii) disturbance to shared representations; package these signals into MergeProbe to predict pairwise and set-level retention and to guide decisions (merge directly, reweight, prune, or route).

Result: On MERGE-PEFT, a five-domain benchmark (math, code, science, instruction following, safety), MergeProbe achieves the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware merge baselines, with substantially lower deployment overhead than full task routing.

Conclusion: Mergeable LoRA adapters can be anticipated rather than discovered post-training; MergeProbe reframes adapter merging as an anticipatory measurement problem and enables efficient, domain-spanning decision-making for merging.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) makes it cheap to train many domain- and task-specific language model adapters, but whether two adapters can be merged is usually discovered only after both have been fully trained and evaluated. This late feedback is costly: adapters that are strong in isolation can interfere destructively once their updates are combined. We ask whether this outcome can be anticipated. We formalize adapter mergeability as the degree to which an adapter preserves its single-task utility after merging, and show that it can be forecast from signals measured in the first few percent of training -- chiefly how the low-rank updates and their gradients align across tasks and how much they disturb shared representations. We package these signals into MergeProbe, a lightweight predictor that estimates pairwise and set-level retention and turns the estimate into a concrete decision: merge directly, reweight, prune, or route. On MERGE-PEFT, a five-domain benchmark spanning math, code, science, instruction following, and safety, MergeProbe attains the best average and worst-case retention among strong interference-aware merge baselines while adding far less deployment overhead than full task routing. This turns LoRA merging from a post-hoc engineering step into an anticipatory measurement problem.

</details>


### [136] [Displacement Is Not Direction: Evaluating Fidelity Metrics for Quantized LLM Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19558)
*Miloš Nikolić,Ali Hadi Zadeh,Enrique Torres Sanchez,Andreas Moshovos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KL divergence against a high-precision reference correlates with benchmark quality across broad model cohorts but loses predictive power in near-baseline silent zones; it mainly measures the volume of disagreement, not its direction, limiting its reliability as a universal proxy for quality. Multivariant robustness exists but fails for per-prompt failure prediction and cross-model disagreement routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether per-token KL divergence (KLD) is a reliable, low-cost proxy for benchmark quality across large model cohorts and diverse tasks, and understand when and why this proxy fails.

Method: Evaluate two model cohorts (Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and Devstral-Small-2-24B) on a suite of downstream benchmarks. Compute per-token KLD against a high-precision reference; examine correlations with benchmark scores across cohorts. Test 14 measurement variants (different KLD aggregations, perplexity formulations, top-1 agreement, calibration corpora, and context lengths). Assess at per-prompt level: code failure-prediction, and cross-model disagreement routing accuracy. Analyze structural decomposition to separate volume of disagreement from direction, and report performance across calibration and context variants.

Result: Across full cohorts, KLD correlates with benchmark scores (ρ = -0.72 for Qwen, ρ = -0.86 for Devstral; p<0.001). In the near-baseline silent zone, correlation collapses (Qwen ρ = +0.00; Devstral ρ = -0.24, p=0.36). The collapse persists across 14 variants. Per-prompt KLD yields weak failure-prediction for code (GM ratios 1.08–1.22) and 42.3–49.4% cross-model disagreement routing accuracy. The collapse is attributable to KLD primarily measuring the volume of disagreement; silent-zone composite ρ = +0.94 (p<0.001) on Qwen and +0.55 (p=0.03) on Devstral, while the direction of disagreements is weak and task-dependent.

Conclusion: KLD is not a robust universal proxy for benchmark quality. It tracks disagreement volume rather than disagreement direction or task-specific signals, and its predictive utility fails in near-baseline regimes. Although robust to multiple variants, reliance on KLD alone is inappropriate for benchmarking; a multi-metric approach that captures directionality and task-conditioned signals is recommended.

Abstract: Fidelity metrics, such as per-token KL divergence (KLD) against a high-precision reference, are often used in practice as low-cost proxies for benchmark quality. We test this practice on a 28-quant cohort of Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and a 41-quant cohort of Devstral-Small-2-24B, evaluated across a suite of downstream benchmarks. We find that KLD is strongly correlated with benchmark score over the full cohort ($ρ=-0.72$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.86$ on Devstral, both with $p<0.001$). However, this relationship collapses to non-significance in the near-baseline silent zone ($ρ=+0.00$ on Qwen and $ρ=-0.24$, $p=0.36$, on Devstral). This collapse persists across 14 measurement variants, including different KLD aggregations, perplexity formulations, top-1 agreement, calibration corpora, and context lengths. At the per-prompt level, KLD has only weak failure-prediction power on code, with failed-vs-passed geometric-mean ratios in $[1.08,1.22]$ across five models on LiveCodeBench, and fails as a cross-model router, achieving only $42.3\%-49.4\%$ accuracy on disagreement prompts. We trace the collapse to a structural decomposition: KLD primarily measures the volume of disagreement with the reference, with silent-zone composite $ρ=+0.94$ ($p<0.001$) on Qwen and $+0.55$ ($p=0.03$) on Devstral, while its relationship to the direction of those disagreements is weak and task-conditional.

</details>


### [137] [Understanding Key Features of Time Series Foundation Models from Epidemic Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19560)
*Alireza Jafari,Judy Fox,Geoffrey C. Fox,Madhav Marathe,Aniruddha Adiga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A mixture-of-experts ensemble of pretrained forecasters yields the strongest influenza forecasting across 1-4 weeks; numerical transformer models are reliable; pretraining helps mainly at longer horizons and when domain-aligned; LLM-based time-series methods underperform; hospitalization data can improve robustness in certain settings; provides guidance on model selection and auxiliary signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically compare modern forecasting architectures (classical neural nets, numerical transformers, pretrained time-series foundations, and LLM-based forecasting) for regional influenza forecasting, with attention to temporal and spatial generalization and multi-horizon accuracy.

Method: Evaluate 1-4-week-ahead forecasts using influenza-like illness surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under temporal and spatial generalization settings. Compare diverse architectures: classical neural networks, transformer-based models, pretrained time-series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting. Also assess the impact of hospitalization signals as covariates and as pretraining data.

Result: Mixture-of-experts ensemble of pretrained forecasters achieved the strongest overall performance. Numerical transformer-based models provided reliable forecasts. Pretraining yielded the largest gains at longer horizons, especially when aligned with influenza dynamics. LLM-based time-series methods underperformed relative to numerical forecasters. Hospitalization information offered complementary improvements in selected settings and helped robustness when combined with other signals.

Conclusion: For influenza preparedness, prioritize heterogeneous pretrained representations and their fusion; rely on numerical transformers and ensure pretraining domain alignment with influenza dynamics for longer horizons; incorporate hospitalization and auxiliary signals to bolster robustness in multi-horizon forecasting; LLM-based approaches currently underperform for this setting.

Abstract: Seasonal influenza infects millions of people and causes substantial morbidity and mortality in the United States each year, making accurate short-term forecasting a core public-health need. Reliable forecasts of epidemic time series can inform vaccination timing, hospital staffing, and resource allocation, yet the comparative behavior of modern forecasting architectures on infectious-disease surveillance data remains insufficiently characterized. We address this gap through a systematic evaluation of regional influenza forecasting using influenza-like illness surveillance and influenza-associated hospitalization time series under both temporal and spatial generalization settings for 1-4-week-ahead prediction. We compare classical neural network architectures, numerical transformer-based models, pretrained time series foundation models, and LLM-based forecasting approaches. Across tasks, we demonstrate that a mixture-of-experts model that fuses multiple pretrained forecasters achieves the strongest overall performance, indicating that heterogeneous pretrained representations provide complementary predictive information. Our results further show that numerical transformer-based models produce reliable forecasts, while pretraining provides the largest gains at longer horizons, particularly when the pretraining domain is mechanistically aligned with influenza dynamics. In contrast, LLM-based time series methods underperform relative to numerical forecasters in this setting. Finally, we examine hospitalization information as both an auxiliary covariate and a pretraining source. Hospitalization signals provide complementary improvements in selected settings and clarify when additional surveillance streams enhance the robustness of multi-horizon forecasting. These findings provide actionable guidance on model selection, pretraining strategy, and auxiliary-signal use for influenza preparedness.

</details>


### [138] [Advances in Scientific Machine Learning for Coupled Fluid Flow and Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19562)
*Gabriel F. Barros,Rômulo M. Silva,Alvaro L. G. A. Coutinho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SciML surrogates for coupled incompressible Navier–Stokes and scalar transport are surveyed, combining linear ROMs (SVD, DMD) with nonlinear NN models (PINNs, β-VAEs). The authors integrate HPC strategies (AMR/Coarsening, data compression) and present two new contributions: PINN-based turbidity-current surrogates and β-VAE–driven disentangled modes in thermal flows, tested on benchmarks like lock-exchange and Rayleigh–Bénard convection. The results indicate fast, accurate approximations with reduced computational cost, while real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The coupled fluid-transport physics governed by the incompressible Navier–Stokes equations with scalar transport are computationally expensive due to nonlinear coupling and multiscale behavior. Efficient surrogate models are needed to enable rapid predictions, parametric studies, and uncertainty quantification without resorting to full-order simulations.

Method: Survey of state-of-the-art SciML techniques for surrogate modeling of coupled PDEs: linear reduced-order methods based on singular value decomposition (e.g., Dynamic Mode Decomposition) and nonlinear neural network approaches such as Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and β-Variational Autoencoders (β-VAEs). Integration with High Performance Computing strategies, including Adaptive Mesh Refinement/Coarsening (AMR/Coarsening) and floating-point data compression. Presentation of two new contributions: (i) surrogate modeling of turbidity currents using PINNs; (ii) extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows via β-VAEs. Governing equations and benchmarks (lock-exchange flows, Rayleigh–Bénard convection) used to illustrate methodologies.

Result: The chapter demonstrates that SciML surrogates can provide fast, accurate approximations of complex coupled systems within specified data regimes and modeling assumptions, achieving substantial reductions in computational cost relative to full-order simulations. However, broad capabilities such as real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active, problem-dependent research directions.

Conclusion: SciML offers a promising framework for efficient surrogate modeling of coupled fluid–transport phenomena. While effective for rapid approximations and potential real-time applications, further work is needed to generalize across regimes and to robustly quantify uncertainty.

Abstract: This chapter reviews recent advances in Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) for modeling coupled fluid flow and transport phenomena governed by the incompressible Navier-Stokes and scalar transport equations. Such systems, found in applications like turbidity currents and thermal convection, feature strong nonlinear coupling and multiscale behavior that make high-fidelity simulations computationally expensive. To address this, the chapter surveys state-of-the-art SciML methods for building efficient surrogate models, including linear reduced-order techniques based on Singular Value Decomposition (such as Dynamic Mode Decomposition) and nonlinear neural network approaches like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) and $β$-Variational Autoencoders ($β$-VAEs). It first covers the authors' work combining these models with High Performance Computing strategies, including Adaptive Mesh Refinement/Coarsening (AMR/C) and scientific floating-point data compression. It then presents two new contributions: surrogate modeling of turbidity currents via PINNs, and the extraction of disentangled nonlinear modes from thermal flows using $β$-VAEs. Governing equations and representative benchmarks, including lock-exchange flows and Rayleigh-Bénard convection, illustrate these methodologies. The chapter is intentionally long, covering both the mathematical and physical foundations of coupled fluid flow and the computational aspects of state-of-the-art modeling. Overall, it demonstrates how SciML enables fast, accurate approximations of complex coupled systems within the specific data regimes and modeling assumptions considered, while substantially reducing computational cost relative to full-order simulations. Broader capabilities such as real-time prediction and uncertainty quantification remain active research directions whose feasibility depends strongly on the problem at hand.

</details>


### [139] [On the QUEST for Uncertainty Quantification via Highest Density Regions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19569)
*Sam Goring,Tom Kuipers,Nicola Paoletti,David S. Watson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QUEST introduces uncertainty quantification via highest-density regions, quantifying uncertainty as the Lebesgue measure of the most probable subset, addressing misalignment of scoring-rule-based UQ with target statistics; axiomatic properties and favorable benchmarks reported.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional regression UQ uses proper scoring rules focused on conditional expectation, which may misrepresent uncertainty when the target statistic differs; need an uncertainty measure that respects distribution concentration and is robust to location shifts.

Method: Define QUEST: uncertainty quantified by the volume of the top-density regions (highest density regions) of the distribution, evaluated at robustness parameter alpha; analyze epistemic/aleatoric uncertainty; relate to information theory/economics; prove axioms like monotonicity under spread and location invariance.

Result:  QUEST measures satisfy adapted UQ axioms and outperform variance and differential entropy on selective-prediction benchmarks; establish connections to classical stats; demonstrate favorable performance.

Conclusion:  QUEST offers a principled, axiomatic UQ framework based on high-density regions, providing robust uncertainty measures beyond scoring-rule-based risk; potential for safer decision-making; calls for further empirical and theoretical exploration.

Abstract: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for reliable decision-making in safety-critical applications in probabilistic machine learning. For regression problems, dominant scalar UQ approaches - notably, those based on proper scoring rules - measure uncertainty via pointwise predictive risk. This can lead to counterintuitive results when the target statistic is not the conditional expectation. We propose an alternative framework, in which uncertainty is characterised by the volume of the most probable subset of a distribution's support. QUEST (Quantifying Uncertainty via highest dEnSiTy regions) is a novel approach to UQ based on the concentration of Lebesgue measure at a distribution's peak(s), evaluated at one or more values of a robustness parameter $α$. We establish connections between our measures and classical statistics from information theory and economics. We show that, unlike popular alternatives based on proper scoring rules, QUEST measures of epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty satisfy a set of axioms adapted from the UQ literature, including monotonicity under distributional spread and invariance to location shifts. Selective prediction benchmarks confirm that QUEST performs favourably against standard measures such as variance and differential entropy.

</details>


### [140] [Unsupervised Causal Abstractions Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19594)
*Théo Saulus,Simon Lacoste-Julien,Dhanya Sridhar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learn a high-level causal abstraction directly from low-level measurements by exploiting low-rank structures; provide identifiability guarantees and a practical objective for learning the high-level SCM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Move beyond hypothesis-testing to data-driven discovery of high-level causal models from low-level data, leveraging ideas from low-rank causal discovery.

Method: Theoretical analysis showing that observations from a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction; derives identifiability results for these latents; proposes a practical learning objective to recover the high-level SCM from data.

Result: Identifiability guarantees for the latent high-level abstraction and a concrete learning objective to estimate the high-level SCM from low-level observations.

Conclusion: Low-rank structure in the low-level causal graph enables recovery of a meaningful high-level causal abstraction, with theoretical identifiability and a practical estimation procedure.

Abstract: Causal abstractions formalize when a high-level structural causal model (SCM) captures the interventional behavior of a lower-level SCM. Existing applications of this notion largely follow a hypothesis-testing paradigm: an expert proposes a candidate high-level model and then evaluates if the low-level system implements it. We study the complementary problem of learning a high-level model directly from low-level measurements. Our contributions leverage hypotheses from low-rank causal discovery, and can be summarized as follows: (1) we show that observations generated by a low-rank graph induce latents that form a causal abstraction, (2) we provide identifiability results about these latents, and (3) we propose a practical objective to learn this high-level SCM.

</details>


### [141] [IHBench: Evaluating Post-Interruption Recovery in Voice Agents with Structured Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19595)
*Ahmad Salimi,Wentao Ma,Yuzhi Tang,Dongming Shen,Mu Li,Alex Smola*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IHBench introduces a benchmark for post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine workflows, evaluating task fulfillment and recovery quality across 10 domains with six interruption types; closed-weight models outperform open-weight models in robustness and show no audio-text gap, while recovery quality is a distinct capability axis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks measure interruption timing but not how agents recover after interruptions. There is a need to evaluate whether agents resume at the correct step, handle interjections, and avoid repeating content.

Method:  IHBench injects six interruption types at controlled points mid-utterance across 10 enterprise domains, with per-interruption rubrics. It evaluates 27 audio-language model configurations (closed- and open-weight) from OpenAI, Google, and open-weight communities. Evaluation metrics include two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality. A human study validates the LLM judge; cross-benchmark analysis connects recovery quality with AudioMultiChallenge.

Result: Recovery quality varies by interruption type. Closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions: higher task fulfillment, ~3.3x slower degradation as conversations lengthen, and no audio-text gap. Open-weight models lag on all metrics. Human evaluation aligns the LLM judge with human annotators. Recovery quality appears to be largely orthogonal to other benchmarks as shown by cross-benchmark analysis.

Conclusion: IHBench provides a targeted framework to assess post-interruption recovery in voice agents, highlighting recovery quality as a distinct capability axis and revealing systematic differences between closed- and open-weight models across interruption types and domains. It emphasizes the need to evaluate how agents recover in real workflows, beyond interrupt timing.

Abstract: Voice agents deployed in structured workflows (customer service, healthcare scheduling, account management) must handle frequent user interruptions while maintaining progress through multi-step procedures. Existing benchmarks for speech-capable models focus on the timing of interruptions: barge-in detection, endpointing, and turn-taking dynamics. They leave unmeasured what happens after the interruption: does the agent resume the workflow at the correct step? Does it address the user's interjection? Does it avoid re-delivering content the user already heard?
  We introduce IHBench (Interruption Handling Benchmark), a benchmark that evaluates post-interruption recovery in voice agents executing state-machine-driven workflows across 10 enterprise domains. Six interruption types are injected at controlled points mid-utterance, with per-interruption evaluation rubrics generated alongside the data. Each interruption is scored on two axes: task fulfillment and recovery quality.
  We evaluate 27 audio-language model configurations from OpenAI, Google, and the open-weight community. Models vary widely, and recovery quality depends strongly on the interruption type. Across our experiments, closed-weight models are consistently more robust to interruptions than open-weight ones: they win far more often on task fulfillment, degrade roughly 3.3x more slowly as conversations grow longer, and show no audio-versus-text modality gap, whereas the open-weight models lose ground on all three. A human study validates the LLM judge against human annotators, and a cross-benchmark analysis against AudioMultiChallenge indicates that recovery quality is a largely distinct capability axis.

</details>


### [142] [Comparing Linear Probes with Mahalanobis Cosine Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19603)
*Zhuofan Josh Ying,Peter Hase,Nikolaus Kriegeskorte*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MCS (Mahalanobis cosine similarity) between linear probes can linearly predict OOD AUROC under Gaussian, balanced-class projections; this relationship is proven and empirically verified across models, layers, and concept domains; MCS is a theoretically grounded alternative to Euclidean cosine for probe comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability research relies on comparing linear probes; standard cosine similarity ignores test-data covariance. MCS incorporates covariance, potentially improving task-aware probe comparisons. The work seeks to establish a theoretical and empirical basis for MCS predicting OOD AUROC.

Method: Theoretically derive that, for balanced classes with Gaussian projections, OOD AUROC and MCS to a reference probe are linear functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on test data; provide closed-form proof; verify the linearity and its breakdown empirically across models, layers, and concept domains.

Result: MCS to a reference probe linearly tracks OOD AUROC due to both being sigmoid functions of the SNR; evidence across models/layers/domains; linearlity holds under Gaussian assumptions; identified conditions where linearity fails; supports MCS as a robust alternative to Euclidean cosine for comparing probes.

Conclusion: MCS offers a theoretically grounded, empirically effective way to compare linear probes, with a predictable linear relationship to OOD AUROC under Gaussian, balanced-class projections; the theory also clarifies the limits of linearity and when to expect deviations.

Abstract: Linear probes are widely used in interpretability research and often compared by cosine similarity. The Mahalanobis cosine similarity (MCS) between two directions, which reweights the inner product by test data covariance, is a natural task-aware refinement. Ying et al. (2026) report that a probe's MCS to a reference probe trained on the out-of-distribution (OOD) data near-perfectly linearly predicts the probe's OOD AUROC (R^2 = 0.98). Here, we extend this empirical finding across models, layers, and concept domains, and prove this general phenomenon in closed form: For balanced classes whose projections are Gaussian, OOD AUROC and MCS to the reference probe are linear because both are sigmoid-shaped functions of the probe's signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) on the test data. The theory also predicts when this linearity fails, which we verify empirically. MCS offers a theoretically grounded and empirically effective alternative to Euclidean cosine similarity for comparing linear probes.

</details>


### [143] [Latent Confounded Causal Discovery via Lie Bracket Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19610)
*Sridhar Mahadevan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces two causal-discovery algorithms under latent confounding grounded in Kan-Do-Calculus: BRIDGE identifies latent-obstruction candidates via non-closing vector fields; SKFM learns intervention fields to reveal latent curvature; together they prune exponential DAG search and recover latent causal structure from intervention geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge passive observation and active intervention using category-theoretic Kan extensions; address latent confounding in causal discovery by leveraging information geometry and geometry of intervention flows.

Method: Model interventional vs observational measures with Radon-Nikodym derivatives to induce local causal vector fields; Frobenius residuals quantify non-closure indicating latent structure. BRIDGE combines an interventional-density engine with a geometric screen to propose admissible arrows and flag latent obstructions; SKFM learns amortized intervention fields and spectrally factors latent curvature to guide BRIDGE.

Result: Experiments show successful discovery of causal models with latent confounders, dramatically reducing the super-exponential DAG search space by orders of magnitude.

Conclusion: Presents a new paradigm in causal discovery where latent structure is inferred from the geometry of intervention-induced flows, uniting category-theoretic causality with information geometry for practical latent-aware discovery.

Abstract: Recent work on Kan-Do-Calculus (KDC) has established that the boundary between passive observation and active intervention in causal inference is a category-theoretic bi-adjunction, with interventions modeled by left Kan extensions and conditioning by right Kan extensions. This paper introduces two causal discovery algorithms under latent confounding, building on the information-geometric and categorical consequences of KDC. In smooth statistical settings, Radon-Nikodym derivatives between observational and interventional measures induce local causal vector fields; failures of these fields to close under Lie brackets become computable Frobenius residuals, which we interpret as witnesses of failed visible integrability and possible latent or unmodeled structure. Our first algorithm, BRIDGE (Bracket Residuals for Interventional Discovery and Geometric Estimation), combines an interventional density or Radon-Nikodym-ratio engine with a geometric screen that proposes a high-recall family of admissible arrows, identifies non-closing visible pairs as latent-obstruction candidates, and passes the reduced family to downstream score-based or differentiable discovery routines. The second algorithmic contribution, Spectral Kan-Do Flow Matching (SKFM), learns amortized intervention fields and factors latent curvature spectrally, exposing the direct Lie-space endpoint toward which BRIDGE points. A detailed set of experiments show that both algorithms are capable of discovering causal models with latent confounders while collapsing the super-exponential space of possible DAGs by many orders of magnitude. This paper introduces a new paradigm in causal discovery, where latent structure is inferred directly from the geometry of intervention-induced flows.

</details>


### [144] [SEAGAN: domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19623)
*Antriksh Srivastava,Soumyashree Kar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-based identification of biochemical limitation states along A-Ci curves using SEAGAN, a domain-specific GNN with edge-aware attention and kNN/ASG connectivity, showing improved F1 and accuracy on synthetic data, especially near transition regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Uncertainty in correctly identifying active biochemical limitation states along A-Ci curves hinders accurate parameter estimation in photosynthesis models; leveraging graph representations can capture local relationships between curve points and integrate auxiliary signals.

Method: Represent each A-Ci curve as a graph where nodes are curve points. Build domain-specific graphs using distance-based kNN and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity. Encode edge attributes with pairwise relationships. Propose SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes) that uses process-aware node features, edge attributes, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss. Compare against conventional baselines, graph-based models, and an automated fitting benchmark on a large synthetic dataset with ground-truth states.

Result: SEAGAN achieves the best performance with F1-score 0.857 and accuracy 0.882. Edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods provides the most effective strategy, outperforming other configurations and baselines, particularly near biochemical transition regions.

Conclusion: Modeling A-Ci curves as graphs with domain-aware features and edge-aware GATs improves biochemical limitation-state analysis, enabling more reliable state identification and potential improvements in leaf/canopy photosynthesis parameter estimation.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) provide a flexible framework for learning from scientific data linked through physical, biological, or functional relationships. One promising domain is plant physiology, where measured responses often arise from multiple interacting processes whose exact separation remains difficult even with manual intervention. In plant physiology, a key example is the A-Ci curve, which relates net CO2 assimilation rate (Anet) to leaf intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci) and is used to estimate photosynthetic parameters in leaf and crop-canopy models. However, reliable estimation requires identifying the active biochemical limitation state at each curve point, which remains a major source of uncertainty. Here, we formulate limitation-state identification along A-Ci curves as a graph-based node classification problem, with curve points as nodes. Domain-specific graph representations are created using distance-based k-nearest-neighbor (kNN) and auxiliary-signal-guided (ASG) connectivity, with edge attributes encoding pairwise relations. The framework was evaluated against conventional learning baselines, graph-based architectures, and an automated fitting-based benchmark. Results on a large synthetic dataset with known ground-truth limitation states show that graph-based models improve classification, particularly near biochemical transition regions. The best-performing configuration, SEAGAN (domain-Specific and Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network for Dynamic Plant Processes), integrates process-aware node features, edge attributes, kNN connectivity, and graph attention with weighted cross-entropy loss, achieving an F1-score of 0.857 and an accuracy of 0.882. The results show that representing A-Ci curves as graphs improves biochemical limitation-state analysis, with edge-aware attention over local kNN neighborhoods providing the most effective strategy.

</details>


### [145] [MassSpecGym in the Wild: Uncovering and Correcting Evaluation Pitfalls in AI-Driven Molecule Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19624)
*Hongxuan Liu,Roman Bushuiev,Ivy Lightheart,Mrunali Manjrekar,Anton Bushuiev,Magdalena Lederbauer,Filip Jozefov,Yinkai Wang,Soha Hassoun,Josef Sivic,James Taylor,Runzhong Wang,David Healey,Tomáš Pluskal,Connor W. Coley*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive audit of MassSpecGym benchmarking reveals widespread evaluation flaws (data leakage, shortcut learning, and implementation/metric bugs) across 17/26 papers; proposes remedies and releases MassSpecGym v1.5 to improve reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark integrity is critical for trustworthy MS/MS molecule-discovery models; subtle experimental/design flaws can mislead conclusions.

Method: Review 26 MassSpecGym benchmark reports, quantify evaluation issues via replication and analysis, classify failures into three categories, assess impact, and derive general recommendations; implement and release MassSpecGym v1.5 addressing identified failure modes.

Result: Evaluation issues detected in at least 17 of 26 papers; three failure classes isolated (data leakage, shortcut learning, implementation bugs/metric divergence); quantified impact; recommendations provided; MassSpecGym v1.5 released to address failure modes (codebase publicly available).

Conclusion: Robust, well-documented evaluation protocols are essential for MS/MS benchmarks. The study offers actionable guidelines and an updated benchmark suite (v1.5) to improve reliability and reproducibility in MS/MS machine learning research.

Abstract: Reliable benchmarking is critical for developing machine learning models for tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) based molecule discovery. Subtle issues in experimental design and model evaluation procedures can degrade the trustworthiness of such benchmarks and lead to erroneous conclusions. We conduct a thorough review of model evaluation issues in the recent MS/MS machine learning literature, using the standard MassSpecGym benchmark suite as a case study to illustrate the impact of these issues. We find evaluation issues in at least 17 of 26 papers reporting MassSpecGym benchmark results in the first year of its adoption. We isolate three classes of failures: (i) data leakage, (ii) shortcut learning, and (iii) implementation bugs and metric divergence. Through extensive experimentation and code replication, we quantify the impact of these issues and show how they corrupt the evaluation standards MassSpecGym was designed to enforce. We distill our findings into recommendations generalizable to MS/MS challenges, benchmarks, and custom evaluation setups. We also release MassSpecGym v1.5, an implementation of our recommendations in the MassSpecGym benchmarking suite which addresses the failure modes identified in this audit. MassSpecGym v1.5 is publicly available at https://github.com/pluskal-lab/MassSpecGym.

</details>


### [146] [Hard or Just Unreached? Diagnosing the Sampling Blind Spot in Math-Reasoning Difficulty Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19636)
*Luca Zhou,Sajel Shah,Emanuele Rodolà,Roberto Dessì*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Pass@k as a sole difficulty signal has a blind spot: a non-trivial fraction of hardest math items are solvable with a deterministic six-chain regime plus cheap internal perturbations, revealing a structurally identifiable latent stratum in the residual stream that ordinary greedy decoding misses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expose blind spots of pass@k as a difficulty proxy, and diagnose whether hard items are solvable via internal representational interventions rather than decoding changes; test whether activation grafting can diagnose and diversify solutions without being a decoding method.

Method: Empirical study on eight open-form math cells (GSM8K, MATH) across four open-weight models. Compare greedy decoding, a six-chain deterministic regime, and five cheap residual-stream perturbations via activation grafting. Measure recoveries of previously unsolved items at matched compute, and assess perturbation distinctness across twelve cells using cross-kind fix-set Jaccard (≤0.47). Treat activation grafting as a diagnostic/diversification tool.

Result: Between 10.3% and 22.9% of examples unsolved by six random seeds are solved by a six-chain deterministic regime at matched compute. Greedy decoding alone solves ≤6% on these items. Adding cheap residual-stream perturbations via activation grafting recovers some instances; perturbations are mechanistically distinct across cells (Jaccard ≤ 0.47). The recovered items indicate the pass@k = 0% stratum is identifiable in the residual stream, not something reached by ordinary inference.

Conclusion: The pass@k metric masks a structurally identifiable hard stratum in internal representations. Activation grafting serves as a diagnostic and diversification tool, revealing solvable items under internal perturbations rather than improving ordinary decoding to reach them.

Abstract: Math and science reasoning benchmarks rely on pass@k, the fraction of sampled chains that reach gold, as the canonical per-example difficulty signal. The same signal drives RL with verifiable rewards, math data curation, synthetic curricula, and verifier training. We show this proxy has a persistent blind spot on its hardest stratum: on the eight free-form math cells we test (GSM8K and MATH across four open-weight models), 10.3-22.9% of the examples that no sampling seed solves in six tries are instead solved at matched compute by a six-chain deterministic regime. These are greedy decoding plus five cheap residual-stream perturbations applied via activation grafting, while greedy alone solves at most 6% on these math cells. Recovery scales with the additional budget, across perturbations whose mechanistic distinctness we verify across all twelve cells (cross-kind fix-set Jaccard <= 0.47 in every setup). Activation grafting is used as an intervention on internal representations, not a decoding method; we use it purely as a diagnostic and diversification tool, and our recovered items show that the pass@k= 0 % stratum is structurally identifiable in the residual stream rather than that the unmodified model reaches them under ordinary inference.

</details>


### [147] [Convex training of Lipschitz-regularized shallow neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19652)
*Chao Yin,Antoine Lesage-Landry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Convex relaxation for Lipschitz-regularized training of shallow nets enables globally optimal post-processing to improve robustness against adversarial attacks in regression tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address non-convexity and lack of global guarantees in Lipschitz-regularized training; improve robustness to adversarial perturbations and leverage a post-processing step that guarantees non-worse performance relative to a pre-trained model.

Method: Introduce a convex restriction (relaxation) of the non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training; solve to global optimum; use as post-processing starting from a pre-trained network; the optimal network under the convex program guarantees no worse objective than the initial one.

Result: On real-world datasets for regression under adversarial settings, the convex program yields lower Lipschitz-regularized objective values than existing methods; in some datasets, the resulting networks are more accurate and more robust to adversarial attacks.

Conclusion: The proposed convex training procedure provides a computationally efficient path to robust shallow networks with performance guarantees and can enhance pre-trained models via a post-processing step.

Abstract: In this work, we introduce a training procedure for shallow neural networks that promotes robustness against adversarial attacks. We solve a non-convex Lipschitz-regularized training program by introducing a convex restriction that can be efficiently solved to global optimality. Our approach can be employed as a post-processing step by taking a pre-trained network as an initial solution to then solving the convex program whose optimal network is guaranteed to be no worse than the initial one. We illustrate the improvements of our training procedure with experiments using real world datasets for regression tasks under an adversarial setting. We show numerically that solving our proposed convex program yields networks with lower objective values on the Lipschitz-regularized program compared to existing methods. Additionally, we show that on certain datasets, networks obtained using our convex training program are both more accurate and robust with respect to adversarial attacks.

</details>


### [148] [LOKI: Memory-Free Null-Space Constrained Lifelong Knowledge Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19679)
*Masih Eskandar,Miquel Sirera Perelló,Stratis Ioannidis,Jennifer Dy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LOKI enables dynamic, data-agnostic lifelong knowledge editing by HSIC-guided layer selection and null-space gradient projection, outperforming prior methods with up to 14% average accuracy gain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting and rigidity of fixed-layer updates in lifelong learning; avoid reliance on access to prior data or statistics; improve efficiency and adaptability.

Method: Dynamically select layers for updates using Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion; project gradient updates onto the null-space of model weights to preserve past knowledge; no need for previous data statistics.

Result: Outperforms existing approaches across diverse experiments; up to 14% improvement in average accuracy.

Conclusion: LOKI provides flexible, memory-efficient lifelong knowledge editing with better retention and performance, and eliminates the need for prior-knowledge access.

Abstract: Lifelong knowledge editing aims to efficiently and sequentially update language models over time, as new knowledge becomes available or when the model makes mistakes, while preserving acceptable performance on past knowledge. One unresolved challenge is that existing methods modify a fixed set of layers for all new knowledge samples, reducing flexibility and increasing catastrophic forgetting. Another is requiring access to previous knowledge and extensive pre-processing to obtain data statistics.
