To address these challenges, we introduce LOKI, a novel approach that uses dynamic layer selection based on the Hilbert-Schmidt Independence Criterion and projects gradient updates onto the null-space of the model weights, bypassing the requirement for previous knowledge access. We show that LOKI achieves superior performance to existing approaches across a wide variety of experiments, achieving up to a 14\% improvement in average accuracy.

</details>


### [149] [Multi-Granular Attention-Driven Reinforcement Learning Framework for Web Intelligent Enhancement Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19690)
*Navin Chhibber,Deepak Singh,Anokh Kishore,Nikita Chawla,K. Anguraj*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MGAR-WIES: a system that combines semantic graph modeling, attention, and adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning to handle heterogeneous web data for personalized services, achieving 80% accuracy against baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in dynamic, data-heterogeneous web environments where conventional ML/DL/RL methods struggle to maintain performance.

Method: 1) Collect heterogeneous (structured, semi-structured, unstructured) web data and unify features. 2) Construct a dynamic semantic graph with entities and relations. 3) Apply graph embeddings enhanced by attention to capture local and global dependencies. 4) Deploy adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning using attention-aware semantic states to optimize actions (e.g., recommendations, navigation, service adaptation). 5) Incorporate online feedback to update graph representations and policies in real time.

Result: Reported accuracy of 80% with comparisons to existing approaches, suggesting improved performance.

Conclusion: MGAR-WIES can deliver improved adaptability and performance in dynamic web environments by integrating semantic graphs, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning with continuous online updates.

Abstract: From the past few years, web intelligent enhancement systems increasingly rely on heterogeneous and dynamic web data to deliver personalized, context-aware services. However, traditional machine learning, deep learning, and reinforcement learning models often struggle with semantic understanding, adaptability, and scalability in continuously evolving web environments. In this research, a Multi-Granular Attention-based Reinforcement Web Intelligent Enhancement System (MGAR-WIES) is proposed to address the challenges by integrating semantic graph modeling, attention mechanisms, and adaptive reinforcement learning. Initially, heterogeneous web data comprising structured, semi-structured and unstructured sources are collected and preprocessed for generating unified feature representations. These representations are transformed into a dynamic semantic graph, where entities and their relationships are modeled by using graph embeddings enhanced by attention mechanisms for capturing both local relevance and global contextual dependencies. Subsequently, an adaptive multi-agent reinforcement learning strategy leverages the attention-aware semantic states to optimize personalized web actions like content recommendation, navigation optimization, and service adaptation. Finally, the continuous online feedback is further integrated to update graph representations and learning policies in real time by ensuring sustained adaptability and performance. The proposed MGAR-WIES acheived better results in terms of accuracy (80%) when compared with existing approaches.

</details>


### [150] [Efficiently Representing Algorithms With Chain-of-Thought Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19697)
*Yanhong Li,Anej Svete,Ashish Sabharwal,William Merrill*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoT transformers can efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms with poly-logarithmic overhead, extending beyond prior results that simulated Turing machines. This holds across finite-width, continuous (vector) reasoning, and a hybrid RNN-enhanced architecture; overhead improves under flatter instruction sets and becomes logarithmic for multiplication-free instructions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between CoT-based reasoning—often analyzed via Turing-machine simulations—and practical algorithmic practice. The paper asks whether CoT can emulate Word RAM algorithms efficiently, which are the standard for real-world algorithm design.

Method: The authors prove simulation efficiency in three model variants: (1) finite-precision transformers with poly-log width and rightmost unique hard attention; (2) continuous CoT where reasoning is vector-based; (3) a hybrid architecture with transformer layers atop a linear RNN. They analyze overhead relative to Word RAM steps and instruction sets.

Result: CoT models can simulate any Word RAM algorithm with poly-logarithmic overhead in the input size n; this overhead reduces to log^2 n for flat instruction sets and to log n for multiplication-free flat instruction sets. This is a marked improvement over the quadratic overhead observed in Turing-machine-based simulations.

Conclusion: CoT reasoning not only matches but can efficiently emulate practical algorithmic processes defined by Word RAM, challenging the view that CoT’s computational power is limited to Turing-machine-like simulations. The result depends on architectural choices (width, attention, and presence of RNN components) and on the instruction set of the Word RAM.

Abstract: The increasing popularity of \emph{reasoning} models -- language models that output a series of reasoning or thought tokens before producing an answer -- is justified, in part, by theoretical results showing that chain-of-thought (CoT) transformers can simulate Turing machines, and thus perform arbitrary computation. However, the Turing machine, while suitable for complexity-theoretic analysis, is not convenient, intuitive, or efficient for discussing algorithms. Algorithms are typically designed and analyzed at a higher level of abstraction, captured by the \emph{Word RAM} model with random-access memory and unit-cost operations on $\bigO(\log n)$-bit words. As a result, Word RAM algorithms can be substantially more efficient than their Turing machine counterparts, raising the question: \emph{Can CoT transformers efficiently simulate Word RAM algorithms?} For instance, can they sort $n$ items in $\bigO(n \log n)$ steps or run Dijkstra's algorithm in $\bigO(E + V \log V)$ steps? We answer affirmatively, up to poly-logarithmic overhead. We first establish this for finite-precision transformers with poly-logarithmic width and rightmost unique hard attention, then strengthen the result to two more practical settings with finite width and log-precision: \emph{continuous} CoT, where reasoning takes the form of vectors rather than tokens, and a \emph{hybrid} architecture in which transformer layers sit atop a recurrent (linear RNN) layer. In all three cases, we find that CoT \emph{can} efficiently simulate any Word RAM algorithm with only a poly-logarithmic overhead in $n$. This overhead reduces to log-square when the Word RAM has a ``flat'' instruction set, and only logarithmic for multiplication-free flat instructions -- in stark contrast to known CoT simulations of Turing machines, which require quadratic overhead over Word RAM.

</details>


### [151] [Efficient Neural Network Model Selection for Few-Class Application Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19712)
*Bryan Bo Cao,Abhinav Sharma,Lawrence O'Gorman,Michael Coss,Shubham Jain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A data-centered measure of few-class distinctiveness enables faster model selection and smaller, efficient models for datasets with few classes, improving efficiency in resource-constrained deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dataset properties strongly influence model choice; real-world tasks often involve far fewer classes than standard benchmarks, but most work evaluates on many-class datasets. There is a need for data-driven methods to guide efficient model selection in few-class settings.

Method: Introduce a data-side difficulty metric (few-class distinctiveness) to quickly compare models and datasets without full training, and to guide model scaling toward smaller architectures. Extend scaled model families beyond the smallest published models and validate across mobile robot, drone, and IoT scenarios.

Result: The metric enables model/dataset comparison 6–29× faster than full training/testing. Demonstrates that one can deploy models up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano for a mobile-robot task while maintaining similar accuracy, achieving efficiency gains in few-class contexts.

Conclusion: Few-class distinctiveness is a practical lever for efficient model selection and deployment. Data-driven pre-selection can yield substantial resource savings in resource-constrained applications without compromising performance.

Abstract: While much effort has focused on developing and benchmarking high-performance neural networks, less attention has been given to how dataset properties, known to practitioners, can guide efficient model selection. Neural models are typically evaluated on datasets with thousands of classes, yet many real-world applications involve fewer than ten. To address this understudied but common setting, we develop a measure of classification difficulty based on data-side properties and show how it enables more efficient model selection for few-class datasets, where traditional approaches are less effective. We term this phenomenon "few-class distinctiveness". Our metric allows comparison of models and datasets 6 to 29$\times$ faster than repeated training and testing. Leveraging this insight, we extend scaled model families below the smallest published models, achieving greater efficiency at similar accuracy, for example models up to 42% smaller than YOLOv5-nano for a mobile robot task. Targeting resource-constrained applications, we demonstrate few-class model selection across mobile robot, drone, and IoT scenarios, highlighting practical gains in efficiency without sacrificing performance.

</details>


### [152] [OnDeFog: Online Decision Transformer under Frame Dropping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19721)
*Daiki Yotsufuji,Kenta Nishihara,Shoma Shimizu,Kento Uchida,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OnDeFog combines DeFog with Online Decision Transformer to address frame-dropping in reinforcement learning, improving online performance under high frame-dropping rates and handling low-reward data better than the offline baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frame dropping due to communication delays or sensor failures degrades RL performance. DeFog, an offline method, struggles to generalize to novel states not well represented in training data.

Method: Integrate DeFog mechanisms into the Online Decision Transformer (ODT) to enable online learning through direct environmental interaction and evaluate against ODT and DeFog.

Result: OnDeFog outperforms ODT in high frame-dropping-rate environments and surpasses DeFog on datasets with substantial low-reward data.

Conclusion: Online integration of DeFog mechanisms yields improved robustness and generalization in challenging frame-dropping scenarios, making OnDeFog a superior alternative to the baselines in the tested settings.

Abstract: In challenging real-world reinforcement learning applications, communication delays or sensor failures often cause frame dropping, in which the agent cannot receive the dropped states and associated rewards. To address the performance degradation caused by frame dropping, the Decision Transformer under Random Frame Dropping (DeFog) was developed by incorporating additional mechanisms into the decision transformer to tackle frame dropping. Although DeFog can mitigate performance degradation in frame-dropping environments, since DeFog is an offline learning method, it struggles to effectively generalize to novel states not adequately represented in the training dataset. In this study, we propose OnDeFog, which integrates the mechanisms in DeFog with the online decision transformer (ODT), an online reinforcement learning method that learns policies through direct environmental interaction. Comprehensive experimental evaluation demonstrates that our proposed OnDeFog achieves superior performance compared to ODT in environments characterized by high dropping frame rate and outperforms DeFog on datasets containing a large amount of low-reward data.

</details>


### [153] [Federated Bilevel Performative Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19734)
*Liangxin Qian,Chang Liu,Xuanyu Cao,Jun Zhao,Kwok-Yan Lam*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Defines and analyzes federated bilevel performative prediction (FBPS) where client data distributions depend on deployed decisions; shows existence and uniqueness under a decoupled-risk formulation; introduces two algorithms, FBi-RRM (linear convergence under contraction) and FBi-SGD (stochastic, communication-efficient) with convergence guarantees; experiments on strategic regression, meta-strategic classification, and CNNs demonstrate stability thresholds and improved meta-generalization in nonconvex settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Performativity, where deployed decisions reshape client behavior and data collection, violates the standard fixed-distribution assumption in federated bilevel optimization. A formal FBPS framework with convergent algorithms is needed to ensure stable, privacy-preserving, and efficient nested learning under decision-dependent distributions.

Method: Develops a decoupled-risk formulation to define the FBPS point. Provides sufficient conditions for existence and uniqueness. Proposes two federated algorithms: (1) FBi-RRM, a contraction-based method with linear convergence; (2) FBi-SGD, a stochastic, communication-efficient method based on federated hypergradient estimation, with convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes and small sensitivities.

Result: The paper establishes existence and uniqueness of FBPS under the proposed framework. It proves convergence of FBi-RRM under a contraction condition and convergence of FBi-SGD with diminishing step sizes when sensitivities are small. Empirically, it validates stability thresholds and improved meta-generalization on strategic regression and meta-strategic classification, with CNN-based experiments showing effectiveness in nonconvex neural networks.

Conclusion: FBPS provides a principled equilibrium concept and practical, convergent algorithms for federated bilevel learning under decision-dependent distributions. The methods enable stable meta-learning and hyperparameter tuning in privacy- and communication-constrained settings, with broader applicability to nonconvex models; future work could explore tighter bounds, adaptive step rules, and broader distributions.

Abstract: Federated bilevel optimization is widely used for nested learning problems across distributed clients, such as federated hyperparameter tuning and meta-learning under privacy and communication constraints. Most existing formulations assume fixed client data distributions, which can be violated by performativity, where deployed decisions reshape client behavior and data collection, inducing client-specific, decision-dependent distribution shift. We study federated bilevel performative prediction, where both upper-level (UL) and lower-level (LL) objectives are evaluated under client-dependent, decision-dependent distributions. We formalize the federated bilevel performatively stable (FBPS) point under a decoupled-risk perspective and provide sufficient conditions for its existence and uniqueness. We then develop two federated methods to compute the FBPS solution: FBi-RRM, which converges linearly under a contraction condition, and FBi-SGD, a communication-efficient stochastic method based on federated hypergradient estimation with convergence guarantees under diminishing step sizes when sensitivities are sufficiently small. Experiments on strategic regression and meta strategic classification validate the predicted stability thresholds and demonstrate improved meta-generalization over non-performative baselines, and CNN-based classification further demonstrates the practical effectiveness of the proposed methods in nonconvex neural network settings.

</details>


### [154] [Manifold Bandits: Bayesian Curriculum Learning over the Latent Geometry of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19750)
*Darrien McKenzie,Nicklas Hansen,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a structure-aware sampling framework that treats problem selection as a manifold-structured, endogenous non-stationary bandit to improve RL for LLM reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard adaptive curricula focus on intermediate difficulty and ignore the structured relationships among problems; exploiting the latent representation manifold can steer learning signals more effectively.

Method: Builds a hierarchical task tree of problems; models problem relations via latent spaces; applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling decisions that adapt to non-stationary learning dynamics (endogenous).

Result: Different sampling strategies trade off learning productivity, coverage diversity, and evaluation utility; structure-aware strategies yield richer learning signals and improved downstream performance compared with difficulty-only approaches.

Conclusion: Difficulty-centric curricula are insufficient; incorporating task structure and type-awareness via a Bayesian manifold framework enhances problem sampling and RL effectiveness.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a central approach for improving reasoning capabilities in large language models (LLMs), where training efficiency depends critically on how problems are sampled during optimization. Existing adaptive curriculum learning methods typically prioritize prompts of intermediate difficulty, treating problem selection as a standard bandit problem with independent arms and overlooking the structured, heterogeneous nature of the task space. In this work, we frame problem sampling as a manifold-structured bandit problem with endogenous non-stationarity: problems are related through the model's latent representation space, and sampling decisions can steer how learning signals evolve across that space. To operationalize this perspective, we introduce Bayesian Manifold Curriculum (BMC), a structure-aware framework that organizes problems into a hierarchical task tree and applies Bayesian learning to guide sampling. Empirically, we find that different sampling strategies induce non-trivial tradeoffs between productivity (learning signal), diversity (coverage of the task manifold), and utility (evaluation relevance). These results show that prioritizing difficulty alone is insufficient for strong downstream performance, highlighting the importance of incorporating structure and type-awareness into problem sampling.

</details>


### [155] [Learning universal approximations for partial differential equations with Physics-Informed Broad Learning System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19754)
*Zhiwen Yu,Derong Yang,Liujian Zhang,Kaixiang Yang,Peilin Zhan,Jianmin Lv,Jane You,C. L. Philip Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PIBLS provides a backpropagation-free, least-squares framework for solving PDEs, improving nonlinear PDE handling with a universal approximation proof, and delivering 1–3 orders of magnitude faster performance and higher accuracy than PINNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the computational cost and mesh dependence of traditional PDE solvers and the convergence instability of PINNs, by introducing a mesh-free, fast alternative for PDE solving.

Method: Introduce Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a backpropagation-free approach that recasts PDE solving as a direct least-squares problem. An enhanced algorithm within PIBLS handles nonlinear PDEs, and a rigorous proof establishes the universal approximation property for these equations.

Result: Empirical results on linear and nonlinear PDEs show PIBLS is 1–3 orders of magnitude faster than conventional PINNs and achieves significantly higher solution accuracy.

Conclusion: PIBLS offers a computationally efficient, high-speed framework for scientific machine learning, suitable for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

Abstract: Partial differential equations (PDEs) play a central role in modeling complex physical, biological, and engineering systems. While traditional numerical solvers are robust, they often incur prohibitive computational costs due to mesh dependencies, whereas recent Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) offer a mesh-free alternative but frequently suffer from slow convergence and optimization instability. To bridge this gap, this article proposes the Physics-Informed Broad Learning System (PIBLS), a novel backpropagation-free framework that reformulates PDE solving as a direct least-squares optimization. We improved an algorithm within this framework to handle nonlinear PDEs efficiently and provide a rigorous mathematical proof establishing the universal approximation property of PIBLS for these equations. Experiments on linear and nonlinear PDEs demonstrate that PIBLS is one to three orders of magnitude faster than conventional PINNs while achieving significantly higher solution accuracy. This framework provides a computationally efficient paradigm for scientific machine learning, offering a practical, high-speed alternative for real-time simulation and design optimization tasks.

</details>


### [156] [An Information Theoretic Framework for Graph Novelty Generation via Latent Mixture Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19770)
*Itsuki Nakagawa,Kenji Yamanishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Novel information-theoretic graph novelty generation using MDL with latent-space finite mixtures; novelty enforced by poor explanation by all existing components; reliability via MDL constraints; theoretical guarantees with explicit thresholds; validated on synthetic and real graph datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for principled generation of novel graph data that remains globally structurally consistent and controllable in risk; provide quantitative guarantees on misclassification probabilities of non-novel or unreliable samples.

Method: Embed graphs into a latent space; fit a finite mixture model to the latent distribution; generate samples by enforcing novelty (poorly explained by all existing mixture components) and reliability constraints expressed via MDL; analyze thresholds to ensure vanishing misclassification rates; validate on synthetic and benchmark graphs.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows that with appropriate thresholds, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero at explicit rates; experimental results on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

Conclusion: Provides a principled MDL-based framework for graph novelty generation that achieves controllable novelty and reliability with provable risk bounds and empirical validation.

Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for graph novelty generation, which aims to generate data that are distinct from existing patterns while preserving global structural consistency. Our approach embeds data into a latent space, models the latent distribution using finite mixture models, and generates novel samples by imposing explicit novelty and reliability conditions formulated in terms of description length. Specifically, novelty is enforced by requiring generated samples to be poorly explained by all existing mixture components, while reliability constrains their impact on the overall mixture structure under the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. We provide a theoretical analysis showing that, with appropriate threshold choices, the probabilities of misclassifying non-novel or unreliable samples converge to zero with explicit rates. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed method enables principled novelty generation with quantifiable risk.

</details>


### [157] [Flow Map Denoisers: Traversing the Distortion-Perception Plane for Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19802)
*Nicolas Zilberstein,Morteza Mardani,Santiago Segarra*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A single trained flow-map model defines a one-parameter family of denoisers (via a lookahead parameter t) that spans the distortion-perception frontier in image restoration, matches optimal DP tradeoffs for Gaussian targets, and extends to general inverse problems in Plug-and-Play settings, validated on CelebA and AFHQ.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fundamental distortion-perception tradeoff in image restoration and reduce reliance on multiple models, supervision, or hyperparameter tuning by enabling a controllable tradeoff within a single model.

Method: Use flow map models (extension of flow matching for few-step sampling) to learn an average denoising field. Introduce a lookahead parameter t that modulates the tradeoff between MMSE and perceptual quality. Demonstrate that varying t recovers the DP frontier for Gaussian targets; extend to general inverse problems within Plug-and-Play solvers.

Result: For Gaussian targets, varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, empirical behavior similar. A single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff and matches or exceeds specialized baselines at both extremes. Experiments on CelebA (128×128) and AFHQ (256×256) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate the claim.

Conclusion: A single flow-map model provides a versatile, controllable mechanism to navigate the DP tradeoff across a range of image restoration tasks, without needing paired supervision or multiple specialized samplers; it achieves competitive or superior performance at both the extreme perceptual and MMSE ends.

Abstract: Image restoration faces a fundamental tradeoff: methods that minimize error produce blurry reconstructions, while those that maximize perceptual quality yield sharp but less faithful images. Existing approaches either commit to a single operating point on this distortion perception (DP) frontier or require paired-data supervision, auxiliary models, or hyperparameter tuning of the sampler to access different points. We show that flow map models, a recent extension of flow matching for few-step sampling that learns an average field, implicitly define a one-parameter family of denoisers that continuously spans the DP frontier. The lookahead parameter t acts as a control knob between the MMSE and perceptual regimes. For Gaussian targets, we prove that varying t exactly recovers the optimal DP frontier; for natural images, we observe similar behavior empirically. Within a Plug-and-Play solver, the same mechanism extends to general inverse problems, where it controls a tradeoff between perceptual alignment and data consistency. Despite the lack of exact optimality guarantees in this setting, a single trained flow map spans the DP tradeoff, matching or exceeding specialized baselines at both extremes. Extensive experiments on CelebA ($128\times 128$) and AFHQ ($256\times 256$) across several linear and nonlinear inverse tasks validate our findings.

</details>


### [158] [Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling for Stable RLHF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19818)
*Licheng Pan,Haocheng Yang,Haoxuan Li,Yichen Sun,Yunsheng Lu,Shijian Wang,Lei Shen,Yuan Lu,Zhixuan Chu,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware reward modeling (UARM) improves RLHF by calibrating reward-model uncertainty via quantile conformal prediction and reweighting GRPO advantages with variance decomposition, reducing reward hacking and improving alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward models in RLHF are often unreliable point estimates; GRPO amplifies unreliable rewards through its optimization; calibrated uncertainty and variance-aware reweighting are needed to prevent reward hacking.

Method: Incorporates quantile-based conformal prediction to produce calibrated uncertainty for reward models; applies heteroscedastic variance decomposition to reweight GRPO advantages; evaluated on HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF.

Result: UARM improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared with standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-aware reward modeling strengthens RLHF by preventing undue influence of unreliable reward signals, demonstrated across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) aligns large language models by training reward models on preference data and optimizing policies to maximize predicted rewards. However, this pipeline faces two fundamental challenges: (1) reward models cannot signal when their predictions are unreliable, since they usually act as deterministic point estimators; and (2) modern group-based policy optimization can amplify unreliable reward signals, as exemplified by GRPO's uniform treatment of rewards during advantage computation. As policies explore increasingly diverse responses, these two limitations create a critical vulnerability: unreliable reward estimates may be granted disproportionate influence, triggering severe reward hacking. We propose Uncertainty-Aware Reward Modeling (UARM), which equips reward models with calibrated uncertainty via quantile-based conformal prediction and reweights GRPO advantages through heteroscedastic variance decomposition. Experiments across HelpSteer, UltraFeedback, and PKU-SafeRLHF demonstrate that UARM significantly improves reward model calibration, reduces reward hacking, and enhances downstream alignment quality compared to standard GRPO and uncertainty-agnostic baselines.

</details>


### [159] [Enhancing Graph Neural Networks Using Proximity Graphs for Dust Source Emission Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19825)
*Maryam Sanisales,Zahed Rahmati,Ali Darvishi Boloorani,Ali Vefghi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GNNs with proximity graphs outperform baselines in dust source emission forecasting by effectively modeling spatial-temporal relationships.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately forecasting dust source emissions requires capturing complex spatiotemporal dependencies; proximity graphs provide meaningful spatial structure for GNNs to perform message passing.

Method: Construct multiple proximity graphs (Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-NN, Yao graph) as inputs to various GNN architectures (GraphSAGE, GCN, GAT); compare against GNNs using random graphs; benchmark against LSTM.

Result: GNNs leveraging proximity graphs significantly outperform those using random graphs and LSTMs in dust source emission forecasting.

Conclusion: Proximity-graph representations enable effective spatial-temporal modeling for dust source forecasting using GNNs and outperform baselines, highlighting the value of graph structure in environmental spatiotemporal prediction.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of dust source emissions is critical for mitigating the significant environmental and health hazards posed by dust storms. Traditional forecasting methods often struggle to capture the complex spatiotemporal dynamics of these phenomena.
  In this paper, we demonstrate that proximity graphs enable Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to effectively model the intricate spatial and temporal relationships between data points. Specifically, we use proximity graphs--such as Delaunay triangulation, Gabriel graph, k-Nearest Neighbor graph, and Yao graph--as the input for GNNs (including GraphSAGE, Graph Convolutional Networks, and Graph Attention Networks) to perform message passing.
  Our approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating proximity graphs with GNNs for robust and accurate dust source forecasting. To emphasize the importance of proximity graph representations, we compare our method against GNNs using random graphs for message passing. The results show that GNNs with proximity graphs significantly outperform those with random graphs and are also far superior to Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model in dust source emission forecasting.

</details>


### [160] [When, Where, and How: Adaptive Binning for Tabular Self-Supervised Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19827)
*Daehwan Kim,Haejun Chung,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Binning introduces a training-adaptive discretization pretext for self-supervised learning on medical tabular data, using a per-feature coarse-to-fine curriculum with plateau-driven refinement and a heterogeneity-aware objective. It yields consistent gains on linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning and is accompanied by a standardized SSL benchmark and released code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical tabular data are abundant but labels are costly; unlabeled data are plentiful. Existing SSL methods for tabular data rely on fixed global discretizations and are not feature-aware, limiting efficiency and transfer. The work seeks to leverage per-feature discretization that adapts during training to improve both value-space and representation-space properties.

Method: A training-adaptive, feature-wise discretization (coarse-to-fine) curriculum guided by plateau detection. The method refines discretization per feature and selects splits that are representation-aware. It uses a heterogeneity-aware objective that combines categorical reconstruction for discrete features with ordinal supervision for numerical features, aiming to align value-space and representation-space. Evaluation follows unified protocols on public medical tabular datasets, with a standardized SSL benchmark.

Result: Experiments show consistent gains for both linear probing and fine-tuning across multiple public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols, without dataset-specific discretization tuning. The approach improves value-space concentration and representation-space coherence and establishes reproducible benchmarks.

Conclusion: Adaptive Binning advances self-supervised learning for medical tabular data by coupling discretization to learning via a per-feature curriculum, offering robust performance gains and a standardized benchmark plus open-source code to enable reproducible progress.

Abstract: Medical tabular data are ubiquitous in clinical research, but deep learning for tables remains underexplored because reliable labels often require costly expert adjudication, even though structured clinical variables are routinely available in tabular form. Self-supervised learning can leverage these unlabeled tables, and recent binning-based pretexts offer a promising inductive bias, but existing objectives fix a single global quantile discretization and apply feature-agnostic supervision. We propose Adaptive Binning, a training-adaptive discretization pretext for tabular SSL that couples discretization to learning through a feature-wise coarse-to-fine curriculum. Motivated by the spectral bias of neural networks and the principles of curriculum learning, our method progressively refines discretization per feature upon plateau detection and selects representation-aware splits to jointly improve value-space concentration and representation-space coherence. A heterogeneity-aware objective unifies categorical reconstruction with ordinal supervision for numerical features, and experiments on public medical tabular datasets under unified evaluation protocols show consistent gains for linear probing and fine-tuning without dataset-specific discretization tuning. We further introduce a medical tabular SSL benchmark with standardized protocols to support reproducible progress in this underexplored domain. Our code is available at https://github.com/labhai/Adaptive-Binning.

</details>


### [161] [Neural Additive and Basis Models with Feature Selection and Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19850)
*Yasutoshi Kishimoto,Kota Yamanishi,Takuya Matsuda,Shinichi Shirakawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a feature selection layer in NAM and NBM to reduce computational cost and enable two-input interactions in high-dimensional data, achieving competitive performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NAM and NBM are interpretable GAM-based models with neural networks as shape functions, but training becomes intractable when using two-input networks or high-dimensional datasets due to computational bottlenecks. A method to reduce cost while preserving interpretability is needed.

Method: Incorporate a learnable feature selection layer into NAM and NBM; update selection weights during training to prune features and allow two-input neural nets for feature interactions; evaluate computational efficiency and predictive performance against vanilla NAM/NBM and state-of-the-art GAMs.

Result: The proposed models are more computationally efficient than vanilla NAM and NBM and achieve better or comparable performance to state-of-the-art GAMs across experiments.

Conclusion: A simple, integrated feature selection mechanism alleviates computational bottlenecks in NAM/NBM, enables two-input interactions in high-dimensional settings, reduces model size, and maintains competitive accuracy while preserving interpretability.

Abstract: Deep neural networks (DNNs) exhibit attractive performance in various fields but often suffer from low interpretability. The neural additive model (NAM) and its variant called the neural basis model (NBM) use neural networks (NNs) as nonlinear shape functions in generalized additive models (GAMs). Both models are highly interpretable and exhibit good performance and flexibility for NN training. NAM and NBM can provide and visualize the contribution of each feature to the prediction owing to GAM-based architectures. However, when using two-input NNs to consider feature interactions or when applying them to high-dimensional datasets, training NAM and NBM becomes intractable due to the increase in the computational resources required. This paper proposes incorporating the feature selection mechanism into NAM and NBM to resolve computational bottlenecks. We introduce the feature selection layer in both models and update the selection weights during training. Our method is simple and can reduce computational costs and model sizes compared to vanilla NAM and NBM. In addition, it enables us to use two-input NNs even in high-dimensional datasets and capture feature interactions. We demonstrate that the proposed models are computationally efficient compared to vanilla NAM and NBM, and they exhibit better or comparable performance with state-of-the-art GAMs.

</details>


### [162] [Physics-Informed Neural Network with Squeeze-Excitation-like Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19853)
*Yun-Fei Song,Long-Gang Pang,Fu-Peng Li,Jun-Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SEA-PINN adds a squeeze-excitation style attention mechanism to PINNs to stabilize initialization and improve optimization; it shows low variance and reduced initial loss on most benchmarks, achieves competitive high-frequency performance without Fourier features, and substantially boosts TSA-PINN when integrated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs often suffer from unstable initializations and difficulty learning complex nonlinear representations; an attention-based recalibration of neuron importance can improve convergence stability and predictive accuracy.

Method: Introduce a squeeze-excitation-like attention module into physics-informed neural networks (SEA-PINN); evaluate on 20 benchmark problems, compare against FNN-PINN and TSA-PINN-a, assess initialization variance and initial loss, and test integration with TSA-PINN.

Result: On 17 of 20 problems SEA-PINN shows near-zero variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic starting point. Without Fourier features or periodic activations, SEA-PINN attains competitive high-frequency performance (e.g., case 7: ~83% accuracy, with ~90% relative improvement over FNN-PINN). Integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN yields a ~42.49% performance boost.

Conclusion: SEA-PINN serves as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

Abstract: We introduce SEA-PINN, a novel architecture that incorporates a Squeeze-Excitation-like attention mechanism into physics-informed neural networks to dynamically recalibrate the importance of neurons across layers. A key feature of SEA-PINN is its highly stable initialization. On 17 out of 20 benchmark problems, SEA-PINN exhibit nearly negligible variance and significantly reduced initial loss, establishing a quasi-deterministic and favorable starting point for optimization. Notably, without employing Fourier feature embeddings or periodic activation functions, SEA-PINN attained competitive accuracy (83\% vs. 90\% improvement relative to FNN-PINN on the high-frequency case 7) as compared with TSA-PINN-a model specifically engineered for high-frequency problems via learnable frequencies in sinusoidal activations. Furthermore, integrating SEA-PINN into TSA-PINN boosted performance by 42.49\%. These results underscore SEA-PINN as a lightweight plug-in module that enhances nonlinear representation power, promotes more robust and efficient convergence, and strengthens the overall reliability of physics-informed learning.

</details>


### [163] [Global Convergence of Gradient Descent for Score Matching in Gaussian Mixtures via Reverse Fisher Divergence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19876)
*Alexander Tyurin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reverse Fisher divergence (RFD) yields a more favorable optimization landscape for learning Gaussian mixtures via gradient descent, with global convergence guarantees under both a single Gaussian teacher and a Gaussian-mixture teacher, under suitable initialization and separation assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forward Fisher divergence used in score matching can lead to initialization-dependent, undesirable convergence even in simple Gaussian mixture settings. The paper explores whether reversing the divergence—taking the expectation with respect to the student—improves optimization properties.

Method: Theoretical GD analysis for Gaussian mixtures. 1) Teacher is a single Gaussian; student is a Gaussian mixture with fixed weights and identity covariances; prove global convergence from arbitrary initialization. 2) Teacher is a Gaussian mixture; under global random initialization and a won-to-tildeΩ(1) separation on target means, show that with high probability each student component converges to its closest teacher component; provide conditions for convergence of the student distribution in total variation. A Lyapunov-based analysis is used to compare the optimization landscapes of reverse vs forward Fisher divergence.

Result: Global convergence guarantees for GD under both settings; components converge near closest teacher components with high probability under a separation assumption; total variation convergence under certain conditions; demonstration that the reverse objective has a more favorable landscape via a Lyapunov-based analysis.

Conclusion: The reverse Fisher divergence yields notably better optimization landscape for GD in Gaussian-mixture fitting than the forward objective, enabling global convergence guarantees and component-wise alignment; suggests broader potential benefits for score matching, diffusion models, and inverse problems.

Abstract: The score matching problem is a central training objective in modern generative modeling, diffusion models, fitting unnormalized statistical models, and inverse problems. A standard approach is to minimize the forward Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the teacher distribution. However, recent results show that even in simple Gaussian mixture model settings, this objective can lead to undesirable and initialization-dependent convergence behavior. In this paper, we study an alternative objective: the reverse Fisher divergence, where the expectation is taken with respect to the student distribution. We analyze gradient descent (GD) for fitting Gaussian mixture models and show that this change in the objective leads to significantly better optimization properties. First, when the teacher distribution is a single Gaussian and the student is a Gaussian mixture model with fixed weights and identity covariances, we prove the global convergence of GD from arbitrary initializations. Second, we extend the analysis to the case where the teacher is also a Gaussian mixture model and prove global convergence guarantees under a global random initialization scheme and a $\widetildeΩ(1)$-separation assumption on the target means. In particular, with high probability, each student component converges near its closest teacher component, and we provide conditions under which the student distribution converges in total variation distance. Our proofs rely on a new Lyapunov-based analysis of the gradient descent dynamics, showing that the reverse Fisher divergence has a much more favorable optimization landscape than the forward Fisher divergence.

</details>


### [164] [On the Oracle Complexity of Interpolation-Based Gradient Descent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19878)
*Dongmin Lee,William Lu,Anuran Makur*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PPI-GD is an inexact gradient method that uses piecewise polynomial interpolation of gradient samples over data-domain patches to approximate full gradients, achieving improved oracle complexity for smooth ERM under bounded data-space dimension; extends spline interpolation error analysis to multivariate tensor-product polynomials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exploit smoothness in the data domain (training data space) rather than only in optimization parameters to accelerate gradient-based ERM methods; reduce oracle complexity by constructing accurate gradient approximations from limited gradient queries.

Method: Query gradient samples at equidistant data-domain points to form polynomial interpolants on patches; use d-variate tensor-product polynomials to approximate full gradient; analyze oracle complexity for strongly convex and non-convex losses under a polylogarithmic bound on data-space dimension; extend error analysis from bicubic splines to higher-dimensional tensor-product polynomials.

Result: Under sufficiently smooth loss, PPI-GD attains better oracle complexity than several GD variants in regimes where data dimension is polylogarithmically bounded; the results cover both strongly convex and non-convex settings.

Conclusion: Shows that data-domain smoothness can yield practical gains in ERM optimization via interpolation-based gradient approximation and contributes new interpolation-error analysis for tensor-product polynomials, with potential broader interest in interpolation theory.

Abstract: Recent work on first-order optimizers for empirical risk minimization (ERM) has suggested that smoothness of ERM loss functions in the training data, rather than in the optimization parameters, can be leveraged to improve the oracle complexity of gradient descent (GD) methods. In this paper, we propose an inexact gradient method, piecewise polynomial interpolation-based gradient descent (PPI-GD), which approximates the full gradient in each iteration by querying the first-order oracle at equidistant points in the data domain to construct polynomial interpolants of the resulting gradient samples over appropriately sized patches of the data domain. We analyze the oracle complexity of PPI-GD for strongly convex and non-convex loss functions when the data space dimension is bounded by a polylogarithmic function of the number of training samples, and find it to outperform several GD variants in key regimes when the loss function is sufficiently smooth. Furthermore, our analysis extends several techniques from the error analysis of bicubic spline interpolants to the setting of $d$-variate tensor product polynomial interpolants which may be of independent interest in interpolation analysis.

</details>


### [165] [Matching Markets meet Cumulative Prospect Theory: Towards Optimal and Adversarially Robust Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19883)
*Ananya Kunisetty,Avishek Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyzes a multi-agent, two-sided matching bandit problem under CPT-based, nonlinear risk weighting. Key results include a CPT-weighted regret of O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}) and an improved regret by restricting active arms, especially when K >> N. Also develops robust CPT-based algorithms for adversarial, potentially corrupted rewards, with known/unknown corruption budgets yielding logarithmic regrets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model human-centric decision making in competitive two-sided markets, leveraging cumulative prospect theory (CPT) to capture nonlinear risk preferences, and to derive regret bounds and robustness results for learning in this setting.

Method: Extend CPT-weighted bandit framework to multi-agent, competitive two-sided matching. Derive player-optimal regret with CPT weights; introduce an active-arm selection strategy to reduce dependence on K and achieve improved regret when K≫N. Also design and analyze robust CPT-based algorithms for adversarial market settings with known and unknown corruption budgets, proving logarithmic regret guarantees.

Result: Derived player-optimal regret of O(K log T (1/Δ)^{2/α}) under CPT-weighted rewards. By pruning exploration to an active arm set, achieved improved (optimal) regret with reduced dependence on K when K≫N. In adversarial markets, developed CPT-based robust algorithms with known and unknown corruption budgets, achieving logarithmic player-optimal regret in both cases.

Conclusion: CPT-weighted learning captures human risk preferences in competitive matching markets, and strategic active-arm exploration yields substantial regret improvements for large arm sets. Robust CPT-based methods extend these results to adversarial environments, with strong logarithmic regret guarantees under both known and unknown corruption budgets.

Abstract: We study a multi-agent multi-armed bandit problem in the competitive setup with two-sided matching markets under a human centric decision making model. To capture human preferences, we use cumulative prospect theory (CPT) that weighs the actions of the agent in a nonlinear fashion using a ($α$-Hölder continuous) weight function. CPT has been widely used in behavioral economics and risk sensitive machine learning to emulate human preferences. We analyze the state-of-the-art learning algorithm with CPT weight distorted rewards and obtain a player optimal regret of $\mathcal{O}(K\log T \left(\frac{1}Δ\right)^{2/α})$, where $K$ denotes the number of arms, $T$ is the learning horizon, and $Δ$ represents (suitably defined) players' minimum preference gap. Noticing the dependence on $Δ$ to be sub-optimal, we further improve this regret by judiciously selecting the active set of arms during exploration, which removes the dependence on $K$ in the dominant term and achieves an improved (optimal) regret guarantees in the setting where the number of arms $K$ is significantly larger than the number of players $N$. In addition, we consider adversarial markets where the observed rewards of the agents may be corrupted. We propose and analyze algorithms for robust markets with CPT as risk sensitive measure in both settings where the total corruption budget is known and where it is unknown, and establish logarithmic player-optimal regret guarantees in both cases.

</details>


### [166] [SL-S4Wave: Self-Supervised Learning of Physiological Waveforms with Structured State Space Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19888)
*Feng Wu,Harsh Deep,Eric Lehman,Sanyam Kapoor,Guoshuai Zhao,Rahul Krishnan,Gari Clifford,Li-wei H Lehman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SL-S4Wave introduces a self-supervised contrastive framework with a tailored S4-based encoder that uses multiscale global convolution to model long-range, multichannel biomedical signals, achieving state-of-the-art performance on ECG arrhythmia tasks, label-efficient learning, and robust generalization to unseen types and EEG tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the difficulty of modeling long-range dependencies and noise-invariant representations in high-resolution, multichannel biomedical time series, where CNN-based SSL struggles and standard S4 lacks multichannel specialization.

Method: A self-supervised contrastive framework (SSL) with a tailored encoder based on structured state space models. The encoder employs multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels to capture both local and long-range temporal patterns in noisy multichannel signals. Training via contrastive objectives, evaluated on arrhythmia detection and EEG tasks.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and SSL baselines on arrhythmia detection; demonstrates strong label efficiency; robust performance on long waveform segments; good cross-domain generalization to unseen arrhythmia types; superior performance on EEG tasks, indicating generalizability.

Conclusion: SL-S4Wave effectively models long-sequence multichannel physiological waveforms, enabling efficient, robust SSL representations that transfer across cardiac and EEG domains; promising for clinical applications with limited labeled data.

Abstract: Modeling long-sequence medical time series data, such as electrocardiograms (ECG), poses significant challenges due to high sampling rates, multichannel signal complexity, inherent noise, and limited labeled data. While recent self-supervised learning (SSL) methods, based on various encoder architectures such as convolutional neural networks, have been proposed to learn representations from unlabeled data, they often fall short in capturing long-range dependencies and noise-invariant features. Structured state space models (S4) excel at long-sequence modeling, but existing S4 architectures fail to capture the unique characteristics of multichannel physiological waveforms. In this work, we propose SL-S4Wave, a self-supervised learning framework that combines contrastive learning with a tailored encoder built on structured state space models. The encoder incorporates multi-layer global convolution using multiscale subkernels, enabling the capture of both fine-grained local patterns and long-range temporal dependencies in noisy, high-resolution multichannel waveforms. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that SL-S4Wave (1) consistently outperforms state-of-the-art supervised and self-supervised baselines in a challenging arrhythmia detection task, (2) achieves high performance with significantly fewer labeled examples, showcasing strong label efficiency, and (3) maintains robust performance on long waveform segments, highlighting its capacity to model complex temporal dynamics in long sequences that most existing approaches fail to efficiently model, and (4) transfers effectively to unseen arrhythmia types, underscoring its robust cross-domain generalization. We additionally evaluate SL-S4Wave on multiple EEG tasks, achieving superior performance over strong baselines, demonstrating generalizability of our approach beyond cardiac waveforms.

</details>


### [167] [Adversarial Bandit Optimization with Globally Bounded Perturbations to Convex Losses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19891)
*Zhuoyu Cheng,Kohei Hatano,Eiji Takimoto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes and analyzes adversarial bandit optimization with a global budget on post-action perturbations for β-smooth convex losses; provides regret bounds that quantify the budget's effect and recover standard bandit CVO bounds when perturbations are absent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how post-action adversarial perturbations under a global budget affect regret in bandit convex optimization with smooth loss components; extend from linear losses to general convex β-smooth losses.

Method: Modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm to accommodate perturbations, develop a new regret analysis that isolates perturbation-induced regret and ties it to the budget; produce explicit dependence on the perturbation budget, β, time horizon, and problem dimension.

Result: Derived expected regret guarantees that quantify the perturbation budget's impact; in the absence of perturbations, the bounds reduce to the classical regret bounds for bandit convex optimization with β-smooth losses.

Conclusion: The framework generalizes globally budgeted post-action perturbations from linear losses to general convex β-smooth losses and provides a principled way to account for adversarial perturbations in regret, with bounds that collapse to standard results when perturbations are absent.

Abstract: We study adversarial bandit optimization in which the loss functions may be non-convex and non-smooth. In each round, the learner selects an action and observes only the loss incurred at that action. The loss consists of an underlying convex and $β$-smooth component and an adversarial perturbation that may be chosen after observing the learner's action. The perturbations are subject to a global budget controlling their cumulative magnitude over time.
  This framework extends the globally budgeted, post-action perturbation model from underlying linear losses to general convex and $β$-smooth losses. For this broader class, we establish expected regret guarantees that explicitly characterize the effect of the perturbation budget.
  To establish these guarantees, we modify a standard bandit optimization algorithm and develop an analysis that controls the additional regret caused by the perturbations. In the absence of perturbations, our results reduce to regret guarantees for the standard bandit convex optimization setting with $β$-smooth losses.

</details>


### [168] [Score Approximation for Diffusion Models on Arbitrary Low-Dimensional Structures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19894)
*Xinhe Mu,Zaijiu Shang,Zhaoqi Zhou,Chuan Zhou,Qi Meng,Guiying Yan,Zhiming Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Universal score-approximation theorem for score-based diffusion models on arbitrary compact supports with upper Minkowski dimension d; ReLU networks approximate the score with complexity exponential in d, not ambient dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current complexity bounds rely on restrictive assumptions (Lipschitz densities, smooth manifolds) that fail for real data with singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters. A theory covering general, non-smooth data structures is needed.

Method: Introduce a discrete-mixture formulation and prove a universal score-approximation theorem. Show ReLU network can approximate the score with complexity that grows exponentially in d. Connect with existing backward-SDE solvability results for arbitrary compact distributions.

Result: The score function can be approximated by a ReLU network with complexity exponential in the Minkowski dimension d of the support, applicable to any distribution on any compact set; this decouples complexity from ambient dimension.

Conclusion: Diffusion models are theoretically robust to irregular, non-smooth data structures, aligning with their strong empirical performance on real-world data.

Abstract: The remarkable success of score-based diffusion models has spurred significant efforts to establish their theoretical foundations. However, existing complexity bounds for score approximation rely heavily on restrictive assumptions like Lipschitz continuous densities or smooth manifold supports, which are routinely violated by the singularities, sharp boundaries, and disjoint clusters inherent to real-world perceptual data. This work establishes a universal score approximation theorem that works for any distribution supported on any compact set of upper Minkowski dimension $d$. Using a novel discrete-mixture formulation, we prove that the score function can be approximated with a ReLU network whose complexity grows exponentially only with $d$, thus breaking the exponential curse of ambient dimensionality. Combined with existing theories on accurately solving the backward diffusion SDE for arbitrary compact distributions, our work shows that diffusion models readily adapt to irregular, non-smooth data structures, explaining their competence in real-world generative tasks.

</details>


### [169] [ADaPT: Token-Level Decoupling for Efficient Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19919)
*Tingyun Li,Zishang Jiang,Jinyi Han,Xinyi Wang,Sihang Jiang,Han Xia,Zhaoqian Dai,Shuguang Ma,Fei Yu,Jiaqing Liang,Yanghua Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADaPT introduces a token-level dual-process framework that decouples efficiency and correctness signals via a mode-selection token, enabling adjustable inference-speed vs reasoning quality and reducing cost without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long chain-of-thought reasoning yields strong performance but high computational cost; coupling efficiency incentives with correctness optimization unfairly penalizes long but correct reasoning. A decoupled training signal is needed to enable adaptive, cost-efficient inference without harming reasoning capability.

Method: Propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT): a token-level dual-process framework. Introduce a mode-selection token that controls fast vs slow reasoning. Apply efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token, decoupling it from correctness signals. Train the model with decoupled objectives. At inference, adjust the generation probability of the mode-selection token to smoothly navigate the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier.

Result: Empirical results show significant inference cost reductions while preserving strong reasoning performance across benchmarks. The model enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time, validating a smooth Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: ADaPT provides a practical mechanism to decouple efficiency and correctness during training and offers tunable, continuous control over inference efficiency without unduly harming reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Large reasoning models rely on long chain-of-thought to achieve strong performance, but applying such reasoning uniformly incurs high computational cost. Existing efficiency-oriented methods attempt to shorten or mix reasoning strategies, yet often degrade reasoning capability. We identify the root cause as sequence-level coupling between efficiency incentives and correctness optimization, which implicitly penalizes long but correct reasoning trajectories. To address this issue, we propose Adaptive Dual-Process Thinking (ADaPT), a token-level dual-process framework that explicitly decouples efficiency and correctness signals during training. ADaPT introduces a mode-selection token to control fast and slow reasoning, applying efficiency-related rewards exclusively to this token to avoid penalizing correct long reasoning while encouraging efficiency when appropriate. Moreover, ADaPT enables precise and continuous control over the efficiency-performance trade-off at inference time: by adjusting the generation probability of the mode-selection token, a single trained model can smoothly move along the efficiency-performance Pareto frontier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ADaPT significantly reduces inference cost while maintaining strong reasoning performance across multiple benchmarks.

</details>


### [170] [Compositionality Emerges in a Narrow Depth-Connectivity Regime: Architecture Constraints and Solution Manifolds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19941)
*Dat H. Do,Rushi Shah,Duc V. Le,Dianbo Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compositionality in neural networks emerges only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime; specific sparse connectivity matters more than overall sparsity. The paper provides practical tools (similarity-based pruning and a depth predictor) and a theoretical framework to explain this phenomenon.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why standard gradient-based training often fails to develop compositional internal structure and to identify the precise connectivity-depth conditions under which compositionality appears, enabling targeted exploitation.

Method: Empirically explore networks with varying depth and sparsity patterns to locate the depth-connectivity sweet spot. Develop similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is likely. Support findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds.

Result: Compositionality emerges only in a narrow, target-dependent depth regime and certain sparse connectivity patterns; it depends on which connections remain rather than sparsity level alone. Outside this regime, gradient descent converges to fractured solutions. SP can recover compositional connectivity, and the depth predictor helps identify favorable depths. The theory explains why compositional solutions are reachable only in this limited regime.

Conclusion: There is a restricted depth-connectivity regime where compositional solutions arise; practical methods (SP pruning and depth prediction) can locate and exploit this regime, and the theoretical framework clarifies the mechanisms behind the emergence of compositionality via sparsity and interference bounds.

Abstract: Compositionality is believed to be the foundation for generalization, enabling models to reuse meaningful primitives in novel combinations. Yet, models trained with standard gradient-based optimization rarely, and often only weakly, exhibit compositional internal structure, and it remains unclear how or why such compositionality forms. In this work, we show that compositionality emerges in a narrow connectivity-depth sweet spot. Along the connectivity axis, compositionality only appears in some specifically sparse networks, heavily depends on which connections remain rather than on weights' sparsity alone. Along the depth axis, compositionality emerges within a narrow, target-dependent regime, peaking at specific depths, while both shallower and deeper networks fail. When either the depth or connectivity condition is violated, gradient descent silently converges to fractured solutions rather than compositional ones. To discover and exploit this emergence, we introduce (i) similarity-based pruning (SP) to recover compositional connectivity and (ii) a heuristic depth predictor to estimate where compositionality is most likely to appear. Finally, we support these empirical findings with a theoretical framework based on compositional sparsity, volume-ratio arguments, and feature-interference bounds, explaining why compositional solutions are reachable only in a narrow depth-connectivity regime.

</details>


### [171] [Towards Graph-Based Deep Learning for Map Generalization: Insights from Building Footprints Simplification and Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19956)
*Yanning Wang,Zhiyong Zhou,Zhouyu Liu,Mengni Yu,Yu Feng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Graph-based DL for map generalization: reformulates simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified GNN framework; GraphSAGE excels at link prediction, while node movement remains hard; aggregation is more complex than simplification; data imbalance and post-processing are limitations; offers directions for future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend map generalization through graph neural networks by unifying simplification and aggregation tasks, assessing common GNN architectures to reveal capabilities and gaps in handling spatial relationships.

Method: Reformulate simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a single graph-learning framework; evaluate GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE on multi-scale building datasets; compare architectures on both tasks; analyze quantitative performance and qualitative challenges, highlighting limitations and future directions.

Result: GraphSAGE provides relative strengths in link prediction; node movement prediction remains imprecise; aggregation is more challenging than simplification; data imbalance and need for post-processing limit practical applicability.

Conclusion: The study provides valuable insights into applying graph-based DL for automated map generalization, identifying key challenges (capturing higher-level spatial relations, data issues) and outlining methodological directions to advance the field.

Abstract: Map generalization remains one of the fundamental tasks in cartography, especially for the simplification and aggregation of complex building footprints. This study presents the first exploratory application of graph-based deep learning to both tasks, reformulating simplification as node movement prediction and aggregation as link prediction within a unified graph learning framework. We evaluate representative graph neural network architectures (GCN, GAT, and GraphSAGE) on multi-scale building datasets, showing that GraphSAGE demonstrates relative strengths in link prediction accuracy, while also revealing persistent challenges in precise node movement prediction. Beyond quantitative performance, the results highlight that aggregation poses greater complexity and challenges than simplification, underscoring the difficulty of capturing higher-level spatial relationships in map generalization with current deep learning approaches. Although limitations such as data imbalance and the need for post-processing remain, the study provides valuable insights and methodological directions for advancing automated map generalization with deep learning approaches.

</details>


### [172] [Low-Energy Reduced RISC-V Instruction Subset Processor for Tsetlin Machine Inference at the Edge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19964)
*Chanda Gupta,Sanidhya Bhatia,Shaurya Priyadarshi,Himani Panwar,Rishad Shafik,Sudip Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor for Tsetlin Machine (TM) inference with a reduced instruction subset, achieving high accuracy and substantial energy savings compared to BNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable programmable, energy-efficient TM inference on edge devices by decoupling TM execution from tightly coupled accelerators and leveraging a modular RISC-V architecture.

Method: Design a reduced RV32IM core tailored for TM workloads after profiling instructions; simplify datapath/control for TM inference; evaluate baseline RV32IM vs reduced core on multiple datasets and compare with BNNs as a hardware-efficient baseline.

Result: TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2 vs 60.0% for BNN); execution time reduced by up to 98%; average energy reduction of 29.7× across datasets.

Conclusion: A programmable TM-focused RISC-V microprocessor and design flow provide efficient edge TM inference with substantial performance and energy benefits over BNN baselines, demonstrating feasibility of TM on customizable microarchitectures.

Abstract: Tsetlin Machine (TM) is a logic-based machine learning approach that relies on simple bitwise operations and finite-state automata, which makes it attractive for edge AI deployments. Recent work has focused on co-processor and accelerator designs based on Tsetlin Machines (TMs). Although these designs achieve high performance, they typically depend on tightly coupled interfaces, microcode-style programming, and external host processors, limiting flexibility and ease of programming. In this work, we present a domain-specific RISC-V microprocessor architecture and design flow tailored for TM inference. Leveraging the modular structure of RISC-V, we design a reduced instruction subset processor that retains programmability while targeting improved performance and lower energy consumption for TM workloads. Instruction profiling is employed to guide instruction reduction, followed by datapath and control path simplifications tailored to TM inference. Both the baseline RV32IM core and the proposed reduced core are evaluated across multiple datasets and compared with Binarized Neural Networks (BNNs), which serve as a hardware-efficient baseline due to their reliance on bitwise operations during inference. Results show that TM achieves comparable or higher accuracy (e.g., up to 88.18% on CIFAR-2 compared to 60.0% for BNN) while reducing execution time by up to 98% across multiple datasets. Furthermore, the proposed design achieves an average $29.7\times$ reduction in energy consumption, demonstrating its effectiveness for programmable and efficient edge AI systems.

</details>


### [173] [The Token Is a Group Element: On Lie-Algebra Attention over Matrix Lie Groups](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20547)
*Przemyslaw Musialski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lie-Algebra Attention: a novel attention mechanism where tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements; scores are closed-form algebra norms of relative poses, enabling equivariant attention on affine and non-compact groups with far fewer parameters than learned kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of vector-token attention and irrep-based methods by using the natural geometry of Lie groups for attention, achieving equivariance and canonical relative geometry without heavy representation theory.

Method: Represent tokens as group elements g_i in a matrix Lie group G. Compute scores s_ij = -||log(g_i^{-1} g_j)||_λ^2 / τ using a chosen logarithm chart. This yields a canonical proximity kernel with no learned kernels or irreps. Works on SE(2), SO(3), Aff(2); diagonal G-action equivariance is automatic; cocycle condition holds. Validate with three sequence-completion experiments.

Result: The closed-form Lie-algebra scores match a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperform it on SE(2), using 50–80x fewer score parameters. A vector-token baseline breaks invariance by 5–12 orders of magnitude.

Conclusion: Lie-Algebra Attention enables fully affine and invariant attention over a broad class of matrix Lie groups with simple, canonical scoring, reducing parameter count and avoiding reliance on irreps or learned kernels; applicable to non-compact groups like Aff(2).

Abstract: We place the attention token on the group: a token is an element $g_i$ of a matrix Lie group $G$ -- a bare transformation, with no feature payload and no external action $ρ(g)$ carrying it. To our knowledge this is the first attention construction whose tokens are bare matrix Lie group elements: their score is the closed-form algebra norm of the relative pose rather than a learned kernel, and it reaches the affine full-frame groups that every irrep- or surjective-exp-based method must exclude. We call it Lie-Algebra Attention. Once tokens are group elements, the rest follows with none of the usual representation-theoretic machinery. The relative geometry of a pair is canonical, $g_i^{-1} g_j$, so the pairwise invariant $w_{ij} = \log(g_i^{-1} g_j)$ is intrinsic rather than designed; equivariance under the diagonal $G$-action is tautological, and the cocycle condition holds automatically. The attention score is the negative squared algebra norm, $s_{ij} = -\|\log(g_i^{-1} g_j)\|_λ^2/τ$: the canonical proximity kernel under a block-weighted Frobenius inner product, with no irreducible representations, spherical harmonics, Clebsch-Gordan products, or learned kernel. The construction applies to any matrix Lie group on a chosen logarithm chart containing the relative poses, including the non-compact non-abelian affine groups with scale and shear that no vector-token attention method reaches: neither the irrep tradition nor surjective-exp methods. Three sequence-completion experiments, on SE(2), SO(3), and Aff(2), bear this out: the closed-form score matches a learned MLP kernel on the same invariant and outperforms it on SE(2), using 50 to 80x fewer score parameters, while a vector-token baseline breaks invariance by five to twelve orders of magnitude.

</details>


### [174] [Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19984)
*Juntian Huang,Jurgen Kurths,Ying Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: KARC replaces recurrent reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by Kolmogorov-Arnold, enabling lightweight, closed-form training that preserves expressive capacity and improves forecasting of dynamical systems, with competitive PDE benchmarks and integration with diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of traditional reservoir computing (limited long-range dependencies, hyperparameter sensitivity) and the computational growth of next-generation RC, by leveraging the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation to achieve efficient, expressive forecasting.

Method: Replace recurrent reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions guided by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem; establish KARC as a lightweight implementation of Kolmogorov-Arnol'd networks (KANs) and enable closed-form training of RC-style architectures; validate on challenging benchmarks (PDEs) and show compatibility with generative diffusion models for text-to-image tasks.

Result: KARC achieves comparable cost while outperforming existing RC methods on challenging dynamical-system benchmarks, including partial differential equations; demonstrates integration with diffusion models for text-to-image generation, indicating broader applicability.

Conclusion: The work bridges reservoir computing and Kolmogorov-Arnold networks, delivering an efficient, high-fidelity framework for dynamical-system forecasting and offering a principled pathway to combine RC with advanced function-expansion networks.

Abstract: Reservoir computing offers a lightweight framework for forecasting dynamical systems but may struggle to capture long-range dependencies due to limited representational capacity. Conventional reservoir computing recurrently uses trainable reservoirs with hyperparameter sensitivity, while the next-generation reservoir computing removes recurrence at the cost of rapidly growing feature dimensions. Here, we develop Kolmogorov-Arnold Reservoir Computing (KARC), which replaces reservoirs with explicit basis-function expansions inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem. We rigorously show that KARC is a lightweight design of Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs), preserving the potential expressive capacity of KANs while admitting efficient closed-form training of reservoir computing.
