At comparable cost, KARC outperforms existing reservoir computing methods on challenging benchmarks including partial differential equations. It can also be integrated with generative diffusion models for text-to-image generation. This work thus establishes a principled bridge between reservoir computing and KANs, enabling efficient and high-fidelity dynamical system forecasting.

</details>


### [175] [Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR): Function-Preserving SVD Compression for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19993)
*Nico Harder,Daniel Becking,Karsten Mueller,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AIR is an SVD-based LLM compression method that guides per-weight low-rank approximations using a backward-signal influence metric, achieving large perplexity gains with modest parameter retention and enabling efficient end-to-end integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compress large language models efficiently while preserving performance; existing low-rank SVD approaches (e.g., SVD-LLM, ACIP, LoRA) leave potential performance gaps and calibration data requirements. AIR introduces a backward-signal influence metric to steer rank-approximation decisions, aiming for better accuracy at similar or lower parameter budgets and efficient inference.

Method: AIR computes a layer-local, activation-aware optimum via SVD, then performs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep that incorporates an element-wise influence metric with a monotone-descent guarantee. It is orthogonal to and can be composed with end-to-end methods (e.g., LoRA).

Result: Compared to SVD-LLM(W), AIR reduces perplexity by more than 18% at ≤60% parameter retention. Alone, AIR surpasses ACIP; AIR combined with LoRA further improves results. AIR achieves these gains with roughly 90% less calibration data and translates parameter savings into reductions in FLOPs, peak memory, and per-token latency.

Conclusion: AIR provides a practical, layer-local, backward-signal-informed low-rank compression framework for LLMs that complements end-to-end methods, reduces calibration data needs, and yields tangible inference efficiency gains without sacrificing quality.

Abstract: We present Activation- and Influence-Aware Ranks (AIR), an SVD-based LLM compression framework that guides each weight matrix's low-rank approximation with a backward-signal influence metric. Starting from the activation-aware optimum of SVD-LLM(W), AIR runs a single closed-form alternating least squares (ALS) sweep that integrates influence element-wise under a monotone-descent guarantee. AIR is layer-local and composes orthogonally with end-to-end methods: alone it exceeds ACIP, and AIR+LoRA outperforms it further. AIR improves perplexity over SVD-LLM(W) by >18% at <=60% parameter retention, matches its quality with ~90% less calibration data, and turns parameter savings into FLOP, peak-memory, and per-token latency gains.

</details>


### [176] [Connect the Dots: Training LLMs for Long-Lifecycle Agents with Cross-Domain Generalization Via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20002)
*Yanxi Chen,Weijie Shi,Yuexiang Xie,Boyi Hu,Yaliang Li,Bolin Ding,Jingren Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A general framework for training LLMs to connect the dots (CoD) via end-to-end RL with long rollout sequences to develop a meta-capability for long-lifecycle agents, including infrastructure, tasks, and evaluation, with proof-of-concept implementations and released code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable LLM-based agents to operate over long lifecycles, continually learning from experiences, updating their environmental context, and improving future task performance beyond isolated, domain-specific tasks.

Method: End-to-end reinforcement learning with long rollout sequences that interleave solve-task episodes and context-update episodes; design of tasks/environments that elicit the targeted meta-capability; proof-of-concept GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment; evaluation across in-domain, cross-domain, and CoD-to Ralph-loop settings.

Result: Empirical results validate end-to-end RL training for the CoD setting and show potential for out-of-distribution generalization within training domains, across domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop configurations.

Conclusion: CoD links prior work, expanding opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents; the authors provide releases for broader adoption and future research at a public GitHub repository.

Abstract: This work presents a general framework for training large language models (LLMs) to "Connect the Dots" (CoD), a meta-capability required by long-lifecycle agents: as an LLM-based AI agent gets deployed in an environment, it solves a long sequence of tasks while continuously exploring the environment, learning from its own experiences, and iteratively self-updating its context about the environment, thereby achieving progressively better performance on future tasks conditioned on the updated context. Major components of the CoD framework include: (1) algorithm design and infrastructure for end-to-end reinforcement learning (RL) with long rollout sequences interleaving solve-task and update-context episodes; (2) tasks and environments for incentivizing and eliciting the targeted meta-capability in LLMs during training, as well as for faithfully measuring progress during evaluation. We present proof-of-concept implementations of the CoD framework, including a GRPO-style RL algorithm with fine-grained credit assignment, as well as tasks and environments tailored to the targeted meta-capability (rather than domain-specific LLM capabilities or standard task-by-task RL). Empirical results validate the efficacy of end-to-end RL training in the CoD setting, and demonstrate the potential for out-of-distribution generalization -- within the training domains, across different domains, and from CoD to Ralph-loop settings -- of the elicited meta-capability. Our investigation of CoD connects several lines of prior works, and opens up new opportunities for advancing LLMs and AI agents. To facilitate further research and applications, we release our implementations at \url{https://github.com/agentscope-ai/Trinity-RFT/tree/research/cod/examples/research_cod}.

</details>


### [177] [StreamKL: Fast and Memory-Efficient KL Divergence for Boosting Attention Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20005)
*Guangda Liu,Yiquan Wang,Chengwei Li,Wenhao Chen,Jing Lin,Yiwu Yao,Danning Ke,Wenchao Ding,Jieru Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: StreamKL enables one-pass, streaming KL divergence between attention distributions by avoiding quadratic materialization, yielding large speedups and near-constant memory, enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Attending to attention distillation requires materializing both query–key distributions, which incurs O(N_Q N_K) memory and IO at long contexts; this limits scalability in knowledge distillation, model compression, continual learning, and sparse-attention LLMs.

Method: Introduce a novel online formulation for the coupled KL reduction that allows a single forward kernel to stream query–key tiles through on-chip SRAM; for the backward pass, recompute attention probabilities tile-by-tile to avoid storing quadratic intermediates; implement optimized GPU kernels with end-to-end stream processing.

Result: Achieves up to 43x speedup in forward and 14x in backward passes over baselines; reduces extra HBM footprint from O(N_Q N_K) to O(1), enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.

Conclusion: StreamKL provides a practical, memory-efficient solution for attention distillation by fusing KL computation with on-the-fly streaming, unlocking scalable long-context distillation and potentially broad applicability to KL-driven distillation tasks.

Abstract: Attention distillation, which trains one attention distribution to match another by minimizing their Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence, is widely used in knowledge distillation, model compression, continual learning, and sparse-attention LLM training. However, existing approaches materialize both attention distributions before computing the KL reduction, incurring $O(N_QN_K)$ memory and IO costs that become prohibitive at long context lengths. We present StreamKL, the first fused GPU primitive for attention KL divergence that eliminates this quadratic materialization. StreamKL derives a novel online formulation for the coupled two-distribution KL reduction, enabling a single one-pass forward kernel that streams query-key tiles through on-chip SRAM. For the backward pass, StreamKL recomputes attention probabilities tile-by-tile, avoiding storage of quadratic intermediates. We further design and implement efficient GPU kernels with dedicated optimizations. Experiments show StreamKL delivers up to $43\times$ and $14\times$ speedups over baseline methods in the forward and backward passes, respectively. Most importantly, StreamKL reduces the extra HBM footprint of attention distillation from $O(N_QN_K)$ to $O(1)$, enabling long-context distillation on a single GPU.

</details>


### [178] [VIMPO: Value-Implicit Policy Optimization for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20008)
*Zhewei Kang,Aosong Feng,Sergey Levine,Dawn Song,Xuandong Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VIMPO is a critic-free policy optimization method for KL-regularized RL, deriving a policy-implied value function from optimality conditions to enable value-like updates without training a critic, while also producing a critic-free actor advantage. It yields improvements over GRPO on math and Olympiad benchmarks, especially under noisy rewards, suggesting better credit assignment with simple training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the trade-off between simplicity and credit assignment in verifiable-reward RL for LLM reasoning. GRPO is simple but assigns trajectory-level advantages; actor-critic methods provide dense signals but require unstable learned value functions. VIMPO aims to combine the best of both: a critic-free scheme that still yields a usable value signal and an actor advantage.

Method: Derive a value recurrence for autoregressive generation from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized RL. Express the value function in terms of policy-reference log-ratios and a terminal condition (no future reward). Resulting value loss incorporates outcome-level rewards without a critic. Additionally yields a critic-free actor advantage, enabling a PPO-style actor update separate from the value loss.

Result: Empirical gains over GRPO on mathematical RLVR benchmarks: MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, OlympiadBench, with larger gains on competition-style tasks. Demonstrates robustness under noisy rewards, maintaining advantage over GRPO and suggesting finer credit assignment without sacrificing critic-free simplicity.

Conclusion: Policy-implied value optimization can provide finer credit assignment for verifiable rewards while preserving the practical simplicity of critic-free training, yielding consistent improvements across diverse mathematical reasoning benchmarks and robustness to reward noise.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become a central tool for improving the reasoning ability of large language models, but current methods face a trade-off between simplicity and credit assignment. Group-relative methods such as GRPO avoid training a critic, but typically assign a trajectory-level advantage to every token. Actor-critic methods provide denser learning signals, but require a learned value function with its own training instability. We introduce VIMPO, a critic-free policy optimization method that derives a policy-implied value function from the optimality conditions of KL-regularized reinforcement learning. For autoregressive generation, the resulting value recurrence can be written in terms of policy-reference log-ratios and anchored by the terminal condition that no future reward remains at the end of a trajectory. This gives a simple value loss that incorporates outcome-level verifiable rewards without training a critic. The same derivation also yields a critic-free actor advantage, allowing VIMPO to separate reward incorporation through the value loss from policy improvement through a PPO-style actor update. On mathematical RLVR benchmarks, VIMPO improves over GRPO across MATH-500, AIME 2024, AIME 2025, and OlympiadBench, with especially larger gains on competition-style evaluations. Under noisy rewards, VIMPO retains a consistent advantage over GRPO, suggesting that policy-implied value optimization can provide finer credit assignment while preserving the practical simplicity of critic-free training.

</details>


### [179] [Self-Adaptive Scale Handling for Forecasting Time Series with Scale Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20010)
*Xu Zhang,Zhengang Huang,Yunzhi Wu,Xun Lu,Erpeng Qi,Yunkai Chen,Zhongya Xue,Peng Wang,Wei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A self-adaptive scale-handling module (AS) for time series forecasting to address scale heterogeneity by learning per-input scale factors, combining Scale Calibrating and Scaling Selection; integrates with existing TSF models and improves performance on real financial fund sales data; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world TSF often features scale-heterogeneous series, unlike common scale-homogeneous assumptions; naive scaling either loses information or amplifies errors, hindering joint modeling.

Method: AS consists of Scale Calibrating (SC) that calibrates prior mean scaling factors via neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS) that decides whether to apply calibration or retain original factor, preventing over-calibration.

Result: Demonstrates consistent performance improvements across TSF models on Ant Fortune and Alipay fund sales datasets; demonstrates plug-in applicability.

Conclusion: AS provides a practical, effective plug-in to handle scale heterogeneity in TSF, preserving semantic discriminability and reducing inverse-scaling errors; code available.

Abstract: Current time series forecasting (TSF) research predominantly focuses on scale-homogeneous data, where different time series share similar numerical magnitude ranges. However, in real-world industrial scenarios such as financial product sales, different time series often differ by orders of magnitude (scale heterogeneity). Since these series share similar temporal patterns, joint modeling is desirable for better data utilization, yet existing scaling methods either compress low-scale signals (global normalization) or destroy semantic discriminability and amplify inverse-scaling errors (window-based scaling). This paper proposes a self-Adaptive Scale-handling (AS) module that learns adaptive scale factors tailored to each input, preserving semantic discriminability while reducing inverse-scaling errors. AS consists of Scale Calibrating (SC), which calibrates prior mean scaling factors through neural networks, and Scaling Selection (SS), which decides whether to apply calibration or retain the original factor, avoiding over-calibration. Experiments on real-world fund sales datasets from Ant Fortune and Alipay show that AS seamlessly integrates into popular TSF models and consistently improves their performance. The code and dataset are available at the link https://github.com/Meteor-Stars/ASTSF.

</details>


### [180] [Hierarchical Control in Multi-Agent Games: LLM-based Planning and RL Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20014)
*Jannik Hösch,Alessandro Sestini,Florian Fuchs,Amir Baghi,Joakim Bergdahl,Konrad Tollmar,Jean-Philippe Barrette-LaPierre,Linus Gisslén*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hierarchical framework where a pretrained LLM acts as a centralized strategic controller to orchestrate pretrained RL skill policies for a multi-agent team, enabling coordinated behavior with reactive low-level execution; achieves competitive performance to hand-crafted baselines and higher perceived believability than end-to-end RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scale multi-agent RL in complex environments by overcoming sparse rewards and large state-action spaces, and achieve coordinated strategies without extensive manual rule engineering.

Method: A two-tier architecture: the LLM selects among specialized RL skill policies (centralized planning) while RL policies execute reactive low-level actions; evaluated in a 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior tree (BT) and Flat RL baselines; performance measured by win rate and user study on believability.

Result: LLM+RL achieved task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4% vs 51.5% win rate, p=0.103) and both outperformed Flat RL; user study (n=15) found 60% perceived LLM+RL as most human-like (p=0.027).

Conclusion: Pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills, delivering competitive multi-agent coordination and higher perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved strong performance in sequential decision-making, yet scaling to complex multi-agent environments remains challenging due to sparse rewards, large state-action spaces, and the difficulty of learning coordinated strategies. We propose a hierarchical architecture where a pretrained large language model (LLM) acts as a centralized strategic controller that selects among specialized RL skill policies for a team of agents, while RL policies handle reactive low-level execution. We evaluate this hybrid system in a competitive 2v2 King of the Hill environment against behavior tree (BT) and \emph{``Flat''} RL (end-to-end training without skill decomposition) baselines. The LLM+RL system achieves task performance statistically equivalent to hand-crafted BT (46.4\% vs 51.5\% win rate, $p=0.103$) while both significantly outperform Flat RL trained without skill decomposition. A user study ($n=15$) reveals that 60\% of participants perceive LLM+RL agents as the most human-like ($p=0.027$), citing behavioral adaptability and tactical variability. These results demonstrate that pretrained LLM reasoning can effectively orchestrate pretrained RL skills, achieving competitive multi-agent coordination and superior perceived believability without manual rule engineering.

</details>


### [181] [Adaptive Distance-Aware Trunk Deep Operator Learning for Long-Span Roadway Bridges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20015)
*Bilal Ahmed,Diab W. Abueidda,Waleed El-Sekelly,Tarek Abdoun,Mostafa E. Mobasher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An adaptive DeepONet (AD-DeepONet) with a load-aware, localized learning domain enables fast, accurate prediction of localized bridge responses under vehicular loading, using a reduced-order model for training and a Schur-complement-based full-field reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently predict highly localized structural responses in long-span bridges under variable vehicle loads, enabling rapid influence surface generation and digital twin workflows without expensive full-field FEM evaluations.

Method: - Build a trunk DeepONet whose input domain is adaptively formed around the load influence zone using a KNN-based strategy. - Introduce distance-aware features encoding geometric relationships between load and structural nodes. - Use a stiffness-informed Schur complement formulation to extend adaptive-node predictions to the full structural field. - Train on response data generated from a reduced-order equivalent shell model to preserve global behavior with lower cost. - Validate on a benchmark bridge and Mussafah Bridge.

Result: - Achieves FEM-level accuracy with relative errors < 5%. - Reduces total response evaluation time (including reconstruction) by ~60x. - AD-DeepONet inference (without post-processing) is up to 10^4x faster than FEM. - Enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary loading, supporting large-scale bridge analysis and digital twins.

Conclusion: Adaptive, physics-informed DeepONet with localized learning domains and a Schur-based reconstruction can deliver high-accuracy, fast predictions of localized bridge responses, making scalable bridge analytics and digital twin applications feasible.

Abstract: Long-span roadway bridges exhibit highly localized structural responses under vehicular loading, making repeated FE analysis computationally expensive for applications such as influence surface generation and structural digital twins. Existing SciML approaches struggle to accurately capture these localized responses. To address this challenge, this study proposes an adaptive-trunk DeepONet for localized structural response prediction in large-scale bridge systems. The framework dynamically constructs a load-dependent learning domain using a KNN strategy, allowing the network to focus on structural influence zones. The trunk network is further enhanced using distance-aware features that encode the geometric relationship between the load and structural nodes. A physics-based full-field reconstruction is incorporated through a stiffness-informed Schur complement formulation, enabling predictions at adaptive nodes to be extended to the entire structural domain. To enable scalable training, response data are generated using a reduced-order equivalent shell model that preserves the dominant global behavior while significantly reducing computational cost. The proposed framework is validated on both a benchmark bridge model and the real-world Mussafah Bridge. Results show that the method achieves FEM-level accuracy with relative errors below 5%, while reducing the total response evaluation time (including full-field reconstruction) by approximately 60x; excluding the post-processing reconstruction step, the AD-DeepONet inference is up to four orders of magnitude faster than FEM. In addition, the framework enables rapid generation of full-field responses, influence lines, and influence surfaces under arbitrary vehicular loading configurations, demonstrating strong potential for large-scale bridge analysis and digital twin applications.

</details>


### [182] [Exploring the potential of AlphaEarth and TESSERA embeddings for Fine-scale Local Climate Zone Mapping: A case study across five cities in Switzerland](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20034)
*Htet Yamin Ko Ko,Clement Atzberger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Embeddings from EO foundation models (TESSERA, AlphaEarth) improve 10 m LCZ mapping with an attention U-Net; TESSERA wins; cross-year transfer remains challenging; data quality is the main lever for accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of coarse ~100 m LCZ maps by leveraging precomputed EO foundation-model embeddings to enable fine-scale (10 m) LCZ mapping for urban climate applications, reducing feature engineering and preprocessing.

Method: Compare embeddings from TESSERA, AlphaEarth, and traditional S1S2 composites in five Swiss cities using an attention-based U-Net to upscale LCZ maps to 10 m; conduct three experiments on multi-city transferability, effect of higher-resolution reference data, and temporal robustness; evaluate with IoU.

Result: IoU test ranges: 0.59–0.69 and 0.77–0.82 in the first two experiments; TESSERA consistently outperforms S1S2 and AlphaEarth; cross-year transfer remains an open challenge; embeddings reduce preprocessing and guide a universal DL-based LCZ workflow; combining embeddings with location-aware attention U-Net enhances regional transferability and scalability; improving reference data quality is the strongest lever for accuracy gains.

Conclusion: EO foundation-model embeddings are a promising path toward universal, scalable, fine-scale LCZ mapping workflows for global urban climate studies, with the caveat that cross-year transferability and data quality are critical areas for further improvement.

Abstract: Understanding urban spatial morphology is critical for climate modeling, risk assessment, and sustainable urban design, and Local Climate Zone (LCZ) mapping provides the basic framework for this. However, many cities still use coarse ~100-m resolution LCZ records, which are unsuitable for fine-scale urban research. In this study, precomputed embeddings from TESSERA (Feng et al., 2025) and AlphaEarth (Brown et al., 2025) are compared to traditional Sentinel-1/2 (S1S2) composites in five Swiss cities to see if they can upscale coarse LCZ maps to 10-m resolution using an attention-based U-Net. Three experiments assess multi-city transferability, the impact of higher-resolution reference data, and temporal robustness to year-to-year phenology changes. We find that all datasets achieve strong performance with test data Intersection-over-Union (IoU) ranging from 0.59-0.69 and 0.77-0.82 in the first two experiments. TESSERA consistently outperforms both S1S2 and AlphaEarth across both settings As expected, we find that the transfer of embedding-based models from one year to another remains an open challenge. Overall, however, our results demonstrate the promising potential of embeddings derived from EO foundation models to reduce time consuming preprocessing, respectively, manual feature engineering tasks and to guide a universal deep learning-based LCZ mapping workflow. When combined with a simple location-aware attention U-Net architecture, the embeddings enhance regional transferability and scalability, supporting the development of comprehensive and reproducible fine-scale LCZ maps for global urban climate applications Improving reference data quality remains the strongest lever for further accuracy gains.

</details>


### [183] [Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis using a Multimodal Approach with 3D MRI and PET](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20037)
*Loukas Ilias,Anthi-Maria Vozinaki,Christos Ntanos,Dimitris Askounis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimodal MRI/PET AD diagnosis with input-adaptive fusion and MoE classifier; three fusion strategies (concat, GMU, gated self-attention) plus sparsely gated MoE; Grad-CAM for interpretability; strong results on NC/MCI and NC/AD, with MoE and GMU/self-attention benefiting performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static fusion and uniform computation across subjects limit robustness to patient/site heterogeneity and can waste computation. There is a need for adaptive fusion and conditional computation to leverage complementary information from MRI and PET.

Method: Extract 3D features from MRI and PET using 3D CNNs, fuse via three strategies (concat, GMU, gated self-attention), and classify with a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) that routes each sample to informative experts. Interpretability via Grad-CAM. Evaluate on three binary tasks: NC vs MCI, MCI vs AD, NC vs AD.

Result: GMU achieved 80.46% (NC vs MCI) and 95.47% (NC vs AD); gated self-attention achieved 82.08% (MCI vs AD). Ablation shows MoE removal degrades accuracy across all tasks, highlighting the value of input-adaptive routing.

Conclusion: Input-adaptive, multimodal modeling with MRI and PET improves AD diagnostic performance and interpretability; MoE routing is beneficial, and attention-based fusion strategies offer robustness to heterogeneity.

Abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is an irreversible neurodegenerative disorder and a leading cause of death worldwide. Early diagnosis plays an important part especially at the Mild Cognitive Impairment stage, where timely intervention can help slow its progression before it advances to AD. Neuroimaging data, like Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scans, can help detect brain changes early by providing structural and functional brain changes related to the disease. Yet, many multimodal models still fuse MRI and PET with static concatenation and apply identical computation to all subjects, which limits robustness to patient/site heterogeneity and can waste computation. To address these limitations, we present the first study of combining 3D convolutional feature extractors with three fusion strategies - concatenation, Gated Multimodal Unit (GMU), and gated self-attention - and a sparsely gated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier that performs input-adaptive routing, activating only the most informative experts per case. Finally, we utilize Grad-CAM to visualize disease-related regions, ensuring model interpretability. Experiments are performed across three binary classification tasks (NC vs. MCI, MCI vs. AD, and NC vs. AD). Results show that GMU achieves accuracies of 80.46 % (NC vs. MCI) and 95.47 % (NC vs. AD), while gated self-attention attains 82.08 % on MCI vs. AD. Ablations show that removing the MoE consistently degrades accuracy across all tasks. These findings underscore the value of input-adaptive, multimodal modeling for AD diagnosis by leveraging the complementary nature of MRI and PET.

</details>


### [184] [Comparative Study of Neural Surrogate Architectures for Autoregressive Prediction of Internal Battery States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20053)
*Gihyun Lee,Thorben Menne,Simon Olma,Jakob Hilgert,Sangyoung Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Systematic comparison of four neural nets (MLP, ResNet, U‑Net, FNO) as autoregressive state-transition operators to emulate DFN internal states; U‑Net achieves ~3% final-step nRMSE over 300 steps and 5.38× solver speed‑up, highlighting spatial inductive bias as key for surrogate performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DFN provides high-fidelity battery dynamics but is too computationally expensive for real-time, pack-scale, and fleet-scale deployment. The work seeks generalizable, fast surrogates that can learn state-evolution dynamics across diverse operating conditions.

Method: Formulate each NN as an autoregressive state-transition operator; train under a unified framework with multi-step unrolling and current-conditioning; compare MLP, ResNet, U‑Net, and FNO on DFN internal states across wide operating conditions; evaluate using multi-step, final-step nRMSE and inference speed.

Result: U‑Net achieves the lowest mean final-step nRMSE (~3%) across all internal states after 300-step rollouts and delivers a 5.38× speed-up relative to the DFN solver; spatial inductive bias (multi-scale features) is identified as a critical determinant of surrogate performance, outperforming the other architectures.

Conclusion: Spatial inductive bias is pivotal for accurate and efficient surrogates of internal DFN states. The study advances real-time BMS/digital twin capabilities by showing that multi-scale U‑Net architectures can robustly capture state evolution across conditions. Future work could pursue integration with physics constraints, uncertainty quantification, and extension to pack-scale dynamics.

Abstract: The Doyle-Fuller-Newman (DFN) model resolves internal electrochemical states in lithium-ion batteries with high fidelity. However, the numerical solution of its governing equations is computationally prohibitive for real-time deployment, limiting scalability from individual cells to pack and fleet-scale applications. While machine learning surrogates can substantially reduce inference latency through GPU acceleration, most existing approaches learn solution approximations tied to specific operating conditions rather than learning generalizable state-evolution dynamics. This work presents a systematic comparison of four neural network architectures (MLP, ResNet, U-Net, FNO) formulated as autoregressive state-transition operators that predict full DFN internal states across a wide range of operating conditions. To ensure a controlled architectural comparison, all models are trained under a unified framework using multi-step unrolling and current-conditioning, isolating the impact of spatial inductive bias. Results demonstrate that the U-Net's multi-scale feature hierarchy achieves a mean final-step nRMSE of 3% averaged across all internal state variables after 300-step autoregressive rollouts, while providing a 5.38x speed-up over the numerical solver. These findings highlight spatial inductive bias as a critical determinant of surrogate performance, advancing the development of surrogates for internal state observability for next-generation battery management systems and digital twins.

</details>


### [185] [PaAno+: Multiscale Encoding and Cross-Variable Attention for Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20055)
*Youji Zhu,Hongbing Wang,Wenchao Liu,Xiaodong Liu,Xiangguang Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight, patch-based time-series anomaly detector (PaAno) that combines multiscale CNN features, cross-scale adaptive attention, cross-variable fusion, a temporal patch-window sorting pretext task, and triplet loss to achieve state-of-the-art accuracy with efficient inference on resource-limited devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational burden of Transformer/large-model detectors and overcome limitations of existing lightweight methods in feature extraction and modeling inter-variable dependencies.

Method: Patch-oriented representation learning framework: (1) multiscale feature extraction backbone with diverse receptive fields; (2) cross-scale adaptive attention with residual connections; (3) cross-variable fusion attention to capture inter-variable correlations; (4) a novel temporal patch-window sorting pretext task; (5) triplet loss to optimize the embedding space. Evaluation on TSB-AD shows improvements over the original PaAno on both univariate and multivariate tasks, with favorable efficiency for real-time deployment.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on both univariate and multivariate tasks on the TSB-AD benchmark; significant performance gains across metrics such as VUS-PR relative to the original PaAno; maintains compact network design suitable for resource-constrained terminals for real-time inference.

Conclusion: PaAno demonstrates that combining patch-based representation learning with multiscale feature extraction, cross-variable attention, and a self-supervised pretext task yields superior anomaly detection performance with efficient computation, supporting its practicality in industrial/medical monitoring contexts.

Abstract: Time-series anomaly detection has significant practical value for industrial and medical monitoring, as well as other critical domains. Current Transformer- and large-model-based detection approaches incur excessive computational overhead, while existing lightweight alternatives are constrained by insufficient feature extraction and inadequate modeling of dependencies across multivariate variables. To mitigate the above drawbacks, this study develops a lightweight, efficient anomaly detection model, dubbed PaAno, within the patch-oriented representation learning paradigm. In the encoder module, a multiscale feature-extraction backbone is constructed using convolutional kernels with differentiated receptive fields to capture hierarchical temporal characteristics; subsequent cross-scale adaptive attention aggregation, combined with residual connection optimization, further stabilizes feature representation learning. A cross-variable fusion attention module is embedded to explicitly characterize inter-variable correlations, empowering the model to identify anomalous patterns amid intricate operational conditions. Moreover, a novel pretext task based on temporal patch-window sorting is customized to uncover intrinsic structural properties of time series, and triplet loss is leveraged to optimize the patch embedding space for enhanced feature discrimination. Extensive experiments on the TSB-AD benchmark demonstrate that the proposed PaAno achieves state-of-the-art detection accuracy on both univariate and multivariate tasks, yielding significant performance gains across evaluation metrics, including VUS-PR, relative to the original PaAno. Leveraging a compact network design, the presented model achieves favorable computational efficiency, enabling deployment on resource-limited terminals for real-time anomaly inference.

</details>


### [186] [What Makes Effective Supervision in Latent Chain-of-Thought: An Information-Theoretic Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20075)
*Xinghao Chen,Chak Tou Leong,Wenjin Guo,Jian Wang,Wenjie Li,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Latent CoT suffers dual collapse (gradient attenuation and representational drift). A principled remedy is to maximize mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps, rather than rigid geometric imitation. The Unified Latent Probe (ULP) quantifies this MI; results show information fidelity governs reasoning accuracy, supporting an MI-maximization supervision paradigm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Weak supervision in latent chain-of-thought leads to poor gradient signals and semantic drift; understanding and improving latent reasoning requires an information-theoretic framework to diagnose and guide supervision strategies.

Method: Perform information-theoretic analysis of Latent CoT, decompose process supervision into Trajectory Supervision (stepwise signals) and Space Supervision (semantic structure). Compare rigid geometric compression with generative reconstruction as semantic anchors. Introduce Unified Latent Probe (ULP) to measure mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps. Empirically evaluate the Information-Performance relationship.

Result: Identification of dual collapse: gradient attenuation along optimization paths and latent-space drift. Rigid geometric compression collapses reasoning space; generative reconstruction preserves information capacity. ULP provides a MI-based measure linking latent information fidelity to reasoning accuracy, revealing an Information-Performance Binding.

Conclusion: The findings advocate shifting from geometric imitation to mutual information maximization for latent reasoning supervision. Provide a principled framework and release code to support MI-aware supervision in Latent CoT.

Abstract: Latent Chain-of-Thought (CoT) internalizes reasoning within continuous hidden states, offering a promising alternative to verbose discrete reasoning traces. However, robust latent reasoning remains difficult because outcome supervision provides weak learning signals and leaves latent trajectories prone to semantic drift. In this work, we analyze Latent CoT from an information-theoretic perspective and identify this failure as a dual collapse: gradient attenuation along the optimization path and representational drift in the latent space. We further decompose process supervision into two complementary dimensions: Trajectory Supervision, which injects dense stepwise reasoning signals, and Space Supervision, which preserves the semantic structure of the latent manifold. Our analysis shows that rigid geometric compression can collapse the reasoning space, whereas generative reconstruction provides a more flexible semantic anchor that better preserves information capacity. To measure these effects, we introduce the Unified Latent Probe (ULP), which quantifies the mutual information between latent trajectories and explicit reasoning steps. Experiments reveal a clear Information-Performance Binding: reasoning accuracy depends on the information fidelity preserved in the latent chain. These findings provide a principled framework for latent reasoning supervision and suggest shifting from geometric imitation toward mutual information maximization. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/EIT-NLP/Supervision-in-Latent-CoT}{this repository}.

</details>


### [187] [Sensorimotor World Models: Perception for Action via Inverse Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20104)
*Petr Ivashkov,Randall Balestriero,Bernhard Schölkopf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A latent world model trained with inverse dynamics regularization to prevent representation collapse and align latent states with controllable actions, enabling offline, reward-free learning with compact, interpretable latent spaces and effective planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard latent world models trained to predict future states can collapse or ignore controllable dynamics; need representations that reflect action relevance and are robust to distractors in offline data.

Method: End-to-end training of a sensorimotor world model (SMWM) using a single inverse dynamics regularizer that enforces that latent transitions encode the action; no frozen encoders, EMA, or complex latent penalties.

Result: SMWM learns compact, interpretable latent spaces and achieves competitive planning performance on simple 2D and 3D control tasks.

Conclusion: Inverse dynamics regularization provides a simple, effective mechanism to avoid representation collapse and to bias representations toward controllable factors, enabling stable offline training and practical planning.

Abstract: Perception for action suggests that representations of the world should be shaped not by visual fidelity alone, but by their relevance for actions. At the same time, latent JEPA-style world models advocate learning compact predictive states from high-dimensional observations to facilitate the prediction of future states, but end-to-end training of these models is nontrivial because representations may collapse if our only goal is to construct a latent state that is easy to predict. We introduce a sensorimotor world model (SMWM): a latent world model trained end-to-end with inverse dynamics regularization. This single regularizer addresses both issues: it prevents representation collapse and induces action-aligned representations. By forcing latent states to preserve information about the action underlying a transition, it biases the model toward the controllable degrees of freedom of the environment while discarding uncontrollable distractors. This yields stable latent world models trained from offline, reward-free trajectories, without frozen encoders, exponential moving averages, or complex latent regularizers. Empirically, SMWM learns compact, interpretable latent spaces and enables competitive planning performance across simple 2D and 3D control tasks.

</details>


### [188] [Quantile of Means: A Bonus-Free Ensemble Method for Minimax Optimal Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20107)
*Asaf Cassel,Aviv Rosenberg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Quantile-based ensemble method for finite-horizon MDPs; count-free uncertainty; achieves variance-dependent regret bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between practical ensemble methods for exploration and theoretical uncertainty estimates, avoiding hard count-based estimates that are costly and brittle.

Method: Extend a recent ensemble-based approach from multi-armed bandits to finite-horizon MDPs using quantile aggregation to form uncertainty without explicit counts.

Result: Establishes optimal variance-dependent regret bounds for the count-free ensemble method, providing theoretical grounding for ensemble-based exploration in RL.

Conclusion: Offers a theoretically grounded alternative to count-based exploration, supporting practical ensembles in RL with provable regret guarantees; invites empirical validation and broader application.

Abstract: Optimal Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms typically rely on carefully constructed count-based uncertainty estimates to drive exploration. Although theoretically sound, such estimates are hard to compute in practical settings and therefore offer limited insight for designing exploration heuristics. Meanwhile, ensembling has emerged as a practical approach, but remains without theoretical justification. Building on a recent ensemble-based method for Multi-Armed Bandits, we propose a quantile-based ensemble method for finite-horizon Markov Decision Processes (MDPs). Our simple count-free approach achieves optimal variance-dependent regret bounds, providing theoretical grounding for ensemble-based exploration in RL.

</details>


### [189] [When Calibration Fails the Vulnerable Hospital: Federated Conformal Risk Control via Risk-Curve Shrinkage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20115)
*Nafis Fuad Shahid*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated conformal risk control for segmentation: pooling calibration across sites protects average performance but harms many institutions; per-site CRC restores coverage at the cost of huge prediction sets; shrinkage-based federated CRC using exchanged G-scalar curves and a single hyperparameter n0 improves worst-case coverage with efficient sets; finite-sample correction is essential and the marginal CRC guarantee is preserved under site mixture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide distribution-free, site-level coverage guarantees in federated medical imaging, addressing heterogeneity across institutions and the inefficiency of naive pooling or per-site calibration.

Method: Introduce a shrinkage-based federated CRC protocol: each site sends its empirical risk curve (G scalars) to a central server, which computes a shrinkage-regularized per-site threshold. A single hyperparameter n0 trades worst-case coverage against prediction-set efficiency. Evaluate via leave-one-site-out analysis on FeTS-2022 (1,251 subjects, 20 institutions) across four targets and three seeds; compare pooled CRC and per-site CRC; examine Lagrangian budgets and finite-sample corrections; ensure no patient data leaves sites and preserve marginal CRC under a site-mixture assumption.

Result: Pooled CRC protects the average hospital but under-covers about 40% of sites; worst site over-shoots the false-negative rate by 7.8 percentage points. Per-site CRC restores coverage but inflates prediction sets by ~83x. The proposed shrinkage-based protocol with n0 around 19 achieves 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch (via leave-one-site-out). Direct Lagrangian optimization concentrates risk on vulnerable hospitals; removing the finite-sample correction triples violations. The marginal CRC guarantee holds under site-mixture; per-site coverage validated across four targets and three seeds; no raw images, masks, or per-volume scores leave any site.

Conclusion: A practical federated CRC solution that preserves marginal guarantees while achieving robust per-site coverage and compact prediction sets. The shrinkage-based protocol mitigates heterogeneity-induced violations, with a tunable n0 enabling a favorable trade-off. Finite-sample correction is essential; Lagrangian-optimized budgets are unreliable in this setting.

Abstract: Conformal risk control (CRC) provides distribution-free guarantees on segmentation quality by calibrating a prediction-set threshold on held-out data. In federated deployments, the standard approach pools calibration scores across sites into a single threshold. We provide the first quantification, on real multi-institutional brain tumor data (FeTS-2022, 1,251 subjects, 20 institutions), showing that this naive pooled CRC protects the average hospital but violates coverage at 40% of individual institutions, with the worst site exceeding the target false-negative rate by 7.8 percentage points. The naive alternative, per-site local CRC, largely restores coverage but inflates prediction sets by 83x, rendering them clinically useless. We propose a shrinkage-based federated CRC protocol: each site transmits only its empirical risk curve (G scalars) to a server, which computes a shrinkage-regularized threshold per site. A single hyperparameter n0 smoothly trades worst-case coverage for prediction-set efficiency; leave-one-site-out sensitivity analysis identifies n0=19, achieving 2.7/20 violations at 2.0x stretch. We further show that direct Lagrangian optimization of coverage budgets fails, concentrating risk on vulnerable hospitals, and that the finite-sample correction term is essential: removing it triples violations. The marginal CRC guarantee is preserved by construction under the stated site-mixture assumption; per-site coverage is validated across four targets with three seeds. No patient-level images, masks, or per-volume scores leave any site.

</details>


### [190] [Multi-Modal Contrastive Learning for Implicit Earth Embeddings via Location Tying](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20167)
*Jonathan Hecht,Lukas Arzoumanidis,Ziyue Li,Youness Dehbi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two multimodal contrastive architectures (MELT and SALT) extend self-supervised pre-training to unpaired geospatial modalities. Both match the strongest two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four downstream tasks, but adding more modalities yields diminishing returns. MELT offers more stable training and a stronger basis for scaling, while the location encoder remains the primary bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity of high-quality ground-truth labels for spatial prediction and explore whether expanding to multiple modalities in self-supervised contrastive learning can improve representations beyond two modalities.

Method: Propose two architectures, MELT (Multimodal Embedding via Location Tying) and SALT (Sequential Alternating Location Training). They perform multimodal contrastive learning using unpaired geospatial data, extending beyond two modalities and comparing against a two-modality baseline. Evaluation on four downstream spatial tasks.

Result: Both MELT and SALT are technically viable and achieve performance on par with SATCLIP across four downstream tasks. Increasing modality count does not consistently improve results, indicating the location encoder is the main limitation and that the contrastive objective saturates early. MELT yields more stable training and provides a stronger foundation for future scaling.

Conclusion: Simply adding more modalities does not guarantee better performance; focus should shift to improving the location encoder and the contrastive objective. MELT is the more stable and scalable option, guiding future multimodal pre-training in spatial prediction.

Abstract: Spatial prediction tasks are often limited by a lack of high-quality labelled ground-truth observations. To overcome this challenge, self-supervised pre-training is a possible solution, with contrastive learning dominant for location encoders. Those approaches usually align geographic coordinates with just one additional modality. We propose two multimodal contrastive learning architectures: Multimodal Embedding via Location Tying (MELT) and Sequential Alternating Location Training (SALT). These architectures expand this framework beyond two modalities by utilising unpaired geospatial data. Both methods are technically viable and match the performance of the strongest two-modality baseline (SATCLIP) across four downstream tasks. However, increasing the number of modalities does not consistently improve performance, suggesting that the chosen location encoder is the main limitation - the contrastive objective reaches its peak early, regardless of modality diversity or pre-training volume. MELT provides more stable training than SALT and presents a stronger foundation for future scaling.

</details>


### [191] [Predicting gestational age at birth in the context of preterm birth from multi-modal fetal MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20172)
*Diego Fajardo-Rojas,Megan Hall,Daniel Cromb,Mary A. Rutherford,Lisa Story,Emma C. Robinson,Jana Hutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-modal fetal MRI ML pipeline predicts gestational age at birth and classifies term vs preterm, using data imputation, feature selection, and regression; results show modest accuracy with potential as a proof of concept.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preterm birth causes mortality and morbidity; developing an ML-based tool to predict gestational age at birth from MRI could aid clinical decisions; addresses gap where prior work treated preterm as a classification problem.

Method: A pipeline combining data imputation, feature selection, and regression models trained on comprehensive multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data from 333 control and 93 preterm births. The GA at birth was predicted; predictions were categorized into term/preterm. Used stratified 10-fold cross-validation and an ablation study. Key features identified include cervical length and placental T2* statistics.

Result: R2 = 0.13; mean absolute error = 2.74 weeks; classification accuracy = 0.77; sensitivity = 0.59; specificity = 0.82. Ablation study validated design. Features such as cervical length and placental T2* statistics were prominent. Code available at the referenced GitHub repository.

Conclusion: The work provides a proof of concept that fast, motion-robust, multi-modal fetal MRI combined with ML can predict gestational age at birth and contrast with prior literature that treated preterm birth primarily as a classification problem. Future work should expand the cohort for finer stratification.

Abstract: Preterm birth is associated with significant mortality and a risk for lifelong morbidity. The complex multifactorial aetiology hampers accurate prediction and thus optimal care. A pipeline consisting of bespoke machine learning methods for data imputation, feature selection, and regression models to predict gestational age (GA) at birth was developed and evaluated from comprehensive multi-modal morphological and functional fetal MRI data from 333 control cases and 93 preterm birth cases. The GA at birth predictions were classified into term and preterm categories and their accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity were reported. An ablation study was performed to further validate the design of the pipeline. Performance was evaluated using stratified 10-fold cross-validation. The pipeline achieves an R2 score of 0.13 and a mean absolute error of 2.74 weeks. It also achieves a 0.77 accuracy, 0.59 sensitivity, and 0.82 specificity across folds. The predominant features selected by the pipeline include cervical length and statistics derived from placental T2* values. The confluence of fast, motion-robust and multi-modal fetal MRI techniques and machine learning prediction allowed the prediction of the gestation at birth. This information is essential for any pregnancy. To the best of our knowledge, preterm birth had only been addressed as a classification problem in the literature. Therefore, this work provides a proof of concept. Future work will increase the cohort size to allow for finer stratification within the preterm birth cohort. Our code is available at https://github.com/dfajardorojas/ml-for-preterm-birth-.

</details>


### [192] [Computational Methods and Challenges in Cell-Free DNA Analysis for Multi-Cancer Early Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20174)
*Nicko Starkey,Marcin W. Wojewodzic,Krzysztof Rzecki*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive review (2022–2025) of cfDNA-based multicancer early detection (MCED), focusing on fragmentomics and epigenetic features, comparing classical statistics, machine learning, and deep learning (including autoencoders), and highlighting multimodal ensembles as the most promising for clinical translation, while noting the need for standardized evaluation protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-invasive early detection of multiple cancers via cfDNA signals, particularly for cancers lacking established screening, necessitating robust computational methods and clearer pathways to clinical adoption.

Method: Survey and synthesis of methods for cfDNA MCED (2022–2025), detailing how fragmentomics and epigenetic features are extracted/analysed, categorizing approaches into classical statistics, machine learning, and deep learning (autoencoders), and evaluating interpretability, validation strategies, and clinical readiness. Also taxonomy of challenges (technical, computational, methodological) and identification of open problems, with emphasis on multimodal ensembles.

Result: Multimodal ensemble approaches show the strongest potential for clinical integration and have the highest readiness. However, standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting is essential to enable fair comparisons and progress. The review also highlights needs for interpretability, robust validation, and clear reporting practices.

Conclusion: Future work should prioritize standardized evaluation and reporting, while leveraging multimodal ensemble methods to maximize clinical readiness for cfDNA MCED; address technical, computational, and methodological challenges to advance clinical translation.

Abstract: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) is a promising avenue for non-invasive multicancer early detection (MCED), in that, it can enable multiple cancer detection simultaneously from a single blood draw, with particular sensitivity to cancers that currently lack established screening programs. Here we review the computational methods developed between 2022 and 2025 for cfDNA-based MCED. We focus on how fragmentomics and epigenetic features are extracted and analyzed to detect cancer at early stages. We first briefly outline the biological basis of cfDNA signals, then review classical statistical and machine learning approaches alongside deep learning frameworks including autoencoder-based models. For each method we discuss biological interpretability, validation strategy, and readiness for clinical integration. Furthermore, we categorize the current challenges into technical, computational, and methodological while outlining open problems in the field. This review shows that multimodal ensemble approaches have the strongest promise for clinical integration and the highest readiness. However, for better assessment of future work and side-by-side comparison, standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting results will be crucial.

</details>


### [193] [Effective Dimension Governs Generalization in Quantum Kernel Vision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20183)
*Jian Xu,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A spectral framework shows that the effective dimension d_eff of the quantum feature kernel governs both generalization and the beneficial effects of noise; depolarizing noise collapses d_eff to 1, while amplitude damping contracts d_eff and can improve test accuracy in over-parameterized regimes, unifying two observed quantum-vision phenomena.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain two empirical phenomena in quantum vision models: (i) more/uniform entanglement correlates with better generalization, and (ii) adding quantum noise can improve test accuracy; provide a unifying principle and practical design guidance.

Method: Quantum-kernel vision models; spectral analysis of the depolarized kernel K_p = (1-p)^2 K + [p(2-p)/D] 11^T; exact decomposition; amplitude-damping analysis; kernel-machine capacity and alignment-risk decomposition; empirical verification up to 12 qubits; discusses d_eff contraction.

Result: d_eff(K_p) → 1 as depolarizing probability p increases; amplitude damping contracts d_eff and raises test accuracy by up to ~13% in an inverted-U regime; one-parameter depolarizing family provides exact kernel decomposition; noise injection aligns with a spectral-filtering frontier; results unify two observed anecdotes under a single principle.

Conclusion: Provides a unified, measurable principle (d_eff) to guide quantum-vision design by tuning entanglement and noise; supports noise-as-regularization and entanglement as a capacity knob; offers a framework to predict when noise helps and how to structure quantum-feature maps.

Abstract: Recent quantum vision models-quantum vision transformers and quantum convolutional networks-report two striking but unexplained empirical phenomena: (i) ansatze with more, or more uniformly distributed, entanglement generalize better, and (ii) injecting quantum noise can improve test accuracy rather than degrade it. These observations are currently treated as curiosities, discovered by grid search and explained, if at all, by hand. We show that both are manifestations of a single, measurable quantity: the \emph{effective dimension} $d_{\rm eff}$ of the (noise-shaped) quantum feature kernel. Working primarily with quantum-kernel vision models-a quantum feature map read out by a kernel classifier-we give a spectral account in which entanglement structure and quantum noise are two knobs that move $d_{\rm eff}$; in an overfitting regime, contracting $d_{\rm eff}$ acts as ridge-like regularization. We analyze the mechanism: an \emph{exact} decomposition of the depolarized kernel $K_p=(1-p)^2K+\tfrac{p(2-p)}{D}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$ with $d_{\rm eff}(K_p)\to1$, a contraction result (and its boundary) for amplitude damping, a kernel-machine capacity bound, and a capacity/alignment risk decomposition; the monotone contraction operative in our entangled experiments is verified empirically, not proven in general. Along the one-parameter depolarizing family the collapse is instead exact by construction; we use it only to confirm the kernel decomposition to machine precision and at up to $12$ qubits, not as evidence for $d_{\rm eff}$. Amplitude damping contracts $d_{\rm eff}$ and lifts test accuracy by up to $+13\%$ along an inverted-U sweet spot; the effect's sign flips between the over- and under-fitting regimes; noise injection matches an explicit spectral-filtering frontier. Our results organize two reported anecdotes into a single measurable principle for designing quantum-vision models.

</details>


### [194] [Learner-based Concept Drift Detection: Analysis and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20216)
*Md Moman Ul Haque Khan,Samira Sadaoui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical and empirical survey of concept drift characteristics and drift detectors, evaluating multiple algorithms on synthetic and real streaming data with abrupt and gradual drift to guide detector applicability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Concept drift degrades model performance in evolving data streams; there is a need to understand drift characteristics and evaluate detectors across diverse contexts to sustain accuracy.

Method: The paper conducts a theoretical examination of drift characteristics, develops a taxonomy of drift detectors across several categories, and performs empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse drift scenarios, including abrupt and gradual changes.

Result: Provides comparative insights into detector behavior across drift types and datasets, highlighting trade-offs and conditions under which detectors perform well, thereby offering guidance on detector selection and applicability.

Conclusion: Enhances understanding of concept drift characteristics and detector behavior, and informs practical applicability across diverse streaming contexts; suggests directions for future research and standardization in evaluation.

Abstract: Machine learning algorithms deployed for evolving streaming environments must handle the non-stationary data distributions, commonly referred to as concept drift. The presence of concept drift poses a major challenge for many real-world applications because it can severely degrade their predictive performance, hindering their ability to support robust decision-making. Consequently, the timely and efficient detection of drift events is critical for sustaining high accuracy over time. This study examines theoretically the concept drift characteristics and numerous drift detection algorithms across several categories. Furthermore, we evaluate their performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse streaming scenarios and drift characteristics, such as abrupt and gradual changes. This study aims to enhance understanding of the complex notion of concept drift characteristics and behavior of drift detectors, along with their applicability to diverse contexts.

</details>


### [195] [Boundary Embedding Shaping with Adaptive Contrastive Learning for Graph Structural Disentanglement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20283)
*Jiaqing Chen,Zidu Yin,Yichao Cai,Yuhang Liu,Zhen Zhang,Dong Gong,Javen Qinfeng Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BES is a boundary-aware adaptive contrastive module for GNNs that suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries, improving node classification and link prediction with minimal parameter changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graph structural entanglement from semantically irrelevant neighbors contaminates node embeddings, especially for boundary nodes; current robust GNNs treat all nodes uniformly and miss boundary vulnerabilities.

Method: Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning plug-in for GNNs that selectively suppresses noise at decision boundaries with minimal parameter perturbation.

Result: BES consistently improves boundary discrimination, outperforming leading methods; average node classification gain ~3.3% (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and better link prediction accuracy.

Conclusion: Boundary-aware contrastive shaping is an effective, lightweight approach to mitigate boundary-region entanglement in GNNs, yielding robust and enhanced performance.

Abstract: Graph neural networks (GNNs) excel at aggregating neighbor information for classification, yet their performance is hindered by graph structural entanglement, where spurious correlations from semantically irrelevant neighbors contaminate node embeddings. This challenge is most acute for nodes near class boundaries in the embedding space, where amplified structural noise blurs decision boundaries and destabilizes predictions. Existing robust GNN methods largely treat all nodes uniformly, ignoring boundary vulnerabilities. In this paper, to improve classification performance, we tackle graph structural disentanglement by identifying boundary-region entanglement as the primary bottleneck and propose Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning GNN plug-in module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal model parameter perturbation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing leading methods. Notably, BES boosts GCN performance by an average of 3.3% in node classification (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction.

</details>


### [196] [Integrating national forest inventory, airborne lidar, and satellite imagery for wall-to-wall mapping of forest structure with computer vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20291)
*Luke J. Zachmann,David D. Diaz,Vincent A. Landau,Chelsey Walden-Schreiner,Tony Chang,Nathan E. Rutenbeck,Katharyn A. Duffy,Kiarie Ndegwa,Andreas Gros,Scott Conway,Guy Bayes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VibrantForests delivers wall-to-wall, annual 10 m forest-attribute maps across the CONUS by a satellite-based model trained on LiDAR samples, enabling concurrent estimates of canopy cover, height, biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter to support forest and wildfire planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for coherent, wall-to-wall, annually updated forest attribute maps to support planning across large landscapes and to avoid confounding from disparate data sources with varying vintages and quality.

Method: A supervised, satellite-based forest-structure model trained on LiDAR-derived samples, deployed across the contiguous United States to produce concurrent estimates of canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10 m resolution (wall-to-wall, annual).

Result: Extends the dynamic range beyond saturation of comparable passive-sensor models and reduces regression-to-mean biases that overestimate in sparse conditions and underestimate in dense conditions; demonstrates predictive capability across sparse to dense forest conditions and provides coherent annual 10 m maps.

Conclusion: VibrantForests addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent wall-to-wall, annually updated forest-attribute maps at 10 m resolution for management decisions.

Abstract: Remote sensing is increasingly relied upon to deliver actionable science for forest and wildfire risk management across large landscapes. Wall-to-wall, annually updated maps are a persistent need for effective forest management. Many planning systems and data collections combine disparate data sources with different purposes, vintages, and prediction quality, which leads to confounding behavior in operational planning systems. We introduce the VibrantForests framework, developed and applied to map forest attributes and provide a coherent foundation for effective forest and wildfire planning. VibrantForests includes a satellite-based forest structure model trained on lidar-derived samples and applied across the contiguous United States to concurrently generate estimates of canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10-meter resolution. We demonstrate predictive capability spanning the full spectrum of forest conditions ranging from sparse-canopy/low-biomass to dense-canopy/high-biomass. Results show that our model extends the range at which saturation is commonly encountered in comparable passive-sensor models, and reduces regression-to-mean behavior that commonly produces overestimation of forest attributes in small/sparse conditions and underestimation in large/dense conditions. The VibrantForests framework addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent wall-to-wall estimates of management-relevant attributes at annual cadence and 10m resolution.

</details>


### [197] [Shifting-based Optimizable Linear Relaxations for General Activation Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20292)
*Philipp Kern,László Antal,Erika Ábráham,Carsten Sinz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces SLiR, a shifting-based linear relaxation framework for ReLUs and other activations that uses only a Lipschitz constant or critical points to parameterize relaxations by slope and shift to get sound bounds, enabling tighter relaxations and up to 7.8x more verified properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formal guarantees for neural network behavior in safety- and security-critical domains. Existing relaxation methods rely on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation, which is labor-intensive and limits applicability to new activations.

Method: SLiR parameterizes linear relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure to ensure sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain. It requires only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points, and is broadly applicable to a wide range of activation functions, enabling efficient optimization during verification.

Result: Empirical results show that SLiR produces tight relaxations across a broad set of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: SLiR offers a general, efficient, and sound relaxation framework that reduces manual engineering for activation functions and extends verifiable properties in NN verification workflows.

Abstract: The use of neural networks (NNs) is rapidly increasing, including in safety- and security-critical domains. To provide formal guarantees about NN behavior, many verification methods rely on optimizable linear relaxations of activation functions. However, existing techniques depend on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation function. Extension to state-of-the-art activation functions therefore requires substantial manual effort. In contrast, our approach SLiR (Shifting-based Linear Relaxations) is broadly applicable, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points. SLiR parameterizes relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain, enabling efficient optimization while maintaining correctness. Our experiments show that SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [198] [Recurrent neural networks approximate continuous functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20325)
*Valentin Abadie,Clemens Hutter,Helmut Bölcskei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fixed-architecture ReLU RNNs can uniformly approximate any continuous function on [-1,1] by increasing computation time; a TMNU model is introduced to realize polynomial-approximation schemes within a fixed network, with matching upper and minimax lower bounds on required runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore whether a single fixed neural network can achieve arbitrary approximation accuracy by extending computation time, treating runtime as a resource; connect neural networks with a programmable computational model to understand limits of fixed architectures.

Method: Introduce the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU) as an intermediate model that can implement polynomial approximation schemes while remaining simulable by ReLU RNNs with fixed width and bounded weights; show how TMNU can realize polynomial approximation algorithms and then simulate it with a fixed RNN to obtain uniform approximations; derive explicit convergence rates tied to classical polynomial approximation theory; establish minimax lower bounds on the required runtime.

Result: Prove that every continuous function on [-1,1] can be uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single fixed-weight ReLU RNN; provide explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitudes in the simulating RNN; obtain convergence rates that reflect polynomial approximation rates; derive minimax lower bounds showing runtime is an unavoidable resource in this fixed-network setting.

Conclusion: Fixed networks can universal-approximate continuous functions by extending computation time, with runtime being a fundamental resource; the TMNU serves as a bridging model to realize and analyze such fixed-architecture, time-driven approximation, and the results align with classical polynomial approximation theory.

Abstract: Classical approximation theorems ask for a new neural network whenever the target accuracy is improved. This paper studies the opposite possibility: can the network be chosen once and for all, and can accuracy be bought only by letting it run longer? We prove that this is possible for every continuous function on [-1,1]. More precisely, each such function is uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single ReLU recurrent neural network with fixed weights and fixed hidden dimension. The mechanism behind the construction is a new intermediate model, the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU). This model retains the algorithmic freedom needed to implement polynomial approximation schemes, while remaining rigid enough to be simulated by RNNs with explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitude. The resulting convergence rates reflect the underlying polynomial approximation rates. We complement the construction with minimax lower bounds showing that runtime is not merely a proof artifact, but an unavoidable resource in this fixed-network approximation paradigm.

</details>


### [199] [Quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks for PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20326)
*Xiang Rao,Yuxuan Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hybrid quantum-classical physics-informed network (QCPIKAN) using Chebyshev KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits to solve PDEs, claiming exponential convergence of high-frequency errors and reduced dispersion, validated on three porous-media seepage problems with superior performance to existing Q-C PINNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve faster, more accurate PDE solving by integrating physics constraints into a quantum-classical framework, addressing high-frequency error behavior and numerical dispersion.

Method: Hybrid model combining Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers with parameterized quantum circuits. Physics-informed loss enforces physical laws, enabling constrained training and better approximation of PDE solutions.

Result: Theoretical results (approximation theory) show exponential convergence of high-frequency components and mitigation of numerical dispersion. Empirical validation on single-phase flow, component transport, and two-phase flow in porous media demonstrates superior global accuracy, local error control, dynamic tracking, and front localization compared to existing quantum-classical PINNs.

Conclusion: QCPIKAN offers a robust, efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs by harnessing quantum-classical resources and physics-informed training, with proven theoretical and empirical advantages over prior methods.

Abstract: We develop QCPIKAN, the first quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network designed to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Built upon Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits, this hybrid framework embeds physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency. Our theoretical investigations grounded in approximation theory prove that this design accelerates high-frequency error convergence to an exponential rate and effectively mitigates numerical dispersion. We validate the framework across three typical seepage scenarios in porous media, including single-phase flow, component transport and two-phase flow. Compared with existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, QCPIKAN achieves superior performance in global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking and displacement front localization. This work provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs.

</details>


### [200] [Constrained hybrid modelling to predict microbial dynamics and organic matter turnover in soil systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20329)
*Paul Collart,Juergen Gall,Andrea Schnepf,Holger Pagel,Lars Doorenbos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A neural-network-based hybrid framework maps metagenome-derived functional traits to biokinetic parameters in a process-based soil organic matter turnover model, constrained by ecological theory; it improves predictive performance and learns dynamics of unobserved components, even with limited data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve process-based soil carbon models by incorporating genomic data; linking genomes to microbial processes remains unsolved, yet integrating metagenomic traits can constrain parameterization for more realistic simulations.
