Method: Develop a hybrid modeling framework that uses a neural network to predict biokinetic parameters of a soil OM turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits; enforce ecological theory constraints to ensure realistic behavior for unobserved state variables; evaluate on synthetic trait datasets of varying complexity and real data, comparing against baselines.

Result: The approach outperforms multiple baselines, effectively learning the dynamics of unmeasurable components of the process-based model, even with small training datasets.

Conclusion: Genomic-trait–driven parameterization via a constrained neural network provides a viable path to calibrate process-based soil models, improving predictions and enabling inference of hidden dynamics under data scarcity.

Abstract: Soil microorganisms control organic matter cycling and largely determine how soil systems can cope with and mitigate climate change and environmental threats. Representing microbial dynamics in process-based soil models is therefore critical to predict carbon cycling in soils, albeit highly challenging to inform from data. One promising approach to improve their parametrisation is the integration of genomic data, yet modelling the complex and unknown relationship between genomes and the processes the microbes are driving is an unsolved problem. In this work, we present the first hybrid modeling framework for deriving biokinetic parameter values of a process-based soil organic matter turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits based on DNA sequencing data. Our model predicts biokinetic parameters of the process-based model from genomic trait data with a neural network and integrates constraints from ecological theory and literature to ensure realistic behavior, even of non-observed state variables. We evaluate our method on synthetic genomic trait datasets of varying complexity and on real data, showing that our approach improves performance over multiple baselines and learns the dynamics of unmeasurable components of the process-based model effectively, even for small training datasets.

</details>


### [201] [Critical Percolation as a Synthetic Data Model for Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20347)
*Aryeh Brill,Tom Ingebretsen Carlson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A principled synthetic data framework with hierarchical latent structure built on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in high-dimensional space, enabling linear decodability of latent variables and scalable data generation, valuable for benchmarking interpretability methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability benchmarks require realistic, multi-scale structure. Existing toy datasets lack hierarchical, fractal, power-law characteristics. Critical percolation provides controllable, analytically tractable structure with ground-truth latent hierarchies.

Method: Define hierarchical latent variables on percolation clusters; map clusters to random trees and additive coalescence to enable an almost linear-time sampler for a random tree and its hierarchy. Generate data points in high-dimensional space with targets set by the latent taxonomy. Probe experiments show linear decodability of latent variables from neural activations.

Result: Ground-truth latent variables are linearly decodable from network activations. The data exhibit sparsity, self-similarity, and power-law statistics. The analytic exponents fix properties, enabling scale-free behavior without hyperparameter tuning. The sampling algorithm scales to arbitrary size.

Conclusion: Critical percolation provides a principled testbed for interpretability research, allowing evaluation of whether neural networks capture hierarchical, multi-scale structure and enabling controlled experiments with known ground-truth latent hierarchies.

Abstract: Neural networks learn features that reflect the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data. Synthetic datasets used to evaluate interpretability methods typically lack this structure, limiting their value as realistic toy models. To close this gap, we introduce a family of synthetic datasets consisting of hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in a high-dimensional data space. The percolation data consists of sparse, low-dimensional fractal clusters with a power-law size distribution. Latent variables modeling a taxonomic hierarchy generate each data point's target value. The data model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents that fix its properties without requiring hyperparameter tuning. We leverage a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to propose an almost linear-time algorithm to jointly sample a random tree and its hierarchical latent decomposition, enabling data generation at arbitrary scale. Using probing experiments, we find that the model's ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations. Together, sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability make critical percolation a principled testbed for interpretability research.

</details>


### [202] [On the Variance of Temporal Difference Learning and its Reduction Using Control Variates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20357)
*Hsiao-Ru Pan,Bernhard Schölkopf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TD variance in phased tabular settings is upper-bounded by Monte Carlo variance; shorter horizons reduce variance for a fixed sample budget; Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) acts as a regression-adjusted control variate, yielding tighter variance bounds in the large-sample regime; numerical experiments validate these properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify variance mechanisms in TD learning, compare with MC, and explore variance reduction via DAE; provide theoretical bounds and empirical validation.

Method: Theoretical analysis of TD variance in a phased, tabular setting; derive asymptotic upper bound against MC estimators; analyze effect of horizon length on variance for fixed sample budget; reinterpret Direct Advantage Estimation as a regression-adjusted control variate; perform controlled numerical experiments.

Result: TD variance is asymptotically bounded above by MC variance; shorter horizon updates reduce variance for a fixed sample budget; DAE yields a tighter variance bound than TD in the large-sample limit; numerical experiments illustrate the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Phased TD with tabular representations reduces variance via trajectory aggregation; DAE offers additional variance reduction through a regression-adjusted control variate; results are corroborated by carefully designed environments.

Abstract: We analyze the variance of temporal difference (TD) learning using the phased setting with tabular representation, and show that one of the mechanisms behind its ability to reduce variance is by effectively aggregating over a larger number of independent trajectories. Based on this insight, we demonstrate that (1) the variance of TD is asymptotically bounded from above by Monte Carlo (MC) estimators, and (2) shorter horizon updates incurs less variance for a fixed number of samples. Beyond TD, we show that Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE), a method for estimating the advantage function, can be seen as a type of regression-adjusted control variate, which achieves a tighter bound on the variance compared to TD in the large-sample limit. Finally, we numerically illustrate the behaviors of these estimators with carefully designed environments.

</details>


### [203] [Train, Retrieve, or Both? A Four-Arm Head-to-Head for Correct Statutory Citation on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20359)
*Ali Asaria,Tony Salomone,Deep Gandhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A four-arm evaluation shows that a small SFT+RAG hybrid on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct outperforms base and SFT-only or RAG-only setups for exact-match statutory citations (Ontario RTA), achieving 0.481 and eliminating hallucinations, but still short of a 0.70 target on a small real eval set; larger models or data do not outperform the cheap hybrid; results are preliminary.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether fine-tuning alone suffices for accurate legal citations or whether hybrid retrieval is necessary to avoid hallucinations and improve exact statutory section/subsection citations in self-represented legal aid contexts.

Method: Four-arm head-to-head evaluation on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: (1) base zero-shot, (2) LoRA SFT-only, (3) RAG-only, (4) SFT+RAG hybrid. Evaluation metric is exact-match citation (section+subsection) on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. Ground-truth is the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation.

Result: The SFT+RAG hybrid achieves the highest exact-match score of 0.481 with zero hallucinated citations. The base model cannot cite the RTA; SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and reduces hallucinations by construction. The hybrid matches or outperforms a pipeline built on larger, specialized retrieval models, and enlarging training data or model size does not improve performance. All results are preliminary on a small eval set with human verification pending.

Conclusion: Retrieval-augmented fine-tuning (SFT+RAG) provides a practical, low-cost path to strong statutory-citation performance, outperforming baselines and even larger retrieval pipelines in this setting, by reducing hallucinations and improving exact-match citations. The approach does not yet reach the aspirational 0.70 target and requires further evaluation on larger, established eval sets.

Abstract: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need to be pointed at the provision of law that actually governs a question, with a correct statutory citation. We study this task on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation, asking the operator's question empirically: is fine-tuning enough, or is hybrid retrieval needed? We run a four-arm head-to-head on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid), scored on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. The base model cannot cite the RTA and SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; and the SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Its edge comes from SFT making provision selection more robust to the higher-recall candidate sets that hurt zero-shot RAG. Notably, this cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats a pipeline built on bigger, specialized retrieval models (a larger embedder and a cross-encoder reranker), and a larger/improved training set does not help either: strong statutory-citation performance here does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact zeroes hallucination and clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. All results are on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set and are reported as preliminary.

</details>


### [204] [Judging to Improve: A De-biased VLM-as-3D-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20364)
*Ali Asaria,Tony Salomone,Deep Gandhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A cross-model VLM-based 3D judge is hardened and used to steer an open 3D generator (TRELLIS) for furniture without human labels. Training/evaluation separation, bias corrections, and failure-mode fixes are proposed; tested with lightweight PEFT on public data. Results show parity with a strong base but do not surpass it; the protocol is reusable but gains from lightweight adaptation are limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether a de-biased VLM judge for ranking single-image-to-3D mesh quality can be specialized to optimize a strong open generator for a specific asset class (furniture) without human supervision, and to identify failure modes that surface when the judge guides optimization.

Method: Train a separate training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B); apply position-bias correction; fix three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs); use quality-contrastive construction; evaluate with six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep; perform optimization and evaluation with lightweight parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) on public models/data; assess using an independent evaluator protocol.

Result: The methods largely match the strong base rather than surpass it; targeting (conditioning repair under severe degradation) achieves parity with the base (0.50) but no method reaches the ≥65% win-rate target; independent base samples show negligible learnable signal (0.94 order-flip rate); calibration evidence shows clear-gap win-rates in 0.83–1.0 range and base-vs-base around 0.5; win-rates are directional at n=8 objects; six adaptations across two input regimes and severity sweep indicate limited gains from cheap adaptation; the protocol is reusable.

Conclusion: Exceeding a strong public base with lightweight adaptation remains challenging; the optimized judge protocol exposes where improvements come from (conditioning repair) and demonstrates that signal must be engineered via quality-contrastive construction; findings suggest comprehensive optimization beyond lightweight PEFT and that the protocol is reusable for future work.

Abstract: A companion study established a de-biased, cross-model VLM-as-3D-judge that reliably ranks single-image-to-3D mesh quality where cheap geometry and CLIP proxies fall short. This paper asks: can that judge's preferences specialize a strong open generator, TRELLIS, on one asset class (furniture), cheaply and without human labels? Taking the judge from ranking to optimization is where the work lives. Pushing a VLM judge into the training and evaluation loop exposes failure modes ranking never triggered, so our contribution is an optimization-grade hardening of the judge: a training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) held distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B) to break circularity; position-bias correction; and fixes for three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, and reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs), with calibration evidence (clear-gap win-rate 0.83-1.0; base-vs-base ~0.5). Using this protocol as an independent evaluator, and working only from public models and data with lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation, we find our methods match the strong base rather than exceed it. Independent base samples carry essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate), so signal must be engineered by quality-contrastive construction. Across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep, the most targeted - conditioner repair under severe degradation - reaches parity (0.50) with the base, while no method clears the >=65% win-rate target. The result is mechanistic: clean inputs saturate the judge, flow-DIT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler, and conditioning repair is the locus that moves geometry. Win-rates are directional at n=8 objects. Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is itself informative: exceeding it needs more than lightweight PEFT on public data, and the judge protocol is reusable.

</details>


### [205] [CRAX: Fast Safe Reinforcement Learning Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20376)
*Tristan Tomilin,Mourad Boustani,Mickey Beurskens,Thiago D. Simão*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A scalable, accelerated safe RL benchmark CRAX using MJX on JAX, delivering ~100x speedups; evaluation across six safe RL methods shows trade-offs; curriculum learning and safety transfer help.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address computational bottlenecks of high-fidelity 3D safety RL benchmarks to enable large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping.

Method: CRAX is built on MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics with vectorized operations and hardware acceleration, providing six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks across three difficulty levels; six safe RL methods are evaluated to study performance-safety trade-offs.

Result: Achieves up to ~100x speedups over CPU-based safety benchmarks; no single RL method dominates across all tasks; curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer improve performance compared to direct training on harder tasks.

Conclusion: Acceleration via MJX-based CRAX enables scalable safe RL research; curriculum design and safety transfer enhance learning efficiency and performance, making it a versatile benchmark for safe RL.

Abstract: Safety is a core concern for deploying reinforcement learning (RL) agents in real-world domains such as robotics and autonomous driving. While benchmarks have been central to progress in RL, existing safety benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics remain computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping. To address this gap, we propose CRAX (Constrained RL Accelerated with JAX). Built on top of the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine with realistic 3D dynamics, CRAX leverages vectorized operations and hardware acceleration, yielding up to ~100x speedups over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. The benchmark features six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each spanning three difficulty levels. Evaluating six popular safe RL methods shows that no single approach dominates across all tasks, and reveals the trade-offs between performance and safety. We find that curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer can improve performance over direct training in harder settings.

</details>


### [206] [Towards Modality-imbalanced Federated Graph Learning: A Data Synthesis-based Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20382)
*Zhengyu Wu,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Zekai Chen,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedMGS is a federated multimodal graph learning framework that synthesizes missing modalities in latent space to handle modality imbalance, using an availability-aware encoder, prototype-guided synthesizer, and reliability-calibrated fusion; achieves up to 17.41% gains across four tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address practical deployment challenges in multimodal federated graph learning caused by client- and node-level modality imbalance; existing graph-agnostic or centralized methods are difficult to adapt to federated setups.

Method: Proposes FedMGS with three core components: (i) an availability-aware graph encoder that prevents missing modalities from contaminating local propagation; (ii) a prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer that establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities; (iii) a reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism that regulates the influence of recovered latent representations before the predictive readout.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four tasks shows FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines, with gains up to 17.41% and favorable efficiency–performance tradeoffs.

Conclusion: Formalizes modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis; the framework mitigates high variance from missing modalities and improves alignment with the original semantic distribution, demonstrating effectiveness across tasks.

Abstract: MultiModal Federated Graph Learning (MM-FGL) offers a natural collaborative training paradigm, but its practical deployment is challenged by two granularities of modality imbalance. Client-level imbalance occurs when certain clients lack entire modalities, while node-level imbalance occurs when individual nodes exhibit missing visual or textual attributes. While several relevant studies exist, our investigation reveals that they predominantly target graph-agnostic or centralized scenarios, rendering them difficult to adapt directly. To address these challenges, we formalize modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem. This paradigm recovers missing modal semantics directly within the representation space, thereby maximizing alignment with the original data's semantic distribution and mitigating the high variance induced by missing modalities. To this end, we propose FedMGS (Federated Modality-aware Graph Synthesis), which integrates three core components. The availability-aware graph encoder prevents missing modalities from contaminating local structural propagation. The prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities. The reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism regulates the impact of recovered latent representations prior to predictive readout. Extensive experiments on four tasks show that FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines with gains up to 17.41% with best efficiency-performance tradeoff.

</details>


### [207] [The Significance of Style Diversity in Annotation-Free Synthetic Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20400)
*Zahra Abbasiantaeb,Zeno Belligoli,Omar Essam,Mohammad Aliannejadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised synthetic data generation for intent classification using only intent definitions, with two attribute types for topic/style and two post-hoc stylizers (Univ, Exam). An LLM-based filter ensures quality. Results reach up to 93.3% of fully supervised performance; style diversity is key and more impactful than topic diversity; generation-time inclusion of style attributes outperforms post-hoc styling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial settings often lack labeled seed data for intent classification. A fully annotation-free pipeline would enable rapid data generation aligned with defined intents, reducing labeling costs and time.

Method: Generate synthetic dialogues using two sets of topic and style attributes to boost diversity. Apply two post-hoc stylization models, Univ and Exam, to alter utterance style. Use an LLM as a judge to filter synthetic data. Evaluate on both industrial and public datasets.

Result: Achieves up to 93.3% of the performance of models trained on human-annotated data. Style diversity more critical than topic diversity; incorporating style attributes during generation is more effective than post-hoc adaptation.

Conclusion: Annotation-free synthetic data can approach fully supervised performance for intent classification when guided by deliberate style and topic diversification and LLM-based quality control; prioritizing style during generation yields the largest gains.

Abstract: Generating high-utility synthetic data for intent classification typically requires human-annotated seed data, which is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings. In this paper, we propose a framework for synthetic dialogue generation that works entirely without human-annotated data, relying solely on intent definitions. Our proposed dialogue generation framework utilizes two different types of topic and style attributes to improve data diversity. Also, we propose two novel post-hoc stylization models called Univ and Exam to transform synthetic LLM-generated utterances into more varied, human-like linguistic styles. To enhance data quality, we utilize an LLM-as-a-judge filtering process. Experimental results on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves up to 93.3% of the performance obtained using human-annotated training data. Crucially, the findings reveal that style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, as it prevents models from learning spurious stylistic correlations. Furthermore, the study shows that incorporating style attributes during the generation process is more effective than post-hoc style adaptation.

</details>


### [208] [Direct Advantage Estimation for Scalable and Sample-efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20411)
*Hsiao-Ru Pan,Bernhard Schölkopf*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extends Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) to partially observable settings and reduces computational overhead via discrete latent dynamics models; evaluated on ALE, showing scalable sample efficiency with larger function approximators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DAE improves sample efficiency but requires full observability and explicit transition-modeling, which is impractical for high-dimensional observations; relaxing observability and reducing computation can broaden applicability.

Method: Theoretical extension of DAE to partially observable domains with minimal modifications; introduce discrete latent dynamics models to efficiently approximate transition probabilities; empirical evaluation on the Arcade Learning Environment.

Result: DAE scales with the capacity of the function approximator while maintaining high sample efficiency in ALE.

Conclusion: The approach addresses the key limitations of DAE—observability and computational overhead—demonstrating practical viability for complex RL tasks such as those in ALE.

Abstract: Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) has been shown to improve the sample efficiency of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. However, its reliance on full environment observability limits its applicability in realistic settings, and its requirement to model transition probabilities incurs substantial computational overhead for high-dimensional observations. In the present work, we address both limitations. First, we extend the theoretical framework of DAE to partially observable domains with minimal modifications. Second, we reduce its computational complexity by introducing discrete latent dynamics models that efficiently approximate transition probabilities. We evaluate our approach on the Arcade Learning Environment and find that DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.

</details>


### [209] [Pseudo-Feature Padding: A Lightweight Defense Against False Data Injection in Power Grids](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20415)
*Farhin Farhad Riya,Shahinul Hoque,Yingyuan Yang,Jinyuan Sun,Kevin Tomsovic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight, model-agnostic padding defense for CPS DNNs against FDIA that uses pseudo-feature padding based on input statistics to increase input dimensionality in a randomized, data-aware way, improving robustness with minimal performance impact; evaluated on IEEE 14/30/118/300-bus systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DNNs in cyber-physical systems are vulnerable to false data injection attacks; conventional defenses may be ineffective given CPS-specific constraints and attack strategies, necessitating a robust, deployable defense that preserves performance.

Method: Introduce an additional input padding layer that injects pseudo-feature values derived from the input distribution to pad samples, creating a randomized, data-aware, higher-dimensional input. The method is model-agnostic and requires no changes to the core architecture, aiming to hinder adversaries by nontransferable perturbations and unpredictable padding structure.

Result: Adversarial experiments show significantly improved robustness against FDIA with negligible accuracy loss on state estimation tasks across IEEE 14/30/118/300-bus systems, effectively mitigating attacks that bypass traditional defenses.

Conclusion: The proposed padding-based defense is lightweight, deployable in real CPS settings, and can enhance DNN robustness to FDIA without modifying the underlying model, though evaluation against adaptive adversaries and broader CPS domains would further validate generality.

Abstract: Deep Neural Networks DNNs have achieved remarkable accuracy in various tasks including their application in CyberPhysical Systems CPS for detecting False Data Injection Attacks FDIA during critical operations However the unique infrastructure of CPS makes DNNs vulnerable to exploitation by attackers aiming to evade detection Additionally the distinct nature of CPS presents challenges for conventional defense mechanisms against FDIA This paper proposes an innovative defense framework that strengthens DNNs against such attacks by introducing an additional input layer that performs padding in the input samples using pseudofeature values derived from the inputs statistical distribution This padding increases the input dimensionality in a randomized and dataaware manner making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible due to the nontransferable nature of crafted perturbations and the unpredictability of the padded structure Our method is lightweight modelagnostic and requires no modifications to the core architecture making it highly deployable in realworld CPS settings We evaluated our framework on critical power grid applications such as state estimation using the IEEE 14bus 30bus 118bus and 300bus systems Experiments under adversarial settings demonstrate that our padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible impact on performance and effectively mitigates attacks that would otherwise bypass conventional defenses

</details>


### [210] [On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20416)
*José A. Chávez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Time-agnostic diffusion models: removing explicit timestep embeddings can still achieve competitive generative performance and may even surpass conditioned models under certain assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the necessity of temporal conditioning in diffusion models and investigate whether the global minimizer of the training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning, aiming for efficiency and simpler architectures.

Method: Theoretical analysis outlining conditions under which a minimizer exists without timestep conditioning; extensive empirical ablations on CelebA and CIFAR-10 comparing U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures, with and without timestep embeddings; evaluation on metrics such as FID, precision, and recall.

Result: Time-agnostic models retain structural fidelity and can even outperform conditioned counterparts in key metrics (e.g., FID, precision, recall); models appear to implicitly infer noise scales from corrupted input, reducing reliance on explicit temporal signals.

Conclusion: Explicit temporal conditioning may be redundant under certain assumptions; results motivate more efficient, structurally centered generative architectures and suggest a shift away from mandatory timestep embeddings in diffusion modeling.

Abstract: Diffusion models rely heavily on explicit timestep embeddings to modulate the denoising process across various noise scales. In this work, we challenge the necessity of these temporal signals by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures. Beyond empirical evidence, we provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that, under certain conditions, the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning. Our findings reveal a surprising robustness when timestep embeddings are completely removed. Extensive ablation studies on the CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets show that these time-agnostic models can maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass their conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics, including FID, precision, and recall. Our analysis suggests these architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions, rendering explicit temporal conditioning redundant. This study challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient and structurally focused generative architectures.

</details>


### [211] [Neural network surrogates with uncertainty quantification for inverse problems in partial differential equations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20417)
*Christian Jimenez-Beltran,Aretha L. Teckentrup,Antonio Vergari,Konstantinos C. Zygalakis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neural-network surrogate DeepGaLA for differential equations enables uncertainty-aware, scalable Bayesian inference for inverse problems; uses a NN surrogate with uncertainty to match GP performance while scaling to high dimensions and can enforce DE constraints; diagnostic via delayed-acceptance MCMC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian inverse problems with complex forward models are computationally expensive; need accurate, uncertainty-aware surrogates that maintain reliability, especially in high dimensions and when data are limited.

Method: Develop DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for DE solvers with uncertainty quantification; evaluate surrogate-induced posteriors with a short delayed-acceptance MCMC diagnostic; compare to Gaussian-process surrogates; extend to nonlinear DE constraints.

Result: Surrogate achieves forward-model accuracy comparable to Gaussian-process surrogates; scales more efficiently with parameter dimension; can incorporate DE constraints; reduces overconfidence in limited-data regimes; enables scalable, reliable Bayesian inference.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates like DeepGaLA can provide scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems, matching GP accuracy while improving dimensional scalability and accommodating differential-equation constraints.

Abstract: Inverse problems for differential equations arise throughout science and engineering, where one seeks to infer unknown model parameters from noisy or incomplete observations. Traditional numerical methods for these problems are often computationally expensive, particularly in Bayesian settings where evaluating the likelihood becomes costly for complex forward models and high-dimensional parameter spaces. To address this challenge, we introduce DeepGaLA, a neural-network surrogate for differential equation solvers that provides uncertainty-aware predictions, reducing overconfident inference when training data are limited. To evaluate the fidelity of the surrogate-induced posterior approximations in practice, we show that a short run of delayed-acceptance Markov chain Monte Carlo can serve as an effective diagnostic. Across a range of numerical experiments, DeepGaLA delivers forward-model approximations with accuracy comparable to established Gaussian-process surrogates, while better maintaining efficiency as parameter dimension grows. Moreover, it can incorporate differential-equation constraints, including in nonlinear settings. Overall, these results indicate that uncertainty-quantified neural surrogates can enable scalable and reliable Bayesian inference for inverse problems in complex systems.

</details>


### [212] [Sparsity, Superposition, and Forgetting: A Mechanistic Study of Representation Retention in Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20431)
*Jan Wasilewski,Jędrzej Kozal,Michał Woźniak,Bartosz Krawczyk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A controllable toy-world framework isolates forgetting mechanisms in continual learning by using a generator-separator pipeline with ground-truth latent features, tunable sparsity/overlap, and measurable metrics. It analyzes retention dynamics with Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) and effective task rank, yielding three nuanced findings about superposition, sparsity, and capacity allocation, and offering falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostics for CL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In CL, forgetting arises from multiple interacting factors that are hard to disentangle in real data. A synthetic, controllable environment with known latent structure enables precise measurement of how representation strength, overlap (superposition), and task-level capacity allocation drive forgetting.

Method: Construct toy-world via generator-separator pipeline, define ground-truth latent features, create tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, define representations and measures (representation strength, superposition). Track retention dynamics by fitting sparse dynamical models (SINDy) between retention, superposition, and exposure history. Characterize task-level capacity usage via effective rank. Conduct controlled experiments to assess interplay among the factors.

Result: Three takeaways: (1) Superposition grows over time with boundary dips, indicating boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher sparsity increases superposition but does not automatically cause forgetting; when representation strength remains high, forgetting can be mitigated despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank increases with sparsity, implying broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Collectively, overlap interacts with strength and capacity allocation; the findings refine the view that more overlap directly causes forgetting.

Conclusion: The toy framework provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL, enabling precise investigation of how representation strength, sparsity, and capacity allocation shape forgetting. It complements real-data studies and suggests diagnostic metrics for CL systems.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) systems often forget previously acquired knowledge, yet the mechanisms driving forgetting remain hard to isolate in practice because real datasets entangle many factors. We present a controlled, toy-world framework that makes these mechanisms observable and testable. Using a synthetic generator-separator pipeline, we define ground-truth latent features, build tasks with tunable sparsity and overlap, and introduce measurable quantities for representation strength and superposition (directional overlap among features). We then study retention dynamics-the temporal change of representation strength by fitting sparse dynamical relations (via SINDy) between retention, superposition, and exposure history. A complementary task-level analysis based on effective rank characterizes how representational capacity is allocated across tasks. Our controlled experiments yield three takeaways. (1) Superposition tends to increase over time with transient dips at task boundaries, suggesting boundary-specific interference rather than steady drift. (2) Higher feature sparsity induces more superposition yet does not inevitably cause forgetting; when representations remain strong, forgetting can be reduced despite overlap. (3) Task-level effective rank grows with sparsity, indicating broader capacity usage under sparse regimes. Together, these results nuance the common intuition that more superposition leads to more forgetting by showing that overlap interacts with representation strength and capacity allocation. Our toy analysis provides falsifiable hypotheses and diagnostic tools for CL.

</details>


### [213] [Evolutionary Two-Stage Hyperparameter Optimization Strategies for Physics-Informed Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20442)
*Fedor Buzaev,Dmitry Efremenko,Egor Bugaev,Andrei Ermakov,Denis Derkach,Daria Pugacheva,Fedor Ratnikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-stage evolutionary optimization framework enhances PINN robustness and accuracy under fixed computational budgets by screening hyperparameter configurations with low-fidelity trials and refining the best candidates with full gradient-based training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs exhibit unstable convergence, plateaus, and sensitivity to hyperparameters due to the multi-term, non-convex physics-informed loss. A robust outer-loop search is needed to reliably identify good configurations under budget constraints.

Method: An evolutionary algorithm-based outer loop is used to optimize PINN hyperparameters. Stage 1 performs low-fidelity, truncated-epoch evaluations to rapidly screen configurations. Stage 2 fully trains the top candidates with standard gradient-based optimization to refine solutions.

Result: Across Advection, Klein-Gordon, and Helmholtz problems, the two-stage approach consistently outperforms standard PINN training, achieving significantly lower mean error within the same computational budget.

Conclusion: A two-stage evolutionary hyperparameter search provides robust PINN training under resource limits by efficiently exploring configurations and allocating full training to the most promising candidates.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) solve Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into neural network training. However, their performance suffers from unstable convergence, training plateaus, and strong sensitivity to architectural and optimization hyperparameters due to the highly non-convex and multi-term structure of the physics-informed loss. In this setting, the outer-loop hyperparameter search is a noisy and black-box optimization problem over heterogeneous parameters, where classical local or gradient-based strategies are easily trapped in suboptimal regions. Evolutionary algorithms, with their population-based exploration and ability to handle mixed, non-differentiable search spaces, provide a more robust mechanism for discovering promising configurations. We propose and investigate a two-stage approach based on evolutionary algorithms that combines exploration and exploitation parts of PINNs training to improve solution accuracy and robustness under fixed computational budgets. In the first stage, we perform low-fidelity training runs with truncated epochs to rapidly screen candidate configurations, treating hyperparameter selection as a black-box outer-loop problem. In the second stage, only the most promising candidates are fully trained with standard gradient-based optimizers to refine the solution. Evaluated on three popular problems, namely Advection, Klein-Gordon and Helmholtz equations, our method consistently outperforms standard training and achieves significantly lower mean error within constrained computational resources.

</details>


### [214] [Data Bias Mitigation under Coverage Constraints & The Price of Fairness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20461)
*Bruno Scarone,Alfredo Viola,Renée J. Miller*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints to ensure representation across sensitive and intersectional groups; uses an ILP to optimize mitigation strategies; analyzes price of fairness and data modification costs; demonstrates accuracy preservation on public datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address discriminatory outcomes at the intersection of sensitive attributes due to underrepresentation and lack of principled bias measures; pursue data-efficient, compliant fairness with explicit coverage constraints.

Method: Extend a bias mitigation framework with coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation of all groups and intersectional subgroups; tolerate small approximation bias for data efficiency; formulate as an integer linear program to optimize mitigation strategies; quantify price of fairness (minimum data modification) as a function of fairness tolerance; evaluate on public datasets with multiple classifiers.

Result: Mitigation preserves predictive accuracy across classifiers; coverage constraints are essential to maintain downstream performance; provides a way to quantify data modification costs and fairness trade-offs.

Conclusion: Coverage-based, data-efficient bias mitigation with ILP optimization offers practical intersectional fairness, aligning with regulatory data governance needs and enabling informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification costs.

Abstract: Machine learning models have been shown to exhibit discriminatory outcomes or degraded performance for individuals at the intersection of multiple sensitive attributes, such as race and gender. This stems in part from two interrelated challenges: the lack of principled measures for quantifying bias (potentially intersectional), and insufficient representation of intersectional subgroups in training data. We extend a recent bias mitigation framework to incorporate coverage constraints that enforce sufficient representation across groups, including intersectional subgroups. Since achieving exactly zero bias for all groups may not be data efficient (meaning it may require large amounts of data), our solution trades small approximation errors in bias for greater data efficiency while satisfying coverage constraints. We also formulate bias mitigation as an integer linear program that optimizes over all mitigation strategies, and characterize the price of fairness, the minimum data modification cost, as a function of fairness tolerance. This is essential both for legal compliance, where regulations may mandate specific fairness thresholds, and for data governance, enabling practitioners to make informed trade-offs between bias reduction and data modification (particularly, data purchasing) costs. We evaluate our techniques on publicly available datasets, demonstrating that bias mitigation via our framework preserves predictive accuracy across multiple classifiers, and that coverage constraints, while motivated by statistical considerations, are essential for preserving downstream ML performance.

</details>


### [215] [Agentic Symbolic Search: Characterizing PDEs Beyond Hand-crafted Expressions, Meshes, and Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20467)
*Zongmin Yu,Liu Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASYS is a prior-guided, differentiable symbolic search framework that combines theory-driven inductive biases with gradient optimization to produce interpretable analytical approximations of PDE solutions, recovering known forms and discovering new ones across various problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between handcrafted PDE analysis and numerical/ML approaches by enabling automated discovery of analytical PDE structures through an inductive-bias-guided search.

Method: Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS) translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. It uses evolutionary search to refine mathematical forms and gradient-based optimization to fit continuous parameters, implementing an automated inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression.

Result: Across five problems (bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, free-boundary focusing), ASYS yields interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel blow-up, producing analytical approximations where no closed-form existed and recovering known analytical forms when available.

Conclusion: ASYS points to a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions that combines analytical structure discovery with automated optimization, offering interpretable analytical forms beyond traditional handcrafted, mesh-based, or neural-network solvers.

Abstract: Mathematicians understand a PDE solution through mathematical structures rather than tables of computed values. Historically, this has been the product of mathematical analysis, carried out by hand for each problem individually. Neither numerical simulation nor neural networks produce those structures directly. We propose Agentic Symbolic Search (ASYS), a prior-guided framework in which an agent translates PDE theory, public problem constraints, and accumulated search experience into testable differentiable symbolic programs. The mathematical forms are refined under evolutionary search, while their continuous parameters are fit by gradient-based optimization. This makes the search an automated form of inductive-bias injection rather than blind symbolic regression. For problems with known analytical forms, ASYS recovers these forms naturally; for other problems, ASYS constructs analytical approximations which can guide mathematicians toward further analysis. In our experiments, across five problems spanning bounded dynamics, finite-time blow-up, and free-boundary focusing, ASYS produces interpretable representations, including a geometric interface formula for Allen-Cahn 2D dynamics and a nine-parameter contraction law for Keller-Segel chemotactic blow-up, in settings where no closed-form description was previously available. ASYS shows the possibility of a new paradigm for characterizing PDE solutions, beyond handcrafted analytical solutions, mesh-based numerical solutions, and neural network approximations.

</details>


### [216] [UltraQuant: 4-bit KV Caching for Context-Heavy Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20474)
*Inesh Chakrabarti,David Limpus,Aditi Ghai Rana,Bowen Bao,Spandan Tiwari,Thiago Crepaldi,Ashish Sirasao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 4-bit KV-cache with rotation and codebook quantization achieves large latency/throughput gains for long-context, multi-turn agents on GPUs; outperforms FP8 KV baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Context-heavy agent workloads place unusual pressure on KV caches due to long reusable prefixes and concurrency; need compact, robust KV representations that preserve quality while boosting residency and throughput.

Method: Frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round workloads; adopt robust design choices (asymmetric K/V, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, block-scale variants); use TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as quality anchors; deploy with vLLM FP8 KV caching; provide GPU-optimized implementations (decode-attention kernels, UltraQuant FP4 path, FP8/FP4 paths, UE8M0 scales, MFMA on CDNA4).

Result: UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in cache-pressured late rounds and 2.3x across all rounds; output throughput increases by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

Conclusion: 4-bit KV caching, with targeted robustness and GPU-specific optimizations, yields significant latency and throughput improvements for long-context, multi-turn agent workloads; the combination of design choices and deployment optimizations is critical to achieving the reported gains.

Abstract: Context-heavy agents place unusual pressure on the key-value (KV) cache: long prefixes are reused across many short turns, while concurrency determines whether the serving system can keep GPUs utilized. We study 4-bit KV-cache compression for this setting, using TurboQuant-style rotation and codebook quantization as a quality anchor and vLLM FP8 KV caching as the deployment anchor. We report three contributions. First, we frame 4-bit KV caching around multi-round agent workloads where task quality, cache residency, and serving throughput must be measured jointly. Second, we describe the practical design choices needed to make the 4-bit path robust, including asymmetric K/V treatment, Walsh-Hadamard rotation, QJL removal, and block-scale variants. Third, we present serving optimizations on AMD GPUs, including optimized decode-attention kernels and UltraQuant, an FP4 approximation path that uses FP8 queries, FP4 KV tensors, UE8M0 group scales, and native scaled-MFMA support on CDNA4. On a long-context, multi-turn agentic workload, UltraQuant cuts P50 time-to-first-token by 3.47x in the cache-pressured late rounds (2.3x across all rounds) and raises output throughput by 1.63x over the FP8 KV baseline.

</details>


### [217] [Marginal Advantage Accumulation for Memory-Driven Agent Self-Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20475)
*Mingyu Yang,Keye Zheng,Congchao Cheng,Yujie Liu,Xingkang Lu,Fan Jiang,Yefei Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAA: a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation framework for batch-style trace distillation that uses EMA-based signed evidence and semantic identity merging to align and compare signals across batches; yields strong empirical performance and major efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In batch-style trace distillation, feedback for a memory operation can be contradictory across batches. There's no cross-batch evidence accumulation to separate stable signals from noise.

Method: Formalizes two structural conditions: alignability and comparability. Proposes Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA): (1) construct differential signals for cross-batch comparability; (2) accumulate signed evidence per operation via EMA; (3) ensure cross-batch traceability via semantic identity merging; implemented as a post-processing architecture.

Result: Achieves best results in 14/16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models; outperforms batch-level distillation baselines; matches or surpasses online alternatives in most settings; reduces optimization-phase token consumption by ~75%.

Conclusion: MAA provides a principled cross-batch evidence accumulation mechanism for operation-level decisions in batch-style distillation, delivering strong empirical performance and efficiency gains.

Abstract: In batch-style trace distillation, the same memory operation may receive contradictory feedback across different batches. Existing methods lack a cross-batch, operation-level evidence accumulation mechanism, making it impossible to distinguish stably effective operations from accidental hits. This paper formalizes the requirement as two structural conditions, alignability and comparability, and proposes Marginal Advantage Accumulation (MAA). MAA constructs differential signals to make them comparable across batches, accumulates signed evidence per operation via EMA, and ensures cross-batch traceability through semantic identity merging. As a post-processing architecture, MAA achieves the best results in 14 out of 16 settings across 4 benchmarks and 4 target models, consistently outperforming existing batch-level distillation baselines and matching or surpassing online alternatives in most settings, while reducing optimization-phase token consumption by approximately 75%.

</details>


### [218] [Contagion Networks: Evaluator Bias Propagation in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20493)
*Zewen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Contagion Networks quantify how evaluator biases propagate among interacting LLM agents, revealing cross-agent contagion, regime boundaries via spectral radius, and mitigation through larger evaluator committees; the framework is open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the propagation of evaluator bias in multi-agent LLM systems, which can degrade decision quality and coherence across networks of agents.

Method: Controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based); compute Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_N; analyze gamma values; identify propagation regimes via the spectral radius rho(Gamma_N); compare homogeneous-model vs cross-model contagion; vary evaluator committee size k from 1 to 3 and quantify mitigation.

Result: Found cross-agent contagion with Gamma_N values in the range [0.157, 0.352]; contagion regimes determined by spectral radius rho(Gamma_N); homogeneous-model agents exhibit 3–5× weaker contagion than cross-model baselines reported in MM-EPC (gamma ~0.85–1.3); increasing committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%; released an open-source Contagion Network experimental framework.

Conclusion: Evaluator biases spread through interacting LLM agents in a measurable, regime-dependent manner; model heterogeneity intensifies contagion;Mitigation via larger evaluator committees is effective; framework provides a reproducible toolkit for measurement and experimentation.

Abstract: When large language models serve as evaluators in multi-agent systems, their systematic evaluation biases propagate through the agent network. We introduce Contagion Networks, a formal framework for measuring how evaluator biases spread across interacting LLM agents. In a controlled 3-agent experiment using DeepSeek-chat with three distinct evaluator bias profiles (structured, balanced, evidence-based), we measure the Cross-Agent Contagion Matrix Gamma_3 and find that evaluator biases consistently propagate between agents (gamma in [0.157, 0.352]), even within the same underlying model. We identify three propagation regimes governed by the spectral radius rho(Gamma_N), and demonstrate that homogeneous-model agents produce contagion coefficients 3-5x weaker than cross-model coefficients observed in prior work (MM-EPC: gamma approx 0.85-1.3), placing them in the suppression regime. We show that increasing evaluator committee size from k=1 to k=3 reduces effective contagion by 72.4%, providing an actionable mitigation strategy. We release the open-source Contagion Network experimental framework.

</details>


### [219] [Execution-State Capsules: Graph-Bound Execution-State Checkpoint and Restore for Low-Latency, Small-Batch, On-Device Physical-AI Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20537)
*Liang Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Execution-state capsules enable graph-bound, full-state checkpoint/restore for on-device LLM serving, yielding sub-millisecond restores and large speedups for token-by-token generation, complementing KV caches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve ultra-low latency on-device LLM serving under tight budgets, addressing the limitation that KV caches only capture a single fragment of execution state and are unsuitable for frequent branch/interrupt patterns in interactive agents.

Method: Introduce execution-state capsules and the FlashRT runtime; a graph-bound checkpoint/restore mechanism that snapshots/restores/forks of the entire execution boundary across a static buffer plan; supports restoring KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, metadata; backend runs captured graph plans with no block-table indirection; evaluates on RTX 5090, Jetson AGX Thor, DGX Spark.

Result: Capsules restore is byte-exact at stored state; token-identical under greedy decoding; KV-only ablation shows recurrent state is load-bearing; GPU-resident snapshot/restore is sub-millisecond; TTFT speedup grows from 3.9x (2k tokens) to 27x (16k tokens); correctness extends to other platforms.

Conclusion: Capsules offer a complementary latency-first path for explicit execution-state reuse, not a replacement for high-throughput KV caches; applicable across edge and data-center hardware.

Abstract: Mainstream LLM serving systems reuse prefix work mainly through paged or radix key-value (KV) caches. This is highly effective for high-throughput, high-concurrency serving, but it manages only one positional fragment of execution state: the KV cache. We study the opposite regime: low-latency, small-batch, on-device physical-AI serving, where interactive LLM agents, speech systems, and robot policies repeatedly branch, reset, interrupt, and re-enter under tight responsiveness budgets. We introduce execution-state capsules, a graph-bound checkpoint and restore mechanism for the complete restorable state at a committed boundary. FlashRT is a white-box, backend-facing kernel runtime whose evaluated NVIDIA CUDA backend runs captured graph plans over contiguous static buffers with no block-table indirection. Because the live state is a closed set of named buffers, a capsule can snapshot, restore, fork, or roll back the whole execution boundary, including KV, recurrent state, convolution state, MTP state, and metadata. This moves reuse from token-addressed KV fragments to graph-bound execution-state boundaries. On an RTX 5090, capsule restore is byte-exact at the stored-state level and token-identical under greedy decode. A KV-only ablation diverges, showing that recurrent state is load-bearing. GPU-resident snapshot and restore are sub-millisecond, and TTFT speedup over cold prefill grows from 3.9x at 2k tokens to 27x at 16k tokens. On Jetson AGX Thor and DGX Spark, the same correctness and structural properties hold. Capsules are not a replacement for high-throughput KV-cache serving; they define a complementary latency-first serving point for explicit execution-state reuse.

</details>


### [220] [Multi-Task Bayesian In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20538)
*Qingyang Zhu,Eric Karl Oermann,Kyunghyun Cho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A transformer-based multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that treats prior information as an in-context prefix, enabling adaptation across families of priors. It achieves oracle-level performance with much faster inference, robust to distribution shift and high-dimensional latent structures, demonstrated on spatiotemporal temperature data; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bayesian predictive inference is principled for uncertainty quantification but exact inference is often intractable; current amortized approaches either depend on the training prior's support or lack robustness to new priors under distribution shift.

Method: Represent prior information as a prefix of in-context data. Train a transformer on sequences of prior and target tasks to learn how to adapt predictions across families of priors, enabling amortized hierarchical Bayesian inference across task distributions.

Result: The approach matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster across increasingly difficult evaluations, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and high-dimensional latent structures.

Conclusion: Explicitly representing priors in-context enables robust adaptation to new priors at test time, improving robustness under distribution shift and practical applicability, as demonstrated on a real-world spatiotemporal benchmark; code provided.

Abstract: Bayesian predictive inference provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification, data efficiency, and robust generalization. However, exact inference is often intractable, and scalable approximations may remain computationally expensive or require restrictive modeling assumptions that degrade predictive performance. Prior-Data Fitted and in-context models have recently emerged as an amortized alternative by learning to map datasets directly to predictive distributions, but existing approaches are tightly coupled to the support of the training prior and lack explicit mechanisms for adapting to new priors at test time, resulting in limited robustness under distribution shift. We introduce a multi-task in-context learning framework for amortized hierarchical Bayesian predictive inference that explicitly represents prior information as a prefix of in-context datasets. A transformer trained on sequences of prior and target tasks learns to adapt its predictions across families of priors. On a suite of evaluations with increasing difficulty, including out-of-meta-distribution priors and priors with high-dimensional latent structures, our method matches oracle Bayesian predictors while being orders of magnitude faster. We further demonstrate its practical relevance on a real-world spatiotemporal temperature prediction benchmark. Code is available at https://github.com/martianmartina/multi-task-bayesian-icl/.

</details>


### [221] [Predictability as a Fine-Grained Measure for Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20546)
*Linda Lu,Karthik Sridharan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fine-grained privacy metric, 'predictability', quantifies leakage given an attacker model and compromised data; it generalizes beyond worst-case DP, is incomparable with DP in general, but implies mutual-information DP in a stringent worst-case scenario; it uses a GMM-based analysis for asymptotic predictability with stationary ergodic mixing data and yields a predictability-calibrated ERM perturbation scheme, serving as a complementary privacy tool to DP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DP is a worst-case guarantee that can be overly conservative for many realistic attack models. There is a need for a finer-grained, attacker-model-aware privacy metric that can provide calibrated privacy-utility tradeoffs for specific sensitive information and data-generating processes.

Method: Define predictability as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information about unknown individuals after observing the algorithm's output, given compromised data generated by a stochastic process. Compare to DP, establishing incomparability in general. Prove that in the extreme worst-case (all but one compromised, all binary queries sensitive), predictability implies mutual-information DP. Develop a general framework using the generalized method of moments (GMM) to analyze asymptotic predictability for stationary, ergodic, mixing processes. Derive a predictability-calibrated output-perturbation scheme for empirical risk minimization (ERM). Position the framework as complementary to DP and usable alongside it.

Result: Establishes a fine-grained privacy metric (predictability) that captures leakage under specific attacker knowledge and compromised data, shows its general incomparability with DP and its conditional implication to mutual-information DP in a strong worst-case, provides a GMM-based asymptotic analysis for predictability with mixing processes, and yields a calibrated perturbation method for ERM that leverages the predictability framework.

Conclusion: Predictability offers a tailored privacy metric that complements DP by enabling fine-grained control over leakage for particular sensitive information and attacker models; it supports a perturbation mechanism for ERM and can be used alongside DP to achieve nuanced privacy-utility tradeoffs.

Abstract: Differential privacy (DP) ensures rigorous individual-level privacy guarantees against even the most knowledgeable attackers, but its worst-case nature can impose a costly privacy-accuracy tradeoff. We introduce privacy via predictability, a fine-grained framework that explicitly incorporates the attacker's core knowledge, a compromised portion of the dataset generated by a stochastic process, and a specified family of queries. Predictability measures privacy leakage as the incremental gain in an attacker's ability to predict sensitive information about unknown individuals after observing the algorithm's output, beyond what can already be inferred from the compromised data. We show that predictability and DP are generally incomparable: each can be small while the other is large. However, in the worst-case regime where all but one individual is compromised, and all binary queries are considered sensitive, predictability implies mutual-information DP. More generally, predictability provides a finer-grained privacy metric tailored to specific sensitive information and specific attacker models. We introduce a general framework, using the generalized method of moments (GMM), to analyze asymptotic predictability when the compromised data is generated by a stationary, ergodic, mixing process. Using this analysis, we derive a predictability-calibrated output perturbation scheme for ERM. Our approach is complementary to DP and can be used alongside DP to provide fine-grained privacy control.

</details>


### [222] [Optimal Deterministic Multicalibration and Omniprediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20557)
*Georgy Noarov,Aaron Roth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deterministic multicalibration achieves minimax-optimal sample complexity ~ũ(ε^{-3}) without randomness, and extends to outcome indistinguishability, yielding deterministic omnipredictors/panpredictors with optimal rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Before this work, minimax-optimal ε-multicalibration required randomized predictors; deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. The open problem asked whether randomness is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration.

Method: Introduce a minimax-optimal deterministic multicalibration algorithm and generalize it to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) for finite or finitely covered test collections; derive deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity.

Result: The proposed deterministic algorithm achieves the minimax-optimal rate ~ũ(ε^{-3}) for ε-multicalibration and, when extended, deterministic predictors satisfy OI and support deterministic omnipredictors/panpredictors with optimal sample complexity.

Conclusion: Randomization is not necessary for achieving optimal multicalibration sample complexity; deterministic methods attain the same minimax-optimal rates and extend to OI/omnipredictor/panpredictor settings, resolving open questions in the literature.

Abstract: A model is multicalibrated on a collection of group weights $G$ if it is calibrated -- i.e. unbiased even conditional on its prediction -- not just overall, but also after reweighting contexts by each $g \in G$. It is a useful property for many downstream applications and is a basic desideratum of trustworthy machine learning. Before this work, all predictors known to attain the minimax-optimal $\widetilde O(\varepsilon^{-3})$ sample complexity rate for $\varepsilon$-multicalibration were randomized, while deterministic predictors were known only with substantially worse sample complexity. Whether randomization is necessary for optimal sample complexity in multicalibration was explicitly asked by [CLNR26] and implicitly in several prior works.
  We resolve this open problem by giving a minimax-optimal multicalibration algorithm that outputs a deterministic predictor. We then generalize the algorithm to produce optimal deterministic predictors that satisfy outcome indistinguishability (OI) with respect to finite or finitely covered collections of tests. As an application, this also gives deterministic omnipredictors and panpredictors with optimal sample complexity, resolving open problems posed by [OKK25] and [BHHLZ25].

</details>


### [223] [How Transparent is DiffusionGemma?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20560)
*Joshua Engels,Callum McDougall,Bilal Chughtai,Janos Kramar,Senthoran Rajamanoharan,Cindy Wu,Arthur Conmy,Asic Q Chen,Jean Tarbouriech,Min Ma,Brendan O'Donoghue,João Gabriel Lopes de Oliveira,Rohin Shah,Neel Nanda*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DiffusionGemma's latent computation initially lowers variable transparency, but an interpretable token bottleneck recovers nearly Gemma 4's level; algorithmic transparency remains harder, with diffusion-specific interpretability phenomena; monitorability is comparable to Gemma 4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transparency of LLM reasoning is crucial for understanding decisions, preventing misuse/misalignment, and debugging surprising behavior. Diffusion-based models compute in continuous latent spaces, raising questions about how to assess and compare transparency with autoregressive models.

Method: Decompose transparency into variable and algorithmic components. Quantify opaque serial depth; map information flow through an interpretable token bottleneck without sacrificing performance; conduct interpretability case studies to identify diffusion-specific phenomena; assess monitorability and compare to Gemma 4.

Result: Naive variable transparency in DiffusionGemma appears poor due to high opaque serial depth (≈28.6x Gemma 4). Introducing an interpretable token bottleneck aligns information flow with the downstream outputs and reduces opaque depth to ≈1.1x Gemma 4, with no performance loss. Algorithmic transparency remains harder for diffusion models because token predictions at each denoising step can change, enabling distributed algorithms. Early interpretability studies reveal diffusion-specific effects such as non-chronological reasoning, token/sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. DiffusionGemma's monitorability is found to be similar to Gemma 4.

Conclusion: Variable transparency in diffusion models can be substantially recovered by treating intermediate states as interpretable and constraining information flow with a bottleneck, bringing it close to autoregressive baselines. Algorithmic transparency remains the principal challenge for diffusion models. Nonetheless, diffusion models can be monitorable to a degree comparable with autoregressive models, and initial diffusion-specific interpretability phenomena warrant deeper study.

Abstract: LLM reasoning transparency is a critical affordance for understanding model decisions, mitigating misuse and misalignment, and debugging surprising model behaviors. However, DiffusionGemma performs a larger fraction of its computation in a continuous latent space; does this make its reasoning less transparent? We study this question by decomposing transparency into two components: variable transparency, whether we understand intermediate snapshots of a model's computational state; and algorithmic transparency, whether we can use these snapshots to reconstruct the process by which the model arrived at its outputs. Naively, DiffusionGemma has poor variable transparency: its opaque serial depth, the amount of serial computation that occurs in between interpretable model states, seems at first 28.6X higher than the corresponding autoregressive Gemma 4 model. However, we show that we can map the information flowing between denoising steps through an interpretable token bottleneck with no decrease in downstream performance. Treating these intermediate states as interpretable reduces the opaque serial depth to just 1.1X that of Gemma 4. Algorithmic transparency is harder for diffusion models than for autoregressive models because all token predictions in the canvas can change at every denoising step, giving the model the power to implement complicated distributed algorithms during the denoising process. To begin bridging this gap, we conduct a suite of interpretability case studies, uncovering initial evidence of novel diffusion-specific phenomena such as non-chronological reasoning, token and sequence smearing, and intermediate-context reasoning. Finally, we test monitorability, a key application of transparency that measures whether model outputs are useful for downstream tasks. We find that DiffusionGemma is similarly monitorable to Gemma 4.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [224] [Semi-Automatic Correction of 3D Tubular Structure Skeletons via Component-Wise MST and Filtered Delaunay Triangulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19949)
*Ruoxuan Yang,Chuan Li*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Semi-automatic centerline correction for tubular skeletons via MST propagation and Delaunay bridging, driven by user-selected source/target points; outputs an ordered correction path for downstream processing; qualitative brain vessel results; C++/Libigl implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automatic skeletonization often yields topological artifacts (false connections, fragmented centerlines) due to noise or missing data. A minimal-input, semi-automatic method is needed to reconstruct plausible connections efficiently.

Method: Combine component-wise minimum spanning trees for stable local propagation with a filtered 3D Delaunay edge graph to bridge gaps and handle ambiguous junctions. Rank candidate steps using a score that accounts for direction continuity, spatial proximity, component consistency, and target-directed progress. Output is an ordered polyline (edge sequence) that can be integrated into downstream skeleton post-processing. Implemented in C++ with an interactive Libigl-based viewer; tested on brain vessel datasets, addressing crossing and dotted artifacts.

Result: Qualitative demonstration showing plausible correction of typical topological artifacts (e.g., crossing and dotted artifacts) on brain vessel data. The approach is lightweight and serves as a practical building block for more comprehensive interactive correction pipelines in biomedical imaging.

Conclusion: The proposed semi-automatic correction method provides a practical, lightweight component to repair skeleton topologies with minimal user input. Validation is qualitative; future work should include quantitative evaluation and integration into end-to-end pipelines for broader applicability.

Abstract: Skeletonization of tubular structures from 3D imaging is essential for tasks such as morphometric analysis, transport or flow simulation, and procedural planning in domains including vascular networks, plant root systems, and neural connectomes. However, automatic skeleton extraction often introduces topological artifacts, such as erroneous connections between nearby branches and fragmented centerlines caused by noise or missing data. Correcting these artifacts manually can be time-consuming and error-prone, especially when precise interaction is required.
  We present a semi-automatic correction method that reconstructs a plausible centerline connection from minimal user input. Given a user-selected source and target point, our method traces a path by combining (i) component-wise minimum spanning trees for stable local propagation and (ii) a filtered 3D Delaunay edge graph for bridging gaps and handling ambiguous junctions. Candidate steps are ranked using a score that accounts for direction continuity, spatial proximity, component consistency, and target-directed progress. The output is an ordered polyline (or edge sequence) that can be used as a suggested correction and integrated into downstream skeleton post-processing workflows.
  We implement the system in C++ with an interactive viewer based on Libigl and demonstrate representative qualitative results on brain vessel datasets, including correction of typical "crossing" and "dotted" artifacts. While our current validation is qualitative, the method is lightweight and serves as a practical building block toward more comprehensive interactive correction pipelines in biomedical imaging and related domains.

</details>


### [225] [Quadratic Forms for Measuring Geometric Trees in 3-dimensional Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20096)
*Yossi Bokor Bleile,Emanuele Cortinovis,Herbert Edelsbrunner,Shota Uka*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: Proposes a quadratic-form based framework to quantify directional spread of 3D geometric graphs and introduces a hexplot visualization with a Fisher-metric triangle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop quantitative tools for analyzing tree-like structures modeled as 3D geometric graphs, enabling measurement, visualization, and statistical analysis of their directional spread using concepts from quadratic forms and information geometry.

Method: Adopts quadratic forms to measure directional spread; introduces a hexplot model equipped with a metric derived from the Fisher metric on the standard triangle for visualization, measurement, and statistics.

Result: The abstract presents a theoretical framework and visualization/modeling approach rather than empirical results; it outlines contributions in metric definitions, hexplot construction, and applicability to statistics.
