Conclusion: The hexplot combined with a quadratic-form based spread metric provides a new framework to visualize, measure, and statistically analyze the directional spread of tree-like geometric graphs in R^3.

Abstract: Tree-like structures appear in many areas of science, and their shapes can help understand the underlying processes they drive or that give rise to them.
  By thinking of these structures as geometric graphs in $\mathbb{R}^3$, we gain access to tools from computational geometry and topology to study them.
  In this paper, we adopt the theory of quadratic forms to measure the directional spread of geometric graphs, and we introduce the hexplot model -- equipped with a metric derived from the Fisher metric on the standard triangle -- to visualize, measure, and collect statistics.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [226] [Physical Atari: A Robust and Accessible Platform for Real-time Reinforcement Learning on Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19357)
*Khurram Javed,Joseph Modayil,Gloria Kennickell,Richard S. Sutton,John Carmack*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost, end-to-end platform (Physical Atari) for studying real-world reinforcement learning using a Robotroller, Atari Devbox, and off-the-shelf components to validate learning directly on hardware and examine distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust, accessible real-world RL research by providing an affordable, reproducible hardware platform that lets learning algorithms operate directly on robotic control of a classic Atari interface, and to probe the impact of distribution shifts between learning and deployment.

Method: Hardware/software design: a Robotroller (bearings-only motion to reduce wear) and an Atari Devbox that renders frames and rewards from the Arcade Learning Environment, all interfaced with off-the-shelf hardware, a 3D-printed frame, a camera, and a desktop PC. The system is built for under $1,000, includes high-frequency servo monitoring with protective intervention, and emphasizes on-device adaptation and robustness.

Result: Demonstrates RL can learn directly on physical robots with weeks of non-stop operation without mechanical failures; shows small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade policy performance, highlighting the need for on-device adaptation.

Conclusion: A robust, affordable physical RL testbed is feasible and valuable for exposing and mitigating sim-to-real gaps, validating on-robot learning, and stressing the importance of hardware-aware design and adaptation for strong real-world performance.

Abstract: We built a robot called the Robotroller that actuates an Atari CX40+ controller and a device called the Atari Devbox that renders the game frame and the reward signal from the Arcade Learning Environment on a screen. The Robotroller and the Atari Devbox, together with an off-the-shelf camera and a desktop computer, constitute a system that can be used to study reinforcement learning algorithms in the physical world. We call the full system Physical Atari. In this paper, we detail the key decisions that make Physical Atari a robust and accessible platform. To make the system robust, we designed the Robotroller so that all movement is done through bearings, which reduces wear. Additionally, we wrote software that monitors the state of the servos at a high frequency and intervenes to limit stress. To make the system accessible, we used affordable off-the-shelf components and parts that can be manufactured using consumer 3D printers. Physical Atari can be built for under $1,000 and has been used for weeks of non-stop reinforcement learning experiments without any mechanical failures. We used it to validate that reinforcement learning algorithms can learn directly on robots and show that even small distribution shifts between learning and deployment can significantly degrade the performance of policies. Our results underscore the importance of on-device adaptation for strong performance on robots.

</details>


### [227] [WorkBenchMark: A LEGO-Based Assembly Benchmark with an Assembly-by-Disassembly Baseline for the Smart Manufacturing League](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19358)
*Wenbo Ma,Daniel Swoboda,Matteo Tschesche,Till Hofmann*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A LEGO Duplo–based robotic-assembly benchmark (WorkBenchMark) with 400 tasks across four complexity tiers is proposed to evaluate planning-driven methods that integrate low-level manipulation with high-level symbolic reasoning under physical constraints. An open-vocabulary perception and an Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline are provided; a planning-based pipeline outperforms a modern vision-language-action approach. The benchmark, simulator, and baseline will be openly released to support the robotic-assembly community.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current end-to-end learning methods struggle to reliably solve tasks that require combining low-level manipulation with high-level symbolic planning under physical constraints. A standardized, open benchmark is needed to evaluate and foster progress in robotic assembly that bridges perception, planning, and manipulation.

Method: 1) LEGO Duplo-based assembly tasks (400 tasks across four complexity tiers). 2) Open-vocabulary perception. 3) Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline solution. 4) Planning-based pipeline designed to handle task-level reasoning with physical constraints. 5) Comparative evaluation against a modern vision-language-action approach. 6) Open release of benchmark, simulation environment, and baseline implementation.

Result: The planning-based pipeline outperforms the modern vision-language-action approach across all tiers.

Conclusion: WorkBenchMark and its accompanying resources will be released openly to support the broader robotic-assembly community and to advance progress in integrating planning and manipulation for assembly tasks.

Abstract: We introduceWorkBenchMark, a LEGO Duplo-based robotic assembly benchmark motivated by the RoboCup Smart Manufacturing League. Robotic assembly couples low-level manipulation with task-level symbolic reasoning under physical constraints, a combination that current end-to-end learning methods do not yet solve reliably. The benchmark provides 400 tasks across four complexity tiers. We provide an open-vocabulary perception, Assembly-by-Disassembly baseline solution. Our planning-based pipeline outperforms a modern vision-language-action approach across all tiers. The benchmark, simulation environment, and baseline implementation will be released openly to support the broader robotic assembly community.

</details>


### [228] [3D Scene Graphs: Open Challenges and Future Directions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19383)
*Dennis Rotondi,Francesco Argenziano,Sebastian Koch,Nathan Hughes,Martin Buechner,Johanna Wald,Lukas Rosenberger Schmid,Daniele Nardi,Abhinav Valada,Liam Paull,Federico Tombari,Luca Carlone,Kai O. Arras*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unified critical review of 3D Scene Graphs that formalizes definitions, surveys modeling choices, pipelines, and evaluation; highlights challenges and future directions, with a community resource website.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fragmentation across communities in 3DSG research hinders comparability and deployment; a common framework and assessment standards are needed.

Method: Formal analysis and synthesis of existing 3DSG formulations; taxonomy of node/edge attributes, hierarchy, dynamics, and affordances; review of construction pipelines, conventions, and evaluation protocols; discussion of applications and a resource website.

Result: A unified framework and critical survey that clarifies assumptions, enables comparisons, and identifies gaps and open challenges; provides a centralized resource (website) for ongoing work.

Conclusion: Encourages standardization, better benchmarking, and robust real-world deployment; outlines future directions and research opportunities in 3DSGs.

Abstract: 3D Scene Graphs (3DSGs) have emerged as a powerful representation for spatial AI by combining geometric grounding with semantic and relational abstractions of the environment. Their expressiveness has made them relevant to a broad range of problems in robotics and computer vision, including manipulation, navigation, task planning, scene understanding, and many others. However, the field remains fragmented: different communities adopt distinct formulations, construction pipelines, and evaluation protocols, making it difficult to compare methods, identify common assumptions, and assess remaining challenges for robust real-world deployment. This survey provides a unified and critical review of 3DSGs, with particular emphasis on open challenges and future directions. We first formalize 3DSGs under a common definition and analyze the principal modeling choices that characterize existing formulations, including node and edge attributes, hierarchical structure, dynamic scene representations, and affordance-aware extensions. We then review how 3DSGs are built from raw sensory observations, discussing the most common terminologies, conventions, and techniques. Finally, we examine downstream applications and evaluation strategies, from intrinsic graph quality to task-level performance. To support the community, we also provide a dedicated website that organizes and extends the surveyed content, accessible at https://3dscenegraphs.com/.

</details>


### [229] [DiffusionVS: A Generative Framework for Robust Visual Servoing Based on Diffusion Policy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19397)
*Hongkang Cui,Rui He,Haoyao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based visual servoing approach using conditional denoising on normalized tag-corner coordinates, with online, interactive data collection, yielding near-perfect simulation performance and high real-world success, and showing broad applicability to improve other visual servoing systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address trajectory jitter and error accumulation in regression-based visual servoing under distribution shifts; enhance robustness and generalization via a diffusion policy that provides temporal consistency and implicit data augmentation.

Method: A diffusion policy framework that takes normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners as input and generates camera velocity through conditional denoising. An online training paradigm continuously expands training data through interactive experience collection, improving diversity and generalization. The diffusion module is shown to improve a broad range of visual servoing networks beyond the proposed architecture.

Result: Simulation success rates approaching 100% and real-world experiments around 93% demonstrate effectiveness. The diffusion-based module improves other visual servoing networks, indicating broad applicability.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based servoing is effective, robust, and generalizable, capable of enhancing a wide class of visual servoing systems beyond the specific pipeline studied.

Abstract: Visual servoing is a fundamental technique in robotic manipulation and navigation. Regression-based visual servoing frequently experiences trajectory jitter as a result of noise-sensitive single-step mappings and the accumulation of errors during distribution shifts. In contrast, Diffusion Policy maintains temporal consistency by predicting action sequences and improves robustness through implicit data augmentation.
  This paper presents a novel diffusion-based servoing method. Based on Diffusion Policy, the proposed approach uses normalized image coordinates of observed tag corners as input and generates camera velocity through conditional denoising. To overcome the generalization limitations of models trained on static datasets, an online training paradigm is adopted, continuously expanding the diversity of training data through interactive experience collection. This strategy substantially enhances both the performance and generalization capability of the model. Comprehensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method, achieving success rates of nearly 100\% in simulation and 93\% in physical experiments. Beyond the specific pipeline, we further validate the generality of the diffusion mechanism. Experiments show that existing visual servoing networks consistently achieve improved performance when integrated with our diffusion-based module. These results indicate that the proposed strategy possesses broad applicability and can enhance various visual servoing systems beyond the specific architecture presented here.

</details>


### [230] [Playful Agentic Robot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19419)
*Junyi Zhang,Jiaxin Ge,Hanjun Yoo,Letian Fu,Zihan Yang,Yaowei Liu,Raj Saravanan,Shaofeng Yin,Justin Yu,Dantong Niu,Zirui Wang,Roei Herzig,Ken Goldberg,Yutong Bai,David M. Chan,Ion Stoica,Angjoo Kanazawa,Jiahui Lei,Haiwen Feng,Trevor Darrell*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Playful Agentic Robot Learning introduces RATs to self-directed play for skill acquisition; resulting reusable code skills improve downstream tasks and cross-domain transfer without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agentic robots depend on explicit instructions; reusable, transferable skills require autonomous, exploratory play.

Method: Propose RATs that generate and execute novel exploratory tasks, produce and refine Code-as-Policy policies, diagnose, retry with dense feedback, and distill successful runs into a persistent code skill library; test-time retrieval of skills into policy context.

Result: Play-learned skills yield large improvements on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces (20.6 and 17.0 pp over CaP-Agent0). Retrieved skills improve RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points without finetuning.

Conclusion: Play-driven skill acquisition via RATs enables reusable, transferable robot-code skills, boosting downstream performance and zero-/few-shot cross-task transfer without additional model training.

Abstract: Current agentic robot systems can write executable Code-as-Policy programs, observe feedback, and revise behavior across multiple attempts, but they remain largely task-driven: reusable skills are acquired only after explicit instructions. We study Playful Agentic Robot Learning, where an embodied coding agent uses self-directed play as a continual skill-learning stage before downstream tasks arrive. We introduce RATs, Robotics Agent Teams designed for play-time skill acquisition. During play, RATs proposes novel yet learnable exploratory tasks, plans and executes robot-code policies, verifies intermediate progress, diagnoses failures, retries with dense, step-level feedback, and distills successful executions into a persistent code skill library. At test time, the agent reuses relevant skills from this frozen library to help solve new tasks. Experiments in LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces show that play-learned skills improve held-out downstream tasks over no-play and random-play baselines, with 20.6 and 17.0 percentage-point gains over CaP-Agent0 on LIBERO-PRO and MolmoSpaces, respectively. Moreover, the learned skills can be plugged into other inference-time Code-as-Policy agents by simply retrieving them into the context, improving RoboSuite and real-world transfer by 8.9 and 8.8 points, respectively, without finetuning the underlying model.

</details>


### [231] [Simulating Robotic Locomotion in Sand: Resistive Force Theory in an Open-Source Physics Engine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19504)
*Ryan Walker Brown,Laura K. Treers,Kathryn A. Daltorio*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: 3D Granular RFT implemented in MuJoCo to simulate sand locomotion; predicts hexapod walking distance and foot sinkage within 20% of experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge resistive force approximations for granular media with 3D physics engines to enable stable, scalable robot simulation on sand without full grain-level modeling.

Method: Implement 3D Granular RFT within MuJoCo, verify across scenarios; analyze influence of end effector shape, speed, and loading; compare predicted walking distance and foot sinkage to experimental data.

Result: Simulations preserve key trends (shape, speed, loading); walking distance and foot sinkage predictions for a 12-DOF hexapod are within 20% of sand experiments; open-source tool.

Conclusion: RFT-based approximations can provide a practical, efficient substrate model in 3D simulators; despite approximations, useful for robot design and testing on granular media.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Resistive Force Theory (RFT) enable approximation of ground reaction forces for locomotion in sand without the computational expense of modeling interactions with individual grains. However, these tools have been absent in 3D physics engines commonly used for robot simulation. We explore if resistive force approximations are sufficient, when integrated with standard dynamics calculations, to provide a stable substrate for a freely walking robot. To determine this, we implement 3D Granular Resistive Force Theory (3D RFT) in a physics simulation engine, MuJoCo. We verify simulations in multiple scenarios to demonstrate that key trends due to end effector shape, speed, and loading are preserved. Our implementation predicts walking distance and foot sinkage of a 12-Degree of Freedom hexapod robot within 20\% of experiments in sand. While RFT has inherent approximations, the open source tool described here has potential to help develop new and improved robot designs to traverse granular media substrates.

</details>


### [232] [Proprioceptive Invariant State Estimation for Humanoid Robots on Non-Inertial Ground](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19512)
*Falak Mandali,Zijian He,Yan Gu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Invariant EKF-based proprioceptive state estimation for humanoids on non-inertial ground, using stance-foot constraints and foot-mounted IMUs. Demonstrates right-invariant filtering, observability analysis, and strong experimental validation with Digit on swaying/pitching ground; reports large gains in convergence speed and position accuracy over prior InEKFs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable real-time estimation of base position and velocity relative to a moving ground using only onboard sensors, removing the need for ground-motion measurements or external sensors.

Method: An invariant extended Kalman filter (InEKF) with a right-invariant measurement model. It leverages kinematic constraints from stance-foot IMUs and foot-mounted sensors to maintain full proprioception. The process and measurement models account for ground-induced nonlinearity, and observability analysis characterizes conditions for relative base state observability on a non-inertial ground.

Result: Experimental validation shows a 96% faster convergence rate and an 80% reduction in position error compared to existing InEKFs. Walking tests on uni-axially rotating ground achieve average position error below 9 cm for initial errors up to 1 m.

Conclusion: The approach yields effective fully proprioceptive state estimation for humanoids on moving ground, with favorable error dynamics, concrete observability conditions, and substantial performance gains demonstrated on the Digit humanoid.

Abstract: This paper presents an invariant extended Kalman filtering (InEKF) approach for real-time state estimation of humanoid robots operating on non-inertial ground using only onboard proprioceptive sensing. The proposed approach estimates the robot's base position and velocity relative to the moving ground frame without requiring direct measurements of ground motion or externally mounted sensors. By exploiting kinematic constraints at the stance foot through foot-mounted IMUs, the filter accounts for ground-induced nonlinearities in the process and measurement models while remaining fully proprioceptive. The estimator is formulated to admit a right-invariant measurement model, enabling favorable error dynamics under large initial uncertainties. Observability analysis establishes conditions under which the robot's relative base position and velocity are observable with respect to the non-inertial ground frame. Experiments with the Digit humanoid robot standing and squatting atop a swaying and pitching ground showcase a 96% speedup in convergence rate and an 80% reduction in position estimate errors over existing InEKFs. Walking experiments on a uni-axially rotating ground achieve an average estimation error of less than 9 cm for an initial error of up to 1 m.

</details>


### [233] [A Categorial and Sheaf-Theoretic Semantics for Autonomic Component Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19525)
*Manuel Hernández,Eduardo Sánchez-Soto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a multi-layer, category-theoretic/sheaf-theoretic model for SCEL-based distributed systems, modelling components as points, ensembles as opens, and distributed knowledge as sheaf data; uses gluing to represent information sharing and sheaf cohomology to quantify failures, converting verification into geometric analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional formal methods struggle with large-scale, decentralized autonomous systems; there is a need for a global, structural, emergent-property-aware semantic framework for SCEL-like languages.

Method: Develops a layered mathematical model where a society of robots is modeled as a sheaf over a topological space: components are points, ensembles are open sets; knowledge forms the data; information sharing corresponds to gluing local data; failures are topological obstructions measurable via sheaf cohomology.

Result: Conceptual framework that reframes verification of distributed systems as geometric analysis of a sheaf on a topological space; enables structural insights into robustness and emergent behavior.

Conclusion: The approach offers deep structural insights for designing robust autonomic systems, re framing verification in terms of geometric/topological properties; future work likely involves instantiation, examples, and practical integration with SCEL tooling.

Abstract: The proliferation of large-scale, decentralized systems of autonomous agents, such as swarms of robots and networked cyber-physical systems, presents a formidable challenge to traditional formal methods. The Software Component Ensemble Language (SCEL) offers a formal model for such systems, but its operational semantics is not ideal for reasoning about global, structural, and emergent properties. This report proposes a new, multi-layered mathematical model for SCEL using category theory and sheaf theory. We argue that a society of robots described in SCEL can be formally modeled as a sheaf on a topological space, where components are points, ensembles are open sets, and distributed knowledge forms the sheaf's data. In this framework, computational processes like information sharing become equivalent to the sheaf-theoretic operation of "gluing" local data. System failures can then be understood and quantified as topological obstructions, measurable by sheaf cohomology. This approach transforms the verification of a complex distributed system into the analysis of the geometry of a mathematical object, providing deep, structural insights for the design of robust autonomic systems.

</details>


### [234] [SCAN-Planner: Spatial Collision-Aware Local Planning for Route-Guided Long-Range Quadruped Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19555)
*Han Zheng,Zhe Chen,Yiwen Fu,Ming Yang,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SCAN-Planner offers a spatially collision-aware local planner for quadrupeds, using a yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint, inflated 3D occupancy maps, and a projected A* on a ground-following surface with z-gradient suppression, plus a robot-centric sliding map for recovery, enabling safe, smooth, and efficient long-range navigation in dense clutter and 3D environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing local planners rely on isotropic inflation or 2.5D representations, which lead to conservative behavior in tight spaces and poor handling of overhanging structures. There is a need for true 3D, whole-body collision awareness and robust long-range planning for quadrupeds in unstructured environments.

Method: 1) Model the robot with a yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint for whole-body collision checks via sparse queries in an inflated 3D occupancy map. 2) Introduce a projected A* search that plans on an interpolated ground-following surface with z-gradient suppression to avoid horizontal obstacles while preserving vertical stability. 3) Implement a robot-centric sliding map with boundary fallback to provide high-resolution local collision checking and recovery from local dead ends for large-scale deployment.

Result: Simulation and real-world experiments show that SCAN-Planner generates safe, smooth, and efficient trajectories in dense clutter, 3D unstructured scenes, stair traversal, and long-range navigation tasks.

Conclusion: SCAN-Planner fills a gap in quadruped navigation by combining whole-body, 3D collision-aware planning with a scalable local-to-long-range framework, enabling robust long-range navigation in complex 3D environments.

Abstract: Quadruped robots are increasingly expected to navigate through narrow passages, cluttered indoor scenes, and large-scale 3D unstructured environments. Existing local planners commonly approximate the robot using isotropic geometric inflation or rely on planar and elevation-map representations, leading to conservative motion in tight spaces and limited reasoning about overhanging structures. This letter presents SCAN-Planner, a spatial collision-aware local planning framework for long-range quadruped navigation. A yaw-aware twin-cylinder footprint is used to model the elongated robot body, enabling whole-body collision evaluation through sparse queries in an inflated 3D occupancy map. We further introduce a projected A* search that generates collision-free guidance on an interpolated ground-following surface, with z-gradient suppression to avoid obstacles horizontally while maintaining vertical stability. For large-scale deployment, a robot-centric sliding map with boundary fallback provides high-resolution local collision checking and recovery from local dead ends. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that SCAN-Planner generates safe, smooth, and efficient trajectories in dense clutter, 3D unstructured scenes, stair traversal, and long-range navigation tasks.

</details>


### [235] [pdSTL: Probabilistic Differentiable Signal Temporal Logic for Stochastic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19561)
*Bennett Dogbey,Hemanth Manjunatha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: pdSTL unifies probabilistic semantics with differentiable robustness over belief trajectories, using interval-valued probabilities and LSTM-style unfolding for linear-time monitoring and end-to-end optimization; validated on simulated and real UAV tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridges the gap between STL robustness (often non-differentiable) and belief-space uncertainty in stochastic/disturbed environments; enables differentiable, probabilistic guarantees for trajectory optimization.

Method: Introduce pdSTL with interval-valued probabilistic semantics; propagate through the STL syntax tree; recast robustness evaluation as a recurrent unfolding of STL operators (LSTM-like), yielding differentiable, linear-time monitoring suitable for end-to-end optimization.

Result: pdSTL achieves efficient optimization with formal probabilistic guarantees; significantly outperforms deterministic differentiable STL in maintaining safety margins under real-world uncertainty; validated on obstacle avoidance, lane-change maneuvers, and Crazyflie quadcopter flights with disturbances.

Conclusion: pdSTL provides a unified framework for probabilistic, differentiable monitoring of belief trajectories, enabling safe, robust end-to-end planning and control under uncertainty across robotic tasks.

Abstract: Autonomous robots operating in uncertain environments must satisfy complex temporal and safety specifications despite stochastic dynamics and sensing noise. While Signal Temporal Logic (STL) offers robustness measures for gradient-based optimization, existing extensions either lack differentiability or ignore belief-space uncertainty. We introduce pdSTL (probabilistic differentiable Signal Temporal Logic), a framework that unifies probabilistic semantics with differentiable robustness over belief trajectories. pdSTL employs interval-valued probabilistic semantics to compute conservative satisfaction bounds, propagated compositionally through the STL syntax tree. We formulate the temporal robustness evaluation as a recurrent, LSTM-style unfolding of STL operators, enabling linear-time, differentiable monitoring suitable for end-to-end trajectory optimization. We validate pdSTL on simulated obstacle avoidance, lane-change maneuvers, and real-world Crazyflie quadcopter flight experiments under aerodynamic disturbances. Results demonstrate that pdSTL achieves efficient optimization with formal probabilistic guarantees, significantly outperforming deterministic differentiable STL in maintaining safety margins under real-world uncertainty.

</details>


### [236] [One Demo is Worth a Thousand Trajectories: Action-View Augmentation for Visuomotor Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19586)
*Chuer Pan,Litian Liang,Dominik Bauer,Eric Cousineau,Benjamin Burchfiel,Siyuan Feng,Shuran Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Augments visuomotor policy training with realistic fisheye-inspired data; uses Gaussian Splatting to model/edit 3D scenes and trajectory optimization to produce feasible novel-view trajectories; shows improved success in sim and real-world manipulation tasks with obstacles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visuomotor policies degrade under slight changes in initial configuration and unseen obstacles; data collection is expensive; need robust, scalable augmentation that preserves physical feasibility and visual realism.

Method: Collect real eye-in-hand demonstrations using a portable parallel gripper with a single fisheye camera; apply Gaussian Splatting adapted for wide FoV fisheye to reconstruct/edit 3D scenes including unseen objects; use trajectory optimization to generate smooth, collision-free action trajectories compatible with novel views; render observations from these views to augment training.

Result: Experiments in simulation and real-world show improved success rates for various manipulation tasks, in both the original scene and augmented scenes with obstacles requiring collision avoidance.

Conclusion: The proposed augmentation pipeline effectively broadens the distribution encountered by visuomotor policies, enabling better generalization to unseen geometry and configurations; the combination of realistic fisheye scene editing and geometry-aware trajectory rendering yields tangible performance gains.

Abstract: Visuomotor policies for manipulation have demonstrated remarkable potential in modeling complex robotic behaviors, yet minor alterations in the robot's initial configuration and unseen obstacles easily lead to out-of-distribution observations. Without extensive data collection effort, these result in catastrophic execution failures. In this work, we introduce an effective data augmentation framework that generates visually realistic fisheye image sequences and corresponding physically feasible action trajectories from real-world eye-in-hand demonstrations, captured with a portable parallel gripper with a single fisheye camera. We introduce a novel Gaussian Splatting formulation, adapted to wide FoV fisheye cameras, to reconstruct and edit the 3D scene with unseen objects. We utilize trajectory optimization to generate smooth, collision-free, view-rendering-friendly action trajectories and render visual observations from corresponding novel views. Comprehensive experiments in simulation and the real world show that our augmentation framework improves the success rate for various manipulation tasks in both the same scene and the augmented scene with obstacles requiring collision avoidance.

</details>


### [237] [Safe, Real-Time Active Model Discrimination and Fault Diagnosis for Nonlinear Systems via Differentiable Reachability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19590)
*Xinpei Ni,Melkior Ornik,Glen Chou,Samuel Coogan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-time, safe fault diagnosis and model discrimination for uncertain nonlinear systems using interval reachability and differentiable overlap-penalized objectives; achieves sub-50 ms discrimination with safety guarantees across diverse robotic platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for fast, provably safe diagnosis among multiple nominal/faulty models under disturbances, enabling timely corrective actions and fault isolation in high-dimensional nonlinear systems.

Method: Output-feedback, time-varying policy optimization with finite-horizon safety constraints. Uses interval over-approximation of state/output reachable sets; a differentiable objective penalizes overlap of reachable outputs across candidate models to enforce diagnosability. Online optimization solved via gradient-based methods (JAX) with differentiable reachability primitives.

Result: Demonstrates reliable model discrimination among up to 11 fault modes across several platforms (simulated quadrotor, fighter-jet model, hardware differential-drive robot, quadrupedal navigation) with discrimination under 50 ms, outperforming baselines in both discrimination success rate and speed, while providing formal safety guarantees.

Conclusion: The framework delivers real-time, provably safe fault diagnosis and model discrimination for high-dimensional nonlinear systems, with strong empirical results across diverse robotic platforms.

Abstract: We present a safe, real-time algorithm for active fault diagnosis and model discrimination for uncertain continuous-time nonlinear systems with process and measurement disturbances. Given a finite set of candidate models representing nominal and faulty modes, including actuator and sensor faults, we formulate an output-feedback, time-varying policy optimization problem that (i) robustly enforces state-input safety constraints over a finite horizon and (ii) drives the system to produce sampled measurements consistent with at most one model, enabling deterministic diagnosis. To solve this problem in real time, we develop a tractable approximation using interval over-approximations of reachable state and output sets, and encode diagnosability via a differentiable objective that penalizes overlap between the reachable output sets of possible models. The resulting optimization is solved efficiently online with gradient-based methods using JAX and differentiable reachability primitives. We evaluate our method on sensor and actuator fault diagnosis (up to 11 fault modes) in several high-dimensional nonlinear robotic systems, including a simulated quadrotor and fighter-jet model, a hardware differential-drive robot, and quadrupedal navigation. Across these case studies, our approach achieves reliable model discrimination in under 50 ms, outperforming baselines in discrimination success rate and speed while providing formal safety guarantees.

</details>


### [238] [Fail-RAG : A Retrieval Augmented Generation Informed Framework for Robot Failure Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19598)
*Ameya Salvi,Jie Hu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces Fail-RAG, a Retrieval Augmented Generation framework for detecting failures in warehouse robotics using embedded failure imagery and context, retrieved via similarity against a failure database, and analyzed by Vision-Language Models with a structured instruction template; validated in simulation and on real robots, yielding about 25 percentage points higher failure detection accuracy than off-the-shelf VLMs across five operation types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unpredictable, dynamic failures in autonomous warehouse robotics undermine rule-based detectors. A retrieval-augmented approach leveraging context and visual data can provide robust, scalable failure detection.

Method: Fail-RAG embeds failure images and contextual information, performs similarity-based retrieval from a failure database, and uses a Vision-Language Model with a predefined instruction template to analyze failures. Evaluation includes both simulation and physical experiments with fixed-arm and mobile manipulator robots across multiple warehouse tasks.

Result: Achieves ~25 percentage point higher failure detection accuracy on average across five robot operation types compared to using off-the-shelf VLMs.

Conclusion: The Fail-RAG framework effectively detects a range of operation-related failures in warehouse robotics, demonstrating the potential of retrieval-augmented generation combined with vision-language analysis for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Industry automation is witnessing an evolution in robotics driven by both technological breakthroughs and societal changes: progress towards generalist robots, embodied and physical artificial intelligence (AI), and increasing labor shortage in manufacturing.An intelligent autonomous robot needs to not only act according to planned motions but also react to any unexpected events. In this study, we focus on such unexpected events in warehouses where robots are used for material handling. Specifically, we refer to any unexpected events as failures and develop methods to detect robot operations related failures. Rule-based detection methods may break since the form of failures could change due to the dynamic nature of both environments and tasks. We propose 'Fail-RAG', a Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)-based failure detection framework where failure images and context information are embedded and queried against a failure database by calculating their similarities. Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are further used to analyze failures and provide details by following our instruction template. We evaluated the performance of Fail-RAG by conducting both simulation and physical experiments using fixed robot arms and a mobile manipulator for multiple tasks that are common in warehouse automation. Fail-RAG achieved 25 percentage point higher failure detection accuracy on average across five types of robot operations compared to using off-the-shelf VLMs, indicating its effectiveness for real-world failure detection.

</details>


### [239] [Formal Verification of Learned Multi-Agent Communication Policies via Decision Tree Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19632)
*Ahmad Farooq,Kamran Iqbal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: End-to-end safety verification for learned multi-agent communication in MARL by distilling neural policies into decision trees, then verifying with probabilistic model checking (PRISM) using PCTL; demonstrated on VQ-VIB drone coordination with 5–7 agents, achieving high fidelity, robust safety thresholds, and transfer of verified properties to original policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide formal safety guarantees for safety-critical MARL deployments where neural policies lack guarantees. Bridge deep multi-agent learning with formal verification by producing interpretable abstractions without sacrificing performance.

Method: Four-stage pipeline: (1) domain-specific feature extraction from agent observations, (2) decision-tree distillation achieving 97.9% ±1.2% fidelity to neural policies, (3) automated translation to PRISM with feature-to-state-variable correspondence, (4) compositional verification of PCTL properties via pairwise decomposition with union-bound aggregation and empirical neighbor modeling. Evaluation on Vector-Quantized VIB policies for 5–7 drones; verified 18 temporal properties across safety, liveness, and cooperation; Monte Carlo validation of original neural policies.

Result: Verifies 18 temporal properties with 88.9% satisfaction; all five safety thresholds met (e.g., collision probability 0.3% vs. 1% threshold); original policies transfer verified properties with ≤0.6 percentage-point deviation (95% CI). Discrete VQ-VIB messages yield 11.6–13.6 percentage-point fidelity gains over continuous methods and enable 3–4x faster verification.

Conclusion: The framework yields empirically validated safety verification for distilled policy abstractions, serving as a practical bridge between deep MARL and formal safety workflows for safe multi-robot deployment.

Abstract: Multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) enables agents to develop coordination strategies through emergent communication, but neural policies lack the formal safety guarantees required for safety-critical robotic deployment in drone swarms and autonomous vehicle fleets. We present the first end-to-end framework for safety verification of learned multi-agent communication policies through policy abstraction: neural policies are distilled into interpretable decision trees, then formally verified, with empirical validation confirming that verified safety properties transfer to original networks. Our four-stage pipeline consists of domain-specific feature extraction from agent observations, decision tree distillation achieving 97.9% +/- 1.2% fidelity to neural policies, automated translation to PRISM probabilistic model checker specifications with complete feature-to-state-variable correspondence, and compositional verification of Probabilistic Computation Tree Logic (PCTL) properties via pairwise decomposition with union-bound aggregation and empirical neighbor modeling. Evaluating Vector-Quantized Variational Information Bottleneck (VQ-VIB) policies for multi-drone coordination with 5-7 agents, we verify 18 temporal logic properties across safety, liveness, and cooperation, achieving 88.9% property satisfaction with all five safety thresholds satisfied (0.3% collision probability vs. 1% threshold). Monte Carlo validation of original neural policies confirms that verified safety properties transfer with <=0.6 percentage-point deviation (95% CI). Discrete VQ-VIB messages provide +11.6 to +13.6 percentage-point fidelity advantages over continuous methods, enabling 3-4x faster verification. Our framework provides empirically validated safety verification for distilled policy abstractions, serving as a practical bridge between deep MARL and formal safety workflows for multi-robot deployment.

</details>


### [240] [CTS-MoE: Implicit Terrain Adaptation via Mixture-of-Experts for Perceptive Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19633)
*Francisco Affonso,Matheus P. Angarola,Ana Luiza Mineiro,Aditya Potnis,Marcelo Becker,Girish Chowdhary*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CTS-MoE uses a dense mixture-of-experts actor with perception-based gating and task-specific value heads to enable task-aware, shared locomotion across discontinuous terrains, trained end-to-end with a concurrent teacher-student setup and deployed via perception routing without a high-level terrain classifier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Perceptive legged locomotion over variable terrain requires balancing shared robust locomotion with task-specific specialization to handle abrupt topology changes without interference between rewards.

Method: A dense mixture-of-experts actor provides shared behavior; a perception-based gate routes inputs to experts. A multi-critic with task-specific value heads mitigates value interference. Training uses a single-stage concurrent teacher-student framework with task labels only during training; deployment uses perception-only routing, no terrain classifier.

Result: In simulation (Unitree Go1) and hardware, CTS-MoE achieves task-aware specialization, lower tracking error, and higher success rates on seen and unseen terrains compared with monolithic baselines.

Conclusion: The approach effectively balances sharing and specialization for adaptive legged locomotion in discontinuous terrain, enabling perception-driven routing at deployment without a high-level terrain selector; future work could explore ablations, scalability to many tasks, and robustness under perception noise.

Abstract: Perceptive legged locomotion over discontinuous terrain (e.g., stairs, gaps, and obstacles) requires adaptive behavior, as a single conservative gait cannot produce the anticipatory maneuvers needed for abrupt topology changes. Cast as multi-task reinforcement learning, this problem introduces a tension between sharing and separation. Tasks use a common locomotion base but have conflicting rewards, so a policy must share behavior while avoiding value interference. Prior work addresses only one side, with monolithic policies sacrificing specialization and hierarchical sub-policies sacrificing generalization across transitions and unseen terrain. We propose CTS-MoE, which combines a dense mixture-of-experts actor with perception-based gating to compose shared behaviors and a multi-critic with task-specific value heads to prevent interference. The model is trained end-to-end in a single-stage concurrent teacher-student setup that handles partial observability and avoids sequential distillation, with task labels used only during training. At deployment, routing depends solely on perception, allowing terrain adaptation without a high-level selector or terrain classifier. Experiments on a Unitree Go1 in simulation and on hardware across seen and unseen terrains show task-aware specialization, with lower tracking error and higher success rates than monolithic baselines. Project Website: https://cts-moe.github.io/ .

</details>


### [241] [Scaling Self-Play for End-to-End Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19641)
*Luke Rowe,Roger Girgis,Rodrigue de Schaetzen,Daphne Cornelisse,Alaap Grandhi,Felix Heide,Eugene Vinitsky,Christopher Pal,Liam Paull*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Gigapixel introduces a high-throughput pixel-based driving simulator for scalable self-play; uses self-play DAgger with a privileged RL teacher to train end-to-end policies directly from pixels; transfers to real-world data via lightweight perception adaptation; achieves competitive results on HUGSIM/NAVSIM-v2 and shows scalable gains with more self-play.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline end-to-end driving models suffer from limited state coverage and lack of closed-loop feedback, leading to compounding errors; existing self-play approaches rely on BEV observations incompatible with pixel-based end-to-end policies.

Method: Develop Gigapixel, a perspective-rendered, high-throughput bounding-box world simulator delivering ~50k agent steps/sec to enable self-play from pixel observations. Train pixel-based policies via self-play DAgger (on-policy distillation from a privileged RL teacher). Bridge sim-to-real with lightweight perception adaptation to real sensor data.

Result: Self-play trained policies, once adapted to real sensor data, achieve competitive performance on HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectories. Scaling self-play yields proportional gains in policy performance.

Conclusion: Self-play is a practical and scalable strategy for training end-to-end driving models; combining high-throughput pixel-based simulation with on-policy distillation enables effective learning and transfer to real-world perception.

Abstract: End-to-end autonomous driving models are typically trained on offline human-demonstration datasets that provide limited state coverage and often no closed-loop feedback, making them prone to compounding errors when deployed in closed-loop and brittle to long-tail agent interactions. To overcome these limitations, we propose an alternative strategy for training end-to-end driving models: large-scale self-play directly from pixels in simulation. While prior self-play approaches have shown promising transfer to real-world driving, they typically assume vectorized Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) observations that are incompatible with end-to-end policies operating directly on sensor observations. To this end, we introduce Gigapixel, a high-throughput batched driving simulator with perspective rendering, enabling scalable self-play directly from pixel observations. Rather than targeting compute-costly photorealistic sensor simulation, Gigapixel renders a simplified bounding-box world that preserves essential scene structure while achieving throughput at 50k agent steps per second. Since direct pixel-space self-play RL is prohibitively sample-inefficient at end-to-end model scale, we propose self-play DAgger training: we train pixel-based policies in self-play via on-policy distillation from a privileged RL teacher. To bridge the sim-to-real gap, we subsequently transfer the self-play trained policies to real-world sensor data through lightweight perception adaptation. Policies trained in Gigapixel and adapted to real-world sensor data achieve competitive performance on the HUGSIM and NAVSIM-v2 benchmarks without human trajectory supervision. Moreover, scaling self-play training yields proportional gains in policy performance, establishing self-play as a practical and scalable strategy for training end-to-end models.

</details>


### [242] [DF-ExpEnse: Diffusion Filtered Exploration for Sample Efficient Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19656)
*Calvin Luo,Chen Sun,Shuran Song*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DF-ExpEnse introduces an exploration-driven framework for fine-tuning pretrained generative control policies by constructing an expressive candidate action set via multimodal modeling and selecting actions through an ensemble of critics to balance quality and exploration; it extends to fleet settings with cross-agent communication, achieving consistent sample-efficient fine-tuning across manipulation and locomotion tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enhance online data collection efficiency and finetuning sample efficiency when adapting pretrained generative control policies to online experience.

Method: Generate an expressive candidate action set using the policy's multimodal modeling; employ an ensemble of critics to pick actions that balance high-quality outcomes with exploration; enable cross-agent communication in fleet scenarios for collaborative exploration; integrate with existing reinforcement learning pipelines that fine-tune pretrained generative control policies.

Result: Empirically shows consistent improvements in sample efficiency across diverse manipulation and locomotion tasks versus standard finetuning and other action-selection methods.

Conclusion: DF-ExpEnse provides a practical, scalable exploration mechanism that improves data efficiency for fine-tuning pretrained generative control policies, with demonstrated benefits in single-agent and fleet settings.

Abstract: A natural recipe for intelligent robotic decision-making is initializing from pretrained generative control policies, which have summarized offline experience, and adapting them to self-collected online experience. We present DF-ExpEnse, an exploration technique that improves the quality of online experience collection, thus increasing finetuning sample-efficiency. DF-ExpEnse leverages the multimodal modeling capabilities of the generative control policy to create an expressive and tractably evaluatable candidate set. It then utilizes an ensemble of critics to identify the action that best balances quality with high exploration interest. In fleet settings, DF-ExpEnse further enables cross-agent communication to facilitate collaborative exploration as a group. DF-ExpEnse can be seamlessly integrated with existing strategies that finetune pretrained generative control policies via reinforcement learning. We experimentally validate consistent sample-efficiency benefits through DF-ExpEnse across a variety of manipulation and locomotion tasks, compared to default finetuning and alternative action selection schemes. Project can be found at https://df-expense.github.io.

</details>


### [243] [Safe Local Navigation for Ackermann-Steered Robots in Unmapped Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19672)
*Christian Schaible,Shahin Sirouspour*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a local-navigation control framework for Ackermann-steered robots in unmapped environments with no global goal. It selects the safest heading by identifying the largest open space ahead, then constructs left/right bounding lines via a convex quadratic optimization to maximize clearance, optionally enforcing parallelism and smoothness with prior steps. A feedback-linearizing controller tracks a local reference path to maintain obstacle clearance. The method is complemented by open-source code and shows faster, safer navigation than some exploration-based planners in experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable safe, real-time local navigation for nonholonomic mobile robots without a global map or goal, prioritizing obstacle clearance and computational efficiency.

Method: 1) Determine the heading along the direction of the largest open space ahead. 2) Construct bounding lines on the vehicle’s left and right sides by solving a convex quadratic optimization that maximizes vehicle-to-obstacle clearance. 3) Optionally impose constraints to preserve parallelism and smooth transitions from previous control steps. 4) Apply a feedback-linearizing controller to regulate distance to the bounding lines, achieving tracking of a local reference path that maintains safety via obstacle clearance maximization. 5) Provide open-source code. 6) Empirical evaluation comparing to exploration-based planners.

Result: Experimental results show that the proposed framework yields safer navigation paths with significantly shorter computation times relative to some existing exploration-based planners.

Conclusion: The approach offers a practical, real-time solution for safe local navigation in unknown environments for Ackermann-steered robots, combining obstacle-clearance optimization with simple tracking control and open-source availability. Limitations may include reliance on accurate obstacle sensing and lack of global planning guarantees.

Abstract: A control framework is proposed for safe local navigation of mobile robots equipped with Ackermann steering in unmapped environments where a global goal is absent. Based on local obstacle detections, the safest heading angle is determined along the direction of the largest open space ahead of the vehicle. Guided by this direction, bounding lines are constructed on the left and right sides of the vehicle to achieve obstacle separation. These bounding lines are obtained by solving a convex quadratic optimization that maximizes vehicle-to-obstacle clearance. Optionally, conditions are imposed on the bounding lines to preserve parallelism and smooth abrupt changes from prior control steps. A feedback-linearizing controller is then used to regulate the vehicle's distance from one or both bounding lines, effectively enabling tracking of a local reference path that preserves safety through obstacle clearance maximization. Open-source code is included for the application of this control scheme. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces safer navigation paths with significantly shorter computation times, compared to some existing exploration-based planners.

</details>


### [244] [ForEnt: A Multi-Modal Dataset for Characterizing Quadruped Robot Entrapments in Forest Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19675)
*Natapat Kirdwichai,Danesh Tarapore*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ForEnt is a multi-modal forest entrapment dataset for quadruped robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the lack of a dedicated dataset on forest entrapment and failure modes to analyze terrain factors and benchmark entrapment-detection methods.

Method: Data collected with the Unitree Go2 quadruped across eight forest sites in UK Southampton Common Woodlands. Approximately 1.7 km of traversals in 11 sequences, yielding 69 entrapment events. Multi-modal sensors: time-synchronized RGB-D, LiDAR, proprioceptive data, and third-person video.

Result: Dataset enables analysis of terrain factors contributing to entrapment and provides labeled sensor streams for reproducible benchmarking of entrapment-detection strategies.

Conclusion: ForEnt lowers the barrier to developing robust quadruped deployments in challenging forest environments.

Abstract: Legged robots are increasingly deployed in forests for ecological surveying and monitoring, yet their autonomy is often interrupted consequent to the challenges posed in traversing forest environments. Forest entrapments, for example, when a robot's legs are ensnared in vines or other vegetation, result in loss of stability and toppling. Such events not only disrupt the mission and require manual intervention, but also risk damage to the robot hardware. To address the absence of a dedicated dataset to investigate these failure modes in forest environments, we present ForEnt, a multi-modal dataset collected with the low-cost Unitree Go2 quadruped across eight forest sites in the Southampton Common Woodlands, UK. For our dataset, over approximately 1.7 km of traversals in 11 sequences were conducted, yielding 69 recorded entrapment events. ForEnt includes time-synchronized RGB-D images, LiDAR scans, proprioceptive data, and third-person video, enabling analysis of terrain factors contributing to entrapment and providing labeled sensor streams for reproducible benchmarking. By supporting the evaluation of entrapment detection strategies, ForEnt lowers the barrier to developing robust quadruped robot deployments in challenging forest environments.

</details>


### [245] [Route-Constrained Robust Fusion Estimation for MEMS/GNSS Integrated Navigation of Unmanned Ground Vehicles in GNSS Degraded Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19687)
*Jingzhi Cui,Chao Zhang,Yuliang Mao,Shaolin Lü,Dongmei Li,Huan Che,Rong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A robust route-constrained localization method for unmanned ground vehicles under GPS outages, using 2D route alignment and an EKF with pseudo-observations to enforce road-level constraints and suppress drift in tunnel environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GPS multipath and occlusion in structured road environments cause cumulative localization drift; there is a need for continuous, road-aligned localization during satellite outages.

Method: During GNSS outage, align historical dead-reckoning with HD-map route segments via a 2D rigid transformation to produce a route-referenced pseudo-position, which is fed into an Extended Kalman Filter. The approach injects road constraints into a unified state estimator and includes engineering strategies: trigger control, matching quality validation, route offset compensation, and single-update correction limiting.

Result: Experiments in long tunnel, multi-segment tunnel, and curved tunnel scenarios demonstrate reduced error accumulation, lower maximum deviation risk, and improved localization continuity and road-level usability.

Conclusion: Integrating route constraints into EKF via a route-referenced pseudo-observation enhances localization robustness during GNSS outages and improves adherence to the mission route.

Abstract: To address cumulative localization drift of unmanned ground vehicles in structured road environments under severe Global Navigation Satellite System signal occlusion, this paper proposes a robust route-constrained state estimation method. During periods without satellite signals, the proposed method establishes the correspondence between the historical dead reckoning trajectory and local segments of the mission route extracted from a high-definition map, and estimates a route-referenced position via a two-dimensional rigid transformation. The estimated position is then formulated as a pseudo-position observation and incorporated into an Extended Kalman Filter update. In this way, route constraints at the road level can be continuously injected into a unified state estimation framework, thereby suppressing position deviation relative to the mission route while indirectly improving azimuth estimation. To enhance practical applicability, engineering strategies, such as trigger control, matching quality validation, route offset compensation, and single update correction limiting, are further introduced. Experiments in three representative scenarios, including a long tunnel, a multi-segment tunnel, and a curved tunnel, show that the proposed method effectively suppresses error accumulation during satellite outages, reduces the risk of large maximum deviation, and improves localization continuity and road-level usability.

</details>


### [246] [Comparative Study on Agility, Efficiency, and Impact Absorption of Bipedal Robots with Active Toes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19699)
*Joong-Gil Kim,Wontae Ye,Geunwoo Cho,Seong-Ho Yun,Se-Hyoung Cho,Yong-Jae Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: 14-DOF biped robot with active toes; high-fidelity simulation shows toe actuation improves efficiency and agility, with reductions in cost of transport, heel-strike ground reaction force, and path deviation under toe-enabled vs toe-ablated configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Toes contribute to the efficiency, agility, and impact absorption observed in human locomotion; prior robotic toe implementations have not faithfully replicated human toe functions or rigorously validated their benefits.

Method: A 14-DOF biped robot emulates human toes’ lightweight, high-torque, robust nature. A high-fidelity simulation environment with coupled transmissions and accurate power consumption is built. An identical RL training procedure is applied to configurations with and without active toes to ensure fair comparison, using a minimal reward function.

Result: At 1.33 m/s walking, toe-equipped robot reduces CoT by 17.5% and heel-strike GRF by 5.0% compared with toe-ablated configuration. Agility improves with average path deviation down 25.0% and maximum path deviation down 34.0%.

Conclusion: Active toes can meaningfully enhance energetic efficiency and agility in humanoid-inspired robots under simulated conditions; benefits depend on actuator dynamics and training setup; hardware validation and broader-speed/terrain testing are warranted.

Abstract: Human legs exhibit high efficiency, agility, and impact absorption, with toes playing a crucial role in these capabilities. While many attempts have been made to implement human-like toes in robots, they have not fully replicated human characteristics nor rigorously validated their benefits. We propose a 14-DOF biped robot emulating human toes' lightweight, high-torque, robust nature. To quantitatively analyze the effectiveness of the active toes in terms of agility, efficiency, and impact absorption, we developed a high-fidelity simulation training environment that reflects actual actuators with coupled transmissions and accurate power consumption. To ensure a fair comparison between configurations with and without active toes, we designed a minimal RL reward function and applied an identical training procedure to both. The simulation results indicate that, at 1.33 m/s walking, the toe-equipped robot reduced CoT by 17.5% and heel-strike GRF by 5.0% compared with the toe-ablation configuration. On the agility test, average and maximum path deviation decreased by 25.0% and 34.0%, respectively.

</details>


### [247] [A Differentiable Composite Approximation Framework for Autonomous Underwater Vehicle Maneuvering Modeling from Sea-Trial Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19711)
*Aobo Wang,Aifei Xia,Zihao Wang,Lizhu Hao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A differentiable composite-approximation model for AUV maneuvering combining polynomial-basis and data-adaptive bases, with gradient-based co-calibration and current compensation, evaluated on sea-trial data showing improved trajectory and velocity predictions over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of fixed polynomial bases and separate data-adaptive models by integrating both into a single differentiable predictor, enabling joint calibration under realistic field conditions including ocean-current effects.

Method: Construct a composite predictor with a polynomial-basis component and a data-driven residual component; apply a gradient-based co-calibration with a sensitivity-aware mechanism to bound polynomial updates; incorporate a turning-motion-based current estimation and compensation to create current-compensated learning targets; train and test on sea-trial data from a 7-meter AUV across multiple maneuvers.

Result: The proposed method yields improved recursive trajectory and velocity predictions compared to polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrid baselines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling using a differentiable composite-approximation framework with current compensation; supports applicability to real-world autonomous operation.

Abstract: Field-based modeling from onboard measurements can produce autonomous underwater vehicle (AUV) maneuvering models that reflect real operating characteristics. From an approximation perspective, conventional maneuvering models use predefined constraint polynomial bases, whereas data-driven models use data-adaptive bases. Motivated by this basis-function view, this paper presents a differentiable composite-approximation formulation, in which the polynomial-basis component and the data-adaptive basis component are treated as differentiable parts of a single predictor and calibrated jointly. A gradient-based co-calibration method is developed for full-scale AUV maneuvering prediction, where a sensitivity-aware mechanism regulates bounded polynomial updates while the neural residual captures remaining nonlinear discrepancies under a shared prediction objective. To account for ocean-current effects in field data, a turning-motion-based current estimation and compensation procedure is incorporated to construct current-compensated learning targets for training and rollout. The framework is evaluated using sea-trial data collected from a 7-meter AUV under multiple maneuvering conditions. Results show that the proposed method improves recursive trajectory and velocity prediction compared with polynomial-only, neural-only, and frozen-prior hybrid baselines, demonstrating its applicability to field-data-based AUV maneuvering modeling.

</details>


### [248] [Bidirectional Tutoring for Developmental Motor Learning in Robots: Co-Developed Interaction Dynamics Support Stable Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19728)
*Rui Fukushima,Jun Tani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Bidirectional tutoring in robots constrains learning with prior experiences, yielding more coherent, generalizable motor policies than unidirectional tutoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test whether bidirectional social interaction guides developmental motor learning in robots, leveraging past experiences as priors to shape co-developed trajectories.

Method: Two experiments (human-robot and AI tutor with adaptive intervention) using a free-energy-principle neural network with generative replay to enable stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes; task: object manipulation on a physical humanoid robot; assessments: behavioral consistency and stage-wise generalization, with tutor guidance declining over time.

Result: Bidirectional tutoring produced more consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization; robots increasingly required less tutor input.

Conclusion:  Embodied, socially grounded bidirectional tutoring serves as an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots, promoting coherence and generalization.

Abstract: Infants are well known to develop their motor skills through dense interaction with caregivers. Although such social interaction is crucial for human development, motor-skill learning in robots is often treated as a unidirectional process in which robots passively receive demonstrations from tutors. This overlooks a key property of social interaction: it is inherently bidirectional, with tutor and learner dynamically adapting to each other. In such interactions, the robot's past experiences may function as prior constraints that shape the dynamics of their co-developed trajectories. We hypothesize that bidirectional tutoring allows such constraints to guide the formation of consistent behavioral patterns that preserve behavioral coherence and support generalization, whereas unidirectional interaction lacks such constraints and leads to broader, less consistent behavioral patterns. To examine this hypothesis, we conducted two experiments with a physical humanoid robot performing an object manipulation task: one involving human-robot interaction and another employing an AI tutor interacting with the real robot through an adaptive intervention mechanism designed to examine whether similar effects would emerge under more controlled conditions. We implement the developmental learning framework using a free-energy-principle-based neural network extended with generative replay, which supports stable sequence-by-sequence learning from single tutored episodes. Across both settings, bidirectional tutoring fostered consistent behaviors and stage-wise generalization, while the robot gradually required less tutor guidance. These results suggest that bidirectional tutoring, as an embodied and socially grounded approach, provides an effective scaffold for developmental motor learning in robots.

</details>


### [249] [VOiLA: Vectorized Online Planning with Learned Diffusion Model for POMDP Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19729)
*Marcus Hoerger,Rishikesh Joshi,Rahul Shome,Ian Manchester,Hanna Kurniawati*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VOiLA introduces a learned, diffusion-based POMDP modeling pipeline with GPU-accelerated online planning; distillation yields large sampling-speedups, enabling strong performance with limited data and successful sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planning under uncertainty with POMDPs requires faithful, tractable models. Obtaining accurate models is hard, and online planning demands efficiency for real-time operation.

Method: Learn task-agnostic POMDP models by training conditional diffusion-based samplers for state transitions and observations, and observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates. Distill diffusion samplers into compact feedforward generators and integrate them with VOPP, a GPU-friendly online POMDP planner.

Result: Distillation reduces sampling cost by up to ~1000×. VOiLA matches or exceeds Recurrent Soft Actor Critic using <10% of training data and generalizes better to unseen environments. In hardware, VOiLA succeeds in all 10 runs using models learned from simulated data.

Conclusion: Learned, generative POMDP models with diffusion-based training plus sampler distillation enable practical, real-time online planning under uncertainty and effective sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: Planning under uncertainty is an essential capability for autonomous robots. The Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) provides a powerful framework for such a capability. Although POMDP-based planning has advanced significantly, its application to real-world problems is often limited by the difficulty of obtaining faithful POMDP models. We present Vectorized Online planning wIth Learned diffusion model for POMDP Agents (VOiLA), a framework that learns task-agnostic POMDP models for online planning under uncertainty. VOiLA learns transition and observation samplers using conditional diffusion models and learns observation-likelihood models for particle-based belief updates. To enable efficient online planning, the diffusion samplers are distilled into compact feedforward generators and integrated with Vectorized Online POMDP Planner (VOPP), an online POMDP planner designed to leverage GPU parallelization. Experimental results indicate the distillation strategy reduces sampling cost by up to nearly three orders of magnitude, making learned generative POMDP models practical for online planning. Evaluation of VOiLA on three benchmark problems indicate that VOiLA achieves equal or better performance than Recurrent Soft Actor Critic while using less than 10% training data, and generalizes much better to unseen environment configurations. Physical robot evaluation indicates VOiLA uses the models learned using only simulated data and generates a policy that successfully accomplish the task in 10 of 10 runs.

</details>


### [250] [Temporal Self-Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19752)
*Yinsen Jia,Boyuan Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL) uses temporally efficient successful trajectories found during training as self-supervision to improve data efficiency, robustness, and fast-behavior revisitation in long-horizon robotic manipulation across 15 tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward shaping can bias learning toward dense rewards and may cause inefficient interaction or forget rare but efficient strategies in long-horizon manipulation. A scalable self-supervisory signal that encodes temporal efficiency is needed to complement or surpass manually engineered rewards.

Method: Mining temporally efficient successful trajectories during learning, deriving configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets from fast successes, and applying efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning to preserve and replay efficient behaviors for policy improvement.

Result: TSIL consistently improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful strategies, and robustness to unstable training conditions across 15 long-horizon manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Temporal structure of successful behavior provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning beyond reward shaping, enabling more efficient and robust long-horizon manipulation policies.

Abstract: Long-horizon robot manipulation policies trained with reward shaping can still exploit dense rewards through inefficient interaction, while rare efficient behaviors may be forgotten during training. We argue that temporal efficiency itself provides a powerful and underutilized source of self-supervision for reinforcement learning. We introduce Temporal Self-Imitation Learning (TSIL), a reinforcement learning framework that mines temporally efficient successful trajectories generated during learning and converts them into reusable supervision for future policy improvement. TSIL progressively refines learning using configuration-conditioned adaptive temporal targets derived from fast successful trajectories, while preserving and replaying efficient behaviors through efficiency-weighted self-imitation learning. Across 15 distinct long-horizon manipulation tasks, TSIL consistently improves learning efficiency, task-completion efficiency, revisitation of fast successful behaviors, and robustness to unstable training conditions. More broadly, our results suggest that the temporal structure of successful behavior itself provides a scalable self-supervisory signal for reinforcement learning beyond manually engineered reward shaping alone.

</details>


### [251] [Data Standards for Humanoid Robotics: The Missing Infrastructure for Physical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19769)
*Shaoshan Liu,Xiugong Qin,Xuan Wu,Xuan Xia,Ning Ding,Jialu Liu,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes humanoid robot data standards as foundational infrastructure for Physical AI, focusing on embodied interaction data, physical coherence, and diminishing non-cumulative data bottlenecks; outlines ISO-based framework with general lifecycle infrastructure and capability-specific domain grammars.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data standards are essential to enable cross-robot knowledge transfer, reuse across tasks and organizations, and scalable learning for Physical AI, addressing data scarcity, silos, and inconsistent evaluation.

Method: Conceptual analysis grounded in the development of ISO/WD 26264-1 within ISO/TC 299/WG 16; articulates three core insights; proposes a standards architecture for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, traceability, and domain grammars for manipulation, locomotion, HRI, and cognition; differentiates general versus capability-specific parts.

Result: Three central insights: (1) data are embodied interaction data requiring preservation of relationships among body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome; (2) data reuse depends on physical coherence (timing, frames, calibration, kinematics, units, synchronization) being inspectable; (3) bottlenecks include non-cumulative data from high collection costs, data silos, and inconsistent evaluation. Standards would render embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable, with a general horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management and domain-specific parts defining manipulation, locomotion, HRI, cognition, and future capabilities.

Conclusion: As AI shifts from digital interfaces to physical bodies, data standards must evolve to structure physical interaction, enabling scalable, interoperable, and interpretable embodied AI across robots and tasks.

Abstract: The scalability of humanoid robots will depend not only on models and hardware, but also on whether physical experience can accumulate across robots, tasks, organizations, and time. Drawing on the authors' work in developing ISO/WD 26264-1, Humanoid robot datasets -- Part 1: General requirements, within ISO/TC 299/WG 16, this article argues that data standards are becoming foundational infrastructure for Physical AI. We develop three insights. First, humanoid robot data is embodied interaction data, not a collection of isolated digital samples; a useful dataset must preserve the relationship among robot body, action, task, scene, execution trace, and outcome. Second, its value depends on physical coherence: multimodal streams are reusable only when timing, coordinate frames, calibration, kinematics, units, and synchronization assumptions remain inspectable. Third, the main bottleneck is not only data scarcity, but non-cumulative data caused by high collection costs, data silos, and inconsistent evaluation. We argue that humanoid robot data standards address these bottlenecks by making embodied experience interpretable, shareable, traceable, and reusable. A general standard should provide horizontal infrastructure for lifecycle management, metadata, provenance, quality, versioning, and traceability, while capability-specific parts should define domain grammar for manipulation, locomotion, human-robot interaction, cognition, and future humanoid capabilities. As AI moves from screens into bodies, data standards must evolve from organizing digital information to structuring physical interaction.
