</details>


### [252] [Start Right, Arrive Right: Asynchronous Execution via Initial Noise Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19774)
*Trong-Bao Ho,Quang-Tan Nguyen,Thien-Loc Ha,Gia-Binh Nguyen,Viet-Thanh Nguyen,Long Dinh,Minh N. Vu,Duy M. H. Nguyen,An Thai Le,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PAINT is a training-free method for asynchronous action chunking in flow-based policies. By backward Euler inversion, it selects an initial noise to enable coherent next action chunk without modifying the flow ODE, using a repainting rule. No gradients or retraining required; improves execution consistency and task performance across 12 simulated benchmarks and 6 real-world tasks across multiple embodiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time control with flow-based policies suffers latency during asynchronous execution, causing chunk-boundary inconsistencies. Existing methods steer generation toward the executed prefix; there is a need for a lightweight, training-free approach that preserves policy dynamics without retraining.

Method: Backward Euler inversion to identify suitable initial noise for the next chunk, followed by a repainting rule to construct the final chunk. reframes asynchronous inference as a noise-selection problem rather than trajectory steering. No gradients, retraining, or policy modification required.

Result: Improved execution consistency and task performance across 12 simulated benchmarks and 6 real-world manipulation tasks spanning single-arm, bimanual, and humanoid embodiments.

Conclusion: Noise-based initialisation via backward Euler inversion enables coherent next chunks without modifying the flow-based policy or requiring training, suggesting broad applicability for asynchronous action chunking in robotics.

Abstract: Action chunking enables robot policies to produce temporally coherent behavior, but generating multi-step action sequences with flow-based policies incurs latency that is incompatible with real-time control. Under asynchronous execution, the robot continues executing the current chunk while the next one is generated, causing even minor delays to create inconsistencies at chunk boundaries. Existing methods address this problem by steering generation toward the already executed action prefix. We instead show that prefix consistency can be achieved by selecting an appropriate initial noise before generation begins, allowing the unmodified flow ODE to produce a coherent next chunk. This reframes asynchronous inference as a noise selection problem rather than a trajectory steering problem. We introduce \textbf{PAINT}, a training-free method that finds this noise via backward Euler inversion and constructs the final chunk through a repainting rule. In summary, \texttt{PAINT} requires no gradients, retraining, or policy modification; yet it improves execution consistency and task performance across \textit{12 simulated benchmarks} and \textit{6 real-world manipulation tasks} spanning single-arm, bimanual, and humanoid embodiments. Website: ~\href{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}{\texttt{https://paint-action-chunking.github.io}}.

</details>


### [253] [EquiVLA: A General Framework for Rotationally Equivariant Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19784)
*Thien-Loc Ha,Quang-Tan Nguyen,Trong-Bao Ho,Long Dinh,Minh Duc Nguyen,Gia-Binh Nguyen,Pham Tri Quang,Minh N. Vu,Duy M. H. Nguyen,An Thai Le,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EquiVLA introduces SO(2)-equivariant vision-language-action models for generalist robot manipulation, with EquiPerceptor and EquiActor enabling approximate/exact equivariance from observations to actions, improving rotation generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of geometric inductive biases in VLA systems, which causes poor generalization across rotational configurations and higher data requirements.

Method: Propose a general framework EquiVLA that combines EquiPerceptor (approximate SO(2)-equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT features) and EquiActor (exact SO(2)-equivariant flow-matching Diffusion Transformer for action heads), applicable to any architecture with a frozen vision-language backbone and flow-matching diffusion action head; instantiated on GR00T N1.5; evaluates on multiple LIBERO suites, CALVIN, and real-robot tasks.

Result:  achieves 92.6% average success on LIBERO versus 78.1% baseline; average sequence length 4.03 on CALVIN vs 3.45; real-robot success rises from 54% to 72%.

Conclusion: Incorporating SO(2) geometric inductive biases via EquiPerceptor and EquiActor yields substantial gains in rotational generalization, data efficiency, and real-robot performance for VLA-based robotic manipulation, and the framework is broadly applicable to frozen backbones.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a powerful paradigm for generalist robot manipulation, yet they lack geometric inductive biases: policies trained at specific orientations require substantially more data to generalize across rotational configurations. We present \textsc{EquiVLA}, the first general framework for end-to-end $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant VLA models, applicable to any architecture coupling a frozen vision-language backbone with a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. \textsc{EquiVLA} introduces \textsc{EquiPerceptor}, which produces approximately $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant visual representations from frozen ViT features; and \textsc{EquiActor}, an exactly $\mathrm{SO}(2)$-equivariant flow-matching Diffusion Transformer action head. Together, they establish an approximate $\mathrm{SO}(2)$ equivariance chain from camera observations to predicted action sequences. Instantiated on GR00T~N1.5 and evaluated across four LIBERO suites, CALVIN ABCD$\to$D, and five real-robot tasks on Mobile ALOHA, \textsc{EquiVLA} achieves $92.6\%$ average success on LIBERO (vs. $78.1\%$ baseline), an average sequence length of $4.03$ on CALVIN (vs. $3.45$), and improves real-robot success from $54\%$ to $72\%$.

</details>


### [254] [TIDY: Thermal Infrared Image Denoising via Wavelet Domain Entropy and Directional Stripe Index](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19813)
*Tai Hyoung Rhee,Dong-Guw Lee,Ayoung Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A real-data trained, lightweight wavelet-domain denoiser (TIDY) for thermal infrared imaging that speeds up denoising to ~34 Hz, improves robustness against indoor noise and stripe artifacts, and enhances downstream robotics tasks; introduces Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index metrics; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Indoor thermal imaging suffers from severe stochastic and fixed-pattern noise, low contrast, which degrades perception; existing denoisers are either slow for online robotics or trained on synthetic noise, limiting robustness.

Method: Formulates denoising in the wavelet domain to separate noise from structural content; train on real clean-noisy TIR pairs; lightweight architecture with reduced spatial complexity; introduces two loss terms—Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index—to suppress stochastic noise and stripe artifacts; achieves fast inference (~34 Hz).

Result: Demonstrates robustness under severe indoor corruption and zero-shot settings; yields consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks such as thermal inertial odometry and monocular depth estimation; code and dataset available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: TIDY shows that wavelet-domain, real-data–driven denoising can deliver fast, robust TIR perception for robotics, with specialized metrics aiding noise and stripe artifact suppression; supports online deployment in challenging indoor environments.

Abstract: Thermal infrared (TIR) imaging has been a popular choice for field robotics due to its robust perception capability under low light visual degradation, but it suffers from severe stochastic and fixed-pattern noise that breaks downstream estimation. This noise is intensified indoors due to low thermal contrast and uniform temperature distributions, contributing to the relative lack of indoor TIR deployments. Existing TIR denoising methods exhibit a poor accuracy-efficiency tradeoff, either too slow for online deployment required in robotics or insufficiently robust to severe degradation, while typically being trained on synthetic noise. Addressing these problems, we propose TIDY, a lightweight wavelet-domain denoiser trained on real clean-noisy TIR data. By reformulating TIR denoising in the wavelet domain, TIDY explicitly disentangles noise from structural content, enabling targeted suppression with reduced spatial complexity, significantly improving inference speed over prior methods (~34Hz). TIDY introduces two new metrics, Wavelet Entropy and Wavelet Directional Stripe Index, as complementary loss terms to explicitly suppress stochastic noise and stripe artifacts. Across severe indoor corruption and zero-shot settings, TIDY improves robustness and yields consistent gains in downstream robotics tasks including thermal inertial odometry and monocular depth estimation. Code and dataset is available at: https://github.com/williamrheeth/TIDY

</details>


### [255] [World Engine: Towards the Era of Post-Training for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19836)
*Tianyu Li,Li Chen,Caojun Wang,Haochen Liu,Kashyap Chitta,Zhenjie Yang,Yuhang Lu,Naisheng Ye,Yihang Qiu,Yufei Wang,Luoxi Zou,Jiaxin Peng,Jin Pan,Zhaoyu Su,Andrei Bursuc,Shengbo Eben Li,Andreas Geiger,Peng Su,Hongyang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: World Engine synthesizes high-stakes driving interactions from real logs to enable post-training RL that aligns policies with safety constraints, yielding fewer rare-event failures and safer deployment; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical long-tail events are scarce in real-world driving data, limiting reliability and safety guarantees. Demonstrating a scalable way to expose policies to hazardous interactions without risking real-world deployment.

Method: Reconstruct high-fidelity interactive driving environments from real-world logs and extrapolate them into safety-critical variations. Use reinforcement-based post-training to align policies with safety constraints within these synthesized environments.

Result: On a nuPlan-based benchmark, the approach substantially reduces failures in rare safety-critical scenarios and outperforms data-scale improvements alone. In production-scale deployment, it reduces simulated collisions and improves on-road testing.

Conclusion: Post-training on synthesized, safety-critical interactions offers a scalable and effective pathway to safer autonomous driving by enabling safe exploration and alignment with safety constraints, with code released to the public.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles must operate safely in the real world, where errors can have severe consequences. Although modern end-to-end driving policies excel in routine scenarios, their reliability is limited by the scarcity of safety-critical ``long-tail'' events in real driving datasets. These rare interactions define the practical safety boundary of the learned policy, yet they are difficult to collect at scale in the real world. Here we show that this fundamental limitation can be addressed by post-training pre-trained driving models on synthesized high-stakes interactions. We introduce World Engine, a generative framework that reconstructs high-fidelity interactive environments from real-world logs and systematically extrapolates them into realistic safety-critical variations. This paradigm enables reinforcement-based post-training to align policies with safety constraints, circumventing the physical risks inherent in real-world exploration. On a public benchmark built on nuPlan, World Engine substantially reduces failures in rare safety-critical scenarios and yields significantly larger gains than scaling pre-training data alone. Furthermore, when deployed on a production-scale autonomous driving system, the resulting policy reduces simulated collisions and demonstrates measurable improvements in on-road testing, showing that post-training on synthesized, safety-critical interactions offers a scalable and effective pathway to safer autonomous driving. The full codebase suite, including training, is released to the public.

</details>


### [256] [MMD-SLAM: Structure-Enhanced Multi-Meta Gaussian Distribution-Guided Visual SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19874)
*Fan Zhu,Ziyu Chen,Peichen Liu,Yifan Zhao,Zhisong Xu,Hui Zhu,Hongxing Zhou,Sixun Liu,Chunmao Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MMD-SLAM introduces structure-aware 3D Gaussian Splatting-based Visual SLAM using Atlanta World priors via a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation and a point-line fusion for pose optimization and a Gaussian evolution strategy, achieving state-of-the-art tracking and mapping results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of existing 3D Gaussian Splatting SLAM methods that underutilize structural information, leading to lower rendering quality and inconsistent maps; leveraging Atlanta World structural priors to improve pose estimation, mapping fidelity, and global optimization.

Method: 1) Point-line fusion for pose optimization by incorporating 3D line segments to strengthen tracking and mapping constraints. 2) Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions encoding AW priors to capture structural information. 3) Gaussian evolution strategy adapting to scene geometry and integrating structural cues into global optimization.

Result: Demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in tracking accuracy and mapping quality. Quantitatively, achieves a 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet and a 5.71% improvement in PSNR on Replica relative to MonoGS.

Conclusion: Integrating AW-based structural priors and explicit geometric features into a 3DGS-SLAM framework yields substantial gains in both pose robustness and map fidelity, enabling more photorealistic rendering and consistent maps in challenging scenes.

Abstract: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has significantly boosted novel view synthesis and high-fidelity scene reconstruction, expanding the potential of 3DGS-based Visual Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) methods. However, most existing systems fail to fully exploit the underlying structural information, which limits rendering quality and often leads to inconsistent maps. To address these limitations, we propose MMD-SLAM, a structure-enhanced Visual SLAM framework that leverages the Atlanta World (AW) assumption to guide a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation for photorealistic mapping. First, we introduce a point-line fusion strategy for pose optimization, where 3D line segments are incorporated to improve tracking robustness and provide additional constraints for mapping. Second, we design a Multi-Meta Gaussian representation with dominant directions, explicitly encoding structural priors from the AW hypothesis. Finally, we propose a Gaussian evolution strategy that adapts to scene geometry and incorporates structural cues into global optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that these innovations enable MMD-SLAM to achieve state-of-the-art performance in both tracking accuracy and mapping quality. e.g., our method achieves a 48.56% reduction in ATE RMSE on ScanNet and a 5.71% improvement in PSNR on Replica, compared with MonoGS.

</details>


### [257] [One-to-Two Acting: A Novel Framework for Single-arm Agent Action Expansion to Dual Arms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19897)
*Youbin Yao,Nieqin Cao,Mingyan Li,Yan Ding,Fuqiang Gu,Chao Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ExS2D is a hierarchical action expansion framework enabling dual-arm manipulation from single-arm supervision. It generates subtasks from text with temporal precedence, grounds subtasks into executable actions, and uses a multimodal LLM-driven coordinator for precedence-aware, collision-free dual-arm planning. It achieves a 54.4% reduction in average execution steps versus a single-arm baseline and demonstrates reliable dual-arm execution on real robots from few-shot single-arm examples without bimanual demonstrations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dual-arm manipulation offers higher throughput but bimanual demonstrations are costly to collect. A teaching-from-single-arm supervision framework is desirable to enable efficient, reliable dual-arm coordination.

Method: ExS2D comprises: (1) generation of structured subtasks from textual instructions with explicit temporal precedence; (2) subtask-guided grounding of each subtask into executable actions through action mapping based on current observations; (3) a coordination step where a multimodal large language model performs precedence-aware action allocation and synchronized planning to select collision-free dual-arm executions.

Result: In simulation, ExS2D reduced average execution steps by 54.4% while maintaining a comparable success rate to a single-arm baseline. Real-robot experiments on four tasks show reliable dual-arm execution under few-shot single-arm samples, with zero bimanual demonstrations.

Conclusion: ExS2D enables efficient dual-arm manipulation from single-arm supervision, delivering substantial runtime efficiency and practical reliability, demonstrating the viability of hierarchy-based task expansion and LLM-driven coordination for dual-arm systems.

Abstract: Dual-arm manipulation can improve throughput via parallel execution, but collecting bimanual demonstrations for training is costly and difficult. We present ExS2D, a hierarchical action expansion framework that enables dual-arm manipulation from single-arm supervision. ExS2D first generates structured subtasks from textual instructions while explicitly capturing temporal precedence. It then grounds each subtask into executable actions through subtask-guided action mapping in observation. Finally, precedence-aware action allocation and synchronized planning are performed by a multimodal large language model driven coordinator to select collision-free dual-arm executions. Simulation experiments demonstrate that ExS2D reduces the average execution steps by 54.4% while maintaining a comparable success rate to a single-arm baseline. Real-robot experiments on four tasks further demonstrate the reliability of ExS2D for dual-arm execution under few-shot single-arm samples, while using zero bimanual demonstrations.

</details>


### [258] [Co-policy: Responsive Human-Robot Co-Creation for Musical Performances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19914)
*Xuetao Li,Wenke Huang,Mang Ye,Zijian Liu,Jinhua Xie,Jifeng Xuan,Miao Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Co-policy offers a three-tier framework for human-robot musical co-creation that separates semantic grounding, constrained musical variation, and visuomotor execution. It uses a fine-tuned F-Qwen planner with semantic anchors to turn speech, seeds, and visuals into structured plans, and a Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy for low-latency, single-pass action generation from notes and context. Real-robot experiments show improved alignment, accuracy, and response rate over diffusion-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The core challenge is linking high-level musical semantics with real-time, physically executable robot actions in embodied, interactive co-creation, requiring low latency and robust grounding of intent.

Method: Propose Co-policy architecture: (1) semantic grounding via pre-inference anchors and a fine-tuned Qwen-vl planner (F-Qwen) to convert multimodal input into structured co-creation plans; (2) constrained musical variation; (3) Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP), a conditional mixture-density policy mapping target notes and visual context to multimodal robot actions in a single forward pass; evaluation via real-robot chime experiments, ablations, and expert scoring, comparing against diffusion-policy baselines.

Result: Demonstrates improved intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency compared with diffusion-policy and ablated baselines in real-robot experiments, supporting the importance of physically grounded action generation for embodied human-AI co-creation.

Conclusion: Physically grounded action generation is a key requirement for embodied human-AI musical co-creation. The separation of semantic grounding, constrained variation, and visuomotor execution enables low-latency, coherent, and controllable robotic musical responses.

Abstract: Art has long stood as a pivotal expression of human creativity. Embodied artificial intelligence offers a route for generative models to participate in that creativity through physical action rather than disembodied digital content. In robotic music co-creation, it is challenging to connect semantic musical understanding with real-time and physically executable performance. We present Co-policy, a framework for human-robot musical co-creation that separates semantic intent grounding, constrained musical variation, and visuomotor execution. To ground musical semantics, Co-policy uses pre-inference semantic anchors and a fine-tuned Qwen-vl planner (F-Qwen) to transform speech, live musical seeds, and visual observations into structured co-creation plans. To support low-latency execution, Co-policy introduces a Gaussian-Mixture Visuomotor Policy (GMP), implemented as a conditional mixture-density policy that maps target notes and visual context to multimodal robot actions in a single forward pass. Unlike robotic playback systems that merely reproduce user-specified notes, Co-policy generates complementary musical responses under both musical and physical constraints. Real-robot chime experiments, ablations, and expert evaluation show improved intent alignment, execution accuracy, and response frequency over diffusion-policy and ablated baselines, supporting physically grounded action generation as a key requirement for embodied human-AI co-creation.

</details>


### [259] [Deep-Unfolded Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19920)
*Hunter Kuperman,Minchan Jung,Rahul V. Ghosh,Alex Oshin,Evangelos A. Theodorou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A deep-unfolding framework, Deep Coordinator, learns to adapt ADMM-DDP hyperparameters online for distributed robotics, using unsupervised training to avoid degeneracy; it yields 6.18–9.44x speedups and scales to 8x larger systems while preserving trajectory quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter tuning in distributed, non-convex optimization for multi-agent robotics is difficult, problem-specific, and penalty parameters of ADMM-DDP significantly affect performance; supervised training can fail due to degenerate solutions.

Method: Unroll a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network. Insert learnable mappings between layers that take the optimizer state and output the next hyperparameters (penalties). Propose an unsupervised learning scheme to train the hyperparameter predictor, avoiding reliance on ground-truth labels.

Result: Simulations with fleets of cars and quadrotors show trajectories of comparable quality achieved 6.18–9.44x faster than conventional solvers. Performance benefits persist when deployed to systems up to 8x larger than the training regime.

Conclusion: Deep Coordinator is the first deep-unfolding framework to adapt penalty parameters of a non-convex optimizer at solve-time; unsupervised training mitigates degeneracy in supervised approaches; demonstrates substantial speedups and scalable performance in distributed robotics optimization.

Abstract: Distributed optimization is a highly scalable and structurally transparent technique to solve multi-agent robotics problems; however, such methods often suffer from the need for highly-specialized, problem-specific hyperparameter tunings. In this work, we propose Deep Coordinator, a deep-unfolding framework that learns to dynamically adjust the hyperparameters of ADMM-DDP, a popular distributed solver for robotics tasks, at solve-time in response to optimizer performance. Our architecture consists of unrolling a fixed number of ADMM-DDP iterations into a neural network with learnable functions between layers mapping the optimizer state to the next hyperparameters. To the best of our knowledge, Deep Coordinator is the first deep-unfolding framework to adapt the penalty parameters of a non-convex optimizer at solve-time; we show that the mainstream supervised approach can yield degenerate solutions when training such models, and propose an unsupervised learning scheme. On simulations with fleets of cars and quadrotors, Deep Coordinator produces trajectories of comparable quality 6.18-9.44x faster than conventional solvers. Furthermore, Deep Coordinator retains its performance benefits when deployed to systems up to 8x larger than trained on.

</details>


### [260] [SWAP: Symmetric Equivariant World-Model for Agile Robot Parkour](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19928)
*Kaixin Lan,Ze Wang,Hongyi Li,Lei Jiang,Chaojie Fu,Chengkai Su,Choi Lam Wong,Yongbin Jin,Hongtao Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SWAP is an end-to-end equivariant world model for legged locomotion that builds symmetry into both the world model and actor-critic networks to remove redundant left-right patterns, enabling efficient latent representations and strong geometric generalization and zero-shot transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome inefficiency in purely data-driven latent world models that redundantly encode symmetric interactions, hindering geometric regularities and downstream policy learning.

Method: Introduce SWAP, an end-to-end equivariant symmetric world model that embeds symmetry into both the world model and the actor-critic networks, enabling shared symmetry-aware representations.

Result: Real-world quadruped parkour achievements: 2.13 m gap jump and 1.63 m platform climb; records for quadruped parkour; strong generalization to unseen mirrored terrains and zero-shot transfer across diverse outdoor environments.

Conclusion: Symmetry equivariance serves as an effective structural prior to extend the physical capabilities of learned legged locomotion via more efficient representation and robust transfer.

Abstract: While latent world models enable the proactive predictions required for extreme parkour, their purely data-driven nature forces them to redundantly encode left-right symmetric interactions as independent patterns. This inflates the learning burden and hinders the capture of geometric regularities, restricting the latent space's efficiency for downstream policies. To address this, we propose SWAP, an end-to-end equivariant symmetric world model. This framework embeds symmetry directly into both the world model and the actor-critic networks. In real-world tests, the robot leaps across a 2.13 m gap and climbs a 1.63 m platform, breaking records for quadruped parkour. Furthermore, the framework exhibits robust geometric generalization to unseen mirrored terrains and exceptional zero-shot transferability across diverse outdoor environments. These results demonstrate that symmetry equivariance is an effective structural prior for pushing the physical boundaries of learned legged locomotion.

</details>


### [261] [Motor Angular Speed Preintegration for Multirotor UAV State Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19929)
*Matěj Petrlík,Filip Novák,Robert Pěnička,Martin Saska*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel UAV state estimation approach (MAS-LO) preintegrates accelerations derived from motor speeds, removing dependence on the IMU for propagation and fusing with LiDAR in a factor graph to achieve improved accuracy and robustness; claims 28% pos, 65% vel improvements, 14% less lag vs LIO-SAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IMU data are corrupted by propeller vibrations reducing accuracy; need higher-frequency, robust state propagation without IMU; utilize motor speeds as a source of acceleration information.

Method: Compute accelerations from motor speed data; preintegrate these accelerations to propagate state; construct a factor in a graph-based optimization using these preintegrated motor speed terms; combine with LiDAR measurements; implement MAS-LO; open-source.

Result: Compared to LIO-SAM, MAS-LO improves position accuracy by 28%, velocity by 65%, reduces measurement lag by 14%; demonstrates robustness to wrong parameter values.

Conclusion: Motor-speed-based preintegration is viable for high-precision UAV odometry, enabling more robust and accurate state estimation in agile flight, reducing reliance on IMU; MAS-LO is effective and open-source.

Abstract: A precise state estimate is crucial for a tight feedback control that enables agile and near-obstacle flights of UAVs. The state-of-the-art methods fuse slow pose measurements with high-frequency inertial measurements to obtain a precise state estimate. However, the inertial measurements from the IMU onboard the UAV are degraded by vibrations from spinning propellers and the precision of the estimated state suffers. We propose a novel approach based on the preintegration of accelerations obtained from motor speeds. We show that the accelerations obtained in this manner can be used for state propagation on their own to achieve better precision without including the IMU. Further, we propose a factor composed of the preintegrated motor speeds that can be directly employed in factor graph optimization frameworks. We combine our factor with LiDAR measurements into the proposed Motor Angular Speed LiDAR Odometry (MAS-LO) algorithm for precise state estimation, which we open-source. Lastly, we evaluate the estimation precision against a state-of-the-art inertial algorithm LIO-SAM to show 28% improvement in position and 65% in velocity estimation accuracy, 14% lower measurement lag, and high robustness to wrong parameter values.

</details>


### [262] [Evaluation of Augmented Reality-based Intuitive Interface for Robot-Assisted Transesophageal Echocardiography: A User Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19971)
*Xiu Zhang*,Matteo Di Mauro*,Sofia Breschi,Angela Peloso,Emiliano Votta,Arianna Menciassi,Elena De Momi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AR-based intuitive interface for robot-assisted TEE improves spatial accuracy and control. Compared 2D-JI, 3D-JI, 3D-TI; 3D-TI with tip-level control and 3D visualization most effective; potential to enhance operator performance and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of designing intuitive interfaces for robot-assisted TEE to reduce operator workload, improve spatial awareness, and enhance safety and efficiency during SHD interventions.

Method: Robotic TEE platform with electromagnetic tracking and a virtual simulator evaluated across three interfaces (2D-JI, 3D-JI, 3D-TI) in 36 participants performing standardized navigation tasks to reach target echocardiographic views. Metrics: position/orientation errors, completion time, NASA-TLX.

Result: 3D visualization significantly improved spatial accuracy (median position error reduced from 13 mm to 3 mm; orientation error halved). Tip-level interaction reduced orientation error by ~50% and decreased interuser variability compared to joint-level control. Overall, 3D-TI was the most effective and ergonomic interface.

Conclusion: AR-based visualization combined with intuitive tip-level control enhances operator performance and procedural safety in robot-assisted TEE and supports integrating such interfaces into next-generation robotic TEE systems.

Abstract: TransEsophageal Echocardiography (TEE) is essential for diagnosing and guiding Structural Heart Disease (SHD) interventions. However, manual TEE manipulation demands significant operator expertise, is physically demanding, and exposes clinicians to radiation when performed alongside fluoroscopy. Robotic-assisted TEE systems have been introduced to improve probe handling and reduce operator fatigue, yet the design of intuitive and effective user interfaces remains an open challenge. This study presents and evaluates a model-enhanced, Augmented Reality (AR)-based intuitive interface for robot-assisted TEE, designed to improve spatial awareness and control intuitiveness. A robotic TEE platform integrated with electromagnetic tracking and a virtual simulator was used to compare three user interfaces differing in visualization and interaction modalities: 2D jointlevel (2D-JI), 3D joint-level (3D-JI), and 3D tip-level (3D-TI). Thirty six participants performed standardized navigation tasks to reproduce target echocardiographic views, with performance assessed via position and orientation errors, completion time, and NASA-TLX workload scores. Results show that 3D visualization significantly improved spatial accuracy, reducing median position error from 13 mm to 3 mm and halving the orientation error compared with the 2D interface. Tip-level interaction yielded a further 50% reduction in orientation error and reduced interuser variability relative to joint-level control. Overall, the 3D-TI configuration, combining immersive visualization with direct tip-level control, proved the most effective and ergonomic interface, supporting the integration of AR-based visualization and intuitive control paradigms into next-generation robotic TEE systems to enhance operator performance and procedural safety.

</details>


### [263] [Tri-Info: Generalizable, Interpretable Failure Prediction for VLA Models via Information Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19998)
*Jinghan Yang,Yunchao Zhang,Wang Yuan,Haolun Wan,Jiaming Zhang,Zhengyang Hu,Yanchao Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Tri-Info: a set of triple information-theoretic signals designed to detect failures in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by analyzing action diversity, temporal consistency, and coupling to state transitions. It forms a closed-loop information pipeline to classify successful vs failed rollouts; demonstrates strong in-domain performance and zero-shot cross-domain transfer (including sim-to-real) with up to 83% real-world accuracy, outperforming prior detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety and interpretability of VLA models are critical as they operate in real, potentially harmful environments. Black-box policies lack generalizable failure detectors; a simple, interpretable diagnostic that generalizes across architectures and domains is highly desirable.

Method: Formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline. Derive triple information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals that quantify action diversity, temporal consistency, and coupling to state transitions. Evaluate across six VLA models and three benchmark environments, then test cross-domain generalization (architecture, environment, sim-to-real) without retraining.

Result: Tri-Info matches the strongest baselines in-domain. It transfers across architectures, environments, and sim-to-real without retraining, achieving 83% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors collapse to chance.

Conclusion: Tri-Info provides a simple, powerful, and interpretable diagnostic framework for VLA failures with strong cross-domain generalization, enabling robust failure detection and insight into underlying failure modes.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are increasingly deployed across diverse tasks, yet they remain black boxes whose physical interactions can cause irreversible harm, making generalizable and interpretable failure detection essential. We observe that successful and failed rollouts carry systematically different information-theoretic signatures. Building on this, we formalize VLA control as a closed-loop information pipeline and derive the Triple Information-theoretic (Tri-Info) signals that capture whether actions remain diverse, temporally consistent, and coupled to state transitions. Across six VLA models and three benchmark environments, Tri-Info matches the strongest baselines in-domain. Moreover, Tri-Info transfers across architectures, environments, and the sim-to-real gap without retraining, reaching 83\% accuracy on real-world tasks where prior detectors collapse to chance. This establishes Tri-Info as a simple yet powerful method that not only detects failures with strong cross-domain generalization, but also delivers interpretable diagnostics of the underlying failure modes.

</details>


### [264] [A Neuromorphic Reinforcement Learning Framework for Efficient Pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20031)
*Junzhe Xu,Zecui Zeng,Lusong Li,Yuetong Fang,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Energy-efficient neuromorphic deployment for RMFS pathfinding via ANN-to-SNN conversion; achieves massive energy savings and latency reduction while preserving policy quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RMFS pathfinding under dynamic environments requires fast, low-power inference on constrained hardware; existing RL approaches face energy and latency barriers on resource-limited devices.

Method: Train full-precision ANN policy with collision-allowing densification; convert to spiking neural network using hard-label knowledge distillation; deploy on neuromorphic hardware with event-driven computation; evaluate against GPU baseline.

Result: Up to 11,281x energy savings; ~2x latency reduction; decision quality comparable to original policy.

Conclusion: Physical neuromorphic inference is a practical, energy-sustainable path for large-scale RMFS operations; demonstrates end-to-end policy deployment from ANN to SNN.

Abstract: Dynamic environmental changes, confined workspaces, and stringent real-time constraints make pathfinding in Robotic Mobile Fulfillment Systems (RMFS) a challenging problem for conventional search- and rule-based methods, which typically suffer from high computational complexity and long decision latency. While reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful alternative, deploying learned policies with extreme energy efficiency on resource-constrained hardware remains an open challenge. We present SDQN-RMFS, an end-to-end framework that achieves high-fidelity deployment of an RL-trained policy from a full-precision artificial neural network (ANN) through to a neuromorphic chip. By computing only when triggered by sparse events, this framework unlocks ultra-low-power RMFS pathfinding. Our full-stack pipeline operates as follows: an ANN policy is first efficiently trained via a collision-allowing strategy to densify informative trajectories, and then converted into a spiking neural network (SNN) via a hard-label knowledge distillation approach. This effectively addresses the output distribution mismatch, preserving policy capability across the ANN-to-SNN pipeline while substantially reducing inference latency. Hardware experiments demonstrate up to 11,281$\times$ energy savings and a nearly two-fold reduction in latency compared to a high-performance GPU baseline, while maintaining decision quality on par with the original trained policy. These results establish physical neuromorphic inference as a practical and energy-sustainable pathway for large-scale RMFS operations.

</details>


### [265] [MirrorDuo: Reflection-Consistent Visuomotor Learning from Mirrored Demonstration Pairs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20048)
*Zheyu Zhuang,Ruiyu Wang,Giovanni Luca Marchetti,Florian T. Pokorny,Danica Kragic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Mirror-based data augmentation and prior leveraging reflection symmetry to generate mirrored demonstrations for image-based behavior cloning, enabling 'collect one, get one for free' and improving data efficiency, including zero/few-shot transfer to mirrored workspaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce data collection cost and improve generalization across workspace variations by exploiting reflection symmetry between original and mirrored workspaces.

Method: Generate mirrored counterparts for each demonstration across image, proprioception, and full 6-DoF end-effector actions; apply as data augmentation, diffusion-policy integration, or as a structural prior for reflection-equivariant networks; exploit overlap between original and mirrored domains.

Result: Improved performance under the same data budget when demonstrations are balanced across both sides; enables transfer to mirrored workspace with as few as zero or five demos in target arrangement.

Conclusion: MirrorDuo offers a practical symmetry-based approach to data-efficient imitation learning and policy learning, reducing data collection burden and enabling cross-workspace transfer.

Abstract: Image-based behaviour cloning leverages demonstrations captured from ubiquitous RGB cameras. However, it remains constrained by the cost of collecting diverse demos, especially for generalizing across workspace variations. We propose MirrorDuo, a reflection-based formulation that operates on image, proprioception, and full 6-DoF end-effector action tuples, generating a mirrored counterpart for each original demonstration, effectively achieving "collect one, get one for free". It can be applied as a data augmentation strategy for existing learning pipelines, such as standard behaviour cloning or diffusion policy, or as a structural prior for reflection-equivariant policy networks. By leveraging the overlap between the original and mirrored domains, MirrorDuo achieves significantly improved performance under the same data budget when demonstrations are evenly distributed across both sides of the workspace. When demonstrations are confined to one side, MirrorDuo enables efficient skill transfer to the mirrored workspace with as few as zero or five demos in the target arrangement.

</details>


### [266] [VFILC: Accurate Frequency Extrapolations in Imitation Learning via Sampling Frequency ILC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20056)
*Nozomu Masuya,Toshiaki Tsuji,Sho Sakaino*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: VFILC combines variable-frequency imitation learning with iterative learning control to reduce frequency errors during speed extrapolation, improving accuracy over VFIL, including at doubled speeds across three tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome open-loop frequency errors and instability of VFIL when extrapolating speeds, enabling accurate imitation across a broader speed range and in friction-rich, contact-heavy tasks.

Method: Integrate VFIL (variable-frequency imitation learning) with iterative learning control (ILC) that includes feedforward (from VFIL) and feedback components to correct frequency errors iteratively.

Result: Extrapolated speeds successfully with reduced frequency errors across three tasks; feedback path lowered frequency errors by 81% (wiping) and 50% (shaking) at twice the training speed; 27% accuracy improvement over VFIL at interpolated frequency for a friction-affected mixing task.

Conclusion: VFILC effectively leverages VFIL for speed extrapolation while using ILC to correct frequency errors, yielding improved accuracy and robustness across extrapolated speeds and frictional tasks.

Abstract: Conventional neural network (NN)-based imitation learning methods for variable-speed motion either restricted their scope to interpolated speeds, or generated unpredictable motions when extrapolating beyond trained velocity ranges. Variable-frequency imitation learning (VFIL) enabled extrapolations of speeds by linking the NN model's sampling frequency to the motion frequency, whereas its open-loop configuration caused frequency errors, especially in the extrapolated high-frequency settings. This study proposes variable-frequency imitation learning with iterative learning control (VFILC) based on a combination of VFIL and iterative learning control (ILC) with both feedforward and feedback parts, the former taking advantage of VFIL and the latter adjusting the frequency errors. The experimental results showed that the proposed method successfully and accurately extrapolated motion speeds and reduced frequency errors in all three tasks, and that the feedback especially reduced the frequency errors by a remarkable 81% in the wiping task and 50% in the shaking task, both compared to simple feedforward VFIL, when extrapolating at double the average speed in the training data. The proposed method also improved accuracy by 27% compared with VFIL even at an interpolated frequency for a contact-rich mixing task affected by complex friction traits.

</details>


### [267] [Pose6DAug: Physically Plausible Multi-view Object Swapping for Robot Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20118)
*Jonghoon Lee,Seong Hyeon Park,Byungwoo Jeon,Minha Lee,Jinwoo Shin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Pose6DAug uses 3D pose-guided augmentation to convert successful episodes into targeted demonstrations for failure modes, enabling multi-view, physically consistent augmentation without new data collection and improving generalization to novel objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA policies generalize poorly to out-of-distribution objects; collecting multi-view teleoperation data for every failure is costly and time-consuming; scalable, physically plausible augmentation that preserves 3D consistency is needed.

Method: From successful episodes, swap the manipulated object while preserving the action trajectory, and render this augmented data in 3D by anchoring the target object with an explicit mesh driven by a temporally coherent 6D pose trajectory, ensuring consistency across all camera views; fine-tune the VLA on this augmented data.

Result: Fine-tuning with Pose6DAug improves success rates by 16.5% relative to the state-of-the-art baseline on novel objects while preserving in-distribution performance.

Conclusion: Multi-view, physically consistent augmentation via 3D object pose manipulation is an effective and scalable path for improving VLA generalization to unseen objects without additional data collection.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) policies have shown strong potential for general-purpose manipulation, yet they often fail on novel, out-of-distribution objects whose appearance or geometry deviates from the training distribution. The standard remedy is to collect multi-view teleoperation data for every failure case, but this scales poorly in both cost and time. We introduce Pose6DAug, a failure-driven data augmentation framework that turns a policy's own successful episodes into targeted demonstrations for its failure modes, without any new data collection. Our key insight is that each successful episode already encodes a physically valid action trajectory together with calibrated multi-view observations. By swapping only the manipulated object while preserving this trajectory, we obtain new and physically grounded demonstrations. However, naive 2D video editing breaks multi-view consistency and physical plausibility, particularly under heavy occlusion and egocentric viewpoints. Our method instead operates directly in 3D, anchoring the target object with an explicit mesh driven by a temporally coherent 6D pose trajectory, ensuring geometrically consistent renderings across all camera views. Fine-tuning a VLA on data augmented by our method improves success rates by 16.5% relative to the state-of-the-art baseline on novel objects, while preserving in-distribution performance. These results show that multi-view and physically consistent augmentation is a practical path to scalable VLA generalization.

</details>


### [268] [Dual-Agent Framework for Cross-Model Verified Translation of Natural-Language Protocols into Robotic Laboratory Platform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20120)
*Hyeonna Choi,Jung Yup Kim,Hyuneui Lim,Seunggyu Jeon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Agent-based framework translating natural-language microplate protocols into executable robotic commands, using a Parser Agent, rule-based mapping, and a heterogeneous LLM Validation Agent with a self-correction loop; evaluated via cross-model verification and demonstrated with Bradford assay end-to-end.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the semantic gap between natural-language experimental protocols and automated microplate-based lab platforms to enable autonomous execution of complex, combinatorial tasks.

Method: A Parser Agent converts NL protocols into structured representations; a rule-based mapping engine deterministically enforces robotic platform constraints to generate device-level commands; a heterogeneous LLM Validation Agent checks completeness, parameter accuracy, and execution order and triggers structured self-correction; evaluation across 7 parsers and 3 validators on ELISA protocols, comparison against end-to-end LLM mapping for latency-accuracy trade-offs, and real-world Bradford assay demonstration on a robotic platform.

Result: Findings show that translation accuracy and pass rates depend on model scale and validator type; rule-based mapping offers different latency-accuracy profile than end-to-end LLM mapping; end-to-end autonomous Bradford assay execution is demonstrated, validating practical applicability.

Conclusion: The framework provides a flexible, scalable approach to narrowing semantic gaps in self-driving microplate laboratories, with modular components enabling error detection and correction; further work could address generalization to diverse assays, richer evaluation metrics, and robustness under language ambiguity.

Abstract: Biological experiment protocols are written in natural language, whereas automation systems rely on predefined control commands, creating a semantic gap that limits autonomous execution. Microplate-based automatic experiments are particularly challenging due to the need to simultaneously control well mapping, sample-reagent combinations, replicate placement, and parallel dispensing. This study proposes an agent-based protocol translation framework that converts natural-language microplate-based protocols into executable control commands for a robotic laboratory platform. A Parser Agent formalizes the natural-language protocol into a structured representation, and a rule-based mapping engine deterministically incorporates the operational constraints of the robotic laboratory platform to generate device-level control commands. A heterogeneous LLM Validation Agent verifies completeness, parameter accuracy, and execution order, and triggers a self-correction loop with structured feedback when errors are detected. A sweep involving 7 Parsers and 3 Validators on randomly selected ELISA protocols evaluates how model scale and Validator type affect translation accuracy and pass rates under cross-model verification. The accuracy-latency trade-off is further verified by comparing the rule-based mapping of the proposed framework with LLM end-to-end direct mapping. Finally, Bradford assay-based protein quantification using a microplate was demonstrated on a robotic laboratory platform, validating end-to-end autonomous execution from natural-language protocols to real-world experiments. The proposed framework provides a flexible approach to narrowing the semantic gap between natural-language protocols and microplate-based self-driving laboratories.

</details>


### [269] [Frequency-Aware Flow Matching for Continuous and Consistent Robotic Action Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20135)
*Jianing Guo,Fangzheng Chen,Zihao Mao,Wong Lik Hang Kenny,Zhenhong Wu,Yu Li,Yishuai Cai,Yuanpei Chen,Yikun Ban,Kai Chen,Qi Dou,Yaodong Yang,Xianglong Liu,Huijie Zhao,Simin Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Frequency-Aware Flow Matching (FAFM) converts discrete action chunks into the frequency domain using DCT, performs flow matching on coefficients, then reconstructs continuous actions with a cosine basis. It adds a Sobolev-type temporal smoothness constraint to promote smoothness without adding parameters. It aims to produce continuous, temporally consistent actions that handle heterogeneous control frequencies, improving performance across benchmarks and on a real robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome brittleness of discretized action chunks in flow-matching policies when demonstrations come from heterogeneous control frequencies and to reduce temporal inconsistency that harms control stability.

Method: Transform discrete action sequences into DCT coefficients; perform flow matching in coefficient space; reconstruct continuous actions via cosine basis expansion; regularize the first-order temporal derivative (Sobolev-type constraint) to enforce smoothness; the approach requires no additional network parameters and applies to standalone flow policies and vision-language models.

Result: On synthetic toy benchmarks, obstacle avoidance, LapGym, LIBERO, FAFM improves success rates, multimodal expressivity, motion smoothness, convergence speed, robustness to mechanical bias and mixed-frequency input; demonstrated on a real Franka robot; code available.

Conclusion: FAFM provides a simple, parameter-efficient method to generate continuous, temporally consistent actions with improved cross-domain performance, broad applicability to flow-matching policies and vision-language action models.

Abstract: Flow matching has emerged as a standard paradigm for robotic manipulation owing to its strong expressive power for modelling complex, multimodal action distributions, alongside similar approaches like diffusion policy. However, existing methods rely on discretized action chunks, making them brittle to demonstrations collected at heterogeneous control frequencies and prone to temporally inconsistent actions that degrade control stability. In this paper, we propose Frequency-Aware Flow Matching (FAFM), which outputs continuous, temporally consistent actions. To handle heterogeneous frequency input, we transform discrete action sequences into the frequency domain with the discrete cosine transform (DCT), perform flow matching over the resulting coefficients, and reconstruct continuous actions via cosine basis expansion. To generate temporally consistent actions, we regularize the first-order temporal derivative to promote smooth actions. This corresponds to a Sobolev-type constraint that suppresses high-frequency errors and discourages abrupt action changes. Our FAFM is simple, introduces no additional network parameters and applies to standalone flow-matching policies and vision-language action models. Across synthetic toy benchmark, obstacle avoidance, LapGym, and LIBERO, FAFM improves success rates, multimodal expressivity, motion smoothness, convergence speed, robustness to mechanical bias and mixed-frequency input. These gains are consistent when deployed on a real-world Franka robot. Code available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/FAFM.

</details>


### [270] [Robust Assembly State Reasoning from Action Recognition for Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20150)
*James Fant-Male,Roel Pieters*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The study systematically compares five state-tracking methods for deriving assembly state from HAR inputs across two diverse datasets. No single method is optimal for all tasks; NN and HMM perform well under low variability, while logic-based approaches offer greater robustness in other scenarios. Incorporating action duration modeling is important for repetitive tasks without additional sensing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust tracking of assembly state in human-robot collaboration by translating HAR signals into reliable state estimates, addressing variability across tasks and noise in inputs.

Method: Systematic evaluation using two diverse datasets and five state-tracking approaches (including logic-based, Hidden Markov Model, and neural network methods). The study uses simulated inputs with varying noise and realistic inputs from a HAR model to assess robustness across tasks.

Result: Optimal state-tracking method is task-dependent. NN and HMM perform well in scenarios with limited variability; logic-based approaches are more robust in other cases. Models that encode expected action durations help in repetitive actions when no extra sensing is provided.

Conclusion: There is no one-size-fits-all solution for assembly-state tracking from HAR. Task characteristics should guide method selection, and duration-aware models should be employed for repetitive tasks without additional sensing. Consider combining methods or adopting adaptive strategies based on variability and noise.

Abstract: Human Action Recognition (HAR) is frequently investigated in Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC) research to understand what actions have been performed and hence the state of a collaborative task. Accurately tracking an assembly state from HAR is however not fully investigated, and in realistic scenarios is not a trivial task. This research systematically investigates and compares methods for tracking assembly state using action recognition inputs. Investigations using two diverse datasets and five state tracking approaches, including logic-based, Hidden Markov Model (HMM), and neural network (NN) methods, show that optimal approaches are not uniform across different tasks and that different methods fail under different circumstances. Testing is performed using both simulated inputs with varying noise levels and realistic inputs from a HAR model. Results show NN and HMM methods can perform well in tasks with limited variability, but for other scenarios logic-based approaches can be more robust. Methods which model expected action duration are also important for tasks with repeated actions where no additional sensing is provided.

</details>


### [271] [Belt-Finger: An Affordable Soft Belt-Driven Gripper for Dexterous In-Hand Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20193)
*Boya Zhang,Andreas Zell,Georg Martius*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost double-soft-belt finger upgrade to parallel grippers adds three in-hand DoFs (translation, pitch, roll) while preserving opening/closing, enabling improved dexterity; integrated with MPC and a lightweight teleoperation interface, it yields higher task feasibility across challenging manipulation tasks versus standard parallel grippers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parallel grippers lack in-hand mobility and dexterity, especially in confined workspaces. A simple, inexpensive upgrade is sought to extend dexterous manipulation without sacrificing reliability or incurring high manufacturing costs.

Method: Design and integrate a double-soft-belt finger module onto a standard parallel gripper, preserving its actuation. This upgrade introduces three in-hand DoFs (translation, pitch, roll) while maintaining nominal opening/closing. Two control pipelines are demonstrated: (1) a model predictive controller (MPC) for in-hand manipulation of known objects, and (2) a lightweight teleoperation interface enabling simultaneous control of the robot arm and gripper (totaling 10 DoFs). Validation occurs across a suite of challenging manipulation tasks conducted via teleoperation, MPC, and learned policies.

Result: Across the tested tasks and control modalities, the upgraded gripper consistently demonstrates improved dexterity and higher task feasibility compared with a conventional parallel gripper.

Conclusion: The proposed double-soft-belt finger module offers a simple, inexpensive retrofit that expands parallel grippers' in-hand manipulation capabilities without compromising reliability, enabling more versatile manipulation in constrained environments; integration with MPC and teleoperation demonstrates practical applicability.

Abstract: Parallel-jaw grippers are the default manipulator choice in robotics because they are simple, robust, and inexpensive. Their limited in-hand mobility, however, often forces large arm motions and restricts dexterous manipulation in confined workspaces. We present a parallel-gripper upgrade: a double-soft-belt-based finger module that preserves standard opening/closing while adding three in-hand degrees of freedom (DoF): translation, pitch, and roll. The mechanism is deliberately kept simple and engineered for inexpensive manufacturing and straightforward integration, preserving the reliability and precise control of traditional parallel grippers while greatly broadening the range of manipulation capabilities. To demonstrate the utility of the added DoFs, we integrate the gripper in two control pipelines. First, we adapt a model predictive controller for in-hand manipulation of known objects. Second, we introduce a lightweight teleoperation interface that enables simultaneous control of the robot arm and gripper (10 DoFs total) with minimal hardware. Across a suite of challenging manipulation tasks executed via teleoperation, MPC, and trained policies, the proposed gripper consistently improves dexterity and task feasibility compared to a conventional parallel gripper

</details>


### [272] [Stable Transformer-Actor-Critic Model Predictive Control: A Contraction Analysis Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20197)
*Antonio Marino,Valerio Modugno,Marco Cognetti*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A Transformer-Actor-Critic MPC with formal robustness guarantees leveraging δISS and Riemannian contraction to certify a robust policy, validated on a nonlinear drone task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guarantee closed-loop stability for sequence-based learning in MPC and enable certifiable robustness in complex, non-convex control problems.

Method: 1) Prove global incremental Input-to-State Stability (δISS) for Transformer networks. 2) Apply Riemannian contraction theory to analyze the plant–predictive model interconnection. 3) Incorporate the derived bounds as a training regularizer to train a certifiably robust policy within an Actor-Critic MPC framework.

Result: Derives certifiable robustness guarantees for the policy and demonstrates improved robustness in simulation/experiments on a nonlinear 3D drone performing target-reaching and obstacle avoidance.

Conclusion: The framework provides formal, certifiable robustness guarantees for learning-based MPC with sequence models, enabling safer deployment in complex robotic tasks; potential limitations relate to the conservatism of bounds and computational overhead.

Abstract: Actor-Critic Model Predictive Control (MPC) effectively addresses complex, non-convex control problems, but guaranteeing the closed-loop stability of sequence-based learning models within these pipelines remains challenging. This paper introduces a novel Transformer-Actor-Critic MPC architecture with formal robustness guarantees. First, we prove that Transformer networks can satisfy global incremental Input-to-State Stability ($δ$ISS). We then leverage Riemannian contraction theory to analyze the interconnected dynamics between the physical plant and the predictive neural network. Finally, we integrate these theoretical bounds as a training regularizer to yield a certifiably robust policy. The framework is validated on a nonlinear 3D drone model executing target-reaching and obstacle-avoidance maneuvers.

</details>


### [273] [FlowMaps: Modeling Long-Term Multimodal Object Dynamics with Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20209)
*Francesco Argenziano,Miguel Saavedra-Ruiz,Sacha Morin,Charlie Gauthier,Daniele Nardi,Liam Paull*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FlowMaps is a latent flow matching model that predicts multimodal, continuous 3D future locations of dynamic objects conditioned on past human interactions; it captures spatio-temporal object routines to improve dynamic object navigation, validated in simulation and real-world settings (>600 episodes) with state-of-the-art gains; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots in daily homes must understand how objects move over time due to human activity. Objects change positions as people interact with them, creating spatio-temporal regularities that can be exploited for navigation; without modeling this, robust matching between current observations and past objects is hard.

Method: Introduce FlowMaps, a latent flow matching model that learns implicit dependencies among objects and their temporal evolution to estimate multimodal distributions over future 3D locations of dynamic objects. Predictions are conditioned on past human interactions and generalize to unseen environments sharing object routines, operating in continuous 3D space.

Result: FlowMaps outperforms state-of-the-art approaches across more than 600 episodes in a dynamic Object Navigation task in both simulated and real-world environments.

Conclusion: Modeling object dynamics via continuous, multimodal spatio-temporal distributions improves robotic search and navigation in changing household environments; FlowMaps demonstrates this utility and is accompanied by code and additional materials.

Abstract: Joint spatial and temporal understanding of 3D scenes is a crucial requirement for robots deployed in everyday household environments. Such agents must not only comprehend and navigate spatial layouts, but also reason about how these spaces evolve over time. In particular, humans interact with objects daily, causing them to change position throughout the environment and making it difficult for robots to reliably associate current observations with previously seen objects. However, these interactions are not random: human habits and routines induce spatio-temporally consistent patterns in object locations, which robotic agents can potentially learn and then exploit for downstream tasks such as navigation. To this end, we introduce FlowMaps, a latent flow matching model for estimating multimodal distributions over the future locations of dynamic objects in a continuous 3D space. By learning the implicit dependencies among objects and their temporal evolution, FlowMaps predicts likely changes in object locations conditioned on past human interactions, while supporting generalization across previously unseen environments that share similar object routines. To demonstrate the utility of this method, we deploy FlowMaps in a downstream dynamic Object Navigation task in both simulated and real-world environments. Across more than 600 episodes, FlowMaps outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, showing that modeling object dynamics through continuous, multimodal spatio-temporal distributions improves robotic search and navigation in changing household environments. Code and additional material is available at https://fra-tsuna.github.io/flowmaps/.

</details>


### [274] [Mobile Target Search with Imperfect Perception: A Partially Observable Stochastic Game Theoretical Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20232)
*Hanzheng Zhang,Shu Liang,Shuyu Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This work studies mobile target search under imperfect perception using a POSG framework, introducing a detectability criterion and a server-assisted distributed algorithm that combines aggregative potential games and KL-divergence-based target prediction; simulations validate effectiveness and the proposed detectability conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address imperfect perception in mobile search problems caused by sensor limitations, jamming, or noise, and to guarantee eventual target detection under uncertainty.

Method: Model the interaction as a partially observable stochastic game (POSG) with bounded mobility, define a detectability concept with stochastic recurrence analysis, and develop a server-assisted distributed algorithm leveraging an aggregative potential game structure for searchers and KL-divergence-based reduction for target prediction.

Result: Numerical simulations demonstrate the proposed algorithm's effectiveness and corroborate the detectability analysis.

Conclusion: Provide sufficient detectability criteria for guaranteed eventual detection and offer a scalable server-assisted framework for robust search under perceptual uncertainty.

Abstract: This paper investigates mobile target search under imperfect perceptions caused by sensor limitations, malicious jamming, or communication noise. Searchers and targets operate in a grid-shaped area with bounded mobility, leading to a dynamic interplay between search and evasion. To capture this adversarial interaction under imperfect perceptions, we adopt the partially observable stochastic game (POSG) approach, which generalizes partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) by incorporating target intelligence. To handle false alarms and missed detections caused by perceptual uncertainties, we propose a novel detectability concept to determine whether a search strategy guarantees eventual detection, and provide sufficient detectability criteria based on stochastic recurrence analysis. We further develop a server-assisted distributed algorithm that utilizes the aggregative potential game structure for searchers and a KL-divergence-based reduction for target prediction. Numerical simulations validate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm and support the detectability analysis.

</details>


### [275] [Finetuning Vision-Language-Action Models Requires Fewer Layers Than You Think](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20246)
*Gia-Binh Nguyen,Trong-Bao Ho,Thien-Loc Ha,Khoa Vo,Philip Lund Møller,Quang T. Nguyen,Long Dinh,Tuan Dam,Vu Duong,Tung M. Luu,Trung Le,Tran Nguyen Le,Minh Vu,An Thai Le,Ngan Le,Daniel Sonntag,James Zou,Jan Peters,Duy M. H. Nguyen,Ngo Anh Vien*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Training-free structural compression discovers and removes redundant layers in Vision-Language-Action models, reducing depth by up to 50% via Centered Kernel Alignment, yielding 40–50% faster training and up to 30% faster inference with preserved or improved performance across multiple benchmarks and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large Vision-Language-Action models are computationally expensive to fine-tune and deploy. Despite diverse training trajectories, they exhibit layer-wise representational redundancy. A training-free compression can dramatically reduce compute without requiring full model access for optimization.

Method: A training-free compression pipeline uses a single forward pass and Centered Kernel Alignment to identify redundant layer features. Twin layers are removed to permanently compress model depth by up to 50% simultaneously in the Vision-Language Model backbone and the continuous control policy head, without learning new token reductions or dynamic layer selectors. Downstream fine-tuning trains a streamlined architecture.

Result: Compressed models achieve 40–50% reduction in training time and up to 30% faster real-time inference, while matching or exceeding full-scale base model performance. Validation spans LIBERO, RoboCasa, SimplerEnv benchmarks and 10 real-world manipulation tasks across 4 robotic embodiments.

Conclusion: Vision-Language-Action systems require significantly fewer layers than previously assumed. The proposed training-free structural compression offers a compute-efficient path for scalable robot learning, maintaining performance while reducing compute during both training and inference.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models pre-trained on massive video-robot datasets have revolutionized robotic manipulation, yet their multi-billion parameter architectures impose prohibitive computational burdens during downstream fine-tuning and real-time inference. In this work, we reveal a highly non-trivial architectural characteristic of these continuous control foundation policies (e.g., pi_0, GR00T-N1.5): despite being trained on diverse physical trajectories, they exhibit severe layer-wise representational redundancy. To exploit this, we introduce a structural compression pipeline that is entirely training-free, bypassing the need of existing methods to load full-scale models to learn optimized token reductions or dynamic layer selectors. Instead, using only a single forward pass via Centered Kernel Alignment to identify redundant layer features, we remove twin layers to permanently compress the model depth by up to 50% across both the VLM backbone and the continuous control policy head. Downstream fine-tuning of this streamlined architecture yields a dual acceleration benefit: a 40-50% reduction in training time and up to 30% faster real-time inference, while matching or exceeding full-scale base model performance. We comprehensively validate our method across three simulation benchmarks (LIBERO, RoboCasa, SimplerEnv) and 10 diverse real-world manipulation tasks across 4 unique robotic embodiments. These results prove that advanced VLAs require significantly fewer layers than previously assumed, offering a highly compute-efficient paradigm for scalable robot learning.

</details>


### [276] [Efficiently Linking Real Scenes with Synthetic Data Generation for AI-based Cognitive Robotics and Computer Vision Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20272)
*Paul Koch,Vivek Chavan,André Sers,Adem Karakurt,Paul Hofmann,Mohamad Zaher Ziadeh,Jörg Krüger*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Survey of AI vision for robotics highlighting sim-to-real data and architecture challenges; discusses current limits/trends and ongoing work to bridge domain gaps via training data generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data efficiency, architectural synergy, and domain gaps that limit precision, scalability, and transfer from simulation to real world in 6D pose estimation and grasping.

Method: Literature survey of state-of-the-art methods for semantic scene analysis and 6D/pose estimation, with a discussion of limitations and trends; presentation of ongoing work aimed at bridging sim-to-real by linking simulation and real-world data generation.

Result: No empirical results are reported in the abstract; it outlines a survey of current limits and trends and outlines ongoing work to bridge the sim-to-real gap via training data generation.

Conclusion: Highlights the importance of bridging the simulation-to-reality gap and positions training data generation as a key axis for improving robustness and scalability of AI vision in robotics.

Abstract: AI vision models are a driving factor for the potential use case scenarios of cognitive robotics within in the industry and household applications. A large array of methods from semantic environment analysis towards 6D and grasping pose estimation have been proposed based on the latest AI achievements. However, such advancements require further strong and efficient methods w.r.t. training data and AI-architectures, which are capable in synergy to tackle current challenges, precision limits, and scalability beyond domain gaps. In this paper, we discuss these current limits and trends in the related state-of-the-art which are challenging those. Further we discuss our current work in progress on bridging the domain gap between simulations and real world applications by linking those in the training data generation.

</details>


### [277] [Co-VLA: Coordination-Aware Structured Action Modeling for Dual-Arm Vision-Language-Action Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20285)
*Yandong Wang,Jiaqian Yu,Xiongfeng Peng,Lu Xu,Yamin Mao,Weiming Li,Jaewook Yoo,Dongwook Lee,Daehyun Ji,Mingbo Zhao,Chao Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Explicit coordination priors via a Structured Action Expert (SAE) and a Latent-Aware Controller (LAC) improve bimanual VLA models, achieving stronger coordination, better OOD generalization, and faster task completion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end implicit coordination may fail for tightly coupled bimanual tasks where reliability, interpretability, and safety are critical; explicit structural priors can guide action generation and real-time control.

Method: Replace the monolithic action head with a Structured Action Expert (SAE) that imposes modular coordination-aware structure on shared and residual latents (task-level coordination vs. per-arm adjustments). A Latent-Aware Controller (LAC) then interprets these representations to modulate synchronization, asymmetry, smoothness, and safety in real time, without requiring force/impedance control; compatible with standard control pipelines.

Result: Across simulation and real-world benchmarks, Co-VLA yields a 27% improvement in tight-coordination task success, more than double OOD real-world performance (13% to 27%), and up to 25% faster task completion.

Conclusion: Introducing explicit coordination priors via SAE and adaptive latency-aware control improves reliability, interpretability, and efficiency of VLA-based bimanual manipulation, with strong empirical validation in both simulation and real-world settings.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models show strong capabilities in single and dual-arm robotic manipulation. Prior works show coordinated bimanual behaviors can emerge from end-to-end learning, leveraging large vision-language backbones with continuous action prediction. However, as bimanual tasks become tightly coupled and execution constraints become critical, implicit coordination alone is insufficient to ensure reliable, interpretable, and stable behavior.
  In this work, we propose Co-VLA, a coordination-aware bimanual manipulation framework introducing explicit structural priors into VLA models. We instantiate our method on a state-of-the-art vision-language backbone by replacing its monolithic action head with a Structured Action Expert (SAE) designed for bimanual coordination. Specifically, we introduce explicit structure at the action generation level with a modular coordination-aware loss that shapes shared and residual latents according to task-specific structures. The shared latent encodes task-level coordination intent, while residual latents capture execution adjustments for each arm.
  At deployment, a Latent-Aware Controller (LAC) interprets the learned representations to modulate synchronization strength, execution asymmetry, smoothness, and safety constraints in real time. LAC operates at the joint-command level and remains compatible with standard control pipelines without requiring force or impedance control. Experiments across simulation and real-world benchmarks show Co-VLA significantly outperforms monolithic baselines, achieving a 27% success rate gain in tight-coordination tasks, more than doubling performance in OOD real-world scenarios (from 13% to 27%), and reducing task completion time by up to 25%.

</details>


### [278] [Towards 3D karst underwater scene reconstruction from rotating sonar data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20322)
*Georgios Evangelos Margaritis,Lionel Lapierre,Simon Rohou,Zhi Yan,Andreas Nüchter,François Goulette*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SLAM-corrected sonar pipeline with a two-stage deep-learning surface reconstruction to create navigable 3D meshes of underwater karst conduits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Karst aquifers are critical freshwater resources but are difficult to map due to sparse/noisy sonar data and navigation drift; an accurate 3D reconstruction enables hydrogeological analysis and exploration safety.
