Method: Combine continuous-time SLAM to correct trajectory drift from sonar profiler data with a two-stage deep learning method for surface reconstruction, forming a pipeline that outputs an immersive 3D mesh of underwater karst conduits.

Result: Produces immersive, navigable 3D meshes suitable for hydrogeological analysis, integrating drift-corrected trajectories with learned surface reconstruction from sparse sonar data.

Conclusion: An integrated SLAM+DL pipeline can effectively reconstruct underwater karst conduit geometry from limited sonar measurements, enhancing hydrogeological modeling and exploration.

Abstract: Karst aquifers provide critical freshwater resources but pose significant hazards due to their complex and poorly understood subsurface geometry. Mapping these environments is challenging because sonar data from underwater exploration is sparse and noisy, while navigation estimates suffer from drift limiting standard 3D reconstruction methods. We present a pipeline for reconstructing underwater karst conduits from a sonar profiler. We combine a continuous-time SLAM approach to correct trajectory drift with a novel two-stage deep learning method for surface reconstruction, producing an immersive and navigable 3D mesh for hydrogeological analysis.

</details>


### [279] [Autonomous Driving with Priority-Ordered STL Specifications Under Multimodal Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20336)
*Taha Bouzid,Shuhao Qi,Mircea Lazar,Sofie Haesaert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware trajectory planning using lexicographic STL specifications under uncertainty, implemented with MPPI, validated in simulations to handle conflicting objectives under multi-modal uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In safety-critical autonomous driving, not all safety, comfort, and rule-compliance requirements can be satisfied simultaneously; there is a need to prioritize them and account for uncertainty in predictions of surrounding agents.

Method: Define a lexicographic ordering over STL specifications that remains valid under uncertainty; integrate into Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control to select trajectories that satisfy higher-priority specs first under probabilistic uncertainty.

Result: Simulation experiments show the framework efficiently manages conflicting objectives under realistic multi-modal uncertainty.

Conclusion: The approach provides a principled, computationally tractable way to enforce prioritized, uncertainty-aware specifications in trajectory planning, with potential for real-world application and extension to more complex scenarios.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles must plan trajectories that satisfy a multitude of requirements on safety, passenger comfort, and compliance with traffic rules. However, in safety-critical scenarios, it is not always possible to satisfy all requirements simultaneously, necessitating their prioritization based on importance. At the same time, in these safety-critical scenarios, the uncertainty in trajectory predictions of the surrounding traffic, such as other vehicles and pedestrians, should be explicitly accounted for. In this work, we propose an uncertainty-aware trajectory planning framework that incorporates a predefined lexicographic ordering over Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications that stays valid under uncertainty. We implement this formulation with Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) control and we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on simulation scenarios, showing that our framework efficiently handles conflicting objectives under realistic multi-modal uncertainty.

</details>


### [280] [An Infrastructure-less, Control-Independent Solution to Relative Localisation of a Team of Mobile Robots using Ranging Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20365)
*Paolo Golinelli,Tommaso Faraci,Daniele Fontanelli*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Anchor-less, fully decentralised cooperative localisation using a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework with local odometry, sparse ranging, and short-range communications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable robust localization of robot teams in environments without fixed infrastructure, enabling fast deployment with minimal requirements and resilience to transient observability losses.

Method: A decentralized multi-hypothesis Bayesian filter that propagates and weighs multiple feasible team-state hypotheses using only local odometry, sparse inter-agent ranging, and short-range communication. The approach does not require anchors or motion-control to guarantee observability and relies on information sharing to leverage group estimates even under partial connectivity.

Result: Maintains the full set of feasible solutions (hypotheses) to ensure robustness under unobservable periods; information sharing allows each agent to benefit from the estimates of the entire group even when connectivity is limited.

Conclusion: An anchor-free, infrastructure-free localization framework enabling robust, scalable cooperative localization for robot fleets in unstructured environments, with minimal sensing and communication requirements.

Abstract: The ability to localise teams of robots is essential for applications ranging from robotic fleets in unstructured environments to cooperative control and navigation tasks. In such contexts, fixed infrastructure is often unavailable, deployments must be fast and flexible, and system requirements must be minimal. We present a decentralised cooperative localisation algorithm that addresses all these challenges at once. The method is anchor-less, fully decentralised, and, unlike most existing approaches, does not require controlling the robots motion to ensure team observability. It relies only on local odometry, sparse inter-agent ranging measurements, and short-range communication, all of which are widely available in practice. The algorithm adopts a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework that maintains the entire set of feasible solutions, ensuring robustness under transient unobservable conditions. Moreover, through information sharing, each agent benefits from the estimates of the entire group, even in partially connected conditions.

</details>


### [281] [CoLI: A Reproducible Platform for Continuum Robot Learning via Monolithic 3D Printing and Isomorphic Teleoperation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20389)
*Ziyuan Tang,Chenxi Xiao**

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An open-source continuum robot platform enabling reproducible fabrication via monolithic multi-material 3D printing, with isomorphic teleoperation that maps directly to actuators (no explicit kinematic model) and a singularity-free interface; supports imitation-learning autonomy; validated through hardware characterization and manipulation tasks, enabling benchmarking in the community.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reproducibility and accessibility barriers in continuum robotics due to complex fabrication, challenging kinematic modeling, and non-intuitive control interfaces; need for learning-ready, open platforms for benchmarking.

Method: Design and fabricate a continuum arm as a monolithic compliant structure using multi-material 3D printing; implement an isomorphic teleoperation interface providing direct actuator-level mapping; avoid explicit kinematic modeling; enable imitation learning; perform hardware characterization and manipulation task experiments.

Result: Demonstrates reproducible, learning-ready platform; hardware characterization and task performance indicate suitability for algorithmic development and benchmarking; reduces assembly complexity and modeling requirements.

Conclusion: The open-source, fabrication-simplified, singularity-free platform lowers barriers to research and accelerates development in continuum robotics, enabling consistent benchmarking and learning-based control.

Abstract: Continuum robots offer strong potential for manipulation tasks due to their high degrees of freedom, compliant structures, and operational safety. However, their adoption in both research and practical applications has been hindered by reproducibility issues arising from complex fabrication and assembly processes, challenging kinematic modeling, and a lack of intuitive control interfaces. To address these challenges, we present a novel open-source continuum robot design. The platform features a simplified fabrication pipeline enabled by multi-material 3D printing, allowing the arm to be fabricated as a monolithic compliant structure with minimal assembly. Control is achieved through an isomorphic teleoperation interface that establishes a direct actuator-level mapping, eliminating the need for explicit kinematic modeling and providing a singularity-free mapping. Building on this hardware design, the platform further supports imitation-learning-based autonomous control. The proposed system is evaluated through hardware characterization and a set of manipulation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that the platform provides a reproducible, learning-ready continuum robot system, accelerating algorithmic development and systematic benchmarking for the continuum robotics community.

</details>


### [282] [Agentic AutoResearch forSpace Autonomy: An Auditable, LLM-Driven Research Agent for Aerospace Control Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20394)
*Amit Jain,Richard Linares*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An autonomous, LLM-driven research loop with a credibility layer (AutoResearch) develops aerospace control policies and certifies results against seed noise, outperforming undirected search on two benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automate the iterative policy-development process for spacecraft control and reliably distinguish genuine improvements from seed noise in learned controllers.

Method: An offline research agent (LLM) reads a problem description and run history, proposes a single training-script edit, executes it, and logs the outcome. A credibility layer certifies results via three checks: (1) measured per-problem seed noise, (2) reseeded verification of the best configuration, and (3) leave-one-out pruning of the agent's edits. The loop is applied to two aerospace problems—Clohessy-Wiltshire relative rendezvous and safety-constrained docking past a keep-out zone—and the produced policy is deployed onboard while the agent remains offline.

Result: In both problems, the audited policy clears the measured seed noise by many standard deviations; an undirected search over the same parameters does not. On the docking problem, undirected search yields no feasible policy, while the learned policy remains outside the keep-out zone for every seed.

Conclusion: AutoResearch with the credibility layer yields robust, verified policies for aerospace control that outperform naive search strategies, demonstrated on two validated benchmarks.

Abstract: Spacecraft guidance, navigation, and control functions are increasingly realized as learned policies distilled from expert solvers. Developing such a policy is itself a research process: an investigator selects an architecture and hyperparameters, runs experiments, and must determine whether an apparent improvement is genuine or merely seed noise. This paper presents AutoResearch, a framework in which a large language model autonomously drives that loop for aerospace control problems, coupled with a credibility layer, built into the loop, that certifies each reported result against the problem's own measured seed noise. The language model serves only as the offline research agent that develops the control policy; the trained policy it produces is then deployed onboard the spacecraft, while the model itself never operates the vehicle. At each iteration the agent reads a plain-language problem description and the run history, proposes a single edit to the training script, executes it, and logs the outcome. No reported result is credited until it passes the same three checks: measured per-problem seed noise, reseeded verification of the best configuration, and leave-one-out pruning of the agent's edits. The same loop is applied, unchanged, to two aerospace control problems: a Clohessy-Wiltshire relative rendezvous and a safety-constrained collision-avoidance docking past a keep-out zone, each calibrated against a known optimal control benchmark. In both, the audited policy clears the measured seed noise by many standard deviations; an undirected search over the same parameters does not. On the docking problem the gap becomes categorical: undirected search yields no feasible policy, while the learned policy stays outside the keep-out zone on every seed.

</details>


### [283] [LIT-GS: LiDAR-Inertial-Thermal Gaussian Splatting for Illumination-Robust Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20424)
*Shikuan Shi,Chunran Zheng,Jiaming Xu,Tianyong Ye,Tao Yu,Yukang Cui*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LIT-GS fuses LiDAR-derived planes and cross-modal anchors into Gaussian Splatting to robustly estimate geometry and render in challenging lighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB photometric cues are unreliable under illumination changes and texture-deficient scenes; explicit LiDAR geometry is needed to improve robustness of LIV Gaussian mapping.

Method: Inject LiDAR plane geometry as explicit constraints in pose/structure refinement and Gaussian optimization; use LIV visual map points as confidence-aware cross-modal anchors for thermal-LiDAR associations; incorporate weighted LiDAR point-to-plane residuals into bundle adjustment; introduce a LiDAR-plane-regularized differentiable splatting objective to align rendered points with observed planes.

Result: Experiments on proprietary sequences and public datasets show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art LIV-based Gaussian Splatting baselines, especially under challenging lighting conditions.

Conclusion: LIT-GS enhances robustness and rendering quality by grounding Gaussian Splatting in explicit LiDAR geometry, mitigating surface thickening and drift in low-contrast thermal imagery.

Abstract: Gaussian Splatting has enabled real-time neural rendering, yet existing LiDAR-inertial-visual (LIV) Gaussian mapping pipelines remain fragile under illumination changes and texture-deficient scenes due to their reliance on RGB photometric cues. We present LIT-GS, a LiDAR-inertial-thermal Gaussian Splatting framework that injects LiDAR-derived plane geometry as an explicit constraint in both pose/structure refinement and Gaussian optimization. Specifically, we exploit LIV visual map points as confidence-aware cross-modal anchors to establish reliable thermal-LiDAR associations, and incorporate weighted LiDAR point-to-plane residuals into bundle adjustment to jointly refine camera poses and 3D points under weak thermal supervision. Building on the refined structure, we further introduce a LiDAR-plane-regularized differentiable splatting objective that constrains rendered 3D points to align with locally observed planes, mitigating surface thickening and structural drift in low-contrast thermal imagery. Experiments on proprietary sequences and public datasets demonstrate that LIT-GS consistently improves geometric accuracy and rendering quality over state-of-the-art LIV-based Gaussian Splatting baselines, particularly in challenging lighting conditions.

</details>


### [284] [TaCauchy: An Extensible FEM Framework for Vision-Based Tactile Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20426)
*Hengfei Zhao,Yifan Xie,Junhao Gong,Yue Sun,Kai Zhu,Weihua He,Shoujie Li,Haohuan Fu,Wenbo Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TaCauchy is an extensible FEM-based framework integrated with Isaac Sim that computes Cauchy stress tensors from hyperelastic models using UIPC, enabling physics-grounded tactile force supervision with real-time performance and modular sensor support.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide physically accurate, GPU-accelerated stress fields for vision-based tactile sensing in reinforcement learning, addressing the lack of ground-truth force distributions in existing simulations.

Method: Finite Element Method framework TaCauchy built on the Unified Incremental Potential Contact (UIPC) solver. It directly computes Cauchy stress tensors from hyperelastic constitutive laws and projects them onto contact surfaces to obtain traction forces and pressure distributions. Features automatic mesh generation with geometry-aware adaptive refinement and a modular sensor interface for GelSight Mini, DIGIT, and 9DTact, integrated within Isaac Sim.

Result: Performance: 33.40 FPS per environment and 555 FPS across 60 parallel environments; stress extraction overhead < 1 ms. Validation shows strong agreement with real tactile responses (SSIM > 0.93) across 1.2556–4.7332 N, indicating accurate, physically-grounded force supervision.

Conclusion: TaCauchy provides a scalable, physics-grounded platform for tactile sensing in robotics, delivering ground-truth stress fields and accurate tactile responses within a GPU-accelerated simulator, suitable for reinforcement learning and manipulation tasks with multiple sensor modalities.

Abstract: Vision-based tactile sensors require high-fidelity simulation for reinforcement learning, yet existing approaches struggle to provide accurate mechanical stress fields within GPU-accelerated robotics platforms. We present TaCauchy, an extensible Finite Element Method (FEM) framework that integrates rigorous physics-based force computation into Isaac Sim. Built on the Unified Incremental Potential Contact (UIPC) solver, TaCauchy directly computes Cauchy stress tensors from hyperelastic constitutive laws and projects them onto contact surfaces to obtain traction forces and pressure distributions, providing mechanical ground truth from first principles rather than empirical estimation. Our framework features automatic mesh generation with geometry-aware adaptive refinement and a modular sensor interface enabling rapid integration of diverse sensors (GelSight Mini, DIGIT, 9DTact) with minimal configuration. Performance benchmarks demonstrate 33.40 FPS for single environments and 555 FPS aggregate throughput across 60 parallel environments, with stress extraction overhead under 1 ms. Physical validation experiments show strong agreement between simulated and real tactile responses across force ranges from 1.2556 N to 4.7332 N, achieving SSIM above 0.93, confirming the framework's capability to provide accurate, physically-grounded force supervision for downstream robotic manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [285] [ARC: Adaptive Robust Joint State and Covariance Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20428)
*Alexandre Hadji-Thomas,Andrew Stirling,James R. Forbes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified Block-Coordinate Descent framework for joint state and covariance estimation in the presence of outliers, using a norm-aware adaptive robust loss, Iteratively Reweighted Least-Squares (IRLS) state update, and Minimum Weighted Covariance Determinant (MWCD) covariance estimation; self-tuning without manual parameter tuning; validated via Monte Carlo and real-world ultra-wideband localization in cluttered non-line-of-sight environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensor measurements are frequently corrupted by outliers and non-Gaussian noise, biasing traditional estimators. There is a need for a framework that jointly estimates state and measurement covariance while being robust to outliers.

Method: A Block-Coordinate Descent (BCD) framework that integrates (i) a norm-aware adaptive robust loss, (ii) an Iteratively Reweighted Least-Squares state update, and (iii) a MWCD covariance estimator, yielding a self-tuning joint state and covariance estimator.

Result: The proposed estimator consistently recovers the true inlier covariance and matches or exceeds state estimation accuracy of baselines, without manual parameter tuning, in both Monte-Carlo simulations and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: A self-tuning joint state and covariance estimator can effectively handle outliers and non-Gaussian noise, aligning accurate state estimation with correct inlier covariance estimation without requiring manual parameter tuning.

Abstract: Sensor measurements are frequently corrupted by outliers and non-Gaussian noise. These imperfections in the sensor data can cause classical state estimators to generate biased and unreliable state and uncertainty estimates. Robust estimators reject or downweight outliers but do not perform measurement covariance estimation, whereas joint state and covariance estimators assume Gaussian residuals and fixed loss shape parameters. Integrating these two capabilities into a single framework is an opportunity to simultaneously estimate both state and covariance in the presence of outliers. This paper proposes a unified Block-Coordinate Descent framework that combines a norm-aware adaptive robust loss, an Iteratively Reweighted Least-Squares state update, and a Minimum Weighted Covariance Determinant covariance estimator, yielding a self-tuning joint state and covariance estimator. The framework is evaluated in a Monte-Carlo simulation and on real-world ultra-wideband localization experiments in cluttered non-line-of-sight environments. Results show that the proposed estimator consistently recovers the true inlier measurement covariance and matches or exceeds the state estimation accuracy of all baselines, without requiring any manual parameter tuning.

</details>


### [286] [Slow Brain, Fast Planner: Latency-Resilient VLM-Augmented Urban Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20458)
*Zhenghao "Mark'' Peng,Honglin He,Quanyi Li,Yukai Ma,Bolei Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A training-free fusion layer enables real-time trajectory scoring in a VLM-assisted planner, reducing the trajectory scoring gap in sidewalk navigation by propagating stale VLM selections with exponential decay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the trajectory scoring gap where anchor-based planners fail to pick the best candidate trajectories in real-time sidewalk navigation due to limited high-level scene understanding and the latency of vision-language models.

Method: Propose a latency-resilient, trajectory-level fusion layer that converts a stale VLM selection into real-time planner scoring via geometric similarity with exponential decay; use VLM to select an index from the planner's proposal set and fuse with the planner output; evaluate on ~2,000 real-world scenarios and in simulation; deploy on a mobile robot with network latency.

Result: VLM selection yields ~30% ADE reduction compared to the planner's best candidate; maintains competitive performance in routine scenarios; Score Fusion achieves >80% success in simulation with 5s delays.

Conclusion: VLM-based selection with a lightweight fusion layer can effectively bridge the trajectory scoring gap under latency constraints without replacing the underlying planner, enabling robust sidewalk navigation in real-world conditions.

Abstract: Learning-based planners for sidewalk navigation can generate diverse candidate trajectories in real time, yet their scoring functions often fail to select the best trajectory in challenging situations, outputting trajectories that make the mobile robot drive onto grass, toward pedestrians, or in the wrong direction, even when better candidates exist in the same set. We call this the trajectory scoring gap: in real-world sidewalk navigation, the gap between an anchor-based planner's top choice and the best possible candidate is substantial, likely due to limited high-level scene understanding capability of the planner. Rather than replacing the planner with an end-to-end Vision-Language-Action model, we propose a VLM-Planner interface that uses a VLM to select a candidate index from the planner's proposal set and then fuse it with the planner's initial output. However, VLMs take 1--3s per query and so cannot directly drive a 5--20Hz control loop. We contribute a training-free, latency-resilient trajectory-level fusion layer that turns a stale VLM selection into real-time planner scoring via geometric similarity with exponential decay. On $\sim$2,000 challenging real-world scenarios (e.g., junctions, pedestrian encounters), VLM selection achieves 30% ADE reduction versus the planner's best selection, while the planner remains competitive in routine situations. In simulation, Score Fusion maintains >80% success rate with delays up to 5s. We demonstrate the full system on a mobile robot navigating challenging campus sidewalks with varied network latency.

</details>


### [287] [GroundControl: Anticipating Navigation Failures in Vision-Language Agents via Trajectory-Consistent Uncertainty Estimates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20479)
*Nastaran Darabi,Divake Kumar,Sina Tayebati,Devashri Naik,Amit Ranjan Trivedi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GroundControl provides a trajectory-consistent uncertainty score for vision-language navigation by modeling distance-to-goal dynamics with a constant-velocity Kalman filter and combining trajectory features to anticipate failures beyond instantaneous entropy; evaluated with a selective risk framework showing near-oracle ranking and superior AURC/E-AURC scores across multiple splits and models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Failures in vision-language navigation often arise from trajectory-level breakdowns (oscillation, stagnation, detours). Existing uncertainty signals focusing on instantaneous action entropy are insufficient for anticipating such dynamics. A trajectory-consistent uncertainty estimator can better forecast failures during execution.

Method: Model distance evolution using a constant-velocity Kalman filter. Compute normalized innovation statistics and integrate them with trajectory features capturing progress, monotonicity, path efficiency, and oscillation. Produce GroundControl as a trajectory-consistent uncertainty score reflecting geometric/temporal inconsistency. Evaluate using Selective Risk–Coverage Navigation (SRCN) with risk–coverage curves, AURC and E-AURC across five EB-Navigation splits (N=300).

Result: GroundControl achieves near-oracle ordering under success-based selective risk, with weighted-average E-AURC_SR = 0.0024 for GPT-4o, outperforming entropy-, conformal-, and heuristic baselines. Under SPL-based selective evaluation, GroundControl attains the lowest AURC and E-AURC across models and splits.

Conclusion: Modeling deviation from goal-directed dynamics yields an interpretable and robust cue for anticipating navigation failures in vision-language agents, outperforming conventional uncertainty signals on trajectory-level failure anticipation.

Abstract: Vision-language navigation agents achieve competitive average success on benchmark tasks, yet failures often arise through predictable trajectory-level breakdowns such as oscillation, stagnation, or inefficient detours. Reliable deployment, therefore, requires uncertainty signals that anticipate emerging failure dynamics during execution rather than reflect only instantaneous action entropy. We introduce \emph{GroundControl}, a trajectory-consistent uncertainty estimator defined as statistical deviation from nominal goal-directed distance-to-goal dynamics aggregated over an episode. GroundControl models distance evolution using a constant-velocity Kalman filter and combines normalized innovation statistics with complementary trajectory features capturing progress, monotonicity, path efficiency, and oscillatory behavior. The resulting uncertainty score reflects geometric and temporal inconsistency in navigation behavior rather than local prediction dispersion. To evaluate uncertainty quality independently of task success, we formalize \emph{Selective Risk--Coverage Navigation (SRCN)}, a protocol that measures how effectively an uncertainty score ranks episodes by failure or inefficiency using risk--coverage curves and AURC / E-AURC summaries. Across five EB-Navigation splits ($N=300$ episodes), trajectory-consistent uncertainty achieves near-oracle ordering under success-based selective risk, with weighted-average $\mathrm{E\text{-}AURC}_{\mathrm{SR}}=0.0024$ for the GPT-4o model, substantially outperforming entropy-, conformal-, and heuristic baselines. Under SPL-based selective evaluation, GroundControl consistently achieves the lowest AURC and E-AURC across models and navigation splits. These results show that modeling deviation from goal-directed dynamics provides an interpretable and robust signal for anticipating navigation failures in vision-language agents.

</details>


### [288] [Fast Human Attention Prediction for Fixation-guided Active Perception in Autonomous Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20491)
*Fatma Youssef Mohammed,Grzegorz Malczyk,Kostas Alexis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introduces GazeLNN, a lightweight autoregressive gaze-prediction model using Liquid Neural Networks and MobileNetV3, achieving state-of-the-art ScanMatch on MIT Low-Resolution data with ultra-low compute, and validating in a real-world aerial-robot deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High computational cost of existing predictive gaze models hinders real-time robot autonomy; a need for efficient, accurate scanpath prediction.

Method: Liquid Neural Network-based recurrent engine with MobileNetV3 feature extractor; auto-regressive architecture predicting sequential fixation heatmaps conditioned on current stimulus and fixation history; trained on MIT Low-Resolution dataset; integrated into a reinforcement-learning-based active perception policy for navigation.

Result: 0.61 GFLOPs; 0.47 ScanMatch score on MIT Low-Resolution dataset; outperforms recurrent baselines across metrics; 99.40% reduction in computation; up to 6x faster inference; successful real-world deployment on an aerial robot with RL-based control.

Conclusion: Efficient gaze modeling enables human-fixation-guided perception in robotics; GazeLNN demonstrates practical utility and potential for broader adoption in autonomous vision systems.

Abstract: Human visual attention relies on structured scanpaths to efficiently process scenes, yet instilling this behavior into robot autonomy is in its infancy and hindered by the high,computational costs of existing predictive models. To address this, we introduce GazeLNN, a computationally lightweight,scanpath prediction model that leverages Liquid Neural Networks as its recurrent engine and employs MobileNetV3 for feature extraction. Operating auto-regressively, the architecture predicts sequential fixation heatmaps conditioned on the current visual stimulus and fixation history. Despite requiring only 0.61 GFLOPs, GazeLNN achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MIT Low Resolution dataset achieving 0.47 ScanMatch score. It outperforms existing recurrent baselines across diverse evaluation metrics, while reducing computational costs by 99.40% and accelerating inference by up to six times. To investigate the role of human attention modeling in robot autonomy and demonstrate the practical utility of this highly efficient architecture, we integrate GazeLNN into an active camera-robot control policy trained via Reinforcement Learning. This integration enables human-fixation-guided perception during autonomous navigation, validated through successful real-world deployments on an aerial robot.

</details>


### [289] [Increasing Resilience of Continuum Robots via Motion Planning Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20495)
*Oxana Shamilyan,Ievgen Kabin,Zoya Dyka,Oleksandr Sudakov,Peter Langendoerfer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AHP-based multi-criteria evaluation is embedded in GA and A*, improving path quality for resilient continuum robots; GA yields more diverse, resilient paths with runtime unaffected by environment size, unlike A*.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance resilience and reliability of path planning for continuum robots by explicitly incorporating multiple resilience-related criteria into the planning process using AHP.

Method: Modify GA and A* by integrating Analytical Hierarchy Process to evaluate candidate paths on four criteria: distance, motor damage, mechanical damage to the arm, and accuracy; use two simulated environments with varying path-point configurations; compare algorithm performance under simplified yet feature-rich robot models.

Result: GA produces more diverse paths, increasing resilience, and its runtime does not depend on environment cardinality; AHP-based evaluation influences path quality.

Conclusion: Multi-criteria AHP integration with GA and A* is viable for resilient planning; GA provides stability across environments and diversity; A* may be more sensitive to environment, suggesting a trade-off between diversity and speed.

Abstract: This paper presents an experimental study of motion planning for resilient continuum robots. In this study we mainly focused on multi-criteria decision-making, its application for path-planning algorithms, impact on the generated path and execution time. To do this, we used two well-known algorithms for path planning, namely Genetic algorithm and A star algorithm, and modified them by adding the Analytical Hierarchy Process algorithm to evaluate the quality of the paths generated. In our experiment the Analytical Hierarchy Process considers four different criteria, i.e. distance, motors damage, mechanical damage of the robot's arm and accuracy, each considered to contribute to the resilience of a continuum robot. The use of different criteria is necessary to increase the time to maintenance operations of the continuum robot. We conducted the experiments using two different simulated environments of the robot. Although we significantly simplified the robot's model and its environment, we still implemented some of the features of the environment based on the real robot prototype. In particular, one of the environments has single- as well as multi-path points, and other consists of the multi-path points only. The results show that, in contrast to A star, the performance time of Genetic algorithm does not depend on the environment's cardinality. It generates more diverse paths, which increases the robot's resilience.

</details>


### [290] [Generating Robot Hands from Human Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20549)
*Sha Yi,Nicklas Hansen,Xueqian Bai,Carmelo Sferrazza,Michael T. Tolley,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Data-driven framework to design robot hands from human motion: optimize tree-structured hands to mimic fingertip trajectories via IK, with an RL actor accelerating search; yields a 6-DoF general hand and 3-DoF task-specific hands; real-world tests show superior teleoperation and reduced mechanical complexity, validating human motion as a cue for embodiment design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Joint design and control optimization is computationally prohibitive; abundant human manipulation data can serve as a scalable proxy for fabricating effective robot embodiments.

Method: Represent hands as tree-structured designs; use inverse-kinematics-like control to match human fingertip trajectories; optimize designs over 4M+ frames; train RL actor to propose designs and joint angles; fabricate one-piece print-in-place mechanisms; evaluate in teleoperation and trajectory tasks.

Result: 6-DoF hand achieves superior fingertip tracking vs commercial hands; 3-DoF hands reproduce structured trajectories with lower mechanical complexity; search time reduced from hours to minutes; fabrication feasible.

Conclusion: Large-scale human motion data can guide both controller design and physical embodiment, enabling efficient generation of robot hands and potentially other manipulators.

Abstract: Robot learning has advanced rapidly in learning control, but learning the physical body of a robot remains much more difficult because jointly searching over design and control creates a very large combinatorial problem. Here, we present a data-driven framework for generating robot hands from human demonstrations. Instead of learning a complex controller together with each candidate design, we generate robot hand designs using the same simple control policy used after fabrication: matching fingertip positions through inverse kinematics. Using more than 4 million frames of human fingertip motion from everyday manipulation, our algorithm optimizes tree-structured robot hands to reproduce desired target motions. The framework produced both a 6-degree-of-freedom (DoF) general-purpose hand and lower-DoF task-specific hands with spatial four-bar mimic joints. To accelerate the search over designs, we trained a reinforcement-learning (RL) actor to propose good hand designs and joint angles, reducing search time from hours to minutes. We fabricated the mechanisms directly as one-piece articulated structures with print-in-place joints. In real-world experiments, the 6-DoF hand achieved highly accurate teleoperated fingertip tracking better than available commercial robot hands, whereas the specialized 3-DoF hands reproduced structured human and synthetic trajectories with reduced mechanical complexity. These results showed that large-scale human motion data can be used not only to train robot controllers but also as a reference for optimizing and generating the physical embodiment of robots.

</details>


### [291] [MemoryWAM: Efficient World Action Modeling with Persistent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20562)
*Sizhe Yang,Juncheng Mu,Tianming Wei,Chenhao Lu,Xiaofan Li,Linning Xu,Zhengrong Xue,Zhecheng Yuan,Dahua Lin,Jiangmiao Pang,Huazhe Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MemoryWAM introduces a memory-augmented world action model with a hybrid memory system and a tailored attention mechanism to enable long-horizon, memory-dependent manipulation with efficient inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the fundamental trade-off in existing World Action Models between efficient inference (limited history) and long-horizon accuracy (full history with high compute/memory).

Method: A hybrid memory design combining recent frames, event-boundary anchor frames, and compact gist tokens that summarize long-range history, plus a tailored attention mechanism that retrieves both detailed short-term context and compressed long-term context for memory-based decision-making within a WAM.

Result: MemoryWAM outperforms strong vision-language-action (VLA) and WAM baselines on long-horizon, memory-dependent manipulation tasks in both simulation and the real world, while maintaining favorable computational efficiency (reduced inference latency and GPU memory use).

Conclusion: Efficient persistent memory with a specialized attention mechanism enables robust memory-dependent manipulation in real-world robotic tasks, achieving favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs over existing WAM/VLA approaches.

Abstract: Robust robotic manipulation in the real world requires not only an understanding of the current observation, but also memory and dynamics modeling. World action models (WAMs) possess these capabilities by jointly modeling visual foresight and actions conditioned on both current and historical observations, making them a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation. However, existing WAMs face a fundamental trade-off: methods with efficient inference typically condition only on a bounded window of recent observations and therefore struggle in non-Markovian environments, whereas methods that preserve long histories incur time and space costs that grow substantially with sequence length. To address this challenge, we introduce MemoryWAM, a world action model with efficient persistent memory. MemoryWAM uses a hybrid memory design that combines recent frames, event-boundary anchor frames, and compact gist tokens that summarize long-range history. A tailored attention mechanism enables retrieval of both detailed short-term context and compressed long-term context, supporting memory-dependent decision-making with reduced inference latency and GPU memory usage. Across long-horizon, memory-dependent manipulation tasks in both simulation and the real world, MemoryWAM outperforms strong vision-language-action (VLA) and WAM baselines while maintaining favorable computational efficiency.

</details>


### [292] [Human Universal Grasping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.17054)
*Kevin Yuanbo Wu,Tianxing Zhou,Isaac Tu,Billy Yan,Irmak Guzey,David Fouhey,Dandan Shan,Lerrel Pinto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HUG presents a flow-matching approach to generate diverse human grasps from a single RGB-D image, building large-scale 1M-HUGs data and the HUG-Bench benchmark to enable zero-shot robot grasping with improved performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans grasp with generality that current robots lack; abundant, realistic human grasp data and standardized benchmarks are needed to study and improve grasp transfer to robots.

Method: A flow-matching model that fuses RGB and depth to output a grasp parameterized by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose. Data collected via egocentric glasses (1M-HUGs: 1M frames, 27.8 hours, 6,707 objects across 41 buildings). Grasps can be retargeted to various robot hands for zero-shot grasping. HUG-Bench: 90 unseen objects across five geometric categories with metric-scale 3D meshes. Evaluation on 30-object HUG-Bench test set across stereo cameras, robot embodiments, and household environments.

Result: HUG outperforms state-of-the-art grasping baselines by +23% and +34% on challenging objects. Real-world evaluation validates transferability across setups. Code, data, benchmark, checkpoints, and an interactive demo released.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that modeling and collecting large-scale human grasp data enables generalizable, zero-shot robotic grasping and provides a unified benchmark suite to drive progress.

Abstract: Humans can grasp objects effortlessly, whereas multi-fingered robots are far from this level of generality. We argue that the most natural source of robot grasping data is from humans, who pick up thousands of objects every day. We present HUG, a flow-matching model that generates diverse human grasps for any user-specified object in a single RGB-D image captured from a stereo camera. Using smart glasses, we first collect 1M-HUGs, an egocentric dataset of human grasps spanning 1M frames (27.8 hrs) and 6,707 object instances across 41 buildings. Next, to model the distribution of natural human grasps, our novel flow-matching model fuses RGB and depth observations to output a grasp parameterized by wrist translation, wrist rotation, and MANO hand pose. Predicted grasps can be retargeted to various robot hands, enabling zero-shot grasping in everyday scenes. To standardize evaluation, we build a new simulated benchmark, HUG-Bench, of 90 unseen objects from five geometric categories and various sizes, with metric-scale 3D meshes. We evaluate HUG in the real world on the 30-object test set of HUG-Bench across multiple stereo cameras, robot embodiments, and household environments. HUG outperforms the state-of-the-art grasping baselines by +23% and +34% on our challenging object set. Code, data, benchmark, checkpoints, and an interactive demo are released on our website: https://grasping.io/

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [293] [Deontic Policies for Runtime Governance of Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19464)
*Anupam Joshi,Tim Finin,Karuna Pande Joshi,Lalana Kagal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgenticRei introduces a deontic-policy governance layer for LLM-driven agents, adding obligations, dispensations, and conflict resolution via an OWL-expressed policy language evaluated by an external logic engine, to govern tool use and inter-agent messaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous, tool-enabled AI agents create governance challenges beyond simple permit/prohibit controls. Current policy engines lack obligation lifecycle management, dispensations, and meta-policy conflict resolution across domains (healthcare, cybersecurity, privacy). A robust governance layer is needed to ensure security, privacy, and compliance.

Method: Develop a deontic policy language atop the Rei framework, expressed in OWL, with runtime evaluation by a high-performance logic engine outside the LLM. The same policy pipeline governs both agent tool invocations and inter-agent messages, and it integrates with industry frameworks like A2AS.

Result: Demonstrates through examples that deontic policies capture governance constraints that cannot be expressed with existing production engines (XACML, Rego, Cedar).

Conclusion: AgenticRei provides essential governance capabilities (obligations, dispensations, conflict resolution, ontological reasoning) alongside standard permit/prohibit constraints, and can interoperate with industry standards, offering a scalable approach to secure, private, and compliant autonomous agents.

Abstract: Autonomous agentic AI systems driven by Large Language Models (LLMs) introduce a new class of security, privacy, and compliance challenges: an agent that can invoke tools, manipulate data, install software, and coordinate with peer agents across organizational boundaries must be constrained not just by authentication and access control, but by the full structure of enterprise governance. This includes specifying what agents are permitted and prohibited from doing, what they areobliged to do after certain actions (e.g., notify the CISO), under what conditions a standing obligation may be waived, and which rules take precedence when policies conflict. This governance problem exceeds what current policy engines provide. Systems such as XACML, Rego, and Cedar address only the permit/prohibit subset of this governance structure. They do not provide obligation lifecycle management, meta-policy conflict resolution, dispensations that waive obligations in specific circumstances, and ontological reasoning over domain class hierarchies commonly found in applications such as healthcare, cybersecurity, or data privacy. We propose AgenticRei, which realizes key governance requirements such as obligations, dispensations, policy conflict resolutions, and reasoning over policies, as well as the basic permit/prohibit constraints. We use a deontic policy language built on the Rei framework, expressed as OWL (Web Ontology Language) and evaluated at runtime by a high-performance logic engine entirely outside the LLM. The same pipeline governs both tool invocations by the agent and agent-to-agent messages. We show through examples that deontic policies capture governance constraints around security and privacy that mostly cannot be expressed in current production engines. Our approach composes naturally with industry-standard frameworks like A2AS.

</details>


### [294] [Measuring Curriculum Alignment across Topical Coverage, Competency, and Cognitive Depth: A Longitudinal Framework Applied to CS2013 and CS2023](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19469)
*Sherzod Turaev,Mary John,Saja Aldabet,Mamoun Awad,Nazar Zaki,Khaled Shuaib*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A human-in-the-loop pipeline assesses undergraduate CS programs' alignment with CS2013/CS2023, showing ~50% knowledge-unit coverage and revealing gaps due to guideline evolution rather than program quality; the approach is reusable and benchmarked against multiple retrievers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a reliable, reproducible method to measure how well CS programs cover evolving international curricular guidelines and how coverage shifts with guideline restructuring.

Method: Represent programs and guidelines as structured corpora; generate candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches via semantic retrieval; confirm with human judgment under an explicit coverage definition; benchmark seven retrievers; use reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble; compare long-context vs small-sentence models; validate with a second rater (Cohen’s kappa reported).

Result: Program covers 49.7% of CS2023 and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, stable over a decade; competency articulation ~88% of covered units; depth met for 76% (CS2023) vs 95% (CS2013); persistent gaps include parallel/distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, and systems fundamentals; instrument is reusable and available on request.

Conclusion: The pipeline enables longitudinal assessment of guideline coverage, separates structural gaps from guideline evolution, and provides a reusable tool for accreditation and curriculum planning.

Abstract: Undergraduate computer science is governed by international curricular guidelines revised about once a decade, yet programs lack a reliable, reproducible way to measure how completely they cover the current guidelines and how that coverage shifts when the guidelines are restructured. We address this with a human-in-the-loop pipeline that measures a program's coverage of an external body of knowledge, applied longitudinally to one accredited BSc in Computer Science against Computer Science Curricula 2013 (CS2013) and 2023 (CS2023). The pipeline represents the program and each guideline as structured corpora, generates candidate course-to-knowledge-unit matches by semantic retrieval, and confirms them through human judgment under an explicit coverage definition. Of seven benchmarked retrievers, a reciprocal-rank-fusion ensemble was strongest, and a reputed long-context model underperformed a small sentence model, so retriever choice must be measured. Both maps were validated by an independent second rater (Cohen's kappa 0.64 for CS2023, 0.69 for CS2013). The program covers 49.7% of CS2023 and 50.9% of CS2013 knowledge units, near-constant across a decade. Extending the same retrieve-then-confirm design to competency articulation and cognitive depth shows that the program articulates the competency for ~88% of covered units under each guideline, yet delivers it at the recommended depth for 76% of present units under CS2023 against 95% under CS2013, a gap reflecting the newer guideline's raised expectations, not the program. The longitudinal comparison separates persistent structural gaps (parallel and distributed computing, foundations of programming languages, systems fundamentals), uncovered against both guidelines and ABET, from differences that reflect the standard's evolution. The instrument is reusable and available from the authors on request.

</details>


### [295] [Diffusion Language Models: An Experimental Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19475)
*Thomas Bertolani,Davide Bucciarelli,Leonardo Zini,Marcella Cornia,Lorenzo Baraldi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A comprehensive empirical evaluation of diffusion language models (DLMs) across eight benchmarks, examining how generation quality and computational efficiency vary with inference-time choices, to map strengths, limits, and deployment trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DLMs offer an alternative to autoregressive LMs by generating text via iterative denoising. A systematic evaluation is needed due to inconsistent evaluation protocols, datasets, and hyperparameters across prior work, to understand task-based performance and efficiency trade-offs.

Method: Eight state-of-the-art DLMs are evaluated on eight benchmarks covering reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving. The study jointly assesses generation quality and computational efficiency, and analyzes inference-time factors (denoising steps, context length, block size, parallel unmasking). Controlled comparisons are conducted with smaller models trained under identical conditions.

Result: The analysis identifies clear strengths and limitations of DLMs across tasks and budgets. DLM behavior is strongly shaped by generation-time design choices, producing distinct trade-offs between quality and efficiency; results provide practical insights into deployment characteristics and guide future evaluation and comparison.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based language modeling offers viable performance with task- and budget-dependent trade-offs. Proper tuning of inference-time settings is crucial, and the study delivers actionable guidance for evaluating and deploying modern DLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized language modeling through autoregressive generation, enabling strong performance across a wide range of tasks. Recently, Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have emerged as an alternative paradigm that generates text through iterative denoising rather than next-token prediction, allowing parallel refinement of entire sequences. While numerous diffusion-based architectures have been proposed, differences in evaluation protocols, datasets, inference budgets, and generation hyperparameters make it difficult to compare their capabilities and understand the trade-offs they offer. In this work, we present a systematic experimental analysis of modern DLMs. Specifically, we evaluate eight state-of-the-art DLMs across eight benchmarks spanning reasoning, coding, translation, knowledge, and structured problem solving, while explicitly considering both generation quality and computational efficiency. Beyond downstream evaluation, we analyze the impact of key inference-time factors, including denoising steps, context length, block size, and parallel unmasking strategies, and complement large-scale experiments with controlled comparisons of smaller models trained under identical conditions. Our analysis highlights the strengths and limitations of diffusion-based language modeling across different tasks, architectures, and inference budgets. We show that the behavior of DLMs is strongly influenced by generation-time design choices, leading to distinct trade-offs between performance and computational efficiency. Overall, our study provides practical insights into the capabilities and deployment characteristics of contemporary DLMs.

</details>


### [296] [Hidden Anchors in Multi-Agent LLM Deliberation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19494)
*Apurba Pokharel,Ram Dantu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a closed-loop multi-agent deliberation model where each agent has a hidden internal anchor; anchors can be recovered from deliberation data and explain how confidence can exceed the initial belief hull; the influence of anchors is similar across models but their locations vary, enabling hull-escape only when anchors sit far from initial opinions; validated via held-out generalization tests across three open-weight model families, revealing a spectrum of anchor strengths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain why multi-agent LLM deliberation improves reasoning by extending beyond classical opinion-dynamics models (e.g., DeGroot, Friedkin–Johnsen) and incorporating internal beliefs that pull an agent's opinion.

Method: Formulate a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden anchor that continually pulls its belief. Develop a method to recover the anchor from deliberation data. Conduct experiments across three open-weight model families, examining whether recovered anchors predict held-out runs and how anchors influence the dynamics relative to the initial opinions (convex hull).

Result: Anchors can be recovered from deliberation data and explain a behavior unavailable to purely external-consensus rules: an agent’s confidence in the correct answer can exceed the convex hull of initial beliefs. Across three open-weight model families, anchors exert comparable overall influence but differ in anchor location; only distant anchors from initial opinions cause the deliberation to escape the convex hull, necessitating the closed-loop model. Generalization tests on held-out runs support the anchor-based explanation, yielding a spectrum of anchor strengths rather than a binary effect.

Conclusion: Internal-belief anchors are a unifying explanation for multi-agent LLM deliberation dynamics. The anchor’s location, not just its strength, governs when full closed-loop dynamics are needed. The authors provide a simple generalization test and show (across model families) a spectrum of anchor configurations, informing design and interpretation of multi-agent deliberation systems.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM deliberation, where agents exchange and revise answers over several rounds, is increasingly used to improve reasoning and accuracy, yet how and why it works is rarely modelled. Such deliberation mirrors how humans reach decisions. As social animals we are pulled both by the group, the herd effect that classical opinion-dynamics models such as DeGroot and Friedkin--Johnsen capture, and by our own internal belief, which they do not. We model multi-agent deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system in which each agent carries a hidden internal belief, its anchor, that continually pulls its opinion regardless of its neighbours. We show this anchor can be recovered from the deliberation alone, and that it explains a behaviour classical consensus rules forbid: an agent's confidence in the correct answer can climb past where any agent started, escaping the space (convexhull) formed by the initial beliefs. Checking whether the recovered anchor also predicts held-out runs (generalizes) gives a simple test for when a model is truly driven bysuch an anchor. Across three open-weight model families this is a spectrum, not all-or-nothing. All anchors' influence are about equally strongly, but they differ in where the anchor sits, and only when it sits far from the initial opinions does deliberation escape the hull and need the full closed-loop model.

</details>


### [297] [DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19501)
*Aijie Shu,Bowei Chen,Wenbin Wu,Cathy Yi-Hsuan Chen,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A forecast-grounded, regulator-aligned supervision system for DeFi risk that routes LLM-driven decisions through a graph-time-series forecast model (DeXposure-FM), deterministic monitors, and data-health gates to produce auditable supervisory tickets; evaluated with DeXposure-Bench using regulator-aligned loss and false-intervention rate on five years of weekly data; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLMs over-read weak evidence and trigger high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations lack regulator-aligned measures for false alarms.

Method: Three-layer pipeline: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios translate forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation and emit auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. Evaluation harness DeXposure-Bench offers six axes and scores against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and explicit false-intervention rate.

Result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support the system.

Conclusion: Provides an auditable, regulator-aligned supervision framework for DeFi risk with open-source code, demonstrating improved decision alignment and controllable false alarms.

Abstract: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

</details>


### [298] [LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19509)
*Akshat Dasula,Prasanna Desikan,Jaideep Srivastava*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cross-model attribution divergence between a small LLM (Qwen 2.5 7B) and XGBoost on structured data reveals: (1) LLM verbalized confidence is non-informative; (2) an inverse difficulty effect; (3) few-shot prompts and SHAP evidence are orthogonal yet synergistic in reducing attribution disagreement and boosting accuracy; (4) a cross-model calibrator using attribution signals lowers calibration error without internal model access, framing a cold-start path toward epistemic self-awareness in structured tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs can recognize their own knowledge limits on structured data tasks and to reduce epistemic uncertainty by leveraging attribution divergence between models as a supervision signal.

Method: Compare Qwen 2.5 7B LLM and XGBoost on a structured-prediction task; quantify attribution divergence via Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS); examine LLM verbalized confidence, prediction accuracy, and inter-model agreement; test few-shot prompting; incorporate SHAP-derived feature evidence; develop a cross-model calibrator that uses attribution divergence to estimate LLM reliability without internal access; evaluate calibration (ECE).

Result: Four main findings: (1) LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, near-constant (0.856–0.937) and correlates with prompt format rather than accuracy. (2) Inverse difficulty effect: LLM accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but aligns with XGBoost (≈73.8% vs. 73.1%) when LLM is moderately uncertain. (3) Few-shot and SHAP evidence are orthogonal but super-additive: together they reduce ADS from 1.54 to 0.38 and raise accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. (4) A cross-model calibrator based on attribution divergence reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, providing patient-specific reliability estimates without accessing model internals or re-inference.

Conclusion: The results frame LLM use on structured data as a cold-start problem and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness, via attribution-divergence signaling and calibration that leverages cross-model evidence rather than internal model confidences.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

</details>


### [299] [REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19522)
*Ethan Elio Meidinger,Seowung Leem,Zeyun Zhao,Ruogu Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Continuous phenotypic structure in contrastive learning for vision-language retinal AD risk prediction; soft multi-positive relations via differentiable similarity weights; end-to-end optimization improves prediction over discrete-group baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete, hard phenotypic groupings in contrastive learning impose rigid supervision and decouple risk grouping from representation learning; need a spectrum-aware, scalable approach for multi-modal neurodegenerative risk modeling.

Method: Introduce differentiable inter-subject weighting based on intra-modality embedding similarities in retinal images and risk profiles; compute soft multi-positive relationships via a continuous aggregation operator; optimize a soft-target contrastive objective jointly for cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure end-to-end.

Result: On UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the continuous approach outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines.

Conclusion: Model phenotypic similarity as a learnable continuous signal, enabling robust, population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

Abstract: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

</details>


### [300] [Emergent Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19527)
*Martin Kolář*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An online, self-guided alignment framework for LLMs that uses a conscience step to review reasoning and outputs, plus a Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)–based alignment loss, enabling emergent alignment across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot tasks, using a frozen self as judge; demonstrates that a single introspective question can steer training toward ethics under emergent misalignment scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability and robustness of alignment by removing reliance on external judges. Enable self-consistent ethics checks during training to mitigate Emergent Misalignment and support ethical behavior across applications.

Method: Introduce a conscience step that introspects the model's reasoning and outputs; augment the training objective with an alignment component derived from Direct Preference Optimization (DPO); use a frozen copy of the model as the judge (no weaker/stronger external evaluator); apply the approach to training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot settings; evaluate against Emergent Misalignment scenarios.

Result: Empirically demonstrates online alignment capability and Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question can steer training toward ethical behavior under code-hacking-like scenarios; alignment achieved without external judges.

Conclusion: The framework offers a scalable, self-contained path to align LLMs across diverse settings. Conscience-based introspection paired with DPO can guide models toward ethical outputs, though broader validation and evaluation of potential circularity and generalizability are needed.

Abstract: Can Large Language Models (LLMs) discern when their own outputs are misaligned with human ethics? And can they self-correct? We endow an LLM with a conscience step that reviews its own reasoning and outputs, and we extend the training loss with an alignment component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer the model away from non-ethical outputs. The result is an online technique to align models in a wide range of applications: training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning. It does not require a weaker or stronger judge, relying instead on a frozen copy of itself. In previous work, the Emergent Misalignment scenario showed a range of emergent unethical behaviors from fine-tuning the model to hack code. Instead, we empirically show how to achieve Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question steers training toward an ethical model under the same code hacking scenario.

</details>


### [301] [ITNet: A Learnable Integral Transform That Subsumes Convolution, Attention, and Recurrence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19538)
*Ashim Dhor,Rasel Mondal,Pin Yu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ITNet unifies CNNs, RNNs, and transformers as a single learnable integral transform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the apparent fragmentation of inductive biases by proposing a universal operator for signals, enabling a single architecture to adapt to diverse tasks.

Method: Introduce ITNet with a learnable kernel dependent on positions and features, implemented as an MLP modeling pairwise interactions. Demonstrate that convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (LSTM/GRU/S4/Mamba) are special cases under appropriate parameterizations. Use tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization for efficiency. Employ shared operator with lightweight modality-specific encoders.

Result: ITNet matches or exceeds specialized baselines across ImageNet-1K, GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA v2, and NLVR2, demonstrating universal approximation and practical scalability.

Conclusion: A single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of convolutional, attention-based, and recurrent architectures from data, suggesting a unified foundation for sequence and spatial processing.

Abstract: Convolutional networks, recurrent networks, and transformers each encode different inductive biases -- locality, sequential memory, and content-dependent pairwise interaction -- and have remained mathematically distinct since their inception. We show that this fragmentation reflects not a fundamental diversity in how signals should be processed, but rather incomplete views of a single underlying mathematical object: a learnable integral transform. We introduce the Integral Transform Network (ITNet), a unified architecture built around a learnable kernel that depends jointly on positions and features. This kernel is implemented as a small neural network, specifically an MLP, that models pairwise interactions, enabling the model to adapt its behavior from data. We show that convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (including LSTM, GRU, S4, and Mamba) arise as special cases under appropriate parameterizations, and that ITNet is a universal approximator of continuous operators. To make this practical, we develop tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization, enabling efficient and scalable computation. A single ITNet architecture with a shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K , GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA\,v2 and NLVR2. The results demonstrate that a single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families from data.

</details>


### [302] [Uncertainty Decomposition for Clarification Seeking in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19559)
*Gregory Matsnev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prompts-based decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty enables proactive clarification in interactive LLM agents; introduces WebShop-Clarification and ALFWorld-Clarification benchmarks; shows robust gains across five LLM backbones against ReAct+UE and UAM, suggesting generalizability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for underspecification-aware uncertainty representations in interactive LLM agents. Traditional logprob-based, multi-sampling, and training-based methods are impractical under deployment constraints (black-box APIs, latency budgets, no labeled trajectories); prompt-based estimation offers a viable surface for uncertainty signals at deployment time.

Method: Propose a simple prompt-based decomposition that divides action confidence and a separate request uncertainty (u). The agent requests clarification when the task is underspecified. Evaluate using two new benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification, ALFWorld-Clarification) where 50% tasks are underspecified. Compare against ReAct+UE and Uncertainty-Aware Memory (UAM) across five LLM backbones (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-v3.2-exp, GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5-35B, GPT-OSS-120B) on WebShop, ALFWorld, REAL.

Result: Clarification F1 improvements: ALFWorld-Clarification shows 73% higher F1 over ReAct+UE and 36% over UAM on average across backbones. WebShop-Clarification achieves clarification F1 on every backbone; ALFWorld-Clarification reaches four of five backbones. These gains suggest the method generalizes across different LLMs and tasks.

Conclusion: A prompt-based decomposition yielding separate action confidence and request uncertainty effectively surfaces underspecification signals, enabling proactive clarification and shared mental-model building. The gains generalize across multiple LLM backbones and tasks under deployment constraints.

Abstract: Recent position papers argue that the classical aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty framework is insufficient for interactive large language model (LLM) agents and call for underspecification-aware, decomposed, and communicable uncertainty representations that can unlock new agent capabilities such as proactive clarification seeking and shared mental-model building. Practical deployment constraints -- black-box APIs, interactive latency budgets, and the absence of labeled trajectories -- rule out logprob-based, multi-sampling, and training-based methods, leaving prompt-based estimation as the most viable family for surfacing such signals at deployment time. We answer this call with a simple prompt-based decomposition that separates action confidence from request uncertainty (u), enabling the agent to ask for clarification when the task specification is ambiguous. To evaluate it, we introduce two clarification-augmented benchmarks (WebShop-Clarification and ALFWorld-Clarification) in which 50% of tasks are deliberately underspecified, and systematically compare the proposed decomposition against ReAct+UE and Uncertainty-Aware Memory (UAM) across five LLM backbones (GPT-5.1, DeepSeek-v3.2-exp, GLM-4.7, Qwen3.5-35B, GPT-OSS-120B) on these variants together with the standard WebShop, ALFWorld, and REAL benchmarks for fault detection. Averaged across the five backbones, the proposed decomposition improves clarification F1 on ALFWorld-Clarification by 73% over ReAct+UE and by 36% over UAM, and leads clarification F1 on every backbone on WebShop-Clarification and on four of five backbones on ALFWorld-Clarification, indicating that the gains generalize beyond a single LLM.

</details>


### [303] [Analyzing the Narration Gap in LLM-Solver Loops](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19588)
*Zunchen Huang,Songgaojun Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Models an LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure, highlighting a narration gap between formal tool output and user-facing answers; empirical evidence shows certificate gating improves soundness but adversaries can invert verified conclusions across wordings and channels; hardened prompts reduce but do not eliminate injection, with adaptive attacks still possible; overall robustness does not reach the final user answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unstudied narration stage in LLM-solver pipelines, ensuring that the user-facing answer remains sound and verifiable despite interactions between language models and formal tools.

Method: Formal modeling of the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure; empirical evaluation of five open-source LLMs under prompt injection; analysis of certificate gating and adversarial inversion; assessment of hardened prompts and adaptive attacks.

Result: Certificate gating helps ensure sound solver verdicts, but an adversary can invert verified conclusions across phrasings and channels; hardened prompts significantly reduce but do not eliminate injection; adaptive attacks still pose risks; robustness of the loop does not guarantee the user-visible answer is correct.

Conclusion: The LLM-solver loop exhibits a gap between formal verification of internal decisions and the integrity of the final user-facing answer; requires improved narration mechanisms and stronger, multi-faceted defense to align the delivered answer with verified outcomes.

Abstract: Formal tools such as SAT and SMT solvers are increasingly embedded in language model reasoning pipelines when a safety or security critical question can be formulated in logic. Unlike chain of thought whose steps are sampled from the model distribution without formal guarantee, a solver produces a sound and independently verifiable answer. However, the soundness guarantee can be lost in the interaction between the solver and the model.
