The hybrid pipeline has three components: formalizing the question, deciding it, and narrating the result. Prior work has studied the formalization and decision, but not narration, which is the step that turns a formal tool's output into the user answer. To fill the narration gap, we first model the LLM-solver loop as a verified decision procedure. We further evaluate five open-sourced models under prompt injection, and we find certificate gating makes the solver verdict sound, while an adversary can invert a verified conclusion across phrasings and channels. We study the mitigation through hardened prompt that reduces injection significantly but cannot eliminate it and still suffers under adaptive attack. Combining the formal analysis and empirical studies, we show in the LLM-solver loop, robustness does not reach to the answer that the user finally reads.

</details>


### [304] [Configurable Clinical Information Extraction with Agentic RAG: What Works, What Breaks, and Why](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19602)
*Osman Alperen Çinar-Koraş,Marie Bauer,Sameh Khattab,Merlin Engelke,Moon Kim,Stephan Settelmeier,Shigeyasu Sugawara,Fabian Freisleben,Felix Nensa,Jens Kleesiek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ACIE enables agentic, on-premise RAG over full patient contexts with factual grounding to source passages; achieves 96.5% agreement with clinician verification across 7,326 judgments in a lymphoma registry study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Document-level metadata is often missing or incomplete; standard retrieval-augmented generation struggles with temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and missing metadata; a reliable, groundable extraction pipeline is needed for clinician retrieval and triage.

Method: Deploy ACIE: an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages for clinician verification; evaluation includes an independent retrospective lymphoma registry study with physicians verifying extracted values against cited sources; 7,326 judgments.

Result: Clinicians accepted 96.5% of extractions; per-type acceptance ranged from 80% to 99%.

Conclusion: ACIE demonstrates high-fidelity, groundable information extraction in a real clinical context, addressing metadata gaps and enabling clinician-verified retrieval/triage; on-premise deployment and type-variability considerations remain relevant for broader adoption.

Abstract: Patient contexts span hundreds of heterogeneous documents and thousands of structured data points, yet the document-level metadata that AI systems need for retrieval and triage is absent or incomplete. Standard retrieval-augmented generation fails on this data, mishandling temporal reasoning, cross-document dependencies, and missing metadata. We deploy ACIE (Agentic Clinical Information Extraction) at University Medicine Essen: an on-premise agentic RAG pipeline that reasons over complete patient contexts and grounds every answer in source passages for clinician verification. We quantify the metadata gap, trace the architectural decisions it shaped, and evaluate extraction alongside an independent retrospective lymphoma registry study, in which nuclear-medicine physicians verify every extracted value against its cited sources. Across 7,326 judgments, clinicians accepted 96.5\% of extractions, with per-type acceptance ranging from 80\% to 99\%.

</details>


### [305] [Which Pairs to Compare for LLM Post-Training?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19607)
*Jiangze Han,Vineet Goyal,Will Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework for budgeted comparison curation in preference-based post-training (DPO) that derives a design-dependent information bound and shows how to select informative pairs to maximize downstream policy performance, yielding improved sample efficiency over heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human preference labeling is costly; better to allocate limited labels to the most informative comparison pairs rather than exhaustively labeling all generated completions.

Method: Formulates comparison curation as a sampling-design problem, derives upper and lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap for DPO, identifies a single design-dependent information matrix linking label allocation to parameter estimation error and policy suboptimality, and proposes concrete optimization criteria and sampling designs; validates on synthetic tasks and LM post-training benchmarks.

Result: The proposed designs improve sample efficiency and performance over common heuristics, with theoretical bounds clarifying the role of the information matrix.

Conclusion: Design-aware, budgeted labeling for preference-based post-training is effective; the information-matrix framework provides guidance for selecting informative comparison pairs and improving downstream LM policies.

Abstract: Preference-based post-training has become a central paradigm for aligning language models. A common data-collection strategy is to generate a small set of completions for each prompt and label the resulting comparison pairs. However, human preference labels are often much more expensive than generating additional completions, suggesting a different use of the same labeling budget: generate a larger pool of completions, but label only the most informative comparison pairs. This paper studies which pairs should be compared in preference-based post-training. We formulate comparison curation as a sampling-design problem and evaluate designs by the quality of the final policy under the preference-based post-training objective. We instantiate this framework for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), analyzing how the choice of labeled pairs propagates through DPO training to downstream policy performance. Our main results provide matching upper and lower bounds on the post-training optimality gap of the DPO-trained policy. The bounds show that comparison selection affects downstream performance through a single design-dependent information matrix, which links label allocation to parameter estimation error and policy suboptimality. This yields an explicit optimization criterion for budgeted comparison curation and motivates practical sampling designs for selecting informative pairs from large generated completion pools. Experiments on synthetic settings and language-model post-training benchmarks show that the proposed designs consistently improve sample efficiency over common comparison-selection heuristics.

</details>


### [306] [Toten: Knowledge-Based Ontological Tokenization Of Physical Quantities And Technical Notation In Brazilian Portuguese](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19626)
*Antonio de Sousa Leitão Filho; Allan Kardec Duailibe Barros Filho; Fabrício Saul Lima; Selby Mykael Lima dos Santos; Rejani Bandeira Vieira Sousa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TOTEN provides a knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework for engineering texts, outperforming statistical baselines in ontological atomicity and numeric reconstruction by grounding tokenization in a formal ontology (OEE) and three external oracles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Statistical tokenization (e.g., Byte-Pair Encoding) is efficient for vocabulary compression but semantically blind to structured technical entities (quantities, units, numbers, expressions). A knowledge-based approach aims to preserve semantic structure in engineering text.

Method: Formalize TOTEN as <O, classify, {inst_tau}>. O gathers types, structural principles, composition relations, and invariants; classify maps raw text into typed regions; inst_tau yields a self-descriptive structured representation. Robustness via three external oracles: Pint (dimensional), Unicode Character Database (typographic), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology). Evaluation on EngQuant (N=800) and four Brazilian Portuguese corpora (N=1771 eligible cases). Measures: ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, numerical reconstruction; recall for detection; compare to eight baselines (notably Quantulum3).

Result: TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity in all contrasts and numerical reconstruction in external corpora 0.775–0.904 (vs. 0.627–0.703 for Quantulum3); EngQuant: 0.780 (TOTEN) vs. 0.340 (baseline). Differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm correction). Spearman ranking shows concurrent validity with the control benchmark; dimensional equivalence statistically on par with Pint.

Conclusion: Replacing statistical derivation with declarative, ontology-grounded tokenization yields semantically faithful representations of engineering text, with superior numerical reconstruction and consistent ontological granularity across corpora; the framework is robust through external oracles and shows strong generalization across Portuguese corpora.

Abstract: Byte-Pair Encoding tokenization is statistically efficient for vocabulary compression, but semantically blind to structured technical entities, fragmenting physical quantities, numbers, units, and symbolic expressions into lexically arbitrary subwords. We present TOTEN, a knowledge-based ontological tokenization framework that replaces statistical derivation with declarative classification grounded in a formal ontology of engineering entities (OEE). We formalize TOTEN as the triple <O, classify, {inst_tau}>: the ontology gathers types, structural principles, composition relations, and preservable invariants; the classification function maps raw text into typed regions; and the instantiator family yields a self-descriptive structured representation. Robustness derives from deterministic coupling with three external oracles: Pint (dimensional), Unicode Character Database (typographic), and RSLP (Portuguese morphology). Intrinsic evaluation covers four properties verifiable by construction -- ontological atomicity, dimensional equivalence, typographic robustness, and numerical reconstruction -- over an internal, physically validated benchmark (EngQuant, N=800) and four Brazilian Portuguese external corpora (N=1771 eligible cases). We also report detection recall, distinguishing coverage from conditional atomicity. Against eight state-of-the-art baselines, TOTEN achieves unit ontological atomicity in all contrasts and numerical reconstruction of 0.775-0.904 on external corpora, vs. 0.627-0.703 for the best baseline (Quantulum3); on EngQuant, 0.780 vs. 0.340. Differences are statistically significant (McNemar with Holm correction). Spearman correlation between internal and external rankings confirms concurrent validity of the control benchmark. Dimensional equivalence shows statistical parity with Pint, the oracle from which the system inherits dimensional authority.

</details>


### [307] [AI4SE and SE4AI Exploration: A Decade Looking Back and Forward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19630)
*H. Sinan Bank,Daniel R. Herber,Thomas Bradley*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Three-phase view of AI in SE, plus a large-scale human–AI relevance study; identifies five gaps; and provides an open Explorer tool for benchmarking relevance judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Synthesize AI maturation in SE, quantify human–AI agreement, and guide practitioners amid AI adoption and workforce shift.

Method: Phase-based literature synthesis (foundational, applied, LLM inflection); human–AI agreement study using six AI models to rate 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications; release of AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer.

Result: Identifies five critical gaps; presents agreement data; enables practitioners to compare judgments; demonstrates community engagement via workshop growth.

Conclusion: AI in SE has progressed but significant gaps remain; the provided data and tools support benchmarking, reproducibility, and informed adoption.

Abstract: The March 2020 INCOSE INSIGHT special issue on AI and Systems Engineering (SE) became the most downloaded issue in the publication's history and launched a research community that now draws over 250 registrants to its annual workshop. In this article, we trace the progress in AI and SE across three phases (labeled here foundational, applied, and LLM inflection) based on the authors' reading of the field's core papers, and describe our opinions of where the community has converged and where critical gaps remain. Separately, a human-AI agreement literature review leveraging both human expertise and six AI models was performed to assess the relevance of 1,712 INCOSE INSIGHT articles and 889 SERC publications. The results identify five critical research gaps and offer guidance for practitioners navigating AI adoption, assurance, and workforce transformation in SE. We share the agreement data and the AI4SE/SE4AI Explorer web application so readers can compare their own relevance judgments with the human and AI raters.

</details>


### [308] [BrainG3N: A Dual-Purpose Tokenizer for Controllable 3D Brain MRI Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19651)
*Max Van Puyvelde,Ibrahim Gulluk,Wim Van Criekinge,Olivier Gevaert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A volumetric MAE-based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion; freezes a 3D MAE encoder to produce clinically informative embeddings and uses a CNN decoder for voxel reconstruction, pretrained on 35k volumes from 18 public cohorts; yields strong linear-probing performance and enables a conditional diffusion model trained on embeddings for controllable generation and longitudinal forecasting, implying a single embedding space for both tasks and generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the encoder-decoder trade-off in latent diffusion for 3D MRI by decoupling encoding and decoding via a masked autoencoder (MAE) tokenizer that preserves clinical information while still enabling anatomically faithful reconstruction.

Method: A fully volumetric MAE-based tokenizer with a frozen 3D MAE encoder that outputs embeddings; a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of these embeddings. Pretraining uses 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts, spanning four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ acquisition sites. Evaluation includes (i) 23-task linear probing where the encoder is compared against state-of-the-art models, and (ii) training a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) on the embeddings to enable six-variable conditional generation and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting.

Result: The encoder matches or outperforms SOTA on 21 of 23 linear-probing tasks. A DiT trained on the embeddings supports conditional generation across six variables and longitudinal forecasting, demonstrating a unified 3D brain-MRI embedding space for both downstream tasks and controllable generation.

Conclusion: Decoupling encoding and decoding with a volumetric MAE-based tokenizer yields a clinically informative, invariant embedding space that supports diverse tasks and controllable generation in 3D brain MRI, with broad pretraining data supporting generalizability across cohorts and sites.

Abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) brain MRI is central to clinical neurology and neuro-oncology, where generative models could augment under-represented cohorts, simulate disease trajectories, and support privacy-preserving data sharing. Latent diffusion has been the go-to solution for modeling imaging data, but it places two competing demands on the tokenizer: encoder embeddings must retain the clinical information that downstream tasks act on, and the decoder must reconstruct anatomically faithful volumes. Existing reconstruction-driven tokenizers achieve the second at the expense of the first. To address this, we introduce a fully volumetric masked-autoencoder (MAE) based tokenizer for 3D brain MRI latent diffusion, decoupling encoder and decoder: a frozen 3D MAE encoder produces clinically informative embeddings, while a dedicated CNN decoder reconstructs voxels from a linear projection of those embeddings. We pretrain the encoder on 35,309 volumes from 18 public cohorts spanning four modalities, ten disease categories, and 200+ acquisition sites, and demonstrate its dual utility in two settings. First, on a 23-task linear-probing benchmark, the encoder outperforms or matches SOTA models (i.e., BrainIAC, BrainSegFounder, and MedicalNet) on 21 of 23 tasks. Second, a conditional diffusion transformer (DiT) trained on these clinically informative embeddings supports both conditional generation across six variables and patient-specific longitudinal forecasting. Together these results establish a single 3D brain-MRI embedding space capable of both downstream clinical tasks and controllable generation.

</details>


### [309] [Denoising Implicit Feedback for Cold-start Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19658)
*Gaode Chen,Shicheng Wang,Shikun Li,Rui Huang,Xinghua Zhang,Yunze Luo,Shipeng Li,Shiming Ge,Ruina Sun,Yinjie Jiang,Jun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes DIF, a model-agnostic denoising method for implicit feedback in cold-start items. It uses content-based similarity to warm items to generate pseudo-labels, models pseudo-label confidence via content similarity, aggregates multiple pseudo-labels, and estimates uncertainty using relative entropy and cold-start status to adaptively correct noisy labels. The approach is theoretically justified, validated on real data, and deployed at a billion-user scale (Kuaishou) with commercial gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cold-start items are prone to noisy implicit feedback; existing denoising methods rely on heuristic noise signals and struggle in cold-start contexts. There is a need for a denoising framework that leverages content-based signals to address noise for new items.

Method: 1) Infer pseudo-labels for cold items by leveraging content similarity to warm items with known preferences. 2) Model the confidence of these pseudo-labels using content similarity to improve accuracy, and aggregate multiple pseudo-labels per sample. 3) Explicitly estimate the uncertainty of the noisy label by considering relative entropy and the cold-start status, adaptively weighting pseudo-labels to correct labels at the sample level. 4) Provide a model-agnostic denoising framework (DIF) applicable across recommendation models. 5) The approach is supported by theoretical justification and large-scale experiments, including deployment on Kuaishou.

Result: DIF achieves superior denoising performance for cold-start data, supported by theoretical analysis and extensive experiments on real-world datasets. In deployment on Kuaishou’s billion-user platform, it significantly improves commercial metrics in cold-start scenarios.

Conclusion: DIF provides an effective, scalable, and model-agnostic solution for denoising implicit feedback in cold-start recommendations. It leverages content similarity to generate and weight pseudo-labels, explicitly models uncertainty, and demonstrates strong empirical and theoretical support as well as practical deployment benefits.

Abstract: Implicit feedback is widely used in recommender systems due to its accessibility and generality, yet it usually presents noisy samples (e.g., clickbait, position bias). Meanwhile, recommenders inevitably face the item cold-start problem due to the continuous influx of new items. We identify that cold items are more prone to noisy samples due to the aforementioned factors, and researchers often overlook the significance of denoising implicit feedback for cold items. Previous denoising studies usually identify noisy samples based on heuristic patterns, such as higher loss values, and mitigate noise through sample selection or re-weighting. However, these methods have limited adaptability and are ineffective in cold-start scenarios. To achieve denoising implicit feedback for cold-start recommendation, we propose a model-agnostic denoising method called DIF. First, user preferences for content remain stable, which allows us to infer pseudo-labels indicating whether a user is interested in a cold item through content-similar warm items. Furthermore, to improve pseudo-label accuracy, we model the confidence of pseudo-labels based on the content similarity between the cold item and warm items, and then aggregate multiple pseudo-labels for each sample. Finally, we explicitly estimate the uncertainty of the noisy sample label by considering its relative entropy and the cold-start status of the item, which adaptively guides the role of pseudo-labels to correct the noisy labels at the sample level. DIF's superiority is supported by both theoretical justification and extensive experiments on real-world datasets. The method has been deployed on a billion-user scale short video application Kuaishou and has significantly improved various commercial metrics within cold-start scenarios.

</details>


### [310] [Exit-and-Join Dynamics for Decentralized Coalition Formation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19683)
*Quanyan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Decentralized coalition formation via exit-and-join dynamics using Aumann-Dreze values; equilibria characterized; potential/Lyapunov structures identified; costs influence local stability; finite-time stabilization and a convex-game benchmark analyzed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Link cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative dynamic behavior to understand stability and efficiency of partitions formed through local deviations; provide theoretical characterizations and computational validation.

Method: Model where agents repeatedly decide to exit and join coalitions unilaterally. Payoffs are computed from the Aumann-Dreze value within the agent's current coalition. The analysis derives equilibrium conditions, examines when the dynamics admit a scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representation, and studies how switching and acceptance costs affect stability. Numerical experiments assess finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and benchmark against a convex-game case.

Result: Terminal partitions are precisely coalition structures with no profitable exit-and-join deviations. The paper provides equilibrium characterizations and identifies conditions for Lyapunov or exact-potential representations. It also clarifies how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical tests demonstrate finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and behavior in a convex-game benchmark.

Conclusion: The work links cooperative payoff allocation to noncooperative dynamics, offering theoretical and computational insights into when decentralized exit-and-join dynamics converge to stable partitions and how costs impact stability.

Abstract: This paper studies coalition formation as a decentralized dynamical process driven by unilateral exit-and-join decisions. Agents evaluate local moves using the Aumann-Dreze value, so payoffs are computed within the agent's current coalition rather than through a globally negotiated coalition structure. The resulting model links cooperative payoff allocation with noncooperative best-response behavior: a terminal partition is precisely a coalition structure with no admissible, individually profitable exit-and-join deviation. We establish equilibrium characterizations, identify conditions under which the dynamics admit scalar Lyapunov or exact-potential representations, and analyze how switching and acceptance costs shape local stability. Numerical experiments test finite-time stabilization, cost sensitivity, and a special convex-game benchmark.

</details>


### [311] [Beyond Static Leaderboards: Predictive Validity for the Evaluation of LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19704)
*Dhaval C. Patel,Kaoutar El Maghraoui,Shuxin Lin,Yusheng Li,Tianjun Feng,Chun-Yi Tsai,Yihan Sun,Wei Alexander Xin,Akshat Bhandari,Tanisha Rathod,Aaron Fan,Sanskruti Vijay Shejwal,Tomas Pasiecznik,Sagar Chethan Kumar,Tanmay Agarwal,Rohith Kanathur,Sam Colman,Amaan Sheikh,Dev Bahl,Ann Li,Krish Veera,Alimurtaza Mustafa Merchant,Shambhawi Baswaraj Bhure,Sajal Kumar Goyla,Chengrui Li,Kirthana Natarajan,Rui Li,Thomas Ajai,Rujing Li,Vivek G. Iyer,Sanjaii Vijayakumar,Yitong Bai,Ayal Yakobe,Darief Maes,Yassine Jebbouri,Tianyang Xu,Thai Quoc On,Vera Mazeeva,Winston Li,Yuval Shemla,Yeshitha Bhuvanesh,Rushin Bhatt,Siddharth Chethan Gowda,Alisha Vinod,Caroline Cahill,Shriya Aishani Rachakonda,Yunfeng Chen,Aryaman Agrawal,Aman Upganlawar,Mao Le Jonathan Ang,Yubin Sally Go,Madhav Rajkondawar,Yang-Jung Chen,Trisha Maturi,Ananya Kapoor,Andrew Li,Shrey Arora,Mana Abbaszadeh,Shen Li,Charles Xu,Byeolah Kwon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Aggregated analysis of an industrial MCP-based agent benchmark reveals that aggregate scores poorly predict deployment performance; proposes predictive-validity-based rankings and a 12-tier measurement framework, plus three falsifiable OOD criteria, to guide next-gen benchmarks; includes a pre-registered pilot and field-wide vision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current benchmarks capture only a subset of deployment-relevant dimensions, leading to unstable and non-transferable rankings. There is a need for benchmarks that align with deployment reality and that expose the sensitivity of rankings to out-of-distribution shifts.

Method: Synthesis of fourteen parallel implementation studies across new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes, consolidated with seven prior agent benchmarks. Evaluation includes analysis of in-sample vs out-of-sample rank correlation, followed by proposing a twelve-tier measurement apparatus and three falsifiable OOD criteria; ends with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for future benchmarks.

Result: The study finds that aggregate-score leaderboards underspecify deployment evaluation and that in-sample rankings do not reliably predict out-of-distribution performance. A predictive-validity ranking approach is proposed, along with a twelve-tier measurement framework to reveal deployment-relevant dimensions (HELM-era collapse). Empirical support exists but is incomplete; a pre-registered pilot and field-wide vision are provided.

Conclusion: Aggregate scores are insufficient for deployment; benchmarks should report predictive validity across a twelve-tier, deployment-aligned measurement apparatus and adopt falsifiable OOD criteria. The work outlines a concrete pilot and a roadmap for next-generation agentic benchmarks.

Abstract: Agent benchmarks are growing fast, but no single benchmark touches more than four or five of the dimensions that deployment exposes. This paper aggregates the largest coordinated deep-dive of one MCP-based industrial-agent benchmark to date: fourteen parallel implementation studies covering new asset classes (including a multi-modal visual extension), alternative orchestrations, retrieval strategies, reasoning modes, infrastructure optimizations, and evaluation-methodology probes. Consolidating those studies with seven prior agent benchmarks, we argue that aggregate-score leaderboards systematically underspecify deployed-agent evaluation. Rankings derived from aggregate scores do not transfer to out-of-distribution settings; recent public-to-hidden competition retrospectives provide direct empirical evidence of this rank instability. We propose ranking configurations by predictive validity, the correlation between in-sample and out-of-sample rank, rather than in-sample mean, and report a twelve-tier measurement apparatus that exposes the deployment-relevant dimensions HELM and its agent-era successors collapse. The position is operationalized through three falsifiable out-of-distribution criteria with explicit thresholds; existing evidence partly supports it but is too thin to confirm. We close with a pre-registered pilot design and a field-level vision for what the next generation of agentic benchmarks should report.

</details>


### [312] [GLARE: A Natural Language Interface for Querying Global Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19735)
*Bhavan Vasu,Rajesh Mangannavar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An LLM-enabled interface allows natural-language querying of global explanations for black-box image classifiers by translating questions into SQL over local explanations, enabling flexible aggregation and visualization while maintaining accessibility for human-centered XAI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global explanations help understand vision models across datasets and decisions but are often monolithic and hard to explore. Users typically want targeted, natural-language answers rather than static artifacts, necessitating a mediator to translate inquiries into structured data queries and assist interpretation.

Method: An LLM serves as a mediator that translates natural-language questions into structured SQL queries over a local explanation dataset. The system returns statistics-augmented natural-language responses, supports local explanations, and provides intent-aligned visualizations, enabling flexible aggregation without exposing low-level representations.

Result: The system is evaluated on intent interpretation, query-mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors. Results indicate that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI.

Conclusion: LLM-mediated querying enhances accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI by enabling natural-language interaction with structured explanation data and guided visualizations.

Abstract: While global explanations are crucial for understanding vision models across datasets, classes, and decision contexts, their complex and monolithic nature often hinders practical exploration. Because users typically seek targeted answers to specific questions rather than static artifacts, we present an LLM-based interactive interface that provides natural language access to global explanations for black-box image classifiers. The system's core LLM acts as a mediator, translating natural language questions into structured SQL queries over local explanation data. This enables flexible aggregation without exposing users to low-level representations. For each query, the interface outputs statistics-augmented natural language responses, supporting local explanations, and intent-aligned visualizations. We evaluate the system on intent interpretation, query mapping accuracy, generalization to novel queries and datasets, and robustness to linguistic errors. Our results demonstrate that LLM-mediated querying substantially improves the accessibility and usability of global explanations for human-centered XAI.

</details>


### [313] [Interpreting Neural Combinatorial Optimization via Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19741)
*Haocheng Duan,Yuxin Guo,Jieyi Bi,Anqi Xie,Sirui Li,Yining Ma,Cathy Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EPB proposes a framework to interpret neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) policies by distilling them into human-readable program portfolios. It uses an LLM to evolve a bank of programs whose per-step action distributions act as bottlenecks, and employs a two-block iterative process to optimize the bank and gradually adapt its capacity. The distilled portfolios largely match the original performance and reveal that NCO behavior shifts during optimization and can be approximated by classic heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NCO models are powerful yet opaque; standard interpretability approaches (e.g., CBMs) struggle due to dynamic, state-dependent decisions and lack of a fixed concept vocabulary. There is a need for interpretable, human-readable representations of sequential decision policies.

Method: EPB maintains a bank of programmatic bottlenecks (program portfolios) via an LLM that autonomously evolves programs. Block I fixes bank capacity and uses a hybrid gradient scheme: numerical gradients update the student (NCO) router, while textual gradients update the LLM-driven program revisions. Block II dynamically adjusts bank capacity through fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. The per-step action distribution of each program serves as the bottleneck for interpretation.

Result: Experimental results show the distilled program portfolios largely preserve original performance. EPB captures that NCO behavior evolves over optimization stages and can be viewed as a composition of classic heuristics. The framework demonstrates broad applicability and provides interpretable insights into sequential decision-making policies.

Conclusion: EPB advances interpretable NCO by enabling distillation of black-box policies into human-readable program portfolios. It offers a first step toward reliable, interpretable sequential decision-making models and has potential as a tool for diagnosis and deployment of NCO systems.

Abstract: Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) achieves strong performance, yet its black-box nature remains a key roadblock to deployment and scientific diagnosis. Standard interpretability tools, such as Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs), are ill-equipped for NCO, whose decisions are dynamic, state-dependent, and lack proper concept vocabulary definition. To close this gap, we introduce Evolving Programmatic Bottlenecks (EPB), to our knowledge, the first framework for interpreting NCO policies by distilling black-box NCO models into human-readable program portfolios. EPB employs an LLM to autonomously evolve a bank of programs, where each program's per-step action distribution serves as the bottleneck. EPB works through an iterative framework: Block I fixes program bank capacity and introduces a hybrid textual-numerical gradient descent scheme that couples numerical gradients for student router updates and textual gradients for LLM-based program revision; Block II dynamically adapts bank capacity via fault-targeted expansion and redundancy pruning. Extensive experiments demonstrate EPB's effectiveness and broad applicability, where the distilled program portfolios largely match original performance. EPB also reveals that NCO behavior shifts across optimization stages and can be approximated as a composition of classic heuristic variants. Our work advances interpretable NCO and establishes EPB as a promising tool for interpreting sequential decision-making models.

</details>


### [314] [A Comparative Study of Pretrained Transformer Models for Quranic ASR: Speech Representations, Label Formats, and Dataset Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19747)
*Nabil Mosharraf Hossain,Riasat Islam,Unaizah Obaidellah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Fine-tuning SSL Transformer ASR models on a Quranic corpus yields substantial WER gains; Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 is strongest; Arabic without diacritics helps; training time reduced; future work on dataset quality and phoneme-aware Tajweed models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high WER and limited coverage of Quranic content in ASR by systematically exploring domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained transformers with self-supervised speech features on a large Quranic dataset.

Method: Fine-tune Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R on 870+ hours of Quranic audio (professional and user recitations); perform ablations over feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations; evaluate on EveryAyah and EveryAyah+Tarteel; compare against Citrinet baseline.

Result: Best WER: 0.08 (EveryAyah) and 0.11 (EveryAyah+Tarteel); ~5 percentage-point improvement over Citrinet baseline (0.163); training time reduced from 140h to 40h; Arabic text without diacritics yields best fine-tuning results; Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation.

Conclusion: Domain-specific fine-tuning of SSL Transformer models is effective for Quranic ASR, with clear gains from model choice and data representation; key factors include feature extractor, label format, and data handling; future work to improve dataset quality and develop phoneme-aware models for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

Abstract: Quran Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) aims to convert Quranic recitation into text, enabling applications such as aided memorisation tools and Quranic search engines. However, existing ASR models often exhibit high Word Error Rates (WER) on user-recited verses and lack full coverage of the Quranic corpus. This paper presents a systematic empirical study of domain-specific fine-tuning of pretrained Transformer-based models for Quranic ASR, using advanced speech feature extraction methods: Wav2Vec2.0, HuBERT, and XLS-R. These models apply self-supervised learning by masking portions of input audio and using Transformer architectures to learn context-aware speech features. The pretrained models are fine-tuned on a filtered Quranic dataset exceeding 870 hours of professional and user recitations. Through comprehensive ablation studies across feature extractors, output label formats, training strategies, and clip durations, we identify the key factors that affect transcription accuracy in this domain. Our best-performing configuration achieves a WER of 0.08 on the EveryAyah subset and 0.11 on the combined EveryAyah+Tarteel setting, representing roughly a five-percentage-point gain over the Citrinet baseline (WER = 0.163) while reducing combined-model training time from 140 hours to 40 hours. Arabic text without diacritics yields the best fine-tuning results, and Wav2Vec2-XLSR-53 provides the strongest overall representation. Future work includes improving dataset quality and developing phoneme-aware models to extract deeper speech feature representations for Tajweed-sensitive applications.

</details>


### [315] [Benchmarking Agentic Review Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19749)
*Dang Nguyen,Wanqing Hao,Yanai Elazar,Chenhao Tan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AI-assisted review systems can approach human-quality judgments and detect errors, with OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 achieving up to 83.0% pairwise accuracy; however, error-detection remains incomplete (71.6% recall on perturbations; 83.3% union), and user deployments report mixed feedback due to false positives; overall, promising but room for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address evaluation gaps for AI review systems amid AI-assisted research, understanding alignment with human quality judgments, error detection capabilities, and user experience.

Method: Compare two open-source (OpenAIReview, coarse), one proprietary (Reviewer3), and a zero-shot baseline across six LLMs (frontier to efficient). Evaluate on ICLR/NeurIPS papers for correlation with quality via external signals; construct a perturbation benchmark injecting four error types across eight arXiv classes; deploy OpenAIReview publicly and collect user votes and complaints.

Result: All systems perform above chance; OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 best at 83.0% pairwise accuracy. Perturbation recall: strongest config 71.6%; union across models 83.3%. Public deployment: comments skew 1.44 to 1; most complaints about false positives and minor nitpicks.

Conclusion: AI reviews can track human quality judgments and catch important errors; yet substantial room for improvement; design improvements and better cross-model ensembles could boost performance; user feedback highlights practical issues to address (false positives).

Abstract: A new class of agentic review systems are emerging as a remedy to the pressure placed on peer review systems by AI-assisted research, but it is unclear how they should be evaluated. We evaluate two open-source systems (OpenAIReview and coarse), one proprietary system (Reviewer3), and a zero-shot baseline, across six LLMs spanning frontier and efficient models. First, we study whether AI reviews on ICLR/NeurIPS papers track with papers' quality as approximated by external signals such as citations and acceptance decisions. Every system performs above chance in pairwise accuracy, and the best is OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5 at 83.0%. Second, to test whether systems can catch errors with known ground truth, we construct a perturbation benchmark that injects four categories of errors into papers across eight arXiv subject classes and measure detection recall. The strongest configuration (OpenAIReview + GPT-5.5) catches 71.6% of injected errors, leaving substantial room for improvement. The union of detections across six models reaches 83.3% recall, suggesting different models detect different errors and better harness design can potentially increase performance. Beyond these benchmarks, we study a public deployment of OpenAIReview with real users. Votes on its comments skew positive at 1.44 to 1, and the most common complaints are about false positives and minor nitpicks. Together, by evaluating full review systems backed by state-of-the-art models on real research papers, we show that while AI reviews still have room for improvement, they can already track human quality judgments well, catch important errors, and earn positive feedback from real users.

</details>


### [316] [Grounded Inference: Principles for Deterministically Encapsulated Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19753)
*Marty O'Neill*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework of four AI-embedded architectural primitives and two anti-patterns to deterministically encapsulate probabilistic models within traditional systems, enabling safer AI integration and paving the way for next-generation generative interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To de-risk the integration of generative AI into traditional, deterministic systems by providing a concrete architectural framework and warnings against common missteps observed in industry.

Method: Define four primitives of AI-blended architecture that enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models; identify two overarching anti-patterns as industry warnings; articulate a design foundation intended to guide engineers and AI model providers.

Result: Proposes a cohesive architectural framework with explicit primitives and anti-patterns intended to facilitate safer, more reliable integration of generative models into traditional systems; serves as guidance for practitioners and interface designers.

Conclusion: The framework offers a structured path for integrating AI into legacy systems, mitigating risk and guiding future generative model interface development; empirical validation may be needed to assess practical effectiveness.

Abstract: The incorporation of generative models into traditional computational systems presents both enormous opportunity and tremendous peril. Although many early adopters have realized these perils at great expense, the field still requires foundational frameworks to de-risk incorporation of AI into traditional systems. This manuscript establishes this foundation through the definition of four specific primitives of AI blended architecture, designed to enable deterministic encapsulation of probabilistic models. It further establishes two overarching anti-patterns broadly represented across industry to serve as warnings for engineers in this field. This framework was designed to enable successful integration of AI into traditional systems while providing a foundation upon which generative model providers could build the next generation of generative model interfaces.

</details>


### [317] [Optimal Scheduling in a Question-Answering Forum of Knowledge Workers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19759)
*Rohit Negi,Mustafa Yilmaz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Modeling and optimizing topic-specific QA forums using schedulers and collaboration to maximize system capacity and stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address capacity and stability in QA forums by incorporating topic-specific experts and scheduling policies; explore whether expert collaboration can raise throughput.

Method: Queuing-theoretic model with multiple topic-related servers (experts) having topic-dependent service rates; design and analyze schedulers that assign incoming requests to suitable experts; evaluate the impact of expert collaboration on capacity.

Result: Derives capacity conditions for stability, presents schedulers that achieve capacity, and shows that collaboration among experts can increase system capacity.

Conclusion: Scheduling policies and inter-expert collaboration can significantly enhance the handling of requests in topic-based QA forums, suggesting practical directions for system design and implementation.

Abstract: As individuals turn to the Internet to find answers to questions they may have, several Question Answering (QA) forums have evolved, where users knowledgeable in certain topics can contribute their expertise to answering these requests for information. While these are currently volunteer based, we consider a future version employing knowledge workers who are experts in certain topics. In such a system, the request-answer processes forming the queuing system may utilize schedulers that assign requests in different topics to the experts in the forum, who may be able to answer them according to their expertise levels in different topics. With this model, we calculate the capacity of the system for handling the requests while keeping the system stable, and design schedulers that achieve capacity. We also investigate how collaboration between experts in answering requests can potentially increase capacity.

</details>


### [318] [Beyond Entropy: Learning from Token-Level Distributional Deviations for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19771)
*Xuanzhi Feng,Zhengyang Li,Zeyu Liu,Haoxi Li,Yuming Jiang,Bing Guo,Jingcai Guo,Jie Zhang,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ICT: An entropy-aware, distribution-focused update framework for RL-based LLM reasoning that uses Jensen-Shannon divergence on token logits to selectively update top tokens, stabilizing training and improving pass@4 on math, commonsense, and Olympiad tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL reward optimization for LLMs (RLVR) faces two instabilities: entropy collapse from uniform updates and entropy explosion from excessive exploration. A method that can regulate both is needed to stabilize training and improve solution quality.

Method: Introduce Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework that shifts focus from scalar uncertainty to distributional properties of token logits. Use Jensen-Shannon divergence between token distributions to identify distinctive tokens as critical branching points. The analysis leverages Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy to show that updating these tokens reduces overall entropy while controlling probability concentration. Empirically update only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) to guide exploration.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows updating the top 10% unique tokens yields average pass@4 improvement of 4.58% and up to 14.9% on seven benchmarks (math, commonsense, Olympiad) across Qwen2.5 variants, outperforming GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines.

Conclusion: ICT provides a principled way to stabilize RL-based LLM reasoning by balancing distributional uncertainty and concentration via selective token updates guided by token-logit JS divergence, with measurable gains across diverse benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning; however, it faces a fundamental optimization instability: uniform token updates precipitate entropy collapse, leading to premature convergence to suboptimal strategies, whereas excessive Shannon Entropy maximization can cause entropy explosion, driving blind exploration toward incoherent reasoning chains. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce the Independent Combinatorial Tokens (ICT) framework, which shifts the optimization focus from scalar uncertainty to the distributional properties of token logits. By leveraging the Jensen-Shannon (JS) divergence between token logits distributions, ICT identifies tokens with distinctive distributional patterns as critical branching points for guiding effective exploration in LLM reasoning. Our theoretical analysis, grounded in both Shannon and second-order Rényi entropy, proves that selectively updating on these tokens regulates policy concentration: it reduces the overall distribution uncertainty measured by Shannon entropy, while controlling probability concentration captured by second-order Rényi entropy. This dual effect prevents over-concentrated token generation from weakening exploration and effectively stabilizes the training landscape. Empirical results demonstrate that updating only the top 10% of unique tokens on Qwen2.5 (0.5B/1.5B/7B) models yields an average pass@4 improvement of 4.58%, with a maximum gain of 14.9%, over GRPO, 20-Entropy, and STAPO baselines across seven benchmarks spanning math, commonsense, and Olympiad-level problems.

</details>


### [319] [AgentFinVQA: A Deployable Multi-Agent Pipeline for Auditable Financial Chart QA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19782)
*Aravind Narayanan,Shaina Raza*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentFinVQA introduces an auditable, on-premise multi-agent pipeline for financial chart QA that records a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. It achieves sizable accuracy gains over a proprietary backbone and open-weight baselines while preserving data residency, and provides a verifier-based confidence signal to aid human-in-the-loop review. Code is released for reproducible evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regulated financial settings require trustable, auditable QA results and strict data residency; existing chart-QA systems prioritize accuracy, rely on external APIs, or lack auditability, making them unsuitable for on-prem deployment.

Method: A multi-agent pipeline decomposes a query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification. Each step is recorded in a per-sample MEP to enable traceability. Evaluations on FinMME compare a proprietary Gemini-3 Flash backbone and an open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 backbone, both deployed on-premises. A verifier component provides verdicts that serve as confidence signals.

Result: AgentFinVQA improves +7.68 percentage points over the baseline (71.24% vs 63.56%; McNemar p ≈ 1.1×10^-16) and +4.84 points over the open-weights model when served locally. The verifier’s verdict yields 68.2% exact accuracy on confirmed answers versus 55.6% on revised answers, aiding human-in-the-loop routing. Error analysis finds that question misunderstanding, legend confusion, and extraction errors account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier.

Conclusion: Auditable, on-prem financial chart QA is practical with substantial accuracy gains and data residency preservation. The open-weights model retains most gains. The MEP framework and verifier provide useful auditability and confidence signals, though remaining error modes suggest directions for improvement; code is released to support reproducible evaluation.

Abstract: Financial chart question answering in regulated settings demands more than accuracy: practitioners must know which answers to trust before acting on them, and many institutions cannot send client data to external model providers. Yet existing chart-QA agents are accuracy-focused and opaque, and most assume proprietary API access; to our knowledge, none combines auditability with on-premise deployability without significant accuracy compromise. We present AgentFinVQA, a multi-agent pipeline that decomposes each query into planning, OCR, legend grounding, visual inspection, and verification, recording every step in a traceable Model Evaluation Packet (MEP) per sample. On FinMME, AgentFinVQA improves $+7.68$ pp over a primary-backbone matched zero-shot baseline with a proprietary backbone (Gemini-3 Flash; 71.24% vs. 63.56%, McNemar $p \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-16}$), and $+4.84$ pp with open-weights Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 served locally. The verifier's verdict also serves as a useful confidence signal (68.2% vs. 55.6% exact accuracy on confirmed vs. revised answers), enabling human-in-the-loop review routing. Error analysis shows that question misunderstanding, legend confusion and extraction error account for nearly two-thirds of failures and are the categories least detected by the verifier, identifying clear directions for future work. Together these results show that auditable, on-premise financial chart QA is practical and that the open-weights system keeps most of the accuracy gains while enabling full data residency. We release our code to support reproducible evaluation.

</details>


### [320] [ORAgentBench: Can LLM Agents Solve Challenging Operations Research Tasks End to End?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19787)
*Jiajun Li,Mingshu Cai,Yixuan Li,Yu Ding,Ran Hou,Guanyu Nie,Xiongwei Han,Wanyuan Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ORAgentBench introduces an execution-grounded, end-to-end benchmark for autonomous OR agents; 107 tasks, isolated environments, agents must generate/run code; evaluated on schema validity, feasibility, and objective quality; results show current agents are far from reliable OR practice; best agent passes 35.51% tasks and 20.59% hard tasks; main errors are strategic weaknesses; progress requires dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing OR evaluations that separate modeling from solving and rely on pre-formalized/text-only instances; provide an end-to-end, artifact-rich benchmark that reflects real-world OR workflows and decision-making.

Method: Create ORAgentBench with 107 tasks; each task includes natural-language brief, multi-file data, configurations, and a required submission schema; evaluation via hidden validators for schema validity, feasibility, and normalized objective quality; test fourteen frontier agent-model configurations; analyze errors.

Result: Best agent achieves 35.51% task pass rate and 20.59% on hard tasks; many feasible submissions fail the required quality; strategic weaknesses (missed rules, brittle formulations, poor feasible-solution construction, limited improvement) dominate errors; OR-specific procedural skills help hard-task feasibility but do not reliably improve quality or pass rate.

Conclusion: Advancement in OR agents requires shifting from plausible optimization code to dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents for multi-step tasks in executable environments, yet their ability to perform realistic operations research (OR) work remains unclear. Existing OR evaluations often decouple modeling from solving, rely on pre-formalized or text-only instances, and rarely test the full workflow from operational artifacts to validated decisions. In this work, we introduce ORAgentBench, an execution-grounded benchmark for evaluating autonomous agents on challenging end-to-end operations research tasks. It contains 107 human-reviewed tasks across diverse operational scenarios, each packaged in an isolated environment with a natural-language brief, multi-file data, configuration artifacts, and a required submission schema. Agents must write and run solution code, and their submissions are evaluated by hidden validators for schema validity, hard-constraint feasibility, and normalized objective quality. Experiments with fourteen frontier agent-model configurations show that current agents remain far from reliable OR practice. The best agent passes only 35.51% of all tasks and 20.59% of hard tasks, and many feasible submissions still fall below the required quality threshold. Failure analysis further shows that errors are dominated by strategic weaknesses, including missed operational rules, brittle formulations, weak feasible-solution construction, and insufficient solution improvement. OR-specific procedural skills increase hard-task feasibility, but do not reliably improve solution quality or pass rate. These results suggest that progress in OR agents requires moving beyond plausible optimization code toward dependable, high-quality operational decision-making.

</details>


### [321] [CombEval: A Framework for Evaluating Combinatorial Counting in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19788)
*Yuxu Zhou,Ondřej Kuželka,Yuyi Wang,Yuanhong Wang,Yi Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CombEval is a dynamic benchmark for combinatorial counting in LLMs using typed Cofola specifications to generate solvable, verifier-checked counting problems; it enables systematic variation and serves as a diagnostic tool, with public code and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static benchmarks often fail to probe combinatorial counting generalization, robustness, and reasoning depth. A controllable, solver-verified, dynamically adjustable evaluation is needed to diagnose when and why LLMs struggle with combinatorial reasoning.

Method: Problems are represented as typed Cofola specifications over entities, combinatorial objects, dependencies, and constraints. This allows controlled natural-language counting problem generation with exact solver-verified answers. CombEval varies object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth; evaluations are conducted on 11 LLMs in direct and code-augmented settings.

Result: LLMs show brittleness on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis attributes failures to constraint interpretation and counting principles. CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying the limits of LLMs in combinatorial reasoning.

Conclusion: CombEval offers a flexible, verifiable diagnostic testbed for LLM combinatorial reasoning; the code and generated benchmark suites are publicly available at the authors’ repository.

Abstract: We present CombEval, a dynamic benchmark for evaluating combinatorial counting in large language models. CombEval represents each problem as a typed Cofola specification over entities, combinatorial objects, object dependencies, and constraints, enabling controlled generation of natural-language counting problems with exact solver-verified answers. Unlike static collections, CombEval supports systematic variation of object type, entity scale, constraint count, and reasoning depth. We evaluate 11 LLMs under direct and code-augmented settings and find that models remain brittle on ordered objects, indistinguishable elements, relatively positional constraints, and nested object dependencies. Error analysis further identifies failures in constraint interpretation and counting principles. CombEval provides a diagnostic testbed for studying when and why LLMs fail at combinatorial reasoning. The code and generated benchmark suites are publicly available at \url{https://github.com/YuxuZhou-CN/combination-problem-generation}.

</details>


### [322] [Think Again or Think Longer? Selective Verification for Budget-Aware Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19808)
*Sajib Acharjee Dip,Dawei Zhou,Liqing Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation (Sevra) improves deployment efficiency by gating test-time verification; yields similar or better accuracy with fewer tokens across multiple tasks, though cost-benefit depends on the dataset and initial solve budget. The proposed rule: set an initial budget, then apply selective recovery when checks, retries, auditability, or regression risk matter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Test-time reasoning is not universally valuable; extra verification can repair failures but may waste compute or cause harmful answer changes. A deployment-time controller is needed to allocate verification resources adaptively.

Method: Freeze a solver (Qwen3-4B); log intervention outcomes; train recoverability-aware gates using serving-visible attempt state; deploy gating policy to decide whether to preserve the initial answer or trigger active verification; evaluate on MathFive, GSM (GSM8K transfer), and CommonsenseQA; compare against always-verify baselines and variations with different initial solves.

Result: MathFive: Sevra achieves 76.3% accuracy vs 75.5% with always-verify; reduces post-generation tokens by 26.8%; lowers harmful flips from 2.2% to 1.0%. For an 8k-token initial solve, accuracy remains 76.0% with 28% fewer tokens. GSM transfer: Sevra verifies only 3.0% of examples; accuracy 93.4%->94.5%; verification tokens reduced by 91.2%; longer initial solve matches accuracy with fewer tokens. CommonsenseQA: always-on verification hurts; Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at ~5x the token cost. Concludes deployment rule: set initial budget first, then apply selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression risk matter.

Conclusion: Selective verification via Sevra is a practical deployment knob that can improve efficiency and maintain or improve accuracy in many settings, but its benefits are dataset- and budget-dependent. A pragmatic policy is to allocate an initial verification budget, then selectively recover when needed to balance risk and compute.

Abstract: Test-time reasoning is increasingly used as a serving-time control knob, but extra reasoning is not uniformly valuable: it can repair failed attempts, waste compute on already-correct answers, or introduce harmful answer changes. We study this as a deployment allocation problem rather than a new-verifier problem. We introduce \sevra, Selective Verification for Reasoning Allocation, a serving-layer controller that decides whether to preserve a frozen solver's initial answer or invoke active verification. Using a frozen Qwen3-4B solver, we log intervention outcomes and train recoverability-aware gates from serving-visible attempt state. On \mathfive, selective verification reaches 76.3\% accuracy, compared with 75.5\% for always verifying, while reducing post-generation tokens by 26.8\% and harmful flips from 2.2\% to 1.0\%. However, an 8,192-token initial solve reaches 76.0\% accuracy with 28\% fewer total model tokens, showing that selective recovery is useful but not the best tested cost frontier. In frozen transfer to \gsm, the selective policy verifies only 3.0\% of examples, improves accuracy from 93.4\% to 94.5\%, and reduces verification tokens by 91.2\% relative to always verifying; again, a longer initial solve matches its accuracy with fewer realized tokens. On CommonsenseQA, always-on verification hurts, while Self-Consistency@5 improves accuracy at about five times the realized token cost. The resulting deployment rule is: tune the initial budget first, then use selective recovery when explicit checks, bounded retries, auditability, or regression-risk control matter.

</details>


### [323] [Human-on-the-Loop Orchestration for AI-Assisted Legal Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19812)
*Anushree Sinha,Srivaths Ranganathan,Abhishek Dharmaratnakar,Debanshu Das*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trajectory collapse in autonomous legal LLM workflows for e-discovery; introduces a four-layer verification architecture (planning, reasoning, execution, uncertainty quantification); and shows via synthetic simulations that calibrated uncertainty thresholds with human-on-the-loop escalation can substantially reduce privilege-waiver risk (up to 61%) while keeping attorney review load under 25%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate multi-step, error-prone agentic reasoning over privileged documents in e-discovery where early misclassifications propagate and cause legal malpractice; provide a structured taxonomy and a practical safety architecture to intercept failures.

Method: (1) Develop a taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval organized by functional stage; (2) Propose a four-layer verification architecture including planning, reasoning, execution, and uncertainty quantification to intercept failures; (3) Conduct a preliminary simulation on a synthetic e-discovery corpus comparing fully autonomous baselines to systems with mandatory HOTL escalation thresholds.

Result: Uncertainty-based gating with HOTL reduces privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% relative to fully autonomous deployment and can route fewer than 25% of documents to attorney review in the simulated setting.

Conclusion: Calibrated uncertainty thresholds and a structured verification architecture offer a practical path to safer, more reliable agentic e-discovery systems. Further work should validate on real datasets, optimize thresholds, and explore integration with privilege policies.

Abstract: Autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed in electronic discovery (e-discovery), where compounding errors across multi-step reasoning chains can constitute legal malpractice. Unlike single-turn retrieval, agentic workflows operating over privileged document corpora exhibit a class of failure we term "trajectory collapse": an early misclassification silently propagates, rendering an entire privilege review invalid. This paper makes three contributions. First, we propose a structured taxonomy of agentic failures in legal information retrieval, organized by functional stage. Second, we introduce a four-layer verification architecture -- spanning planning, reasoning, execution, and uncertainty quantification -- designed to intercept these failures before they compound. Third, we present a preliminary simulation study on a synthetic e-discovery corpus that demonstrates how mandatory Human-on-the-Loop (HOTL) escalation thresholds reduce privilege-waiver risk relative to fully autonomous baselines. Our results suggest that calibrated uncertainty thresholds can reduce privilege-waiver risk by up to 61% versus fully autonomous deployment, while routing fewer than one quarter of documents to attorney review.

</details>


### [324] [TelcoAgent: A Scalable 5G Multi-KPM Forecasting With 3GPP-Grounded Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19821)
*Geon Kim,Dara Ron,Sukhdeep Singh,Suyog Moogi,Pranshav Gajjar,V V N K Someswara Rao Koduri,Een Kee Hong,Vijay K. Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TelcoAgent uses a foundation-model-based framework to forecast multiple key performance measures (KPMs) across 5G cells without per-site training, by (i) automatically building a 3GPP knowledge graph from spec documents, (ii) employing a time-series foundation model (TSFM) for zero-shot forecasting, and (iii) providing a reasoning/explanation layer for actionable diagnostics. Evaluated on 3 months of real-world city-scale data (200 cells, 7 KPMs), it achieves high accuracy and offers explainability and operational guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting KPMs for 5G/6G networks faces scalability and explainability challenges in ML-based approaches. A knowledge-grounded, scalable, zero-shot framework can deliver accurate forecasts across diverse cells without site-specific training while enabling actionable diagnostics for network management.

Method:  (i) An automated three-agent pipeline constructs a 3GPP knowledge graph directly from specification documents. (ii) A scalable time-series foundation model (TSFM) performs zero-shot forecasting of multiple KPMs across cells. (iii) A reasoning and explanation pipeline outputs domain-grounded diagnostics and actionable guidance for network issues.

Result: On a real-world dataset spanning 3 months, city-scale 5G KPMs across 200 cells and 7 metrics per cell, TelcoAgent achieves high forecasting accuracy for all KPMs and provides explainable insights with actionable remediation instructions.

Conclusion: TelcoAgent demonstrates a scalable, accurate, and explainable KPM forecasting solution that does not require site-specific training, with potential applicability to broader KPM sets and networks.

Abstract: Key Performance Measurement (KPM) forecasting is essential for proactive network management of 5G and next-generation telecom networks. However, existing machine learning (ML) approaches face significant limitations in scalability and explainability, restricting their effectiveness in real-world deployments. We propose TelcoAgent, a foundation model-based framework that enables accurate, scalable, and explainable forecasting of multiple KPMs across diverse network cells without the need for site-specific training. Specifically, the framework comprises three key components: (i) an automated three-agent pipeline that constructs a 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) knowledge graph directly from specification documents, (ii) a scalable, time-series foundation model (TSFM)-based prediction pipeline to deliver accurate, zero-shot forecasting, and finally (iii) a reasoning and explanation pipeline that provides actionable, domain-grounded diagnostics. Evaluated using a 3-month, real-world, city-scale 5G KPM dataset from a U.S.-based network operator, TelcoAgent demonstrates high forecasting accuracy for all 7 considered KPMs per cell across 200 cells, while delivering explainable insights and actionable instructions to address network degradations.

</details>


### [325] [A Systematic Evaluation of Black-Box Uncertainty Estimation Methods for Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19868)
*Jiayi Wang,Xu-Yao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A systematic review and benchmark of black-box uncertainty estimation (UE) for LLMs, organizing methods into five categories, and evaluating 24 approaches across four models and four dataset settings. No single method dominates; candidate-aware reasoning and hybrid approaches perform well. Benchmark data and a unified framework are released to support reproducible comparisons and guide future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can hallucinate and produce unreliable outputs, and many APIs restrict access to internal signals (logits/hidden states). This makes black-box UE essential but the field lacks a unified empirical comparison and cohesive methodology.

Method: Proposes a unified evaluation framework and classifies black-box UE methods into five categories: verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid. Benchmarks 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings to compare performance.

Result: Across settings, no single method dominates. Methods that reason over and compare multiple candidates in the answer space tend to be effective, and hybrid approaches that fuse multiple uncertainty signals perform well under most conditions.

Conclusion: Release of benchmark data and the unified framework to enable reproducible comparisons and guide future development of black-box UE methods for LLMs.

Abstract: Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities across a wide range of tasks, their outputs often remain unreliable and may contain hallucinations, making uncertainty estimation (UE) essential for building trustworthy LLMs. In practice, many mainstream LLMs are only accessible through restricted APIs, where internal signals such as logits and hidden states are unavailable, making black-box UE especially important. However, existing work on black-box UE for LLMs remains fragmented in methodology and lacks a unified empirical comparison. To address this gap, we present a systematic review of black-box UE methods and organize them into five categories: verbalization-based, sampling-based, explanation-based, multi-agent, and hybrid methods. We further build a unified evaluation framework and benchmark 24 representative methods across 4 models and 4 dataset settings. Our results show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings. Nevertheless, methods that reason over and compare candidates in the answer space are generally effective, and hybrid methods that combine multiple uncertainty signals perform well under most conditions. By releasing the benchmark data and a unified evaluation framework, we aim to facilitate reproducible comparisons and support future research, while our empirical findings provide practical guidance for developing future black-box UE methods for LLMs.

</details>


### [326] [MetaResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Self-Reflective Reinforcement Learning in Adversarial Virtual Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19893)
*Wei Yu,Suxing Liu,Minjie Yu,Jiahao Wang,Zhijian Zheng,Haocheng Deng,Bing Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MetaResearcher presents a four-pronged framework to train deep research agents: (1) an Evolving Virtual World with dynamics and misinformation; (2) Discovery-Oriented Tasks beyond fact retrieval; (3) a Self-Reflective Meta-Reward in the GRPO framework for multiple objectives; (4) a Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm (Scout/Filter/Synthesizer) for coordinated RL, built on LiteResearcher with zero marginal API cost. The paper outlines framework design, training methodology, and planned experiments targeting GAIA and Xbench-DS benchmarks and improved epistemic robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current training of deep research agents is hampered by static simulation environments, fact-retrieval-only tasks, and inefficient outcome-based RL. There is a need for scalable, robust training that fosters credible source evaluation, dynamic reasoning, and collaborative strategies across agents while maintaining practical training costs.

Method: Four synergistic dimensions: (i) Evolving Virtual World introducing temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation to enforce credibility assessment and temporal reasoning; (ii) Discovery-Oriented Tasks including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution to push authentic research behavior; (iii) Self-Reflective Meta-Reward within GRPO optimizing answer correctness, search efficiency, reflection depth, and tool-call diversity to break repetitive action loops; (iv) Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm with specialized Scout, Filter, Synthesizer roles trained through coordinated reinforcement learning; implemented on LiteResearcher with zero marginal API cost.

Result: The abstract focuses on framework design, training methodology, and planned experimental validation rather than reporting empirical results. It anticipates improvements in benchmark performance (GAIA, Xbench-DS) and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions, with forthcoming experiments to validate these claims.

Conclusion: MetaResearcher proposes an integrated approach to scalable, robust deep research agent training by combining dynamic environment design, richer discovery tasks, self-reflective rewards, and collaborative multi-agent coordination. The work outlines a concrete framework and methodology and indicates forthcoming experimental validation to demonstrate gains in performance and epistemic resilience.

Abstract: Deep research agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in autonomous information gathering and synthesis, yet their training remains constrained by the static nature of simulated environments, the limits of fact-retrieval-only task designs, and the inefficiency of outcome-based reinforcement learning. In this work, we propose MetaResearcher, a novel framework that scales deep research agent training across four synergistic dimensions. First, we introduce an Evolving Virtual World that injects temporal dynamics and adversarial misinformation into the training environment, forcing agents to develop source credibility assessment and temporal conflict resolution skills. Second, we design Discovery-Oriented Tasks -- including hypothesis generation and contradiction resolution -- that transcend simple fact retrieval and push agents toward genuine research behaviors. Third, we propose a Self-Reflective Meta-Reward mechanism within the GRPO framework that jointly optimizes for answer correctness, search path efficiency, reflection depth, and tool call diversity, directly addressing the repetitive action loop problem observed in prior work. Fourth, we introduce a Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Swarm architecture comprising specialized Scout, Filter, and Synthesizer models that learn collaborative research strategies through coordinated reinforcement learning. Built upon the LiteResearcher infrastructure, MetaResearcher requires zero marginal API cost for training while targeting substantial improvements in both benchmark performance (GAIA, Xbench-DS) and epistemic robustness under adversarial conditions. We present the complete framework design, training methodology, and planned experimental validation.

</details>


### [327] [Multi-Agent Transactive Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19911)
*To Eun Kim,Xuhong He,Dishank Jain,Ambuj Agrawal,Negar Arabzadeh,Fernando Diaz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MATM provides a population-level repository for agent-generated trajectories to enable reuse across heterogeneous agents, improving task performance and reducing interaction steps without coordination or joint training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To avoid discarding or siloing agent-generated procedural knowledge, and to scale knowledge reuse across a population of agents in open ecosystems, especially where trajectories encode rich, long-horizon behavior.

Method: Introduce Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM): producers add trajectories to a shared repository; consumers retrieve relevant trajectories to assist task execution. Experiments in ALFWorld and WebArena show retrieval of trajectories improves performance and reduces interaction steps without explicit coordination or joint training.

Result: Retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces the number of interaction steps, validating population-level knowledge sharing as a practical design pattern.

Conclusion: MATM is a viable design pattern for knowledge sharing in open agent ecosystems, enabling reusable procedural knowledge across a population of agents without centralized coordination.

Abstract: The decentralized deployment of LLM agents with diverse capabilities across diverse tasks motivates infrastructure for knowledge sharing across heterogeneous agent populations. Just as search engines index human-generated artifacts to support human problem solving, retrieval systems can organize agent-generated artifacts for reuse across agent populations. We extend retrieval-augmented generation - which demonstrates the value of human-authored artifacts to individual agents - to retrieval of agent-generated artifacts supporting a population of agents. In particular, agent trajectories encode reusable procedural knowledge, yet these artifacts are typically discarded after a single use or retained only by the producing agent, forcing newly instantiated agents to repeatedly rediscover existing solutions. We propose Multi-Agent Transactive Memory (MATM), a framework for population-level storage and retrieval of agent-generated trajectories, where producer agents contribute trajectories to a shared repository and consumer agents retrieve them to improve task execution.
