We focus on interactive environments (ALFWorld and WebArena), where trajectories are long and encode especially rich procedural structure. Our experiments demonstrate that retrieving trajectories from MATM improves downstream task performance and reduces interaction steps without coordination or joint training. These results position MATM as a design pattern for population-level experience sharing in open agent ecosystems.

</details>


### [328] [eCNNTO: A Highly Generalizable ConvNet for Accelerating Topology Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19921)
*Shengbiao Lu,Xiaodong Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: eCNNTO uses a residual CNN to predict near-final densities in density-based topology optimization, addressing spatial correlation gaps in prior per-element DBN, trained on final-stage histories; yields substantial iteration reductions and strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational cost of density-based topology optimization on high-resolution meshes by incorporating spatial context and efficient training data usage, overcoming the lack of spatial correlation in element-wise DBN approaches.

Method: Introduce an element-based CNN with residual connections to capture spatial correlations among neighboring elements. Train on final-stage density histories rather than early iterations to reduce data requirements, building on the prior DBN approach (Kallioras et al., 2020).

Result: Demonstrates up to 90% reduction in iterations for 2D problems and 97% for 3D problems. Shows generalization across boundary conditions, loading cases, design domain geometries, mesh resolutions, and non-design domains with a small training dataset.

Conclusion: eCNNTO is an effective, data-efficient CNN-based accelerator for density-based topology optimization, preserving connectivity and generalizing across diverse problems, enabling high-resolution designs with limited training data.

Abstract: This work proposes an element-based Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to accelerate density-based Topology Optimization (TO), termed eCNNTO. TO generally undergoes a large number of iterations, where finite element analysis is performed in every iteration, leading to the efficiency bottleneck especially when dense meshes are used to achieve high-resolution designs. To address this limitation, eCNNTO is proposed to build upon Kallioras et al. (2020), where a Deep Belief Network (DBN) was trained for every element to predict its near-optimal density from its early history, thereby skipping the great majority of iterations and significantly accelerating the TO procedure. However, the method lacks spatial correlations among neighboring elements and may lead to disconnected features in the final structure. The proposed method employs CNN with residual connections to address this issue. On top of it, a novel training strategy is introduced to further enhance the optimization efficiency, where the training dataset consists of the final stage density histories rather than early ones. This change can also help reduce the required training data size. eCNNTO requires only a small dataset to train and yet it can be generalized to problems with largely different boundary conditions, loading cases, design domain geometries, mesh resolutions, as well as non-design domains. In the end, the generalization capabilities and efficiency of eCNNTO are demonstrated through a variety of examples in two and three dimensions, achieving up to 90% and 97% reduction of iterations, respectively.

</details>


### [329] [The Tao of Agency: Autotelic AI, Embedded Agency and Dissolution of the Self](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19924)
*Aritra Sarkar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autotelic AI studies agents that generate their own goals, tracing consequences through intrinsic motivation, priors, learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; embeddedness is necessary but non-sufficient for autotelic agency due to non-unique self-individuation. The core issue is how the agent defines and relativizes its self-boundary. A unified framework is extended in three directions: quantum agent-environment cuts, philosophical critique of non-dual traditions, and a concrete LLM-based instantiation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the standard assumption that goals are exogenously specified and to explore what happens when agents autonomously generate and regulate their goals, with implications for autonomy, alignment, and cognitive architecture.

Method: The analysis synthesizes concepts (intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, embeddedness) to form a unified framework; it then extends this framework along three directions: (1) a quantum formulation where the agent–environment boundary is physical, (2) a philosophical reading against non-dual contemplative traditions, and (3) a concrete LLM-based agentic implementation.

Result: Embeddedness is a necessary but not sufficient condition for autotelic agency. Self-individuation is non-unique: the same dynamics admit multiple valid partitions, each yielding a different candidate self. The deepest problem lies in how the agent generates and relativizes the self to which goals are assigned. The authors consolidate developments into a single framework and propose three extensions (quantum, philosophical, LLM-based).

Conclusion: Autotelic agency hinges on a robust, stabilized self-model; boundary belief is essential to action, yet the boundary must be permeable to enable understanding. The proposed framework paves the way for quantum, philosophical, and practical instantiations, highlighting that self-definition, not goal-generation, is the core challenge.

Abstract: Most artificial intelligence systems are built on the assumption that goals are exogenous and specified by the designer. Exploring what happens when an agent begins generating its own goals opens the field of autotelic AI. Agents are expected not merely to pursue objectives but to discover them. In this article, we trace its consequences through intrinsic motivation, resource-driven priors, causal-interventional learning, homeostasis, and embeddedness; the last of which is found to be a necessary but not sufficient condition for autotelic agency. Embeddedness individuates the agent at the cost of revealing that the individuation is non-unique, such that the same dynamics admit many valid partitions, each defining a different candidate self.
  The deepest problem with autotelic AI is therefore not how the agent generates goals, but how it generates and relativizes the self to which the goals are assigned. The agent must believe in its own boundary in order to act, and see through that boundary in order to understand. We consolidate these developments into a single framework and extend it along three directions: a quantum formulation in which the agent-environment cut becomes physical, a philosophical reading against non-dual contemplative traditions, and a concrete LLM-based agentic instantiation.

</details>


### [330] [PhysDrift: Bridging the Embodiment Gap in Humanoid Co-Speech Motion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19935)
*Zhangzhao Liang,Xiaofen Xing,Mingyue Yang,Wenlve Zhou,Xiangmin Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Embodiment-aware, robot-native co-speech motion generation via IK-EER retargeting and PhysDrift direct speech-to-trajectory modeling, improving embodiment consistency, alignment, and real-time performance over human-centric pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the embodiment gap between human motion manifolds and humanoid constraints in co-speech generation, which degrades diversity, prosody–motion synchronization, and physical executability when retargeting human motions to robots.

Method: 1) IK-EER: a robot-native motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech–motion temporal alignment during retargeting; 2) PhysDrift: an embodiment-aware model that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech with physical regularization; builds on a curated robot-native motion dataset and validates through extensive experiments and real-world deployment.

Result: Embodiment-aware robot-native generation substantially improves speech–motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability; retargeting alone compresses motion diversity and weakens prosody-motion synchronization.

Conclusion: Embedding embodiment constraints into both the training and inference of co-speech motion generation yields more natural, executable, and robust humanoid interactions; the proposed framework sets a path toward fully robot-native pipelines for expressive, real-time humanoid behavior.

Abstract: Humanoid robots require co-speech motions that are not only expressive and speech-aligned, but also physically executable under embodiment constraints. Existing co-speech generation pipelines are predominantly human-centric: motions are first generated in human-body representations such as SMPL-X and subsequently retargeted to humanoid robots. In this work, we identify a fundamental embodiment gap in this paradigm, where the mismatch between human motion manifolds and humanoid embodiment constraints disrupts embodiment consistency during motion transfer and physical execution. Through extensive analysis, we show that although retargeting can preserve coarse motion semantics, it significantly compresses motion diversity and weakens prosody-motion synchronization, limiting expressive humanoid behaviors. To address this problem, we first propose IK-EER, a prosody-preserving humanoid motion curation framework that jointly optimizes kinematic feasibility and speech-motion temporal alignment during retargeting. Building upon the curated robot-native motion dataset, we further introduce PhysDrift, an embodiment-aware co-speech motion generation framework that directly predicts executable humanoid joint trajectories from speech without relying on intermediate human-body representations. Unlike conventional human-centric pipelines, PhysDrift maintains embodiment consistency throughout both training and inference while incorporating physical regularization to stabilize robot motion dynamics. Extensive experiments and real-world humanoid deployment demonstrate that embodiment-aware robot-native generation substantially improves speech-motion alignment, physical plausibility, motion smoothness, inference efficiency, and real-time interaction capability.

</details>


### [331] [Advancing DialNav through Automatic Embodied Dialog Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19948)
*Leekyeung Han,Sangwon Jung,Hyunji Min,Jinseong Jeong,Minyoung Kim,Paul Hongsuck Seo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A large-scale training dataset (RAINbow) for DialNav with 238K episodes, plus two training innovations (Dual-Strategy Training and a localization model) leading to state-of-the-art performance on Val Seen and Val Unseen.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address data scarcity and misalignment in the dialog–navigation loop for embodied agents by generating multi-turn dialog data from VLN datasets and improving training strategies and localization.

Method: (1) Automatic pipeline converting VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog (RAINbow). (2) Dual-Strategy Training to align navigation training with the dialog-navigation loop. (3) Localization model that leverages VLN knowledge.

Result: Significant performance gains: Val Seen 58.24 (+89%), Val Unseen 29.05 (+100%), establishing a new state of the art for DialNav evaluation.

Conclusion: The combination of large-scale generated dialog data and task-aligned training strategies substantially boosts dialog-guided navigation performance in photorealistic indoor environments.

Abstract: For embodied agents capable of physical interaction, the capability to create and understand dialog is crucial to ensure both safety and effectiveness. While DialNav~\cite{han2025dialnav} provides a framework for holistic evaluation of the dialog--execution loop in photorealistic indoor navigation, its performance remains limited by a critical scarcity of training data (2K episodes). To address this, we propose an automatic generation pipeline, and construct the \textbf{RAINbow} dataset, a large-scale training dataset with 238K episodes for DialNav. Our pipeline converts existing VLN datasets into multi-turn dialog and creates cost-efficient and high-quality dataset. Then, we introduce two additional complementary advances to unlock the data's full potential: (1) Dual-Strategy Training, a navigation training scheme to align the navigation training with the dynamic dialog-navigation loop, and (2) a localization model that leverages VLN knowledge. By combining these complementary solutions, our model substantially outperforms the baseline in success rate on both \textbf{Val Seen} (58.24, \textbf{+89\%}) and \textbf{Val Unseen} (29.05, \textbf{+100\%}) splits, establishing a new state of the art.

</details>


### [332] [ENPIRE: Agentic Robot Policy Self-Improvement in the Real World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19980)
*Wenli Xiao,Jia Xie,Tonghe Zhang,Haotian Lin,Letian "Max" Fu,Haoru Xue,Jalen Lu,Yi Yang,Cunxi Dai,Zi Wang,Jimmy Wu,Guanzhi Wang,S. Shankar Sastry,Ken Goldberg,Linxi "Jim" Fan,Yuke Zhu,Guanya Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ENPIRE is a four-module harness that closes the loop between real-world manipulation learning and coding agents, enabling autonomous policy refinement with Environment, Policy Improvement, Rollout, and Evolution modules; it enables scalable, fleet-scale, near-99% success on dexterous tasks with reduced human input.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the bottleneck in real-world robotic manipulation caused by heavy human supervision and engineering, and to bridge the gap where coding agents excel only in digital environments by introducing a repeatable, physical feedback loop for policy improvement.

Method: Introduce ENPIRE with four core modules: Environment (automatic reset and verification in the real world), Policy Improvement (trigger policy refinement based on feedback), Rollout (evaluate policies with one or more physical robots in parallel), and Evolution (analyze logs, consult literature, and improve training infrastructure and code). The system forms a closed-loop: reset → execute policy → verify outcome → refine next iteration, enabling controllable optimization across training recipes and agent variants.

Result: Empirically, coding agents trained through ENPIRE autonomously achieve up to ~99% success on challenging dexterous tasks (e.g., organizing a pin box, fastening a zip tie, tool use) and speedups with fleet deployment, demonstrating scalable, autonomous real-world manipulation learning.

Conclusion: ENPIRE provides a practical, scalable path for autonomously advancing robotics in the physical world by turning real-world manipulation learning into a controllable optimization problem with minimal human effort and robust ablations across variants.

Abstract: Achieving dexterous robotic manipulation in the real world heavily relies on human supervision and algorithm engineering, which becomes a central bottleneck in the pursuit of general physical intelligence. Although emerging coding agents can generate code to automate algorithm search, their successes remain largely confined in digital environments. We conjecture that the missing abstraction to automate robotics research is a repeatable feedback loop for real-world policy improvement: reset the scene, execute a policy, verify the outcome, and refine the next iteration. To bridge this gap, we introduce ENPIRE, a harness framework for coding agents that instantiates this physical feedback routine with four core modules: an Environment module (EN) for automatic reset and verification, a Policy Improvement module (PI) that launches policy refinement, a Rollout module (R) to evaluate policies with one or multiple physical robots operating in parallel, and an Evolution module (E) in which coding agents analyze logs, consult literature, improve training infrastructure and algorithm code to address failure modes. This closed-loop system transforms real-world manipulation learning into a controllable optimization procedure, minimizing human effort while allowing fair ablations across training recipe and agent variants. Powered by ENPIRE, frontier coding agents can autonomously train a policy to achieve a 99% success rate on challenging, dexterous manipulation tasks, such as organizing a pin box, fastening a zip tie, and tool use, a process that further accelerates when we dispatch an agent team on a robot fleet. Our results suggest a practical and scalable path toward deploying coding agents to autonomously advancing robotics in the physical world.

</details>


### [333] [Reward as An Agent for Embodied World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.19990)
*Pu Li,Zhigang Lin,Qiang Wu,Yongxuan Lv,Fei Wang,Shan You*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Broader exploration in embodied world models is pursued via a verification-grounded RL framework (Reward as an Agent) and a dynamic-aware rollout diversification method (DynDiff-GRPO), addressing reward hacking and improving accuracy beyond conservative rollout regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conservative rollouts limit exploration and behavioral diversity; without robust verification, broader exploration risks reward hacking under distribution shifts; embodied world models provide a rigorous testbed for scalable RL amid complex dynamics.

Method: Introduce Reward as an Agent for agentic reward signals; propose DynDiff-GRPO to diversify action-space rollouts; unify these components to enable RL with richer exploration under robust verification; evaluate on open-source world models.

Result: Significant accuracy gains and diversified sampling; mitigated reward hacking across multiple world models; improved exploration beyond near-distribution rollouts.

Conclusion: Broader, verification-grounded exploration can scale in RL for complex dynamics when paired with robust reward evaluation; the proposed framework reduces reward hacking and yields better policy quality.

Abstract: While RL has become a promising tool for refining world models, existing methods largely rely on conservative rollouts near the training distribution, limiting exploration, behavioral diversity, and richer dynamic discovery. In this work, we challenge this conservative paradigm. We argue that the core limitation is not exploration itself, but the lack of reliable verification strategies to support broader exploration. Without reliable verification, expanded exploration becomes highly susceptible to reward hacking, where policies exploit imperfect rewards without achieving genuine improvement. To evaluate this motivation, we instantiate our method in embodied world models, where physical plausibility, and task completion provide a rigorous testbed for scalable RL under complex dynamics. On the verification side, we introduce Reward as an Agent, an agentic reward framework that actively evaluates generated behaviors to provide robust reward signals and mitigate reward hacking under distribution shifts. On the exploration side, we introduce Dynamic-Aware Rollout Diversification through DynDiff-GRPO, which explicitly expands action-space exploration to diversify trajectories, broaden state-action coverage, and encourage richer embodied behaviors beyond conservative rollout regimes. By unifying Reward as an Agent with DynDiff-GRPO, we enable RL on a more reliable reward foundation with substantially diversified sampling, effectively mitigating reward hacking while yielding significant accuracy gains across multiple open-source world models, thereby demonstrating that broader exploration can scale successfully when grounded in robust verification.

</details>


### [334] [Autonomous Event-Driven Multi-Agent Orchestration for Enterprise AI at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20058)
*Harsh Rao Dhanyamraju,Leonidas Raghav,Aaron Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Enterprise-scale multi-agent orchestration is bottlenecked by agent discovery noise; Task Manager improves latency and related-event correctness; DAG Plan & Execute favors small-scale precision but has higher overhead, while ReAct offers robustness at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move enterprise AI toward continuous event monitoring, detection, and action across specialist agents, and to evaluate scalable multi-agent architectures beyond discrete request–response workflows across large-scale enterprise settings.

Method: Evaluate DAG Plan and Execute and ReAct across 208 production-derived enterprise scenarios spanning Persona (<10 agents), Department (20–80), and Enterprise (200) scales; introduce a Task Manager implementing priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption to enable continuous operation.

Result: Scale, not task complexity, dominates orchestration performance. At enterprise scale, agent discovery noise is the primary bottleneck; simple tasks degrade more sharply than complex ones. DAG Plan and Execute provides higher precision and structured parallelization at small scales but incurs higher overhead; ReAct is more robust by handling failures incrementally. The Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by over 20 percentage points at enterprise scale.

Conclusion: For enterprise-scale orchestration, focus should be on mitigating discovery-noise and robustness; DAG Plan & Execute suits small-scale precision with lower overhead, ReAct offers robustness at larger scales, and Task Manager yields substantial gains in latency and correctness, indicating a path toward scalable, resilient enterprise MAS.

Abstract: Enterprise AI aims to move toward continuous event monitoring, detection, and action across specialist agents, yet existing multi-agent systems largely assume discrete request-response workflows and remain underexplored at enterprise scale. We evaluate DAG Plan and Execute and ReAct across 208 production-derived enterprise scenarios spanning Persona (<10 agents), Department (20-80), and Enterprise (200) scales, and introduce a Task Manager for continuous operation via priority inference, related-event merging, and preemption. Results show that scale, not task complexity, dominates orchestration performance: both architectures perform well at small scale but degrade at enterprise scale as agent discovery noise becomes the primary bottleneck, with simple tasks degrading more sharply than complex ones. DAG Plan and Execute offers higher precision and structured parallelization at smaller scales, but its higher overhead worsens at enterprise scale; ReAct is more robust by handling failures incrementally. The Task Manager reduces high-priority queue latency by 14-75% and improves related-event correctness by over 20 percentage points at enterprise scale.

</details>


### [335] [Process-Verified Reinforcement Learning for Theorem Proving via Lean](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20068)
*Minsu Kim,Se-Young Yun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A framework uses Lean's verification signals as dense, structured rewards for RL in formal reasoning, improving tactic-level guidance over outcome-only signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between sparse, outcome-based RL rewards and rich, structured feedback from symbolic proof systems; provide denser, verifier-grounded credit signals to accelerate learning in formal proof tasks.

Method: Parse proof attempts into tactic sequences; Lean elaboration marks identify locally sound steps and the earliest failing step; integrate these signals into a GRPO-style RL objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit; train with STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5 on MiniF2F and ProofNet.

Result: Tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, yielding improvements on MiniF2F and ProofNet benchmarks; demonstrates that symbolic proof assistants can serve as process-level reward oracles during training.

Conclusion: Symbolic verifiers can function as process-level feedback mechanisms in RL for formal reasoning, enabling scalable, reliable learning by combining structured symbolic verification with language-model-based search.

Abstract: While reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) typically has relied on a single binary verification signal, symbolic proof assistants in formal reasoning offer rich, fine-grained structured feedback. This gap between structured processes and unstructured rewards highlights the importance of feedback that is both dense and sound. In this work, we demonstrate that the Lean proof assistant itself can serve as a symbolic process oracle, supplying both outcome-level and fine-grained tactic-level verified feedback during training. Proof attempts are parsed into tactic sequences, and Lean's elaboration marks both locally sound steps and the earliest failing step, yielding dense, verifier-grounded credit signals rooted in type theory. We incorporate these structured rewards into a GRPO-style reinforcement learning objective with first-error propagation and first-token credit methods that balances outcome- and process-level advantages. Experiments with STP-Lean and DeepSeek-Prover-V1.5 show that tactic-level supervision outperforms outcome-only baselines in most settings, delivering improvements on benchmarks such as MiniF2F and ProofNet. Beyond empirical gains, our study highlights a broader perspective: symbolic proof assistants are not only verifiers at evaluation time, but can also act as process-level reward oracles during training. This opens a path toward reinforcement learning frameworks that combine the scalability of language models with the reliability of symbolic verification for formal reasoning.

</details>


### [336] [Residual-Space Evolutionary Optimization via Flow-based Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20084)
*Zhuo Cao,Lena Krieger,Fernanda Nader,Xuan Zhao,Hanno Scharr,Ira Assent*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes residual-space evolutionary optimization for flow-based generative editing, using a model-agnostic approach that operates in residual space and combines local residual refinement (self-pollination) with cross-sample residual recombination (cross-pollination). Validates concept on MorphoMNIST and crystal data, showing balanced target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity, with applicability beyond images to scientific domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Flow-based editing often relies on differentiable objectives and gradient searches; such assumptions fail for non-differentiable or black-box objectives. A model-agnostic framework is needed to enable effective editing in residual space without relying on gradients.

Method: Introduce residual-space evolutionary optimization (RSEO) that leverages conditional flow matching (CFM) to disentangle condition-controlled factors from instance-specific residuals. Operate in residual space and implement two search regimes: self-pollination for local exploitation via residual refinement, and cross-pollination for global exploration by recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples.

Result: Conceptual framework validated via proof-of-concept experiments on MorphoMNIST (counterfactual generation) and crystal data, demonstrating that the exploration–exploitation decomposition balances target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity, and generalizes beyond images to real-world scientific domains.

Conclusion: RSEO provides a model-agnostic, flow-based editing paradigm that integrates evolutionary search in residual space, enabling effective editing under non-differentiable objectives and expanding applicability of conditional flow-based methods to diverse domains.

Abstract: Data editing with generative methods typically requires differentiable objectives and gradient-based search. However, these assumptions break down in flow-based settings, where edits are performed through forward and backward integration and often involve non-differentiable or black-box objectives. We introduce residual-space evolutionary optimization, a model-agnostic framework that addresses this gap by combining flow-based generative editing with evolutionary algorithms. Building on the observation that conditional flow matching (CFM) can disentangle condition-controlled factors from instance-specific residuals, our framework directly operates in residual space and separates two complementary search regimes: self-pollination performs local exploitation through feature-preserving residual refinement, and cross-pollination promotes broader exploration by recombining residuals across heterogeneous samples. As a proof of concept, we validate on MorphoMNIST, a benchmark dataset for counterfactual generation, and on crystal data, demonstrating that this exploration--exploitation decomposition provides a useful mechanism for balancing target alignment, instance preservation, and diversity, and extends beyond images to real-world scientific domains.

</details>


### [337] [Multi-Head Attention-Based Feature Extractor Integration with Soft Actor-Critic for Porosity Prediction and Process Parameter Optimization in Additive Manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20087)
*Kianoush Aqabakee,Leonardo Stella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Continuous-action SAC with multi-head attention improves porosity optimization in laser powder bed fusion, achieving faster convergence and higher final reward vs standard RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome slow convergence and local optima in discrete-action RL for high-precision AM by using continuous actions and attention-enhanced SAC.

Method: Integrates a multi-head attention feature extractor into Soft Actor-Critic, operating in continuous action space; applied to porosity prediction and process-parameter optimization in laser powder bed fusion.

Result: Faster convergence and higher final rewards; reported convergence value 322.79 within 14 episodes; outperforms DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC; stable training.

Conclusion: Attention-based continuous-action SAC effectively handles AM parameter optimization with improved exploration-exploitation and robustness to local minima, outperforming mainstream RL baselines.

Abstract: Additive manufacturing process optimization requires precise parameter control to minimize defects such as porosity. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) approaches using discrete action spaces suffer from slow convergence and susceptibility to local optima, limiting their effectiveness for high-precision manufacturing tasks. This study addresses these limitations by employing a continuous action space combined with a novel architecture that integrates a multi-head attention mechanism with the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm. The attention-based feature extractor enhances the agent's ability to capture subtle variations in low-dimensional input features, enabling more effective exploration-exploitation balance for navigating value spaces with local minima. We validate our approach on porosity prediction and process parameter optimization in laser powder bed fusion, demonstrating faster convergence and higher final reward values compared to standard RL methods including DQN, PPO, TD3, and vanilla SAC. The proposed methodology achieves a convergence value of 322.79 within 14 episodes, outperforming existing approaches while maintaining stability throughout training.

</details>


### [338] [ScaffoldAgent: Utility-Guided Dynamic Outline Optimization for Open-Ended Deep Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20122)
*Zhibang Yang,Xinke Jiang,Yuzhen Xiao,Ruizhe Zhang,Yue Fang,XinFei Wan,Zhengxing Song,Yuxuan Liu,Yuheng Huang,Xu Chu,Junfeng Zhao,Yasha Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dynamic, utility-guided outline optimization for open-ended deep research via ScaffoldAgent, employing Expansion, Contraction, and Revision to adapt the report scaffold during multi-round retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended deep research demands iterative retrieval and long-form generation. Fixed or locally refined outlines suffer scaffold drift and delayed evaluation of outline edits; a dynamic, guidance-driven outline is needed.

Method: Models outline evolution as a structured decision process with three operations (Expansion, Contraction, Revision). Introduces a utility-guided feedback mechanism that estimates downstream value from retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality to guide node selection, operation scheduling, and termination during inference.

Result: Experiments on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym show ScaffoldAgent consistently improves long-form report generation and factual grounding over existing deep research agents.

Conclusion: Utility-guided, dynamic outline optimization effectively mitigates scaffold drift in OEDR and improves performance, with demonstrated gains on standard benchmarks.

Abstract: Open-ended deep research (OEDR) requires systems to acquire knowledge through multi-round retrieval and generate coherent long-form reports. The outline plays a central role as a structural scaffold that coordinates retrieval, evidence organization, and generation. However, existing methods either fix the outline before writing or refine it with local heuristics, leading to scaffold drift under continuous information accumulation and delayed feedback for evaluating outline modifications. We propose ScaffoldAgent, a utility-guided dynamic outline optimization framework for OEDR. ScaffoldAgent models outline evolution as a structured decision process with three operations: Expansion, Contraction, and Revision, enabling controlled updates to the report scaffold. It further introduces a utility-guided feedback mechanism that estimates the downstream value of each outline operation from retrieval gain, structural coherence, and trial-generation quality. The resulting utility signal guides node selection, operation scheduling, and termination during inference. Experiments on DeepResearch Bench and DeepResearch Gym show that ScaffoldAgent consistently improves long-form report generation and factual grounding over existing deep research agents.

</details>


### [339] [Learning to Prompt: Improving Student Engagement with Adaptive LLM-based High-School Tutoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20138)
*Po-Chin Chang,Nicholas Hogan,Aske Plaat,Michiel T. van der Meer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Subject-aware prompting with a 14-feature pedagogy-based router improves LLM tutoring performance and efficiency, with a stochastic strategy yielding the highest conversion; sim-to-real transfer demonstrated.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static-prompt tutoring systems fail to adapt to diverse disciplines; need adaptive, pedagogy-grounded prompt routing to tailor tutoring strategies.

Method: Extract 14 pedagogical features from transcripts; train a prompt routing model in simulation; deploy in online high-school tutoring with 359 students (656 conversations); compare against two static baselines; compare greedy vs stochastic routing; evaluate conversion rate and interaction length with statistical tests.

Result: In simulation, the router outperforms baselines (0.694 vs 0.647 and 0.64; p<0.001). A/B testing shows sim-to-real transfer with strategy switching from analytical to scaffolding; adaptive prompting reduces interactions by ~3 turns (p=0.007). Greedy router matches baseline exercise conversion (19.1% vs 19.6%); stochastic router increases conversion to 28.1%.

Conclusion: Subject-aware, adaptive prompt routing with a stochastic strategy enhances tutoring efficiency and effectiveness and generalizes from simulation to real classrooms, offering a viable path for cross-disciplinary, LLM-based tutoring.

Abstract: LLMs can personalize education, although current static-prompt tutoring systems struggle to adapt to diverse academic disciplines. We develop and test a system with subject-aware prompting, based on 14 pedagogical features (e.g., tutor scaffolding, student understanding) extracted from raw transcripts. We first train a prompt routing model in a simulation environment, and then deploy it for online adaptation with actual high-school students. The simulation benchmark shows the router outperforming two static baselines ($0.694$ vs. $0.647$ and $0.64$, $p<0.001$). A/B testing ($N=656$ conversations from 359 students) shows sim-to-real transfer where the model switches from analytical to scaffolding learning strategies. Our adaptive prompt selection mechanism improves instructional efficiency, maintains pedagogical quality and reduces interactions by around 3 turns ($p=0.007$). While a greedy router achieves a comparable exercise conversion rate with the baseline ($19.1\%$ vs. $19.6\%$), a stochastic router that samples strategies leads to a higher conversion rate ($28.1\%$).

</details>


### [340] [RACL: Reasoning-Agent Control Layers for Continuous Metaheuristic Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20142)
*Antón Asla Manzárraga*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces RACL, a Reasoning-Agent Control Layer that sits above a metaheuristic optimizer to learn, test, and apply high-level control policies without altering constraints, demonstrated on vehicle routing with Codex as the reasoning agent; yields performance gains and reproducibility benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable explainable, adaptive control of metaheuristics by a reasoning agent that can observe internal memory, formulate hypotheses, intervene, and explain decisions, without replacing the optimizer.

Method: RACL uses an observing reasoning agent above an existing optimizer; operates by observing operational memory, reasoning over past behavior, formulating bounded hypotheses, testing interventions, evaluating outcomes, applying guardrails, consolidating policies, and explaining decisions. Codex used as in-the-loop reasoning agent; a policy proxy ensures reproducibility.

Result: Improves or ties Operational Memory Policy in all 21 feasible cases; improves or ties STP in 18 of 21; average cost delta -0.641% against STP; Sevilla-9/10 shows -8.337% vs Fixed and -1.605% vs STP; no material computational overhead.

Conclusion: RACL demonstrates a general method to discover, validate, consolidate, and explain algorithmic control rules for metaheuristics, not a new solver; with reproducible evaluation via a policy proxy; promising improvements in routing-like problems.

Abstract: This paper introduces RACL, a Reasoning-Agent Control Layer for metaheuristics. RACL places a reasoning agent above an existing optimizer. The agent does not replace the optimizer and does not modify business constraints. Instead, it controls the optimizer's internal search behavior by observing operational memory, reasoning over past behavior, formulating bounded hypotheses, testing interventions, evaluating outcomes, applying guardrails, consolidating useful policies and explaining its decisions. The experiment uses vehicle routing as a testbed, but the contribution is not a new routing solver, a particular ALNS configuration or a specific set of routing rules. The contribution is the RACL method: a way for a reasoning agent to discover, validate, consolidate and explain algorithmic control rules for a metaheuristic. In the current experimental setting, RACL improves or ties the Operational Memory Policy in 21 of 21 feasible cases and improves or ties a non-reasoning Stagnation-Triggered Policy in 18 of 21 feasible cases, with an average RACL vs STP cost delta of -0.641%. In the Sevilla-9/10 runtime sample, RACL improves average cost by -8.337% versus Fixed and -1.605% versus STP without showing material computational overhead. During the proof-of-concept, Codex was used as an in-the-loop reasoning agent observing executions, interpreting logs and proposing live bounded interventions. The policy proxy was later used only to make quantitative evaluation reproducible.

</details>


### [341] [BIM-Edit: Benchmarking Large Language Models for IFC-Based Building Information Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20146)
*Bharathi Kannan Nithyanantham,Clemens Kujat,Tobias Sesterhenn,Stefan Telgmann,Jörn Plönnigs,Stefan Lüdtke,Christian Bartelt*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BIM-Edit evaluates LLMs on natural-language editing of BIM/IFC models; results show current models struggle with editing structured engineering data, highlighting a gap to real-world workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Engineering design requires editing existing BIM scenes while preserving semantics and relations, not just generating new geometry; existing benchmarks under-test this capability.

Method: 324 editing tasks across 11 real building models and 36 synthetic scenes, framed as direct, spatial, and topological edits in IFC; outputs evaluated on geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency across multiple LLMs.

Result: Best average score is 49.5%; no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks, indicating a substantial gap between LLMs and the requirements of structured engineering design workflows.

Conclusion: Current LLMs are inadequate for robust BIM editing; BIM-Edit provides a challenging benchmark to drive improvements in semantic and relational reasoning within CAD-like contexts and points to the need for better alignment with IFC semantics and validation mechanisms.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to computer-aided design (CAD) to generate design artifacts from textual instructions. In engineering practice, this requires more than creating new geometry, models must also understand existing scenes, edit them correctly, and preserve semantics and relations. However, many CAD benchmarks focus on creating new models rather than editing existing ones, and mostly evaluate geometric correctness. We introduce BIM-Edit, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs on natural-language editing of Building Information Models (BIM) represented in the Industry Foundation Classes (IFC) format. BIM provides a challenging testbed because building models encode geometry together with semantic and relational structure. BIM-Edit contains 324 editing tasks spanning 11 realistic building models and 36 synthetic scenes. Tasks are expressed using three instruction categories - direct, spatial, and topological - covering both explicit and scene-grounded edits. We evaluate outputs along three dimensions: geometric accuracy, semantic validity, and topological consistency. Across evaluated LLMs, the best-performing model achieves only 49.5% average score across the three metrics, and no model fully solves more than 3.4% of tasks. These results demonstrate a substantial gap between current LLM capabilities and the requirements of structured engineering design workflows.

</details>


### [342] [Modularity-Free Conflict-Averse Training for Generalized PINNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20156)
*Heejo Kong,Beomchul Park,Sung-Jin Kim,Seong-Whan Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Overparameterized PINNs develop task-modularization that reduces cross-task interaction; ModSync mitigates this by penalizing task-exclusive connections while preserving interaction pathways, enabling Pareto-stationary convergence and SOTA accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address capacity-induced failures in conflict-averse PINN training where increasing model capacity leads to modularity and poor cross-objective coupling.

Method: Introduce Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync) by embedding structural optimization into training: apply penalties to task-exclusive connections and enforce synchronization of shared pathways to maintain cross-objective interaction.

Result: ModSync prevents capacity-driven modularity across diverse PDE benchmarks, sustains cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy compared to existing conflict-averse approaches; code available.

Conclusion: Capacity-induced modularity in PINNs can be mitigated by joint structural optimization that penalizes exclusivity while preserving interaction-promoting pathways, restoring convergence toward Pareto-stationary points and improving performance.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have become a powerful framework for solving PDEs by embedding physical laws into differentiable objectives. Despite their advances, training PINNs remains fragile: recent conflict-averse optimization schemes alleviate gradient interference between residual and boundary losses, but we show that their effectiveness deteriorates as model capacity increases. In this paper, we identify a capacity-induced failure mode, where overparameterized networks undergo functional modularity, self-partitioning into task-exclusive modules that suppress cross-objective interaction and hinder convergence toward Pareto-stationary points. To address this issue, we propose a novel framework, Modular-Sparsity Synchronization (ModSync), which integrates structural optimization into conflict-averse training by penalizing task-exclusive connections while preserving interaction-promoting pathways. Extensive experiments across diverse PDE benchmarks demonstrate that ModSync consistently prevents capacity-driven failures, sustains robust cross-objective coupling, and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy. Codes are available at \url{https://github.com/heejokong/ModSync}.

</details>


### [343] [Implicit Semantic-Aware Communication Based on Hypergraph Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20162)
*Yiwei Liao,Shurui Tu,Yong Xiao,Yingyu Li,Guangming Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning (HISR) for semantic-aware communication improves modeling of higher-order relations beyond pairwise graphs by using dedicated semantic subspaces per relational context, reducing over-smoothing and increasing robustness to partial information loss; reports up to 36.6% gain in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic-communication approaches largely model semantic content with pairwise graphs, which fail to capture higher-order interactions (group interactions, multi-entity relations). This limits expressiveness and degrades semantic inference, especially under noisy channels.

Method: Introduce a hypergraph-based framework (HISR) where entities and higher-order relations are embedded into context-specific semantic subspaces. This disentangles diverse semantic interactions, mitigates over-smoothing from conventional graph embeddings, and enables robust inference when partial information is lost during transmission.

Result: Empirical results indicate up to 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over state-of-the-art benchmarks.

Conclusion: Hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning enhances semantic expressiveness and robustness in semantic communication, effectively handling higher-order correlations and partial information loss.

Abstract: Semantic-aware communication has emerged as a transformative paradigm for next-generation communication systems, shifting the fundamental goal from transmitting bit-level symbols to reliably recovering and understanding the semantic meaning of information. Previous studies have demonstrated that representing the semantic content of source messages as graph-based structures can significantly improve communication efficiency and the accuracy of semantic inference at the receiver. However, existing solutions typically employ graphs that capture only pairwise relationships, thereby neglecting higher-order implicit correlations commonly observed in real-world scenarios, such as group interactions, multi-entity associations, and complex relational contexts. This limitation reduces semantic expressiveness and makes semantic inference susceptible to ambiguity and performance degradation, particularly under noisy or corrupted channel conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes a novel hypergraph-based implicit semantic reasoning framework, HISR, which leverages hypergraphs to represent complex multi-entity relationships among semantic knowledge entities. In HISR, entities and their associated higher-order relations are mapped into dedicated semantic subspaces tailored to distinct relational contexts. This design not only disentangles diverse semantic interactions to mitigate the over-smoothing effects commonly found in traditional graph embedding methods but also enables robust semantic inference even when partial information loss occurs during transmission. Numerical results show that the proposed HISR achieves up to a 36.6% improvement in implicit semantic interpretation accuracy over the state-of-the-art benchmarks.

</details>


### [344] [Apparent Psychological Profiles of Large Language Models are Largely a Measurement Artifact](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20205)
*Jelena Meyer,David Garcia,Dirk U. Wulff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show measurement artifacts in psychometric profiling; most between-model variance is due to directional response bias rather than true traits; bias decreases with model capability but never disappears; apparent reliability hinges on response orthogonality (trait vs. bias alignment); profiles shift with item content and can be engineered by item choice; call for LLM-specific assessments emphasizing response orthogonality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether human psychometric instruments yield valid trait profiles for LLMs and to determine whether observed differences reflect genuine differences in 'psychological traits' or measurement artifacts.

Method: Administered a battery of personality and risk-preference instruments spanning self-report and behavioral tasks to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs alongside large human reference samples, analyzed within a formal psychometric framework, including variance decomposition to separate trait from bias effects.

Result: Four main findings: (1) between-model differences are driven by directional response bias rather than target traits; 81-90% of variance is due to bias, vs 9-16% in humans. (2) Bias declines with model capability but is not eliminated. (3) Apparent instrument reliability is largely predicted by response orthogonality (proportion of items where trait and bias oppose). (4) Model profiles shift with item content and can be manufactured via item selection.

Conclusion: Apparent psychological profiles of LLMs are artifact of measurement instruments; instruments borrowed from human psychology are rarely orthogonal or valid for LLMs. Recommend developing dedicated assessments focused on response orthogonality.

Abstract: Psychological instruments designed for humans are increasingly used to assign large language models (LLMs) stable psychological profiles that affect their usability, safety assessment, and use as proxies for human participants in research. Using a formal psychometric framework, we show that these profiles are largely a measurement artifact. Administering a battery of personality and risk-preference instruments spanning self-reports and behavioral tasks to 56 instruction-tuned LLMs alongside large human reference samples, we report four findings. First, differences between models are driven not by the traits an instrument targets but by a directional response bias, a tendency to respond toward one end of the scale, or one labeled option, regardless of item content; a variance decomposition attributes 81-90% of between-model variation to this bias, against 9-16% in humans. Second, the bias declines with model capability but is not eliminated by it. Third, because bias rather than trait drives responding, an instrument's apparent reliability is almost entirely predicted by its response orthogonality, a term we coin for the proportion of items for which trait and bias point in opposite directions. Fourth, the profile a model appears to have shifts with the items used and can be manufactured through item selection. These results demonstrate that the apparent psychological profiles of LLMs are artifacts of the instrument used to measure them, not properties of the models themselves. As instruments borrowed from human psychology are rarely fully orthogonal and may inherently lack validity for LLMs, we call for dedicated assessments centered on response orthogonality.

</details>


### [345] [Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20208)
*Guillaume Olivier Delplanque,Pierre Genevès,Nabil Layaïda,Zephirin Faure*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Rule Violation Score (RVS) is a complementary metric that quantifies how well a predictive model adheres to predefined logical rules, distinguishing hard and soft rules, computable via SQL for Horn rules, applicable across relational models and datasets, and useful for diagnosing rule quality and data consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predictive performance metrics miss logical consistency, which is critical in high-stakes domains; there is a need for a standard metric to measure constraint satisfaction and rule compliance.

Method: Define RVS with separate treatment for hard (strict) and soft (statistical) rules, applicable to models built over a relational vocabulary. Compute via automatically generated SQL queries for Horn rules. Evaluate on datasets and models (rule-based, embedding-based, neuro-symbolic) across three benchmarks for knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression.

Result: RVS reveals that models with similar predictive accuracy can exhibit substantially different levels of logical compliance, demonstrating the metric's ability to capture constraint adherence not reflected by standard accuracy metrics; supports evaluating both models and training data for logical consistency.

Conclusion: RVS provides a practical, model- and dataset-agnostic framework to assess logical consistency, enabling detection of poorly defined rules and offering a valuable complement to predictive performance metrics in downstream, constraint-sensitive applications.

Abstract: Machine learning models are predominantly evaluated through predictive performance metrics such as ranking quality, prediction error, or classification accuracy. While these metrics effectively quantify how closely predictions match the ground truth, they do not assess whether model outputs respect predefined logical or domain-specific constraints. In high-stakes applications, including healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems, logical consistency can be as critical as predictive accuracy, yet no standard metric captures this dimension. We introduce the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a complementary evaluation metric that quantifies the extent to which a predictive model respects a given set of logical rules, independently of predictive accuracy. RVS treats hard rules (strict constraints) and soft rules (statistical regularities) differently, can be evaluated on any dataset and on any predictive model expressed over a relational vocabulary, and can be computed using SQL queries that are automatically generated for Horn rules. Beyond evaluating models, RVS can also evaluate the logical consistency of training datasets and help identify poorly defined rules. We evaluate RVS on three benchmarks covering knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression, including rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic predictive models. Our results demonstrate that two models achieving comparable predictive accuracy can exhibit substantially different levels of logical compliance, revealing differences in model behavior that standard metrics fail to capture.

</details>


### [346] [Augmenting Game AI with Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20210)
*Alessandro Sestini,Joakim Bergdahl,Amir Baghi,Jean-Philippe Barrette-LaPierre,Florian Fuchs,Linus Gisslén*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A conceptual framework and guidelines for applying reinforcement learning to game AI, advocating RL-augmented agents to enhance believability, with deployment considerations, sample game examples, and a roadmap of bottlenecks and research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Immersion in games relies on believable in-game characters. Hand-coded AI struggles with behavioral complexity; ML can learn from interaction or player data to produce more human-like behavior. The paper argues for expanding reinforcement learning applications in game AI and across genres, outlining barriers to adoption and a framework to address them.

Method: Proposes a framework of requirements tailored for game AI and development, discusses practicalities of deploying player-facing ML agents, provides examples of games with RL-augmented AI, and analyzes bottlenecks and hard problems to identify promising research directions.

Result: A conceptual contribution consisting of a framework for RL-based game AI, illustrative examples of RL-enhanced agents, deployment considerations for player-facing ML agents, and a roadmap of bottlenecks and future research directions to accelerate adoption in the video game industry.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning holds promise for more believable game AI, but widespread deployment requires overcoming practical bottlenecks, data and training challenges, evaluation, and integration into game pipelines; the paper offers guidance and directions to advance adoption.

Abstract: Immersion in video games depends not only on graphics, audio, and game mechanics, but also on the quality of in-game characters. Producing believable characters, or game AI, remains a significant challenge as behavioral complexity is hard to capture with hand-coded systems. Game AI is a source of immersion and engagement; however, the limitations stemming from the challenges of creating game AI often lead to frustration and the breaking of the illusion of realism within the game. The introduction of machine learning models opens the door to creating more believable, authentic, and relatable characters in games. The promise is that they either learn from interacting with the game, or from player data, to develop true human-like behavior. In this paper, we envision more applications of reinforcement learning for game AI in the future. For this to materialize, current research limitations are prohibitive to broad deployment across game genres. Therefore, we propose a framework for training reinforcement learning models with a set of requirements in mind that are suited towards game AI and game development. We present examples of games with reinforcement learning-augmented game AI and describe the practicalities of deploying player-facing machine learning agents in modern games. Furthermore, we identify bottlenecks and hard problems in these areas, which we believe offer promising research directions to accelerate the adoption of machine learning in game AI for the video game industry.

</details>


### [347] [QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20227)
*Xinyi Zheng,Ling Shi,Tianlong Yu,Yongxin Zhao,Lorenz Goette,Kailong Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: QMFOL is an automated framework to generate deductive reasoning tasks in monadic first-order logic with tunable complexity, paired with a validation step and a large configurable benchmark (QMFOLBench).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current reasoning benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.

Method: Construct formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns with controllable depth, width, label types, and distractors; translate to natural language with LLMs; ensure logical consistency via round-trip verification using an external prover; build 2880 instances across 960 configurations; evaluate on six large reasoning models and two LLMs.

Result: Model performance degrades as logical complexity rises; true-labeled tasks are easier than false or unknown ones; models show sensitivity to semantic variation; computational overhead increases with complexity.

Conclusion: QMFOL provides a scalable and reliable framework for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in reasoning, particularly in deductive reasoning, which is crucial for high-stakes decision-making. As models improve, evaluation benchmarks should evolve to keep pace. However, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.
  To address these issues, we propose QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable and controllable complexity. It constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, enabling precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are then translated into natural language via LLMs, with logical consistency ensured through round-trip verification using an external prover. Based on our framework, we build QMFOLBench, a benchmark comprising 2880 instances with 960 configurations across diverse logical and semantic dimensions. Evaluations on six large reasoning models (LRMs) and two LLMs show that performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity. Models perform better on True-labeled tasks than on False or Unknown ones, and exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation. Overall, QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

</details>


### [348] [Thermodynamic Measure of Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20231)
*Ishanu Chattopadhyay*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Intelligence is defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures; the architecture of recursive self-simulation is shown to be necessary and nearly sufficient for achieving high thermodynamic intelligence, enabling a universal measure of intelligent systems from passive matter to humans and information engines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To answer whether intelligence can be measured and to provide a universal, thermodynamics-based framework that applies across diverse substrates (from physical systems to humans and AI) by linking predictive modeling, self-representation, and action.

Method: Formalize intelligence as a 'rare-valid lift'—the probability increase of rare but admissible futures under an agent's actions—and analyze recursive self-simulation (the agent modeling futures that include its own actions). Derive a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement that tie high lift to high fidelity in identifying rare-valid futures and to the presence of an effective policy, thereby approaching actuation-limited optimum.

Result: The core results establish: (1) a necessity: achieving high rare-valid lift cannot occur unless the internal simulation accurately identifies rare-valid futures; (2) a conditional near-sufficiency: if fidelity is high and the simulation encodes an effective policy, the attainable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum.

Conclusion: Recursive self-simulation is not only plausible but, under stated assumptions, necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence, enabling a universal measure of intelligence across systems from matter and controllers to LLMs and humans, including information engines akin to Maxwell's demons.

Abstract: Can intelligence be measured? We propose that intelligence can be defined as the lawful amplification of rare but valid futures: a system increases the probability of outcomes that would be unlikely under passive dynamics but remain admissible under the constraints of the domain. We start with the premise that an intelligent system must model the world and its own place within it. Because the system is part of the world it models, this leads naturally to recursive self-simulation: the system represents futures in which its own actions are part of the trajectory. Our central results give a necessity statement and a conditional near-sufficiency statement connecting this architecture to a precise thermodynamic measure of lawful amplification of rare-valid futures: high rare-valid lift is impossible unless the internal simulation identifies rare-valid futures with high fidelity; conversely, when rare-valid fidelity is high and the simulation contains an effective policy, the achievable lift approaches the actuation-limited optimum. Thus recursive self-simulation is not merely a plausible feature of intelligence but, under the stated assumptions, is necessary and nearly sufficient for high thermodynamic intelligence. The resulting framework makes intelligence measurable on a universal scale, from passive matter and feedback controllers, large language models, and humans as text generators to Maxwell-demon-like information engines.

</details>


### [349] [A Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20236)
*Federica Filippini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes MAMO, a multi-agent RL framework that learns reward-weights for constrained optimization to improve autonomy and robustness in dynamic environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual tuning of Lagrangian penalty weights in RL-based constrained optimization is sensitive and struggles in non-stationary environments.

Method: Introduce MAMO that decouples task execution from objective design by casting weight selection as a learning problem; use multi-agent RL to adapt weights online.

Result: Positions MAMO as a first step toward autonomous, robust RL-based constrained optimization; no empirical results are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: MAMO enables autonomous balance between primary objective optimization and constraint violation avoidance in dynamic settings.

Abstract: Many decision-making problems in computing and networking systems can be naturally formulated as cost-minimization problems under performance constraints. In dynamic environments, reinforcement learning (RL) is often used to solve such problems at runtime by embedding both costs and constraint violations into a single scalar reward through weighted penalty terms, following a Lagrangian-inspired formulation. However, in this context the behavior of the learned policy critically depends on the choice of these weights, which are typically selected manually. This makes it difficult to identify an appropriate trade-off between optimizing the primary objective and effectively avoiding constraint violations, particularly in non-stationary environments where their relative importance may change. This paper presents MAMO (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization), an approach to tackle this balancing problem through multi-agent RL. MAMO decouples task execution from objective design by formulating the selection of reward weights as a learning problem, providing a !rst step towards more autonomous and robust RL-based solutions for constrained optimization problems in dynamic environments.

</details>


### [350] [Navigating Unreliable Parametric and Contextual Knowledge: Explicit Knowledge Conflict Resolution for LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20245)
*Huang Peng,Jiuyang Tang,Weixin Zeng,Hao Xu,Xiang Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MACR is a multi-agent conflict-resolution framework for LLMs that adaptively retrieves or externalizes knowledge and uses three specialized agents to induce rules, analyze conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies, achieving superior performance and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inconsistencies arising when both internal LLM knowledge and external prompts may contain errors, and to move beyond a binary reliability assumption that current methods adopt by explicitly resolving conflicts.

Method: 1) Adaptive knowledge assessment using a modified semantic entropy to estimate the model's confidence on a query; 2) Based on confidence, externalize internal knowledge as textual representations or retrieve relevant external knowledge to build basic contexts; 3) Inductive multi-agent reasoning with three agents: rule-induction to extract explicit rules, conflict-analysis to identify inconsistencies, and resolution to reconcile across all contexts.

Result: MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on benchmarks and provides interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.

Conclusion: MACR offers a general, interpretable, and robust framework for resolving knowledge conflicts in LLMs by combining internal and external knowledge through a multi-agent reasoning process.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance across a wide range of language-based tasks by leveraging both extensive parametric knowledge and in-context learning ability, enabling them to incorporate external information provided in the input prompt. However, the integration of external knowledge can introduce conflicts, not only between the model's internal parametric knowledge and the external information, but also among multiple pieces of external contexts. Existing approaches typically assume that either the model or the provided context is reliable, overlooking the possibility that both sources may contain errors, and avoid conflicts by privileging one source over the other, rather than actively resolving inconsistencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel framework MACR for LLM knowledge conflict resolution that moves beyond the conventional binary choice paradigm and incorporates an explicit conflict-resolution mechanism based on a multi-agent reasoning approach. Specifically, we first propose an adaptive knowledge assessment and retrieval approach that employs a modified semantic entropy measure to quantify an LLM's confidence in its answer to a given query. Based on this confidence estimation, MACR either externalizes the model's internal knowledge as textual representations or retrieves relevant external knowledge when internal knowledge is insufficient, generating basic contexts for subsequent reasoning. Then we introduce an inductive multi-agent reasoning framework with three specialized agents that, respectively, induce explicit rules, analyze potential conflicts, and resolve inconsistencies across all available contexts. Empirical results demonstrate that MACR significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across benchmarks, while also providing interpretable resolutions of explicit conflicts.

</details>


### [351] [Confidence-Aware Automated Assessment of Student-Drawn Scientific Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20264)
*Luyang Fang,Yingchuan Zhang,Jongchan Park,Zhaoji Wang,Ping Ma,Xiaoming Zhai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Automated scoring of student drawings using a Vision Transformer with confidence-aware selective automation improves reliability and enables a scalable human-in-the-loop for NGSS-aligned middle school assessments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce dependence on costly expert human scoring for student-generated scientific drawings by providing a scalable, trustworthy automated scoring system that can selectively automate high-confidence responses.

Method: Use a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation and a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions to decide which items are scored automatically and which are deferred to humans; evaluated on six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items.

Result: Improved scoring reliability and a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk; demonstrates the benefits of confidence-aware methods for trustworthy educational assessment across six NGSS-aligned items.

Conclusion: Confidence-aware, selective-automation approaches can enable scalable, trustworthy assessment by automating high-confidence responses while deferring uncertain cases to human raters.

Abstract: Student-generated drawings are widely used in science education to assess learners' conceptual understanding in modeling-based tasks aligned with the Next Generation Science Standards (NGSS). However, scoring such drawings requires expert human judgment to interpret complex visual representations, making large-scale assessment costly to implement and sustain in classroom settings. In this work, we study automated scoring of student-generated scientific drawings using a vision-based model. We evaluate a Vision Transformer (ViT) with parameter-efficient adaptation and propose a confidence-aware scoring framework that derives response-level confidence from test-time predictive distributions. This confidence signal enables selective automation by scoring high-confidence responses automatically while deferring uncertain cases for human review. Experiments on six NGSS-aligned middle school assessment items show that the proposed approach improves scoring reliability while supporting a practical trade-off between automated coverage and scoring risk, highlighting the value of confidence-aware methods for trustworthy educational assessment.

</details>


### [352] [Lagrange: An Open-Vocabulary, Energy-Based Sparse Framework for Generalized End-to-End Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20274)
*Shihao Ji,HongXi Li,Zihui Song,Mingyu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Open-vocabulary, sparse driving framework Lagrange uses Masked Latent Fields and vision-language models to form a continuous energy field for kinematically feasible planning, achieving robust open-world autonomy in offline benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the dichotomy between efficient, sparse planners and dense, generalizable perception; address open-world generalization, OOD events, and continuous control requirements in autonomous driving.

Method: Leverage Vision-Language Models to encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic tokens. Introduce an intent-driven masked cross-attention module to filter relevant entities temporally. Decode attended tokens into a continuous energy field over spatial coordinates. Frame decision-making as a Lagrangian action minimization that enforces vehicle kinematics while performing collision avoidance. Avoid dense volumetric reconstructions and closed-set query mechanisms; evaluate offline on nuScenes and CODA.

Result: Offline experiments indicate Lagrange can achieve robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy, demonstrating promise over conventional dense or closed-set approaches.

Conclusion: Lagrange presents a feasible, open-vocabulary, computationally sparse framework for open-world autonomous driving, combining VLM-based semantic tokens with continuous energy fields and Lagrangian optimization; results on standard and long-tail benchmarks support its potential for robust planning and perception integration.

Abstract: Scaling end-to-end autonomous driving to complex, open-world environments requires perceptual models that generalize to anomalous scenarios and planners that produce kinematically valid trajectories. Existing paradigms face a distinct dichotomy between representational efficiency and generalization capacity. Dense models (e.g., occupancy networks), while geometrically robust, incur critical computational bottlenecks and struggle with high-level semantic reasoning. Conversely, sparse, query-based planners are efficient but reliant on closed-set definitions, rendering them vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) events. Although recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer open-vocabulary reasoning, their autoregressive, discrete token generation fundamentally conflicts with the continuous, high-frequency control requirements of vehicle dynamics. To address this, we propose Lagrange, an open-vocabulary, computationally sparse driving framework based on Masked Latent Fields (MLF). Rather than relying on dense volumetric reconstructions or closed-set query mechanisms, Lagrange exploits Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic visual tokens. We introduce an intent-driven masked cross-attention module that temporally filters irrelevant entities, decoding the attended tokens into an implicit continuous energy field defined over spatial coordinates. By framing decision-making as a Lagrangian action minimization problem spanning this energy field, we enforce strict compliance with vehicle kinematics while executing collision avoidance. Extensive offline evaluations on both standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks demonstrate that Lagrange establishes a promising framework for robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy.

</details>


### [353] [Leveraging systems' non-linearity to tackle the scarcity of data in the design of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20323)
*Giancarlo Santamato,Andrea Mattia Garavagno,Massimiliano Solazzi,Antonio Frisoli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DTL-based vibration fault diagnosis under data scarcity using a periodic multi-excitation strategy to convert vibration signals into CNN-friendly images, with a novel visualization/augmentation method; validated on a railway pantograph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome reliance on large labeled datasets in intelligent fault diagnosis by leveraging deep transfer learning and image-based representations of vibration data; exploit system non-linearities to generate informative images.

Method: A periodic multi-excitation level procedure that leverages intrinsic nonlinearities to produce images from vibration data; introduces a data visualization method and augmentation technique; uses pre-trained CNNs for fault diagnosis; experimental evaluation on a railway pantograph structure.

Result: Experimental validation provides effective support for the proposed method, indicating feasibility of accurate fault diagnosis under data scarcity.

Conclusion: The approach offers a viable pathway for DTL-based intelligent fault diagnosis in data-scarce environments, with demonstration on a railway pantograph and potential applicability to other systems; further work could extend quantitative performance and generalizability.

Abstract: Deep Transfer Learning (DTL) allows for the efficient building of Intelligent Fault Diagnosis Systems (IFDS). On the other hand, DTL methods still heavily rely on large amounts of labelled data. Obtaining such an amount of data can be challenging when dealing with machines or structures faults.
