This document proposes a novel approach to the design of vibration-based IFDS using DTL in condition of strong data scarcity. A periodic multi-excitation level procedure leveraging intrinsic non-linearities of real-world systems is used to produce images that can be conveniently analysed by pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to diagnose faults. A new data visualization method and its augmentation technique are proposed in this paper to tackle the typical lack of data encountered during the design of IFDS. Experimental validation on a railway pantograph structure provides effective support for the proposed method.

</details>


### [354] [SoftSkill: Behavioral Compression for Contextual Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20333)
*Xijia Tao,Yihua Teng,Xinyu Fu,Ziru Liu,Kecheng Chen,Yuzhi Zhao,Suiyun Zhang,Rui Liu,Lingpeng Kong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SoftSkill enables latent, trainable, compact context prompts (soft skills) for frozen LMs, achieving strong gains over markdown-based skills and other baselines while using far fewer tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce dependence on lengthy natural-language skill artifacts by representing skills as compact continuous prompts that condition a frozen model’s behavior, improving efficiency and potentially compressing long-horizon task behavior.

Method: Train a 32-token soft skill prefix (SoftSkill) via next-token prediction on a frozen backbone (Qwen3.5-4B). Deploy the resulting latent priors at inference. Compare with no-skill prompting, Markdown-based skills (e.g., SkillOpt), and analyze agentic execution with sparse trajectory imitation across several tasks.

Result: Single-round setting shows improvements: +8.3 on SearchQA, +42.1 on LiveMath, +1.3 on DocVQA with the SoftSkill prefix vs. no-skill. Relative to SkillOpt: +5.2 (SearchQA) and +12.5 (LiveMath). The approach replaces hundreds/thousands of Markdown tokens with a few virtual tokens and demonstrates that latent skills can outperform token-based skill artifacts; agentic execution remains challenging but can leverage sparse signals.

Conclusion: Some task skills are better realized as compact latent controls over how a frozen model enters a task rather than as lengthy natural-language instructions. SoftSkill demonstrates that you can improve performance and efficiency by learning latent prompts for a frozen LM, suggesting a broader applicability of latent skill priors for robust, scalable AI agents.

Abstract: Agent skills are commonly deployed as natural-language Markdown files that encode answer policies, evidence-use habits, and task procedures. These files are readable and portable, but they are consumed indirectly: for each task instance, a frozen language model must translate a long textual artifact into generation-time behavior. This paper asks whether a natural-language skill can instead initialize a compact continuous context object, refined by a trainable soft delta while the base model remains frozen. We propose SoftSkill, a frozen-backbone method that tunes such soft skills with next-token prediction and deploys them as latent behavioral priors at inference time. In our main single-round setting, a length-32 SoftSkill prefix on Qwen3.5-4B improves over no-skill prompting by 8.3 points on SearchQA, 42.1 points on LiveMath, and 1.3 points on DocVQA. Relative to SkillOpt, SoftSkill improves accuracy by 5.2 points on SearchQA and 12.5 points on LiveMath, while replacing hundreds to thousands of Markdown skill tokens with a few virtual tokens. We further study agentic execution as a harder boundary case, where sparse trajectory imitation provides useful signal but does not yet robustly compress long-horizon procedural behavior. More broadly, the results suggest that some task skills are better treated not as additional Markdown to be reinterpreted at inference time, but as compact latent controls over how a frozen model enters the task.

</details>


### [355] [Automating SKILL.md Generation for Computer-Using Agents via Interaction Trajectory Mining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20363)
*Yuexing Hao,Xiaomin Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mined skill clusters from GUI trajectories show readability but do not reliably improve cross-domain policies; diagnostic study highlighting limitations of boundary detection, orderless segments, and offline reward models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether explicit skill libraries derived from interaction data can improve downstream policies and transfer across domains.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: segment GUI trajectories, cluster segments into candidate skills, train skill-aware policy from annotations; evaluate cluster readability via purity against IW labels; assess cross-domain transfer with GRPO and BrowseComp+ metrics; compare to frequency priors.

Result: Five of eight clusters achieve purity ≥ 0.95 against InteraSkill Workflows labels; GRPO improves IW skill-step accuracy only from 18.5% to 20.5%; BrowseComp+ essentially unchanged; outperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics.

Conclusion: Trajectory mining reveals inspectable skill structure, but the boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement; the study is diagnostic rather than enabling transfer.

Abstract: Explicit skill libraries make computer-using agents easier to inspect, but it remains unclear whether such libraries can be mined from interaction data in a way that improves downstream policies. We study this question through a three-stage pipeline that segments GUI trajectories, clusters segments into candidate skills, and trains a skill-aware policy from the resulting annotations. The mined clusters are readable on the source benchmark: five of eight clusters have at least 0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels. However, readability does not imply transfer. GRPO improves IW skill-step accuracy only from 18.5\% to 20.5\%, leaves BrowseComp+ essentially unchanged, and underperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics. We therefore present the method as a diagnostic study: trajectory mining can expose inspectable skill structure, but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement.

</details>


### [356] [Rethinking Shrinkage Bias in LLM FP4 Pretraining: Geometric Origin, Systemic Impact, and UFP4 Recipe](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20381)
*Qian Zhao,Kunlong Chen,Changxin Tian,Zhonghui Jiang,Haitao Zhang,Chaofan Yu,Peijie Jiang,Mingliang Gong,Jia Liu,Ziqi Liu,Zhiqiang Zhang,Jun Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Uniform 4-bit training (UFP4) with E1M2/INT4 grids and Random Hadamard Transform (RHT) improves stability and quantization quality over traditional E2M1 FP4 schemes by removing grid-geometry bias (Shrinkage Bias).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current FP4 implementations (E2M1) suffer from Shrinkage Bias due to non-uniform grids, causing multiplicative error accumulation and training instability—especially when combined with RHT.

Method: Introduce UFP4: apply RHT to all training GEMMs while constraining stochastic rounding to dY; use uniform 4-bit grids E1M2/INT4; compare with E2M1 baselines across Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B pretraining; support with scaling laws and ablations.

Result: UFP4 yields lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1 baselines across all tested scales; uniform grids bypass grid-geometry bias and better translate higher bucket utilization from RHT into quantization quality.

Conclusion: Future accelerators should provide first-class support for E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids alongside E2M1 to improve FP4 training stability and performance.

Abstract: FP4 training promises substantial reductions in memory and computation cost for LLM pretraining, yet current FP4 hardware paths and recipes, including NVIDIA Blackwell/Rubin-class systems and AMD MI350-series GPUs, remain centered on E2M1 data elements. In this study, we identify a fundamental limitation of that choice: non-uniform formats such as E2M1 inherently suffer from Shrinkage Bias, a systematic negative rounding error caused by the geometric asymmetry of their representable bins. We show that this bias accumulates multiplicatively across layers and is amplified by the Random Hadamard Transform (RHT), providing a unified explanation for the training instability observed in existing E2M1-based FP4 recipes. In contrast, uniform grids (E1M2/INT4) bypass this grid-geometry error and better convert the improved bucket utilization from RHT into higher quantization quality. Based on this finding, we propose UFP4, a uniform 4-bit training recipe that applies RHT to all three training GEMMs while restricting stochastic rounding to dY alone. On Dense 1.5B, MoE 7.9B, and MoE 124B long-run pretraining, UFP4 consistently achieves lower BF16-relative loss degradation than strong E2M1-based baselines, supported by scaling-law analysis and ablation studies. Our results suggest that future accelerators should support E1M2/INT4-style uniform 4-bit grids as first-class training primitives alongside E2M1.

</details>


### [357] [Interpretable Sperm Morphology Classification via Attention-Guided Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20438)
*Zahra Asghari Varzaneh,Reza Khoshkangini,Thomas Ebner,Lars Johansson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An attention-guided EfficientNet-B0 framework with CBAM improves sperm morphology classification, achieving ~90–94% accuracy and macro F1 ~0.913–0.948 on SMIDS and HuSHem, with Grad-CAM++ explanations for interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limited interpretability of deep learning models in automated sperm morphology analysis and to provide a clinically actionable, transparent tool for fertility assessments.

Method: Utilizes a pretrained EfficientNet-B0 backbone augmented with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on salient regions of the sperm head. The model is trained/evaluated on public datasets SMIDS and HuSHem, and its predictions are complemented by Grad-CAM++ visualizations to highlight influential features.

Result: Achieves accuracies of 90.2% (SMIDS) and 93.9% (HuSHem) with macro F1 scores of 0.913 and 0.948, respectively, outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0 baselines. Grad-CAM++ visualizations illustrate the features driving decisions.

Conclusion: The proposed attention-guided framework provides a competitive, transparent approach for automated sperm analysis, with potential clinical applicability in fertility clinics. Further external validation and ablation studies would strengthen claims of interpretability and generalizability.

Abstract: Male infertility is a major cause of couple infertility, often linked to abnormal sperm morphology. While deep learning models offer automated analysis, most lack interpretability, limiting their clinical adoption. This study proposes an attention-guided deep learning framework for sperm morphology classification. We combine a pretrained EfficientNet-B0 with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to focus on key areas of the sperm head, improving both accuracy and interpretability. Evaluated on the SMIDS and HuSHem public datasets, our model achieves accuracies of 90.2% and 93.9% (macro F1 scores of 0.913 and 0.948), outperforming SimpleCNN and standard EfficientNet-B0. Furthermore, we use Grad-CAM++ visualizations to highlight features influencing the model's decisions. The results demonstrate that this accurate and transparent framework is a practical tool for automated sperm analysis in fertility clinics.

</details>


### [358] [Context-Aware Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling of IVF Laboratory Environmental Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20459)
*Zahra Asghari Varzaneh,Reza Khoshkangini,Pia Saldeen,Lars Johansson,Thomas Ebner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Context-aware temporal features from incubator environmental data markedly improve IVF outcome prediction and generalize across clinics via hierarchical Bayesian modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution lab environmental data are underutilized in IVF pregnancy-rate modeling; leveraging them could improve predictive accuracy and cross-site generalization.

Method: Engineer 55 context-aware temporal features capturing dynamics of incubator microenvironments; train hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression with partial pooling across Asian and Northern European clinics to share environmental effects while preserving site baselines.

Result: Cross-validated prediction error reduced to 1.27% (from 3–5% using raw averages); hold-out R2 = 0.86 for Northern European clinic; 64% error reduction for age 35–39 versus naive baseline.

Conclusion: Structured environmental monitoring provides transferable, clinically meaningful signal for IVF outcome prediction; environmental features can augment patient-level models and generalize across sites.

Abstract: IVF pregnancy rates are routinely modeled using patient-level variables, while high-resolution laboratory environmental data remain underutilized. We show that this is a missed opportunity. Rather than relying on raw sensor averages, we engineer 55 context-aware temporal features, including rolling thermal stability, simultaneous temperature-humidity adherence, peak stress duration, and post-stress recovery speed, that capture the dynamics of incubator microenvironments. On 61 weeks of data from an Asian IVF clinic, these features reduce cross-validated prediction error to 1.27%, compared to 3-5% for raw averages. We then train a hierarchical Bayesian Beta regression model that shares environmental effects across an Asian and a Northern European clinic via partial pooling, while preserving site-specific baselines. On held-out data from the Northern European clinic, the model achieves R2 = 0.86 and a 64% error reduction for the 35-39 age group over a naive baseline, demonstrating that structured environmental monitoring contains clinically meaningful, transferable signal.

</details>


### [359] [What Do Safety-Aligned LLMs Learn From Mixed Compliance Demonstrations?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20508)
*Sihui Dai,Mann Patel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Benign vs harmful in-context demonstrations are not interchangeable; model responses to demonstrations depend on content, order, and training; preference optimization mitigates harm; there is a strong recency bias; models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the mechanisms of in-context jailbreaking via demonstrations and why benign demonstrations can sometimes increase harmful compliance, and to identify training factors that modulate this risk.

Method: Empirical study mixing benign (non-harmful) and harmful (harmful) compliance demonstrations across four models; vary demonstration content and ordering; test hypotheses about harmful compliance; analyze the role of preference optimization and refusal behavior.

Result: Benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model; preference optimization is critical to preventing benign demonstrations from increasing harm; demonstration ordering shows strong recency bias; models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning (some imitate demonstrated formatting when refusing, others override in-context signals).

Conclusion: Provides a nuanced account of how models extract from compliance demonstrations, showing that outcome depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology; offers guidance for designing safer prompts and training processes to mitigate jailbreaking risk.

Abstract: Prior work has shown that in-context demonstrations can jailbreak language models, but it remains unclear how models interpret different types of compliance demonstrations. We study this by mixing benign compliance demonstrations (non-harmful request, helpful response) with harmful compliance demonstrations (harmful request, helpful response) and testing three hypotheses about how demonstration composition drives harmful compliance. Across four models, we find that benign and harmful demonstrations are not interchangeable: benign demonstrations can either reduce or increase harmful compliance depending on the model. We further show that preference optimization is the critical training stage that prevents benign demonstrations from increasing harmful compliance, that demonstration ordering exhibits strong recency bias, and that models differ in how refusal interacts with in-context learning: some adopt demonstrated formatting even when refusing, while others override all in-context signals upon refusal. Taken together, this work moves beyond showing that demonstration-based jailbreaking works to characterizing how it works: what models extract from compliance demonstrations depends on demonstration content, ordering, and training methodology.

</details>


### [360] [Multi-LCB: Extending LiveCodeBench to Multiple Programming Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20517)
*Maria Ivanova,Pavel Zadorozhny,Rodion Levichev,Ivan Petrov,Adamenko Pavel,Ivan Lopatin,Alexey Kutalev,Dmitrii Babaev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multi-LCB extends the Python-centric LiveCodeBench (LCB) to twelve programming languages by transforming Python tasks into equivalent tasks across languages while preserving contamination controls, enabling cross-language code-generation evaluation. It evaluates 24 LLMs and finds Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and multilingual performance gaps, establishing a rigorous benchmark for multilingual code evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LCB is limited to Python, hindering assessment of LLMs' cross-language generalization vital for real-world software engineering. A cross-language benchmark is needed to study how LLMs perform across programming languages under contamination-aware evaluation.

Method: Translate Python tasks from LCB into equivalent tasks in 12 languages, preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Maintain full compatibility with the original LCB format so updates propagate automatically. Use Multi-LCB to evaluate 24 LLMs on instruction and reasoning tasks across languages.

Result: Empirical evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance. Positions Multi-LCB as a rigorous new benchmark for cross-language code evaluation and highlights critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.

Conclusion: Multi-LCB successfully addresses LCB's primary limitation by enabling cross-language evaluation, enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code-generation competence, and revealing gaps in current LLM performance across multiple languages.

Abstract: LiveCodeBench (LCB) has recently become a widely adopted benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) on code-generation tasks. By curating competitive programming problems, constantly adding fresh problems to the set, and filtering them by release dates, LCB provides contamination-aware evaluation and offers a holistic view of coding capability. However, LCB remains restricted to Python, leaving open the question of whether LLMs can generalize across the diverse programming languages required in real-world software engineering.
  We introduce Multi-LCB, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs across twelve programming languages, including Python. Multi-LCB transforms Python tasks from the LCB dataset into equivalent tasks in other languages while preserving LCB's contamination controls and evaluation protocol. Because it is fully compatible with the original LCB format, Multi-LCB will automatically track future LCB updates, enabling systematic assessment of cross-language code generation competence and requiring models to sustain performance well beyond Python.
  We evaluated 24 LLMs for instruction and reasoning on Multi-LCB, uncovering evidence of Python overfitting, language-specific contamination, and substantial disparities in multilingual performance. Our results establish Multi-LCB as a rigorous new benchmark for multi-programming-language code evaluation, directly addressing LCB's primary limitation and exposing critical gaps in current LLM capabilities.

</details>


### [361] [FlowEdit: Associative Memory for Lifelong Pronunciation Adaptation in Flow-Matching TTS](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20518)
*Harshit Singh,Ayush Pratap Singh,Nityanand Mathur*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FlowEdit enables life-long adaptation for frozen flow-matching TTS by learning pronunciation corrections as token-space edits stored in a Modern Hopfield Network, enabling retrieval for out-of-vocabulary proper nouns with a 92.7% relative phoneme error-rate improvement and minimal impact on general speech quality; ~15s corrections on a single GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Out-of-vocabulary proper nouns cause persistent pronunciation errors in static, deploy-time TTS; need lifelong, non-invasive adaptation without retraining.

Method: Apply token-level perturbations to the input text embedding, store corrections in a Modern Hopfield Network as episodic memory; retrieve via soft attention with a similarity gate at inference to apply corrections for pronunciation.

Result: On a curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to zero-shot baseline; preserves general speech quality; corrections complete in ~15 seconds on a single GPU.

Conclusion: FlowEdit demonstrates a practical approach for lifelong pronunciation adaptation in frozen TTS models, using editable latent conditioning with fast retrieval, enabling robust handling of multilingual proper nouns without retraining.

Abstract: Flow-matching text-to-speech systems achieve remarkable zero-shot quality but remain static after deployment: pronunciation errors on out-of-vocabulary proper nouns persist unless the model is retrained. We introduce FlowEdit, a life-long adaptation framework for frozen flow-matching TTS that learns pronunciation corrections as latent conditioning edits rather than weight updates. When corrective feedback is provided, FlowEdit optimizes a token-level perturbation in the text embedding space, then stores the correction in a Modern Hopfield Network serving as content-addressable episodic memory. At inference, corrections are retrieved via soft attention with a similarity gate, enabling fuzzy morphological matching. On our curated benchmark of 312 multilingual proper nouns across 18 language families, FlowEdit reduces target-word Phoneme Error Rate by 92.7% relative to the zero-shot baseline while maintaining identical general-speech quality. Corrections complete in approximately 15 seconds on a single GPU.

</details>


### [362] [DeepSWIP: Quotient-WMC Counterfactuals for Neural Probabilistic Logic Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20526)
*Saimun Habib,Vaishak Belle,Fengxiang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeepSWIP provides a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog by neural materialization, transforming neural predicates into ProbLog choices and using WMC for counterfactuals. It yields exactness under finite grounding and reports speedups and calibration insights in experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neurosymbolic systems like DeepProbLog are currently associative in inference; counterfactual reasoning requires a causal semantics for interventions and evidence, motivating a unified semantic framework.

Method: Neural materialization converts fixed-context neural predicates into ordinary ProbLog choices, enabling Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs). Counterfactuals are computed via weighted model counting on a single transformed program; analyses rely on finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions.

Result: Exactness relative to the learned materialized factorized causal model (FCM) under stated assumptions. MPI3D experiments show a 2.14× speedup versus DeepTwin with 12,000 queries. A SUMO HOV experiment shows calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates; a randomized-policy AIPW estimator mitigates most first-order bias for population mean and ATE.

Conclusion: DeepSWIP provides a practical and principled approach to counterfactual reasoning in neurosymbolic systems, clarifying intervention semantics and offering improved efficiency and bias control in causal estimates. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Neurosymbolic systems such as DeepProbLog combine neural perception with probabilistic logic, but standard inference is associational. Counterfactual reasoning additionally requires a causal semantics for interventions and evidence. We introduce DeepSWIP, a single-world counterfactual semantics for DeepProbLog programs. Using neural materialization, we reduce fixed-context neural predicates to ordinary ProbLog choices, apply Single World Intervention Programs (SWIPs), and compute counterfactuals by weighted model counting (WMC) over a single transformed program. Under finite grounding and unique-supported-model assumptions, DeepSWIP is exact relative to the learned materialized FCM. The standard quotient-WMC form of ProbLog conditionals identifies active neural probabilities and explains intervention cleaning, calibration sensitivity, and rare-evidence instability. Experiments on MPI3D confirm the transformation against a DeepTwin construction against 12,000 queries, as predicted and a 2.14$\times$ inference speedup from avoiding the Twin's endogenous duplication. A SUMO HOV experiment shows that neural calibration degradation biases plug-in estimates, while a correctly scoped randomized-policy AIPW estimator removes most first-order bias for population mean and ATE estimands. Code is at https://github.com/saibib/deep_SWIP.

</details>


### [363] [LedgerAgent: Structured State for Policy-Adherent Tool-Calling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20529)
*Md Nayem Uddin,Amir Saeidi,Eduardo Blanco,Chitta Baral*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LedgerAgent introduces a separate ledger to maintain observed task states for policy-adherent tool-calling agents, improving consistency and policy compliance. It renders these states into prompts and blocks policy-violating tool calls. Evaluated on four customer-service domains with mixed model weights, it yields higher pass@k than standard prompt-based prompts, especially under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard policy-adherent tool-calling agents embed observations, tool outputs, and policies directly in the prompt, causing implicit, evolving state representations. This leads to stale/missing information influencing decisions and policy violations despite valid tool calls. An explicit, maintained task-state ledger could reduce grounding errors and enforce constraints.

Method: Inferencing-time approach that maintains observed task states in a separate ledger. The ledger is rendered into the prompt to inform decision-making and is checked for state-dependent policy constraints before executing environment-changing tool calls, thereby blocking policy violations.

Result: Empirical evaluation across four customer-service domains with a mixed panel of open- and closed-weight models shows LedgerAgent achieving higher average pass@k than a standard prompt-based baseline, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.

Conclusion: Explicit task-state management via a ledger improves robustness of policy-adherent tool-calling agents, mitigating stale-grounding and policy-violation issues and delivering measurable gains across domains and model types.

Abstract: Policy-adherent tool-calling agents in customer-service domains must maintain task states across turns while calling tools and obeying domain policies. Task states consist of relevant facts, identifiers, constraints, and conditions observed through user interaction and tool calls. In standard agents, task states are not represented separately. Observations, tool returns, and policy instructions are placed in the prompt, leaving agents to reconstruct the relevant states from the prompt each time they decide what to do next. This design makes state management implicit, creating two common failure modes. An agent may retrieve the right facts but later ground its decision in stale, missing, or incorrect information; and a syntactically valid tool call may still violate a domain policy that depends on the current task state. We introduce \textsc{LedgerAgent}, an inference-time method for tool-calling agents that maintains observed task states in a separate ledger and renders the states into the prompt. The ledger is also used to check state-dependent policy constraints before environment-changing tool calls are executed, blocking policy violations. Across four customer-service domains and a mixed panel of open- and closed-weight models, \textsc{LedgerAgent} improves average pass\textasciicircum{}k over a standard prompt-based tool-calling approach, with the largest gains under stricter multi-trial consistency metrics.

</details>


### [364] [How Do Instructions Shape Speech? Cross-Attention Attribution for Style-Captioned Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20532)
*Nityanand Mathur,Hamees Sayed,Wasim Madha,Apoorv Singh,Sameer Khurana,Akshat Mandloi,Sudarshan Kamath*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cross-attention attribution reveals how style-caption words shape CapSpeech-TTS outputs in a diffusion model, showing global conditioning, early-stage/style-strong influence, and prosodic correlations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand precisely how individual words in style captions influence the acoustic output, enabling diagnosis of failure modes and improved controllability in expressive TTS.

Method: Adapt the DAAM cross-attention attribution framework to speech diffusion models and apply it to CapSpeech-TTS. Compute per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps for 3,600 style-caption/text pairs (120 captions × 30 transcripts).

Result: Findings indicate: (1) style tokens exhibit lower temporal variance than content tokens, indicating global conditioning; (2) style attention correlates with F0 and energy; (3) style conditioning peaks in early steps and in deep layers; (4) attention entropy minimizes at layer 17, co-occurring with a peak in style importance, suggesting maximal selectivity at the most style-critical stage.

Conclusion: This work is the first study to analyze how natural language controls cross-attention in speech diffusion models and demonstrates how style captions guide waveform synthesis in CapSpeech-TTS.

Abstract: Style-captioned text-to-speech systems use natural language to control voice characteristics, but how individual words influence acoustic output remains unclear. Understanding this is critical for diagnosing failure modes and improving controllability in expressive TTS. We propose cross-attention attribution for speech diffusion models, adapting the DAAM framework to the speech domain for the first time, and apply it to CapSpeech-TTS. Our method extracts per-token heatmaps across 25 layers and 24 ODE steps. We analyze 3,600 (style caption, text transcript) combinations comprising 120 style captions conditioning the generation of 30 text transcripts each, revealing how caption tokens shape waveforms. Results show: (1) style tokens have lower temporal variance than content/function tokens, confirming global conditioning; (2) style attention correlates with F0 and energy; (3) style conditioning peaks in early steps and deep layers; (4) attention entropy reaches its minimum at layer 17, co-occurring with the style importance peak, indicating maximal network selectivity at the most style-critical stage. This is the first study of how natural language influences cross-attention in speech diffusion models

</details>


### [365] [Toward Calibrated Mixture-of-Experts Under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.20544)
*Gina Wong,Drew Prinster,Suchi Saria,Rama Chellappa,Anqi Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Expert calibration suffices for calibrating MoEs with hard routing under distribution shift; soft routing breaks this guarantee; an adversarial calibration penalty improves accuracy-calibration tradeoffs across models, tasks, and shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand when calibration helps mixture-of-experts (MoE) models under distribution shift and develop methods to enforce robust calibration, with particular attention to routing mechanics.

Method: Theoretical analysis distinguishing hard versus soft MoE routing under distribution shift; show that expert-level calibration implies model calibration for hard-routed MoEs but not for soft-routed MoEs; introduce an adversarial reweighting objective that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift; empirical evaluation across model classes, tasks, and distribution shifts.

Result: For hard routing, expert calibration ensures overall model calibration under broad distribution shifts. For soft routing, expert calibration is not sufficient to guarantee calibration. The proposed adversarial calibration penalty improves the accuracy–calibration tradeoff on average and on difficult data subsets, across model classes, tasks, and distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Calibration of MoEs must account for routing type; expert-level calibration alone is inadequate for soft routing. An adversarial calibration penalty provides robust improvements in calibration and accuracy across diverse settings and shifts.

Abstract: Calibration aligns a model's predictive uncertainty with the frequencies of its empirical outcomes and is important for understanding and trusting reported probabilities. Recent work shows that enforcing calibration at the level of individual predictors can improve ensemble accuracy and calibration, with mixture-of-experts (MoE) models showing strong empirical improvements in particular; however, the conditions under which calibration helps MoE are not well understood. In this work, we study how MoE models behave under distribution shift, focusing on how routing mechanisms interact with expert-level calibration. We show that expert calibration is sufficient to ensure calibration of the overall model under a broad class of distribution shifts in hard-routed models, but is insufficient for calibrating soft-routed models. To address this, we propose an adversarial reweighting that penalizes calibration errors of the routed aggregate under distribution shift, and we demonstrate that it improves the accuracy-calibration tradeoff both on average and on difficult subsets of the data, across model classes, prediction tasks, and distribution shifts.

</details>
